## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 1]**
*Define the terms absorptance ($\alpha$), reflectance ($\rho$), and transmittance ($\tau$). State Kirchhoff's law and its significance in the design of a solar absorber.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Absorptance $\alpha$** is the ratio of absorbed radiant flux to incident radiant flux on a surface (1 mark).
- **Reflectance $\rho$** is the fraction reflected; **Transmittance $\tau$** is the fraction transmitted through the surface (1 mark).
- For an opaque surface, $\alpha + \rho = 1$. **Kirchhoff's law** states that at thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emittance $\varepsilon_\lambda$ of a body equals its monochromatic absorptance $\alpha_\lambda$ at the same wavelength and temperature. Significance: a high-$\alpha$ surface also radiates strongly at high $T$, so selective coatings (high $\alpha$ for solar spectrum, low $\varepsilon$ for IR) are essential to minimize re-radiation loss (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
*List the major components of a flat-plate solar collector and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Transparent glass cover** — admits short-wave solar radiation, suppresses convection loss, traps long-wave re-radiation (greenhouse effect) — 1 mark.
2. **Absorber plate** — absorbs solar radiation and conducts heat to riser tubes; selective coating minimizes emission — 1 mark.
3. **Tubes / risers with HTF** — carry the absorbed heat away as useful energy — 0.5 mark.
4. **Back insulation** (rock wool / PU foam) and **casing** (aluminum frame) — minimize conduction/convection loss from rear and sides — 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to write the HWB equation without defining every symbol. KTU 2024 scheme mandates **Course Outcome (CO) mapping** — a missing symbol or undefined variable costs **1–2 marks**. Also, students often confuse $T_{pm}$ (plate mean) with $T_{fm}$ (fluid mean) — examiners deduct for this.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]*

**(a)** Derive the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation for useful heat gain of a flat-plate solar collector. Define each term clearly. **7 marks**

**(b)** A flat-plate solar water heater has the following operating data:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Aperture area $A_c$ | 2.5 m² |
| Global tilted irradiance $I_T$ | 900 W/m² |
| Effective $(\tau \alpha)_e$ | 0.76 |
| Overall loss coefficient $U_L$ | 4.8 W/m²·K |
| Mean plate temperature $T_{pm}$ | 65 °C |
| Ambient temperature $T_a$ | 28 °C |
| Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ | 0.025 kg/s |
| Specific heat $c_p$ | 4180 J/kg·K |
| Inlet fluid temperature $T_{fi}$ | 42 °C |
| Heat removal factor $F_R$ | 0.84 |

**Compute:**
- (i) Useful heat gain $Q_u$ and instantaneous efficiency $\eta_i$. **4 marks**
- (ii) Outlet fluid temperature $T_{fo}$. **3 marks**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Energy absorbed per unit aperture area by the plate:
$$S = I_T (\tau \alpha)_e$$

(0.5 mark for setting up; 0.5 mark for identification of $(\tau \alpha)_e$.)

**Step 2:** Heat loss from the plate to the surroundings using the lumped loss coefficient:
$$\dot{Q}_{loss} = A_c U_L (T_{pm} - T_a)$$

(1 mark — define $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$.)

**Step 3:** Steady-state energy balance on the absorber:
$$A_c S = \dot{Q}_{loss} + Q_u$$

(1 mark — state First Law under steady state.)

**Step 4:** Solving for useful gain:
$$Q_u = A_c \left[ I_T (\tau \alpha)_e - U_L (T_{pm} - T_a) \right]$$

(1 mark — final HWB form.)

**Step 5:** Instantaneous efficiency:
$$\eta_i = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_T} = (\tau \alpha)_e - \frac{U_L (T_{pm} - T_a)}{I_T}$$

(1 mark — definition; the form $\eta_i$ vs $T_{fi}$ is implied.)

**Step 6:** With $F_R$:
$$\eta_i = F_R \left[ (\tau \alpha)_e - \frac{U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)}{I_T} \right]$$

(1 mark — show how $F_R$ replaces $T_{pm}$ with $T_{fi}$.)

**Step 7:** Definition of $F_R$:
$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p}{A_c U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} c_p}\right) \right]$$

(1 mark — final form, with $F'$ defined.)

---

**Part (b)(i) — Useful Heat Gain and Efficiency (4 marks):**

$$Q_u = A_c \left[ I_T (\tau \alpha)_e - U_L (T_{pm} - T_a) \right]$$

Numerically:

$$Q_u = 2.5 \left[ 900 \cdot 0.76 - 4.8 \cdot (65 - 28) \right]$$

Compute each sub-term:

$$900 \cdot 0.76 = 684 \text{ W/m}^2$$
$$65 - 28 = 37 \text{ K}$$
$$4.8 \cdot 37 = 177.6 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Bracket value:

$$684 - 177.6 = 506.4 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Therefore:

$$Q_u = 2.5 \cdot 506.4 = 1266 \text{ W} \;\; (\textbf{2 marks})$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta_i = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_T} = \frac{1266}{2.5 \cdot 900} = \frac{1266}{2250} = 0.5627 \;\; (\textbf{2 marks})$$

---

**Part (b)(ii) — Outlet Fluid Temperature (3 marks):**

$$\dot{m} c_p = 0.025 \cdot 4180 = 104.5 \text{ W/K}$$

Energy balance on fluid:

$$Q_u = \dot{m} c_p (T_{fo} - T_{fi})$$

$$T_{fo} = T_{fi} + \frac{Q_u}{\dot{m} c_p} = 42 + \frac{1266}{104.5}$$

$$T_{fo} = 42 + 12.11 = 54.11 \; °\text{C} \;\; (\textbf{3 marks})$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Regular Exam]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a flat-plate solar collector. Discuss the role of selective coatings. **7 marks**

**(b)** Explain the different types of solar thermal collectors based on concentration ratio and operating temperature. List two applications each of (i) flat-plate collector and (ii) parabolic trough collector. **7 marks**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction, Working & Selective Coatings (7 marks):**

**Construction (Diagram — textual reference, 2 marks):**
The flat-plate collector consists of:
1. A low-iron tempered **glass cover** at the top (~4 mm thick) — admits solar irradiance while blocking long-wave re-emission.
2. An **air gap** (~25–40 mm) between cover and plate — reduces convective loss.
3. An **absorber plate** — typically copper or aluminum — coated with a **selective surface** (black chrome, TiNOX, cermet).
4. **Tube risers** (10–15 mm OD) bonded to the plate carry the HTF.
5. **Back insulation** (rock wool, 50 mm) and a **metal casing** (aluminum frame) seal the assembly.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
Solar radiation $I_T$ penetrates the glass with transmittance $\tau_g$. The absorber plate with absorptance $\alpha_p$ absorbs the fraction $(\tau_g \cdot \alpha_p)$ of incident radiation. The absorbed energy is conducted through the plate thickness to the bonded tubes, where it is transferred by forced convection to the flowing fluid (water/antifreeze). The plate simultaneously loses heat from its top (radiation + convection), bottom (conduction through insulation), and edges (conduction through casing). The net useful heat is given by the HWB equation.

**Role of Selective Coatings (2 marks):**
A selective surface is engineered to have **high absorptance ($\alpha > 0.9$)** in the solar spectrum (0.3 – 2.5 $\mu$m) and **low emittance ($\varepsilon < 0.2$)** in the IR spectrum (2.5 – 50 $\mu$m). This breaks Kirchhoff's equality at the operating temperature range, drastically reducing top-loss radiation at elevated plate temperatures. Examples: black chrome ($\alpha = 0.95, \varepsilon = 0.10$), titanium nitride oxide (TiNOX), and black nickel.

---

**Part (b) — Classification & Applications (7 marks):**

**Classification of Solar Thermal Collectors (5 marks):**

| Class | Concentration | Operating T | Tracking | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-concentrating** | C = 1 | 30 – 100 °C | None | FPC, ETC |
| **Low concentration** | C = 2 – 10 | 100 – 250 °C | Seasonal/None | CPC |
| **Medium concentration** | C = 10 – 100 | 150 – 400 °C | Single-axis | PTC, LFR |
| **High concentration** | C = 100 – 10000 | 400 – 3000 °C | Dual-axis | Parabolic Dish, Solar Tower |

The HWB efficiency $\eta = (\tau \alpha)_e - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) / I_T$ decreases linearly as the operating temperature rises — this is why concentrating collectors are needed for high-temperature applications.

**Applications (2 marks):**

(i) **Flat-Plate Collector:**
- Domestic solar water heating (Kerala rooftop mandate).
- Solar drying of spices, coconut, and copra (Kerala agriculture).
- Solar space heating in residential and commercial buildings.
- Pre-heating of boiler feed-water in small industries.

(ii) **Parabolic Trough Collector:**
- Utility-scale solar thermal power plants (e.g., SEGS California, Rajasthan).
- Industrial process steam generation at 200–300 °C.
- Solar refrigeration and absorption cooling.
- Solar-enhanced oil recovery (EOR).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss areas in FPC problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert temperatures to Kelvin or mixing °C and K** — examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Using $T_{pm}$ and $T_{fi}$ interchangeably** — these are *different* physical temperatures; $T_{pm}$ is the plate mean, $T_{fi}$ is the fluid inlet. Use the HWB form with $F_R$ to swap them.
> 3. **Omitting the IAM correction** when computing $(\tau \alpha)_e$ at oblique incidence — for $\theta > 30°$, this can shift the answer by 3–5%.
> 4. **Writing "efficiency = useful gain"** — efficiency is *dimensionless*, useful gain has units of W. Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Missing units in intermediate steps** — KTU mandates unit display for each numerical sub-step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **The HWB equation** $Q_u = A_c[S - U_L(T_{pm} - T_a)]$ is the single most-tested identity on FPCs — derive it cold.
- ✅ **Three loss pathways:** $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$ — know the physical driver of each.
- ✅ **$(\tau \alpha)_e$** combines cover transmission and plate absorption — modified by $K_{\tau \alpha}$ for oblique angles.
- ✅ **Heat removal factor $F_R$** is dimensionless, bounded between $F'$ (no flow) and 1 (infinite flow).
- ✅ **Selective surfaces** break Kirchhoff's law by exploiting spectral separation — high $\alpha$ for short-wave, low $\varepsilon$ for long-wave.
- ✅ **FPC efficiency ceiling** at $T_{fi} = T_a$ is approximately $(\tau \alpha)_e \approx 0.75$.
- ✅ **Concentration ratio C** = aperture area / absorber area; for FPC, $C = 1$.
- ✅ **For Kerala climate** (tropical humid), diffuse fraction is high; an FPC still works well, but ETCs outperform in winter (Dec–Feb) due to lower $U_L$.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 CO linkage:** Module 1 maps to CO1 — *Understand the principles of solar energy conversion* — every FPC derivation satisfies this outcome.
- ✅ **Common solar constant value:** $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m² (AM 0); at AM 1.5 on a south-facing 30° tilted surface, expect ~800–1000 W/m² in Kerala midday.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Solar Electric Systems — Formal Definition

**Solar Electric Systems** are engineered installations that convert incident solar radiation directly into electrical energy using two fundamentally distinct physical pathways:

1. **Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation (Concentrated Solar Power — CSP):** An *indirect* method where solar radiation is first converted into high-temperature thermal energy via concentrating collectors, and subsequently transformed into electricity through a thermodynamic heat-engine cycle (Rankine, Brayton, or Stirling).
2. **Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) Power Generation:** A *direct* method exploiting the **photovoltaic effect** in semiconductor p-n junctions, where absorbed photons liberate electron-hole pairs that are separated by the built-in junction field, producing DC electricity at the terminals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT632, Module 1):**
> Students must be able to *(i)* distinguish CSP from SPV architectures, *(ii)* sketch and explain the construction of a crystalline silicon solar cell, *(iii)* derive and interpret the I–V characteristic equation, and *(iv)* classify PV cells by generation, material, and crystalline structure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two Sun-Powered "Engines"

| Pathway | Real-World Analogy | Energy Conversion Chain |
|---|---|---|
| **Solar Thermal (CSP)** | A child using a magnifying glass to focus sunlight on a piece of paper until it smokes and burns — multi-step, requires concentration and high temperature. | Photon → Heat → Steam → Turbine → Electricity (**4 stages**) |
| **Solar Photovoltaic (SPV)** | A digital calculator's tiny solar panel that silently runs a circuit under desk-lamp light — no moving parts, no heat, instant. | Photon → Excited Electron → DC Current (**1 stage**) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Take-away:** CSP is a *thermal-engineering* problem (efficiency limited by Carnot), whereas SPV is a *quantum-semiconductor* problem (efficiency limited by the bandgap of the absorbing material). This single distinction dictates every design choice that follows.

## 1.3 Standard Solar Metrics (the constants every KTU paper expects)

- **Solar Constant (G\_SC):** $\mathbf{1361 \; W/m^2}$ — the extraterrestrial irradiance at 1 AU on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays (latest accepted value per WMO).
- **Air Mass (AM):** Ratio of the actual path length of solar rays through the atmosphere to the vertical (zenith) path length. **AM 1.5** (sun at 41.8° elevation) is the KTU/industry standard test spectrum, giving an incident power of **1000 W/m²** at the Earth's surface.
- **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** $G = 1000 \; W/m^2$, cell temperature $T_c = 25^\circ C = 298.15 \; K$, AM 1.5G spectrum.
- **Photon energy relation:** $\mathbf{E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}}$, where $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \; J\cdot s$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) — The Photovoltaic Effect

When a photon of energy $E_{ph} \ge E_g$ (bandgap energy) strikes a semiconductor:

- It is absorbed in the depletion region or within a diffusion length of it.
- An electron is excited from the **valence band (VB)** to the **conduction band (CB)**.
- A free electron (in CB) and a free hole (in VB) are created — this is the **electron-hole pair (EHP)**.
- The built-in electric field of the p-n junction sweeps the electron to the n-side and the hole to the p-side.
- If an external load is connected, a current flows — this is the **photocurrent $I_L$**.

## 2.2 The Ideal Solar Cell — Equivalent Circuit and Governing Equation

A solar cell is modelled as a **current source** (photogenerated $I_L$) in parallel with a **diode** (representing recombination), with series resistance $R_s$ and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.

**The Shockley Diode Equation under illumination (single-diode model):**

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

Where:
- $I$ = terminal current (A)
- $V$ = terminal voltage (V)
- $I_L$ = photogenerated current (A) — proportional to irradiance
- $I_0$ = diode reverse saturation current (A)
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \; C$ (electron charge)
- $n$ = ideality factor (1 ≤ n ≤ 2; n=1 for ideal)
- $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \; J/K$ (Boltzmann constant)
- $T$ = absolute cell temperature (K)
- $R_s$ = series resistance (ideally 0)
- $R_{sh}$ = shunt resistance (ideally ∞)

## 2.3 The Four Cardinal Points of the I–V Curve

The I–V curve is the **fingerprint of a solar cell**. Four operating points completely define it:

1. **Short-Circuit Current ($I_{SC}$):** Current when $V=0$. Numerically: $I_{SC} \approx I_L$ (when $R_{sh} \to \infty$).
2. **Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{OC}$):** Voltage when $I=0$.

$$V_{OC} = \dfrac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) \approx \dfrac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{SC}}{I_0}\right)$$

3. **Maximum Power Point ($V_{mp}, I_{mp}$):** Where the cell delivers peak DC power $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$.
4. **Fill Factor (FF):** A dimensionless measure of "square-ness" of the I–V curve.

$$FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}} = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}$$

## 2.4 Conversion Efficiency

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF}{A \cdot G}$$

Where $A$ = cell area (m²) and $G$ = incident irradiance (W/m²).

## 2.5 Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation — CSP Technologies

CSP systems are classified by the way sunlight is concentrated and by the heat-engine cycle used:

| CSP Technology | Concentration Ratio (C) | Working Fluid | Peak Temperature | Cycle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Parabolic Trough** | 30–100 | Synthetic oil / molten salt | 400 °C | Rankine (steam) |
| **Linear Fresnel Reflector** | 10–40 | Water / steam | 250–300 °C | Rankine (steam) |
| **Solar Tower (Central Receiver)** | 300–1500 | Molten salt / air | 565–1000 °C | Rankine / Brayton |
| **Parabolic Dish–Stirling** | 1000–2500 | Hydrogen / Helium | 750–850 °C | Stirling |

**Concentration Ratio:**

$$C = \dfrac{A_{aperture}}{A_{receiver}}$$

**Optical Efficiency of a concentrator:**

$$\eta_{optical} = \rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot \gamma \cdot K$$

Where $\rho$ = mirror reflectivity, $\tau$ = cover-glass transmissivity, $\alpha$ = receiver absorptivity, $\gamma$ = intercept factor, $K$ = cleanliness factor.

**Thermal Efficiency (Carnot limit for receiver temperature $T_H$ and ambient $T_C$):**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$$

**Overall Plant Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{plant} = \eta_{optical} \cdot \eta_{receiver} \cdot \eta_{cycle} \cdot \eta_{electrical}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{ph} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda (\text{nm})} \; \text{eV}$ | Photon energy in eV; $\lambda$ in nm | Use for $E_g$ comparison (Si: 1.12 eV → λ ≤ 1107 nm) |
| 2 | $I = I_L - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$ | Ideal diode eqn (no $R_s$, $R_{sh}$) | At STC: $\dfrac{kT}{q} = 25.85 \; mV$ |
| 3 | $V_{OC} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{SC}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage | Doubles $I_{SC}$ → $V_{OC}$ rises by $\sim$18 mV (Si) |
| 4 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{OC} I_{SC}}$ | Fill Factor (0 < FF < 1) | Typical Si: 0.70–0.80 |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OC} I_{SC} FF}{A \cdot G}$ | Conversion efficiency | STC: $G = 1000 \; W/m^2$ |
| 6 | $C = \dfrac{A_{aperture}}{A_{receiver}}$ | Geometric concentration ratio | Dish: C up to 2500 |
| 7 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | Maximum cycle efficiency | $T$ in Kelvin |
| 8 | $I_{mp} \approx I_{SC}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{Z}\right)$ | Approx. current at MPP | $Z = \dfrac{q V_{OC}}{nkT}$ |
| 9 | $\dfrac{dI}{dV}\!\bigg\vert_{V=V_{OC}} = -\dfrac{1}{R_{sh}}$ | Slope at $V_{OC}$ gives $R_{sh}$ | Used in parameter extraction |
| 10 | $\dfrac{dI}{dV}\!\bigg\vert_{I=I_{SC}} = -\dfrac{1}{R_s}$ | Slope at $I_{SC}$ gives $R_s$ | Used in parameter extraction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter in KTU papers:** Almost every numerical/derivation problem (14-mark) involves at least one of these 10 equations. Memorise the symbols *and* the boundary conditions (e.g., at MPP, $\dfrac{dP}{dV}=0$).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **SPV** dominates distributed/residential generation: rooftop arrays, solar pumps, satellites, calculators, EVs.
- **CSP** is the only solar technology with **built-in thermal storage** (molten salt tanks can store 6–15 hours of full-load operation), making it dispatchable — a key advantage over PV.
- The **largest operational CSP plant** (as of 2024) is the *Noor Ouarzazate Complex* (Morocco, ~580 MW). India's largest CSP is the **NP Kunta Ultra-Mega Solar Park** (Andhra Pradesh, 1500 MW PV + planned 50 MW CSP).
- The Shockley–Queisser limit (1961) caps a single-junction cell at **~33.7 %**; multi-junction concentrator cells have surpassed 47 % under concentrated light.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Ideal Solar Cell I–V Equation

### Step 1: Start with the dark (no-light) diode equation
A p-n junction under forward bias $V$ in the dark carries a diode current $I_D$ that obeys Shockley's law:

$$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

### Step 2: Introduce illumination
When light strikes the cell, each absorbed photon (with $E_{ph} \ge E_g$) generates one electron-hole pair. The p-n junction field separates these, contributing a **photocurrent $I_L$** that flows in the *opposite* direction to the conventional forward diode current. By the **superposition principle** (valid when $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$):

$$I = I_D - I_L = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - I_L$$

Rearranging to the conventional form (current delivered to the external load):

$$\boxed{\,I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]\,}$$

### Step 3: Identify the boundary conditions
- **Short-circuit** ($V = 0$): $\;I = I_{SC} = I_L - I_0[e^0 - 1] = I_L - 0 = I_L$. So $\mathbf{I_{SC} = I_L}$.
- **Open-circuit** ($I = 0$): $\;0 = I_L - I_0[e^{qV_{OC}/nkT} - 1] \Rightarrow e^{qV_{OC}/nkT} = 1 + \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} \approx \dfrac{I_L}{I_0}$ (since $I_L \gg I_0$).

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$V_{OC} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$

### Step 4: Add the non-ideal parasitic resistances
The ohmic voltage drop across $R_s$ and the leakage current through $R_{sh}$ modify the equation as:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + I R_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

This is the **Five-Parameter Model** of a practical solar cell.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Condition for the Maximum Power Point

Power delivered to the load:

$$P = V \cdot I = V \left\{ I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] \right\}$$

At the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**, the derivative of power with respect to voltage is zero (the cell operates at the "knee" of the I–V curve):

$$\dfrac{dP}{dV} \bigg\vert_{V=V_{mp}} = 0$$

Applying the product rule:

$$I + V \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{dI}{dV}\bigg\vert_{V_{mp}} = -\dfrac{I_{mp}}{V_{mp}}$$

Substituting the I–V derivative at $V_{mp}$ (with $R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$):

$$\dfrac{dI}{dV} = -I_0 \cdot \dfrac{q}{nkT} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right)$$

Equating:

$$-I_0 \cdot \dfrac{q}{nkT} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right) = -\dfrac{I_{mp}}{V_{mp}}$$

Using $I_{mp} = I_L - I_0[e^{qV_{mp}/nkT} - 1]$, and after algebraic manipulation, the **implicit MPP equation** is:

$$\boxed{\,I_{mp} = I_L \left(1 + \dfrac{nkT}{qV_{mp}}\right)^{-1} \approx I_L \left(1 - \dfrac{nkT}{qV_{mp}}\right)\,}$$

when $\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT} \gg 1$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Expression for Fill Factor (Empirical Green's Form)

The Fill Factor can be expressed in terms of the **normalised open-circuit voltage** $v_{oc} = \dfrac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}$:

$$FF = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

**Step-by-step origin:**

Start by equating MPP current and voltage (no $R_s$, no $R_{sh}$):

$$I_{mp} = I_L - I_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right)$$

At MPP: $I_{mp}/V_{mp} = (qI_0/nkT)\exp(qV_{mp}/nkT)$. Solving simultaneously (Green, 1981) yields the empirical form above. This is the **most-cited FF equation in KTU/KTU-equivalent exams** when no numerical data is given for $V_{mp}$ and $I_{mp}$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A silicon solar cell at STC has $I_{SC} = 3.2 \; A$, $V_{OC} = 0.62 \; V$, $I_{mp} = 2.98 \; A$, $V_{mp} = 0.51 \; V$, and cell area $A = 100 \; cm^2$. Compute FF, $P_{max}$, and $\eta$.

**Solution:**

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 0.51 \times 2.98 = 1.5198 \; W$$

$$FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{OC} I_{SC}} = \dfrac{1.5198}{0.62 \times 3.2} = \dfrac{1.5198}{1.984} = 0.7660$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A \cdot G} = \dfrac{1.5198}{0.01 \; m^2 \times 1000 \; W/m^2} = \dfrac{1.5198}{10} = 0.15198 = \mathbf{15.20\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marker pattern:** KTU examiners award 1 mark each for $P_{max}$, $FF$, and $\eta$ calculations, and 1 mark for the correct unit/substitution. A typical 7-mark sub-part has 3 such "compute-and-substitute" steps.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison — CSP vs. SPV (Important for KTU long-answer)

| Parameter | Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) | Solar Thermal (CSP) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy form | Direct → Electrical | Direct → Thermal → Mechanical → Electrical |
| Prime mover | None (solid-state) | Steam / gas turbine / Stirling engine |
| Efficiency range | 15–22 % (commercial Si), 40 %+ (multi-junction) | 20–40 % (plant net) |
| Storage | Battery / supercapacitor (expensive) | Inherent thermal storage (molten salt) |
| Land use | High per MW | Lower (concentrated sunlight) |
| Best for | Distributed, residential, off-grid | Utility-scale, dispatchable |
| Working temperature | Cell: 25–85 °C | Receiver: 250–1000 °C |
| Cost trend | ↓↓ (learning curve ~24 %/yr) | ↓ (slower) |
| Key Indian example | Bhadla Solar Park (2245 MW PV) | Godawari Solar (50 MW CSP, Andhra) |
