# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms: Solar Constant, Air Mass, and Standard Test Conditions (STC). Mention the numerical values used in solar engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Solar Constant ($G_{SC}$):** The total radiant energy per unit area per unit time received from the Sun on a surface held perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the mean Earth–Sun distance, *outside* the Earth's atmosphere. Value: $\mathbf{1361 \; W/m^2}$ (1 mark).
2. **Air Mass (AM):** The ratio of the actual path length of sunlight through the atmosphere to the vertical (zenith) path length. For $\theta_z = 48.2°$, $\text{AM} = \sec\theta_z = 1.5$ (1 mark).
3. **STC:** Reference test conditions for rating solar cells — irradiance $G = 1000 \; W/m^2$, spectrum AM 1.5G, cell temperature $T_c = 25 °C = 298.15 \; K$ (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) and Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation (CSP) systems in any three aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per valid point):**

| Aspect | SPV | CSP |
|---|---|---|
| Conversion mechanism | Direct (photon → electron → DC) | Indirect (photon → heat → mechanical → AC) |
| Prime mover | None (solid-state) | Steam/gas turbine or Stirling engine |
| Energy storage | Battery (electrochemical) | Thermal storage (molten salt, 6–15 h) |
| Dispatchability | Non-dispatchable (intermittent) | Dispatchable with storage |
| Best suited for | Distributed/rooftop/off-grid | Utility-scale baseload |
| Efficiency | 15–22 % | 20–40 % (with storage) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — KTU Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks. Typical structure: sub-part (a) for 7 marks + sub-part (b) for 7 marks. **Internal choice** = pick *either* Question A *or* Question B (both given below).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a crystalline silicon solar cell. Discuss the role of each layer in photo-current generation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Sketch** (anti-reflective coating, front contact, n-emitter, p-n junction, p-base, back-surface field, back contact) — 2 marks for the labelled diagram.
2. **Working Principle:** The photovoltaic effect — photons with $E_{ph} \ge E_g$ (1.12 eV for Si) are absorbed in the depletion region or within a diffusion length, creating electron-hole pairs. The built-in field at the p-n junction separates the charges, electrons moving to the n-side and holes to the p-side, generating a photovoltage. When a load is connected, current flows from n to p externally. — 3 marks.
3. **Role of each layer:**
   - **Anti-Reflective Coating (ARC):** SiN or TiO₂ layer (~70 nm) reduces reflection from ~35 % to ~3 %, increasing light absorption.
   - **Front Contact (Ag fingers + busbars):** Collects electrons with minimal shading (~5 %).
   - **n⁺ Emitter:** Heavily doped thin n-layer (~0.3 µm) to form the junction.
   - **p Base:** Bulk lightly-doped p-type Si (~200 µm) — main photon absorber.
   - **Back-Surface Field (BSF):** p⁺ layer creates a potential barrier at the back, reducing recombination.
   - **Back Contact (Al):** Collects holes and provides low-resistance contact.
   — 2 marks (1 mark for any 4 layers + 1 mark for reasoning).

---

**Q. (b)** Derive the I–V characteristic equation of an ideal solar cell starting from the diode equation. Also derive the expression for the open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$ and the fill factor (FF). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Dark diode equation:** $I_D = I_0[e^{qV/nkT} - 1]$ (1 mark).
2. **Superposition with photocurrent:** Total current $I = I_L - I_0[e^{qV/nkT} - 1]$ (2 marks).
3. **$V_{OC}$ derivation:** Setting $I = 0$ and solving: $V_{OC} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln(I_L/I_0)$ (2 marks).
4. **Fill Factor:** $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{OC} I_{SC}}$ with the implicit condition $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$ at MPP. (1 mark) Plus empirical Green's relation $FF = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$ where $v_{oc} = qV_{OC}/nkT$ (1 mark).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the parabolic trough concentrated solar power (CSP) system with a neat diagram. Discuss its working fluid, concentration ratio, thermal storage, and overall efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (with diagram):** Parabolic-shaped mirrors focus sunlight onto a receiver tube running along the focal line. The receiver contains a working fluid (synthetic oil like Therminol VP-1, or molten salt) that is heated to 400 °C. — 2 marks.
2. **Working principle:** Concentrated sunlight heats the fluid → heat exchanger generates steam → steam drives a Rankine-cycle turbine → synchronous generator produces AC. — 2 marks.
3. **Concentration ratio:** $C = A_{aperture}/A_{receiver} \approx 30$ to $100$. — 1 mark.
4. **Thermal storage:** Two-tank molten-salt system (hot tank at 565 °C, cold at 290 °C) can store 6–15 h of full-load generation, making the plant dispatchable. — 1 mark.
5. **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_{plant} \approx 20\text{–}30 \; \%$ (with storage), 14–18 % annual capacity factor. — 1 mark.

---

**Q. (b)** With a labelled diagram, explain the **solar tower (central receiver) CSP system**. Compare it with the parabolic dish–Stirling system in terms of concentration ratio, operating temperature, and application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Solar Tower Diagram:** A field of heliostats (computer-controlled flat mirrors) tracks the Sun and reflects concentrated light onto a fixed receiver mounted atop a tower (50–200 m tall). The receiver heats a working fluid (molten salt, water/steam, or air). — 2 marks.
2. **Cycle:** Hot fluid → heat exchanger → steam → turbine → generator. With molten salt (60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃), the fluid is stored and dispatched on demand. — 2 marks.
3. **Concentration ratio:** $C = 300$ to $1500$ (high); receiver temperatures reach 565–1000 °C. — 1 mark.
4. **Comparison with Dish–Stirling:** Dish–Stirling uses a paraboloid dish concentrating to C = 1000–2500 on a Stirling engine at the focus; efficiency 30–40 %, modular (5–50 kW), no storage. Solar tower is utility-scale (10–200 MW) with storage; less efficient per unit but dispatchable. — 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss zones in PEMRT632 Module 1 questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the minus sign** in the diode equation sign convention — write it as $I = I_L - I_0[e^{qV/nkT} - 1]$, *not* $I = I_L + I_0[\ldots]$. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Using 300 K instead of 298.15 K** at STC — the thermal voltage $\dfrac{kT}{q}$ then becomes **25.85 mV** (use this exact number). Using 25 mV is an approximation; examiners may deduct half a mark.
> 3. **Confusing "solar constant" (1361 W/m²) with "STC irradiance" (1000 W/m²).** The first is *outside* the atmosphere; the second is at the *surface* after AM 1.5 losses.
> 4. **Omitting units** in numerical answers. Always write "$\eta = 15.2 \; \%$" not "$\eta = 15.2$".
> 5. **In CSP diagrams, missing the heliostat field labelling** in a solar tower — examiners specifically check for this (1 mark in 7-mark sub-parts).
> 6. **Confusing FF and η** in a numerical — $FF$ is dimensionless (0 < FF < 1), $\eta$ is a percentage (0 < η < 1).
> 7. **In I–V curve sketches, missing the "knee" point** where MPP lies — draw the rectangle $V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$ *inside* the curve and label the unshaded area "losses due to FF < 1".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Solar Constant:** 1361 W/m²; **STC:** 1000 W/m² at AM 1.5G, 25 °C.
- **Two pathways:** **PV (direct, solid-state)** vs. **CSP (indirect, thermal cycle)**.
- **Core I–V equation (ideal):** $I = I_L - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$.
- **Thermal voltage at STC:** $\dfrac{kT}{q} = 25.85 \; mV$.
- **Open-circuit voltage:** $V_{OC} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln(I_{SC}/I_0)$ — increases logarithmically with $I_{SC}$ and decreases linearly with $T$.
- **Fill Factor:** $FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{OC} I_{SC}}$, typically **0.70–0.80** for good Si cells.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OC} I_{SC} FF}{A G}$ — STC gives the "nameplate" efficiency.
- **Solar cell layers (top → bottom):** ARC → Front contact → n⁺ emitter → p-n junction → p base → BSF → Back contact.
- **Cell classifications:**
  - **1st gen:** Mono-Si (η ≈ 22 %), Poly-Si (η ≈ 18 %).
  - **2nd gen (thin film):** a-Si, CdTe, CIGS (η ≈ 10–13 %).
  - **3rd gen:** Multi-junction concentrator (η > 47 %), Perovskite tandems, DSSC, OPV, Quantum dot.
- **CSP types:** Parabolic Trough (Rankine), Linear Fresnel (Rankine), Solar Tower (Rankine/Brayton), Parabolic Dish (Stirling).
- **Concentration ratio:** $C = A_{aperture}/A_{receiver}$ — ranges from 30 (trough) to 2500 (dish).
- **CSP plants have thermal storage** (6–15 h) → dispatchable solar.
- **Shockley–Queisser limit:** ~33.7 % for a single-junction cell under 1-sun AM1.5.
- **Indian context:** Bhadla Park (Rajasthan) is the world's largest PV plant (~2.25 GW); NTPC's NP Kunta combines 1500 MW PV + planned 50 MW CSP.
- **Mnemonic for CSP order of concentration:** **"Trough < Fresnel < Tower < Dish"** (increasing C, increasing $T_H$).
- **Exam tip:** Always label axes (I vs. V), units, and the "knee" (MPP) when drawing the I–V curve. Examiners look for these three.
- **Temperature effect:** As $T$ rises, $V_{OC}$ falls (~–2 mV/°C per cell), $I_{SC}$ rises slightly → **net efficiency drops** at high temperature.
- **Irradiance effect:** $I_{SC} \propto G$ (linear); $V_{OC} \propto \ln G$ (logarithmic); $FF$ slightly increases; $\eta$ roughly constant at moderate G.
- **Photovoltaic effect was first observed by Becquerel (1839); first practical Si cell by Bell Labs (1954, η = 6 %).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Examiner Advice:** In 14-mark Part B questions, allocate ~3 minutes for the diagram, ~5 minutes for the derivations/numerical, and ~3 minutes for the comparison/classification. Always re-state the final answer in a boxed equation or a clear one-line sentence — this is the single biggest factor distinguishing a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar PV Systems - Stand-alone and Grid Connected, Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT632 - RENEWABLE ENERGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Solar PV Systems - Stand-alone and Grid Connected, Applications # 🔆 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Solar PV System? A **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) System** is an engineered arrangement of solar cells, electrical components, and mounting hardware that directly converts sunlight into electrical energy using the **photovoltaic effect** — the generation of a voltage difference across a semiconductor junction when illuminated by photons of energy greater than the bandgap of the material. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A Solar PV system converts solar radiation directly into electricity using semiconductor devices (PV modules). The system may operate either as an **autonomous (stand-alone)** unit feeding local loads or as a **grid-connected (utility-interactive)** unit synchronized with the main power grid. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Solar Leaves" 🍃 Imagine a tree. Each **leaf** is a tiny solar cell that, when sunlight hits it, pushes electrons (sap) through the branch (wire). - A **Stand-alone system** is like a tree standing in a desert — it must store its own water (energy) in a root tank (battery) to survive the night. - A **Grid-connected system** is like a tree in a city garden — it shares its water with the municipal pipeline. If it produces more than it needs, the city takes the excess; if it produces less, the city supplies the deficit through a **net meter**. ## 1.3 The Two Architectural Families > [!NOTE] > **Architecture 1 — Stand-Alone (Off-Grid) PV System** > Operates independently of the utility grid. Requires **energy storage (battery bank)** and a **charge controller** to manage battery health. Used in remote areas, islands, telecom towers, and rural electrification. > [!NOTE] > **Architecture 2 — Grid-Connected (On-Grid / Utility-Interactive) PV System** > Tied to the local electrical grid via a **synchronous inverter** with **anti-islanding protection**. No battery is mandatory (though hybrid variants exist). Excess power is exported to the grid; deficit is imported. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Standard solar constant | $G_{sc}$ | **1367 W/m²** | | Air mass (AM 1.5 reference) | AM | **1.5** | | Solar irradiance at STC | $G$ | **1000 W/m²** | | Standard cell temperature | $T_{cell}$ | **25 °C (298.15 K)** | | Bandgap of silicon | $E_g$ | **1.12 eV** | | Spectral range utilized | $\lambda$ | **0.3 – 1.1 μm** | > [!TIP] > **Remember for KTU:** All PV ratings (Wp — Watt-peak) are quoted at **STC = 1000 W/m², AM 1.5, 25 °C cell temperature**. # 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of a Stand-Alone PV System A stand-alone PV system is essentially an **energy island**. Its components are: 1. **PV Array (Module/Array)** — Converts sunlight to DC electricity. 2. **Battery Bank** — Stores surplus energy for night / cloudy periods. Typically **Lead-Acid** (flooded, VRLA) or **Lithium-Ion (LiFePO₄)**. 3. **Charge Controller** — Regulates battery charging; prevents overcharge & deep discharge. Two main topologies: - **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** — Simpler, cheaper, switches the array on/off. - **MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking)** — Uses DC-DC converter to extract **maximum available power** from the array at all times. 4. **Inverter (DC-AC Converter)** — Converts DC battery/array output to AC for AC loads. Can be **square wave, modified sine wave, or pure sine wave**. 5. **Balance of System (BOS)** — Cables, fuses, MCBs, lightning arrestors, earthing, mounting structures. ## 2.2 Stand-Alone System — Operating Logic - **Day + Sun:** PV array powers the load AND charges the battery via the charge controller. - **Day + Cloudy:** PV array + battery share the load (hybrid mode). - **Night:** Battery alone powers the load through the inverter. - **Battery Low:** System can either shed load or activate a backup (diesel genset in **PV-diesel hybrid systems**). ## 2.3 Components of a Grid-Connected PV System 1. **PV Array** — DC generator. 2. **String Inverter / Central Inverter / Micro-Inverter** — Performs DC-AC conversion AND grid synchronization. 3. **DC & AC Protection** — DC isolator, AC isolator, MCBs, RCDs, SPDs (Surge Protection Devices). 4. **Net Meter / Bi-directional Meter** — Records energy **imported from grid** AND energy **exported to grid**. 5. **Anti-Islanding Protection** — Mandatory per **IEEE 1547 / IEC 61727**. The inverter MUST disconnect within **2 seconds** of grid failure to prevent back-feeding a "dead" grid (which would electrocute linemen). ## 2.4 Grid-Connected System — Operating Logic - **Generation > Load:** Excess energy flows *to the grid* → net meter runs backward (or registers export). - **Generation < Load:** Deficit is drawn *from the grid* → net meter runs forward (or registers import). - **Net-metering Bill:** Customer pays **Net Energy = Energy Imported − Energy Exported**. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table — it is the single most tested item in ESE Module 1.** | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{PV} = G \times A \times \eta_{PV}$ | PV array output power (W) | | 2 | $E_{PV} = P_{PV} \times PSH \times \eta_{sys}$ | Daily energy yield (Wh/day) | | 3 | $E_{load} = \sum (P_i \times t_i)$ | Total daily load energy (Wh/day) | | 4 | $V_{bat} = N_{cells} \times V_{cell}$ | Battery bank voltage | | 5 | $C_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{load} \times DOD_{max}}{V_{bat} \times \eta_{bat}}$ | Battery capacity sizing (Ah) | | 6 | $N_{modules} = \dfrac{V_{bat}}{V_{mod}}$ | Modules in series string | | 7 | $\eta_{MPPT} \approx 95 - 99\%$ | MPPT conversion efficiency | | 8 | $\eta_{inv} \approx 92 - 97\%$ | Inverter efficiency | | 9 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{charge} \times \eta_{disch} \times \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{cable}$ | Overall system efficiency | | 10 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{installed} \times 8760}$ | Capacity Factor (typically **15–25%** for PV) | | 11 | $PR = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{ideal}} = \dfrac{\eta_{sys}}{\eta_{STC}}$ | Performance Ratio (typically **0.7–0.85**) | | 12 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \times I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor of PV cell (typically **0.6–0.8**) | | 13 | $\eta_{cell} = \dfrac{FF \times V_{oc} \times I_{sc}}{G \times A}$ | Cell conversion efficiency | > [!NOTE] > **Units Quick-Reference:** > - Power: **W (or kW, MW)** > - Energy: **Wh (or kWh, MWh)** > - Irradiance: **W/m²** > - Battery Capacity: **Ah** (at a stated C-rate) or **kWh** > - DOD: **Depth of Discharge** (fraction, e.g., 0.5 for lead-acid) > - PSH: **Peak Sun Hours** (hours/day, e.g., 4.5–5.5 in Kerala) ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Rural Electrification:** Stand-alone systems with battery storage bring light to off-grid villages (Kerala's **Agali, Attappadi** tribal hamlets). - **Rooftop Solar (RTS):** Grid-connected systems under **PM-Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana (2024)** target 1 crore households. - **Solar Water Pumping:** PV panels directly drive a DC/AC submersible pump — highly useful for irrigation. - **Space & Satellite Power:** PV is the only reliable power source for satellites (e.g., **ISRO's INSAT series**). - **EV Charging:** Solar carports with grid-tied inverters. # 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Sizing of a Stand-Alone PV System (Worked Analytical Method) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** A remote farmhouse needs a stand-alone PV system. Determine the size of the **PV array**, **battery capacity**, and **charge controller rating**. ### Given Data - Daily Load Energy: $E_{load} = 5{,}000$ Wh/day - System DC voltage: $V_{bat} = 48$ V (standard for off-grid) - Battery: Lead-acid, $DOD_{max} = 0.5$, $\eta_{bat} = 0.85$ (round-trip) - Module: $P_{mod} = 250$ Wp, $V_{mp} = 30$ V - Peak Sun Hours in Kerala: $PSH = 4.8$ h/day - Overall system losses factor: $\eta_{sys} = 0.75$ ### Step 1 — Total Daily Energy Demand The raw daily load energy is given. But we must account for system inefficiencies (cables, inverter, charge controller, battery round-trip loss). $$ E_{req} = \frac{E_{load}}{\eta_{sys}} $$ $$ E_{req} = \frac{5{,}000}{0.75} = 6{,}666.67 \; \text{Wh/day} $$ This is the amount of energy the PV array MUST deliver at the output terminals. ### Step 2 — Required PV Array Size The array must produce the required energy considering available peak sun hours. $$ P_{array} = \frac{E_{req}}{PSH} $$ $$ P_{array} = \frac{6{,}666.67}{4.8} = 1{,}388.89 \; \text{Wp} $$ Add a **safety margin of ~25%** for cloudy days, module degradation (typically 0.5–1% per year), and soiling losses. $$ P_{design} = P_{array} \times 1.25 = 1{,}736.11 \; \text{Wp} $$ ### Step 3 — Number of Modules and String Configuration Each module is 250 Wp. Number of modules required: $$ N_{mod} = \frac{P_{design}}{P_{mod}} = \frac{1{,}736.11}{250} = 6.94 \Rightarrow 7 \; \text{modules} $$ Series modules per string (to match battery voltage — assuming a charge controller with MPPT and step-up capability, we size strings to give safe DC bus voltage, here we take $V_{string} \approx 1.3 \times V_{bat}$): $$ N_{series} = \frac{1.3 \times 48}{30} = 2.08 \Rightarrow 2 \; \text{modules per string} $$ Number of parallel strings: $$ N_{parallel} = \frac{7}{2} = 3.5 \Rightarrow 4 \; \text{parallel strings} $$ Final array configuration: **2S × 4P = 8 modules** (over-designed by 1 module — acceptable engineering practice; alternatively, take 2S × 3P = 6 modules for cost-tight design, accepting ~14% deficit under PSH variation). ### Step 4 — Battery Bank Sizing Energy the battery must supply per day = $E_{load} = 5{,}000$ Wh (battery handles night-time load only). $$ C_{bat} = \frac{E_{load} \times DOD_{safety}}{V_{bat} \times \eta_{bat}} $$ Apply 1 day of **autonomy** and an additional **autonomy factor = 1**: $$ C_{bat} = \frac{5{,}000 \times 1 \times 1}{48 \times 0.85} = 122.55 \; \text{Ah} $$ With a derating factor of 1.25 for battery aging: $$ C_{design} = 122.55 \times 1.25 = 153.19 \;\text{Ah} \Rightarrow 160 \;\text{Ah} $$ Use **4 × 12 V, 160 Ah batteries in series** to obtain 48 V, 160 Ah. ### Step 5 — Charge Controller Sizing Charge controller current must handle the **short-circuit current of the array**: $$ I_{sc,array} = N_{parallel} \times I_{sc,mod} $$ Assuming $I_{sc,mod} = 8.5$ A: $$ I_{sc,array} = 4 \times 8.5 = 34 \; \text{A} $$ Apply safety factor of 1.25: $$ I_{controller} = 34 \times 1.25 = 42.5 \; \text{A} $$ Select a **45 A MPPT charge controller**. ### Step 6 — Inverter Sizing Inverter must handle the peak AC load. Assuming peak load $P_{peak} = 1{,}500$ W: $$ P_{inv} = 1.5 \times P_{peak} = 2{,}250 \; \text{W} $$ Add 25% margin: $$ P_{inv,design} = 2{,}250 \times 1.25 = 2{,}812.5 \; \text{W} \Rightarrow 3 \; \text{kW} $$ Select a **3 kVA pure sine wave inverter**. ## 3.2 Grid-Connected System Sizing — Net Metering Bill Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** A 5 kWp rooftop PV system in Kochi. Compute the annual net energy bill impact. ### Given Data - Installed capacity: $P_{inst} = 5$ kWp - Average daily PSH in Kochi: $PSH = 4.5$ h/day - System efficiency (modules, inverter, cables, losses): $\eta_{sys} = 0.80$ - Annual household consumption: $E_{cons} = 7{,}500$ kWh/year - Tariff: ₹7/unit (KSEB Slab-Domestic) ### Step 1 — Annual Energy Generated $$ E_{gen} = P_{inst} \times PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{sys} $$ $$ E_{gen} = 5 \times 4.5 \times 365 \times 0.80 = 6{,}570 \; \text{kWh/year} $$ ### Step 2 — Net Energy Exchange $$ E_{net} = E_{cons} - E_{gen} = 7{,}500 - 6{,}570 = 930 \; \text{kWh (import)} $$ ### Step 3 — Annual Bill Before and After - **Without PV:** $7{,}500 \times 7 = ₹52{,}500$/year - **With PV:** $930 \times 7 = ₹6{,}510$/year - **Savings:** $₹45{,}990$/year (~87% reduction) ### Step 4 — Capacity Factor Confirmation $$ CF = \frac{E_{gen}}{P_{inst} \times 8760} = \frac{6{,}570}{5 \times 8{,}760} = 0.15 = 15\% $$ This matches typical **15–20% CF for Kerala rooftops**. ## 3.3 Performance Ratio Derivation The Performance Ratio (PR) is a measure of how close the actual PV system performance comes to its rated performance. It excludes external factors (irradiance variation) and isolates system-internal losses. $$ PR = \frac{\text{Actual Energy Output (AC)}}{\text{Theoretical Energy Output (DC at STC)}} $$ Mathematically: $$ PR = \frac{E_{AC,actual}}{G_{POA} \times A \times \eta_{STC}} $$ where $G_{POA}$ is the in-plane irradiance and $A$ is the array area. Equivalently, by efficiency chain: $$ PR = \eta_{temp} \times \eta_{soiling} \times \eta_{cable} \times \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{MPPT} $$ For a well-maintained system: $$ PR = 0.95 \times 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.96 \times 0.98 \approx 0.83 $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** A typical PR between **0.70 and 0.85** is expected for a well-installed system. Anything below **0.65** indicates poor maintenance, soiling, or faulty components. # 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Stand-Alone PV System ```mermaid flowchart LR SUN[Sunlight
G W per m2] --> ARRAY[PV Array
DC Generation] ARRAY --> CC[MPPT Charge
Controller] CC -->|Charging current| BAT[Battery Bank
Energy Storage] BAT -->|DC supply| INV[Inverter
DC to AC] INV --> LOAD[AC Loads
Lights Fans TV] BAT -.->|Low state alarm| ALARM[Load Shed
or Genset Backup] ARRAY --- GROUND[Earth Fault
Protection] INV --- GROUND BAT --- GROUND ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Grid-Connected PV System ```mermaid flowchart LR SUN2[Sunlight] --> ARR2[PV Array
DC Output] ARR2 --> DCISO[DC Isolator
and SPD] DCISO --> INVG[Grid Tie
Inverter MPPT] INVG -->|AC output| ACISO[AC Isolator
MCB RCD] ACISO --> NM[Net Meter
Bidirectional] NM --> GRID[Utility Grid
KSEB] GRID --> HOUSE[Domestic
Loads] HOUSE -.->|Deficit| NM NM -.->|Excess export| GRID INVG -.->|Anti-Island Trip| PROT[IEEE 1547
Protection Relay] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Comparison Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph COMPARE["STAND ALONE VS GRID CONNECTED COMPARISON"] A[Stand Alone PV System] --> A1[Battery mandatory] A --> A2[Charge controller mandatory] A --> A3[No net meter required] A --> A4[Higher BOS cost] A --> A5[Used in remote off grid areas] B[Grid Connected PV System] --> B1[Battery optional hybrid] B --> B2[Anti islanding mandatory] B --> B3[Net meter bidirectional] B --> B4[Lower BOS cost] B --> B5[Used in urban and rooftop] end ``` ## 4.4 Solar PV Applications Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR APP[Solar PV Applications] --> A1[Water Pumping
DC and AC Pumps] APP --> A2[Lighting
LED Solar Lanterns] APP --> A3[Rural Electrification
Solar Home Systems] APP --> A4[Rooftop Grid Tied
Net Metering] APP --> A5[Solar Carports
and EV Charging] APP --> A6[Solar Cold Storage
and Drying] APP --> A7[Space and Satellite
Power Systems] ``` ## 4.5 Solar PV System Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB ROOT[Solar PV Systems] --> SA[Stand Alone Off Grid] ROOT --> GC[Grid Connected On Grid] ROOT --> HY[Hybrid Systems] SA --> SADC[DC Only] SA --> SAAC[DC plus AC with Battery] SA --> SAHY[PV Diesel Hybrid] GC --> GCRES[Rooftop Residential] GC --> GCCOM[Commercial and Industrial] GC --> GCUTI[Utility Scale Solar Farm] HY --> HYGEN[PV plus Battery plus Diesel] HY --> HYWT[PV plus Wind plus Battery] HY --> HYMIC[Microgrid with Storage] ``` # 🎯 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between stand-alone and grid-connected PV systems.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Stand-Alone (Off-Grid) | Grid-Connected (On-Grid) | |---|---|---| | Grid dependency | Independent | Synchronized with grid | | Battery storage | Mandatory | Optional | | Inverter type | Standard (off-grid) | Grid-tie with anti-islanding | | Metering | Unidirectional | Bidirectional (net meter) | | Application | Remote areas, islands | Urban rooftops, solar farms | | Cost | High (storage adds cost) | Lower (no storage needed) | | Reliability | Depends on battery sizing | Inherently backed by grid | --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the function of an MPPT charge controller in a PV system.** **Model Answer:** A **Maximum Power Point Tracker (MPPT)** is an electronic DC-DC converter inserted between the PV array and the battery bus that continuously adjusts the operating voltage of the array to extract **maximum available power** at the prevailing irradiance and temperature. **Working Principle:** - PV array output is non-linear — power varies with voltage. - The MPPT sweeps the array voltage using algorithms like **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** or **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)** to locate the peak of the P-V curve. - Once the maximum power point is located, it regulates the duty cycle of a buck/boost converter to keep the array operating at $V_{mp}$. **Benefits:** - Efficiency gain: **20–30%** more energy vs. PWM controllers. - Allows array voltage to differ from battery voltage (e.g., 60 V array charging 24 V battery). - MPPT efficiency: **95–99%**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ⚠️** > When answering PV sizing questions, students frequently lose marks by: > 1. **Forgetting the autonomy factor** (typically 1–3 days) in battery sizing. > 2. **Ignoring PSH variation** — never use 24 h, always use regional PSH (~4.5–5 h for Kerala). > 3. **Missing the safety/derating factor** of 1.25 on array size. > 4. **Confusing module series/parallel nomenclature** — series adds voltage, parallel adds current. > 5. **Omitting the round-trip efficiency** of the battery (~85% for lead-acid). > 6. **Not including the inverter efficiency** in the load energy calculation. > > **A perfect 14-mark answer must show: (a) component sizing with formula, (b) numerical substitution, (c) safety factors applied, (d) commercially rounded selection.** --- ### **Part B – Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the components and working of a **stand-alone PV system**. Mention the role of each component. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Design a stand-alone PV system for a remote village load of **3,000 Wh/day** with peak load of **1,200 W**. System voltage is 24 V. Use a 200 Wp module (rated $V_{mp} = 24$ V, $I_{mp} = 8.33$ A), lead-acid battery (DOD = 0.5, $\eta_{bat} = 0.85$), PSH = 4.5 h, and overall system efficiency = 0.80. Size the array, battery, and inverter. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Block Diagram & Component Description (7 Marks):** The stand-alone PV system consists of: 1. **PV Array:** Converts incident solar radiation into DC electricity. Multiple modules connected in series-parallel to achieve desired voltage and current. 2. **MPPT/PWM Charge Controller:** - Regulates the voltage and current coming from the PV array going to the battery. - Prevents **overcharging** (high-voltage disconnect). - Prevents **deep discharge** (low-voltage disconnect). - MPPT type extracts ~30% more energy than PWM. 3. **Battery Bank:** - Stores surplus DC energy for use during night and cloudy hours. - Provides autonomy — typical sizing uses 1–3 days. - Common types: Flooded Lead-Acid, VRLA, Li-Ion. 4. **Inverter (DC–AC Converter):** - Converts battery DC voltage to AC voltage (230 V, 50 Hz) for AC loads. - Modern units are **pure sine wave** type with >92% efficiency. 5. **BOS (Balance of System):** Cables, fuses, MCBs, isolators, surge protection, earthing, mounting structure, lightning arrestor. **Working:** During sunny hours, the PV array powers the load AND charges the battery via the charge controller. At night, the battery alone powers the inverter and load. If battery SOC falls below set threshold, low-voltage disconnect sheds load. --- **(b) Numerical Sizing (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Required Daily Energy from Array:** $$ E_{req} = \frac{E_{load}}{\eta_{sys}} = \frac{3{,}000}{0.80} = 3{,}750 \; \text{Wh/day} $$ **Step 2 — PV Array Size:** $$ P_{array} = \frac{E_{req}}{PSH} = \frac{3{,}750}{4.5} = 833.33 \; \text{Wp} $$ Apply safety factor of 1.25: $$ P_{design} = 833.33 \times 1.25 = 1{,}041.67 \;\text{Wp} $$ **Step 3 — Number of Modules:** $$ N_{mod} = \frac{1{,}041.67}{200} = 5.21 \Rightarrow 6 \; \text{modules} $$ Configuration: To match 24 V system with $V_{mp} = 24$ V per module, use **1 module per string × 6 parallel strings = 6 modules total**. **Step 4 — Battery Sizing (1-day autonomy):** $$ C_{bat} = \frac{E_{load}}{V_{bat} \times DOD \times \eta_{bat}} = \frac{3{,}000}{24 \times 0.5 \times 0.85} $$ $$ C_{bat} = \frac{3{,}000}{10.2} = 294.12 \;\text{Ah} $$ Apply derating factor of 1.25: $$ C_{design} = 294.12 \times 1.25 = 367.65 \;\text{Ah} \Rightarrow 400 \;\text{Ah} $$ Use **2 × 12 V, 400 Ah batteries in series** to obtain 24 V, 400 Ah. **Step 5 — Inverter Sizing:** $$ P_{inv} = 1.25 \times P_{peak} = 1.25 \times 1{,}200 = 1{,}500 \; \text{W} $$ Select a **2 kVA pure sine wave inverter** (next standard size up). **Final Selection Summary:** | Component | Specification | |---|---| | PV Array | 1.04 kWp (6 × 200 Wp, 1S × 6P) | | Charge Controller | 60 A MPPT (with 1.25× safety) | | Battery Bank | 24 V, 400 Ah (2 × 12 V in series) | | Inverter | 2 kVA, pure sine wave, 24 V DC → 230 V AC | --- ### **Part B – Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Explain the working of a **grid-connected PV system** with a block diagram. Discuss the role of **anti-islanding protection** and the **net meter**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **10 kWp rooftop PV system** is installed in Thiruvananthapuram. The system performance ratio is **0.78** and average PSH is **4.6 h/day**. Compute: 1. Annual energy generated (kWh). 2. Capacity Factor (CF). 3. Annual CO₂ reduction (in tonnes) assuming grid emission factor = **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh**. If the household's annual consumption is **12,000 kWh** and electricity tariff is **₹7.50/unit**, calculate the annual savings. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Block Diagram & Description (7 Marks):** ``` Sunlight → PV Array → DC Isolator → Grid-Tie Inverter ↓ AC Isolator → Net Meter ↓ Distribution Board ↓ [Loads + Grid] ``` **Components:** 1. **PV Array:** Generates DC electricity; mounted on rooftop with tilt ≈ latitude angle (8.5° for Thiruvananthapuram, but 10–15° improves self-cleaning). 2. **Grid-Tie Inverter (GTI):** Performs three critical functions: - **DC-AC Conversion** using IGBT/MOSFET bridge. - **MPPT** to extract maximum power from the array. - **Grid Synchronization** — locks output voltage, frequency, and phase with the grid. 3. **Anti-Islanding Protection:** Per **IEEE 1547**, the inverter MUST detect grid failure and disconnect within **2 seconds** (typically <100 ms). Anti-islanding prevents the inverter from energizing a "dead" grid section — protecting line maintenance workers from electrocution. Detection methods: - **Passive:** Voltage/frequency window, rate-of-change-of-frequency (ROCOF). - **Active:** Impedance measurement, frequency shift, Sandia voltage shift. 4. **Net Meter (Bi-directional):** Records energy **imported (forward)** and **exported (reverse)** separately. At billing cycle, **net import** is charged. In some states (e.g., Kerala — KSEB RTS Order 2024), exported energy is paid at a feed-in tariff or given as credits. --- **(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):** **1. Annual Energy Generated:** $$ E_{gen} = P_{inst} \times PSH \times 365 \times PR $$ $$ E_{gen} = 10 \times 4.6 \times 365 \times 0.78 = 13{,}099.7 \; \text{kWh/year} $$ (Equivalently: $10 \times 8760 \times CF$ → see below) **2. Capacity Factor:** $$ CF = PR \times \frac{PSH}{24} = 0.78 \times \frac{4.6}{24} = 0.78 \times 0.1917 = 0.1495 \approx 14.95\% $$ Or directly: $$ CF = \frac{E_{gen}}{P_{inst} \times 8760} = \frac{13{,}099.7}{10 \times 8{,}760} = 0.1495 $$ **3. Annual CO₂ Reduction:** $$ CO_2 \,saved = E_{gen} \times \text{EF} = 13{,}099.7 \times 0.82 = 10{,}741.8 \; \text{kg} = 10.74 \; \text{tonnes} $$ **4. Annual Savings:** $$ E_{net} = 12{,}000 - 13{,}099.7 = -1{,}099.7 \; \text{kWh (net export)} $$ Since generation exceeds consumption, the household has a **net surplus** of 1,099.7 kWh which can be: - Carried forward as credits (banking), or - Sold back to KSEB at feed-in tariff. **Bill Without PV:** $12{,}000 \times 7.50 = ₹90{,}000$/year **Bill With PV (assuming 1:1 net metering):** Effectively **₹0** (or even net positive with banking). **Total Savings:** **₹90,000/year** (≈ 100% bill offset, with credit banking). --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for ESE:** - ☑ **STC = 1000 W/m², AM 1.5, 25 °C** — universal reference for all PV ratings. - ☑ **Stand-alone = Off-grid** → Battery + Charge Controller + Inverter mandatory. - ☑ **Grid-connected = On-grid** → Anti-islanding + Net meter mandatory. - ☑ **Anti-islanding:** Inverter must disconnect within **2 s** of grid outage (IEEE 1547). - ☑ **MPPT** gives **20–30% more energy** than PWM controllers. - ☑ **PSH (Peak Sun Hours):** Kerala ≈ 4.5–5 h/day; NOT 24 h, NOT 8 h. - ☑ **Battery sizing formula:** $C_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{load}}{V_{bat} \times DOD \times \eta_{bat}}$ with 1.25× derating. - ☑ **Series → adds voltage; Parallel → adds current.** - ☑ **Lead-acid:** DOD ≤ 50%, $\eta_{round-trip} \approx 85\%$ — **DON'T deeply discharge**. - ☑ **Capacity Factor of PV:** typically **15–25%** (NOT 100% — sun doesn't shine 24 h). - ☑ **Performance Ratio:** typically **0.70–0.85** — indicates system health. - ☑ **Net metering** allows bi-directional energy flow with the grid. - ☑ **Applications:** Solar water pumping, lighting (LED lanterns), rural electrification, rooftop systems, solar carports, satellites, cold storage. - ☑ **Kerala Schemes to Know:** PM-Surya Ghar Muft Bijli Yojana (2024), KSEB RTS scheme, ANERT off-grid programs for tribal colonies. - ☑ **Anti-islanding** protects grid maintenance workers — a critical safety feature. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Memory Trick:** > - **Off-grid components:** **P**V + **C**harge controller + **B**attery + **I**nverter → **"PCBI"** — *"Pee-Cee-Bee-Eye"* — easy to recall! > - **On-grid extra components:** **A**nti-islanding + **N**et meter → **"AN"** — adds to PCBI but **B is optional (hybrid)**.

topics.title: Energy from Ocean - Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) - Principle of OTEC System - Open Cycle (Claude Cycle), Closed Cycle, Tidal Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT632 - RENEWABLE ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy from Ocean - Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) - Principle of OTEC System - Open Cycle (Claude Cycle), Closed Cycle, Tidal Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)

**Formal Definition:**
Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) is a renewable energy technology that converts solar radiation absorbed by the ocean into electrical energy by exploiting the **temperature difference** between the warm upper layers of the ocean and the colder deep ocean water. According to KTU 2024 syllabus, OTEC systems require a minimum temperature differential of **20 °C** between the surface (warm) water and the deep (cold) water to produce usable work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> The principle of OTEC is based on the **Rankine Cycle**, which uses the ocean's natural thermal gradient (warm surface ≈ 25 °C to 30 °C; cold deep water ≈ 5 °C to 10 °C) as the heat source and heat sink respectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two large water tanks connected by a pipe. One tank is filled with warm surface water (heated by the sun) and the other with cold deep ocean water. If you could pump the warm water through a device that vaporizes a working fluid, drives a turbine, and then condenses the vapor using the cold water, you would generate electricity — exactly the way a power plant works, but with the ocean itself acting as both the boiler and the condenser.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of OTEC like a "thermal battery" — the sun charges the upper ocean with heat, and we extract that stored thermal energy by moving a working fluid between the warm and cold reservoirs.

### 1.3 Tidal Energy

**Formal Definition:**
Tidal energy is a form of **hydrokinetic energy** harvested from the gravitational interaction between the Earth, Moon, and Sun. The periodic rise (high tide) and fall (low tide) of sea levels — known as **tidal range** — and the resulting horizontal water movement (tidal streams) can be converted into usable electricity using turbines, barrages, or fences.

**Physical Constants (Standard Metrics):**
- **Density of seawater (ρ): 1025 kg/m³**
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g): 9.81 m/s²**
- **Tidal period (semi-diurnal): 12 hours 25 minutes**
- **Gravitational constant (G): 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** Tidal energy is **predictable** (unlike solar/wind) because the motion of celestial bodies is precisely known centuries in advance.

---

### 1.4 The Three Primary OTEC System Architectures

| S.No. | Cycle Type | Developer / Inventor | Working Fluid |
|-------|-----------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Open Cycle** | Georges Claude (1930) | Warm seawater (vaporized) |
| 2 | **Closed Cycle** | D'Arsonval / Anderson | Ammonia (NH₃) |
| 3 | **Hybrid Cycle** | Combined approach | Seawater + Ammonia |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Principle of OTEC (Foundation):** The first and second laws of thermodynamics govern OTEC. The maximum theoretical efficiency is the **Carnot efficiency**, $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}$, where $T_h$ and $T_c$ are the absolute temperatures (in Kelvin) of the warm and cold reservoirs. For typical ocean temperatures, this efficiency is only **6 % to 8 %**, which is why OTEC plants must process **enormous volumes of water**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of an OTEC System (Step-by-Step)

1. **Warm Water Intake:** Surface seawater at ~28 °C is drawn into the system through a large-diameter cold-water pipe.
2. **Heat Exchange:** This warm water transfers its thermal energy to a working fluid (causing evaporation).
3. **Vapor Expansion:** The high-pressure vapor expands through a turbine, producing mechanical work.
4. **Condensation:** Cold deep-ocean water (~5 °C) absorbs the heat from the vapor, condensing it back to liquid.
5. **Pumping:** The working fluid is pumped back to the evaporator, completing the cycle.

### 2.2 Open Cycle OTEC (Claude Cycle) — Detailed Theory

The Open Cycle, invented by **Georges Claude** in 1930, is the simplest form. It uses seawater itself as the working fluid in a one-pass process.

**Operational Stages:**
- **Flash Evaporation:** Warm surface seawater is introduced into an evacuated chamber (low pressure). Because the chamber pressure is below the saturation pressure corresponding to the warm water temperature, a portion of the water **flash-evaporates** into low-pressure steam.
- **Turbine Expansion:** This steam drives a low-pressure turbine directly coupled to a generator.
- **Condensation:** The exhaust steam is condensed by direct contact with cold deep-ocean water pumped up from ~1000 m depth. This produces **fresh water** as a by-product.
- **Vacuum Maintenance:** A vacuum pump maintains the low pressure inside the evaporator/condenser.

> [!NOTE]
> **Salient Feature:** Open Cycle produces both **electricity AND desalinated fresh water** as a co-product — a major advantage in island regions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse "Open Cycle" with "Open System" in general thermodynamics. In OTEC, "Open" means the working fluid (seawater) leaves the cycle after one pass.

### 2.3 Closed Cycle OTEC (Anderson Cycle) — Detailed Theory

The Closed Cycle uses a **secondary working fluid** (typically ammonia, NH₃, with a boiling point of **-33 °C at 1 atm**). The fluid is continuously recirculated.

**Components of the Closed Cycle:**
- **Evaporator:** Warm surface seawater heats and vaporizes the ammonia in heat-exchanger tubes.
- **Turbine:** High-pressure ammonia vapor expands, driving the turbine-generator.
- **Condenser:** Cold deep-ocean water cools and condenses the ammonia vapor back to liquid.
- **Pump:** A feed pump pressurizes the liquid ammonia and returns it to the evaporator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Ammonia?** Ammonia is chosen because of its low boiling point, high latent heat of vaporization, and excellent thermal conductivity, which allows efficient heat transfer even at the small temperature differentials typical of ocean water (~20 °C).

### 2.4 Tidal Energy — Theoretical Foundation

Tidal energy can be harnessed in three primary ways:
- **Tidal Barrage:** A dam-like structure across a tidal estuary with sluice gates and low-head turbines.
- **Tidal Stream:** Underwater turbines placed in fast-flowing tidal channels (similar to underwater wind turbines).
- **Tidal Fence:** Vertical barriers with rotating blades placed across channels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tidal Range** = Vertical difference between high tide and low tide. **Tidal Prism** = Volume of water exchanged between the basin and the open sea during a tidal cycle.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Application | Typical Units |
|---|--------------------|----------------|--------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}$ | T_h = hot reservoir temp, T_c = cold reservoir temp | Maximum possible OTEC efficiency | Dimensionless (×100 for %) |
| 2 | $\eta_{rankine} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | W_net = net work, Q_in = heat input | Actual cycle efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $W_{turbine} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$ | ṁ = mass flow rate, h = specific enthalpy | Turbine power output | kW |
| 4 | $E_{tidal} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2$ | ρ = seawater density, g = gravity, A = basin area, R = tidal range | Total potential energy stored in a tidal basin | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $P_{tidal} = \dfrac{E_{tidal}}{T}$ | T = tidal period (≈ 12.42 × 3600 s) | Average power from a tidal basin | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $P_{stream} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^3$ | A = swept area, V = flow velocity | Power from tidal stream turbines | Watts (W) |
| 7 | $P_{barrage} = 2 \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A^2 \cdot R^2 \cdot C_{eff} / T$ | C_eff = efficiency coefficient (0.2–0.4) | Actual electrical output from a barrage | Watts (W) |
| 8 | $h_{head} = R/2$ | Average head over the cycle | Mean head of water in tidal range | Meters (m) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** OTEC plants (e.g., the 100 kW plant in Kume Island, Japan) are ideal for tropical island nations. Tidal energy (e.g., 240 MW La Rance, France) provides baseload power unlike solar/wind.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Carnot Efficiency of an OTEC System

The **Carnot efficiency** establishes the theoretical upper limit for any heat engine. For OTEC, this becomes critically small because of the small temperature difference between the warm surface water and the cold deep water.

**Given Data (Standard OTEC Operating Point):**
- Warm surface temperature: $T_h = 300 \text{ K}$ (≈ 27 °C)
- Cold deep-sea temperature: $T_c = 278 \text{ K}$ (≈ 5 °C)

**Step 1: Write the Carnot efficiency formula.**

$$
\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$
\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{278}{300}
$$

**Step 3: Perform the division.**

$$
\dfrac{278}{300} = 0.9267
$$

**Step 4: Subtract from unity.**

$$
\eta_{carnot} = 1 - 0.9267 = 0.0733
$$

**Step 5: Convert to percentage.**

$$
\eta_{carnot} = 7.33 \text{ \%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Even under ideal (Carnot) conditions, OTEC can convert only about **7.33 %** of the absorbed solar energy into useful work. This is the primary reason OTEC requires enormous heat exchangers and very high mass flow rates of seawater to be economically viable.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Potential Energy in a Tidal Basin

Consider a tidal basin of area **A** that fills from a low-tide level to a high-tide level of **R** meters. The total gravitational potential energy stored in this water column is derived by integrating the energy of infinitesimally thin slices of water from the bottom to the top.

**Step 1: Consider an infinitesimal horizontal slab of water at height h with thickness dh.**

The mass of this slab is:

$$
dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot dh
$$

**Step 2: The potential energy of this slab above the baseline (h = 0) is given by:**

$$
dE = dm \cdot g \cdot h = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot h \cdot dh
$$

**Step 3: Integrate from h = 0 to h = R to find total potential energy.**

$$
E = \int_{0}^{R} \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot h \cdot dh
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integral.**

$$
E = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot \int_{0}^{R} h \cdot dh
$$

**Step 5: Perform the integration.**

$$
\int_{0}^{R} h \cdot dh = \left[ \dfrac{h^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{R} = \dfrac{R^2}{2}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute back.**

$$
E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2
$$

**Step 7: Plug in standard values for verification (A = 1 km², R = 5 m):**

$$
E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (1 \times 10^6) \cdot (5)^2
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 25
$$

$$
E \approx 1.257 \times 10^{11} \text{ J} \approx 125.7 \text{ GJ}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** A modest 1 km² basin with a 5 m tidal range stores about **125.7 GJ** of potential energy per tidal cycle — enough to power roughly **12,000 homes** for one hour.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Open vs Closed vs Hybrid OTEC

| Parameter | Open Cycle (Claude) | Closed Cycle (Anderson) | Hybrid Cycle |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------|
| Working Fluid | Warm seawater (vapor) | Ammonia (NH₃) | Seawater + NH₃ |
| Pressure | Very low (vacuum) | Moderate (≈ 9–10 bar) | Combination |
| Heat Exchanger | Not required (direct contact) | Required (shell-and-tube) | Required |
| Turbine Size | Large (low-pressure) | Compact (high-pressure) | Intermediate |
| By-products | Fresh water (desalination) | None | Fresh water |
| Maintenance | High (corrosion, biofouling) | Lower | High |
| Conversion Efficiency | Low | Higher than open | Highest |
| Cost | Low initial | High (heat exchangers) | Very high |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Average Power Output of a Tidal Barrage

The average mechanical power available from a tidal barrage can be obtained by combining the potential energy formula with the appropriate time period. For a **single tidal cycle** (one filling or emptying operation), the period is $T = 6.21 \text{ hours} = 22,356 \text{ seconds}$ (one-quarter of the lunar day).

**Step 1: Recall the energy expression for one basin fill (from §3.2).**

$$
E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2
$$

**Step 2: Include the efficiency factor C_eff of the turbine and gearbox system.**

$$
E_{useful} = C_{eff} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2
$$

**Step 3: Average power over one tidal half-cycle (T = 22,356 s).**

$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{T} = \dfrac{C_{eff} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{2 \cdot T}
$$

**Step 4: For a barrage that operates twice per day (both ebb and flood), double the power.**

$$
P_{daily} = 2 \cdot P_{avg} = \dfrac{C_{eff} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{T}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical example — La Range (France) parameters:** A ≈ 22 km², R ≈ 8 m, C_eff ≈ 0.25.

$$
P_{daily} = \dfrac{0.25 \cdot 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (22 \times 10^6) \cdot 64}{22356}
$$

$$
P_{daily} \approx 1.58 \times 10^8 \text{ W} = 158 \text{ MW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** La Rance's actual installed capacity is **240 MW**, which is in the same order of magnitude — confirming the validity of the formula.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Open Cycle (Claude Cycle) OTEC System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Warm Surface Seawater 27 C] --> B1[Flash Evaporator Vacuum Chamber]
    B1 --> C1[Low Pressure Steam]
    C1 --> D1[Turbine Generator]
    D1 --> E1[Condenser Cold Deep Water 5 C]
    E1 --> F1[Fresh Water By Product]
    E1 --> G1[Used Seawater Discharge]
    H1[Cold Deep Seawater Intake] --> E1
    D1 --> I1[Electrical Output AC Power]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Warm water enters the evacuated evaporator, flash-boils into low-pressure steam, drives the turbine, and is then condensed by cold deep water. The condenser yields **fresh water** as a valuable co-product.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Closed Cycle (Anderson Cycle) OTEC System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Ammonia Liquid] --> B2[Pump High Pressure]
    B2 --> C2[Evaporator Warm Seawater Heats NH3]
    C2 --> D2[Turbine Generator]
    D2 --> E2[Condenser Cold Deep Water Cools NH3]
    E2 --> A2
    C2 -.Heat In.-> SW1[Warm Surface Seawater 27 C]
    E2 -.Heat Out.-> SW2[Cold Deep Seawater 5 C]
    D2 --> OUT2[Electrical Output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed Loop Property:** Notice how ammonia is **recycled** — the working fluid never leaves the cycle. This is the defining characteristic of a closed (Anderson) cycle.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Complete OTEC Power Plant Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SW1[Warm Surface Seawater 27 C] --> P1[Warm Water Pump]
    SW2[Cold Deep Seawater 5 C 1000 m] --> P2[Cold Water Pump]
    P1 --> EVAP[Evaporator / Flash Chamber]
    EVAP --> TURB[Steam / Vapor Turbine]
    TURB --> GEN[Electrical Generator]
    GEN --> TRANS[Step Up Transformer]
    TRANS --> GRID[Power Grid]
    TURB --> COND[Condenser]
    P2 --> COND
    COND --> DISP[Waste Brine Discharge]
    EVAP --> FW[Fresh Water Collection Basin]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Tidal Energy Conversion System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Ocean Tidal Rise and Fall] --> T2[Tidal Basin Estuary]
    T2 --> T3[Sluice Gates and Barrage]
    T3 --> T4[Low Head Kaplan Turbine]
    T4 --> T5[Generator]
    T5 --> T6[Step Up Transformer]
    T6 --> T7[National Grid]
    T3 --> T8[Lock Gate for Shipping]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A tidal barrage is essentially a **hydroelectric dam with reversible flow** — water enters the basin at high tide through turbines, and is released back at low tide, generating electricity on **both directions** of flow.

---

### 4.5 Schematic of Tidal Stream Energy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TIDE1[Fast Tidal Current] --> TURB1[Horizontal Axis Turbine]
    TURB1 --> GEN1[Permanent Magnet Generator]
    GEN1 --> CABL[Submarine Cable]
    CABL --> SHORE[Onshore Substation]
    SHORE --> GRID1[Power Grid]
    TURB1 -.Marine Growth and Biofouling.-> MAINT[Scheduled Maintenance]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the working principle of an Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) system. Mention the minimum temperature difference required for its operation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Principle:** OTEC is a heat-engine-based renewable energy system that exploits the **temperature gradient** between warm surface seawater (heat source) and cold deep-ocean water (heat sink) to vaporize a working fluid, drive a turbine, and generate electricity. It essentially operates on the **Rankine cycle**.

**Minimum ΔT:** For economically viable operation, the temperature difference between the warm surface water and the cold deep water must be at least **20 °C** (≈ 36 °F). *(1 mark for principle, 1 mark for ΔT value, 1 mark for the Rankine cycle mention.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three advantages and two limitations of tidal energy.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Predictable** — Tidal cycles are known centuries in advance (unlike solar/wind).
2. **High energy density** — Seawater is ~832 times denser than air, so tidal streams carry enormous kinetic energy.
3. **Zero carbon emissions** during operation and **long lifespan** (> 75 years for well-designed barrages).

**Limitations:**
1. **High initial capital cost** and limited suitable geographical sites.
2. **Environmental impact** on marine ecosystems, sediment transport, and local fishing communities. *(1 mark per advantage set, 1 mark for limitations.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mark Loss:**
> - **Do not confuse** the Open Cycle (Claude) and Closed Cycle (Anderson) — write the **inventor’s name** in the answer to score the full mark.
> - When asked for **efficiency**, write both **Carnot efficiency formula** AND a **numerical value** (e.g., 7 %) to earn full credit.
> - For tidal energy derivations, **never skip the integration step** — examiners specifically test $\int h \, dh$ as a check for understanding.
> - Always **state the working fluid and its key property** (e.g., "Ammonia with boiling point -33 °C") in closed-cycle answers.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic diagram, explain the **Open Cycle OTEC system (Claude Cycle)** in detail. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **total potential energy stored in a tidal basin** of area A and tidal range R. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Open Cycle OTEC (7 Marks)

The Open Cycle OTEC, invented by French engineer **Georges Claude** in 1930, uses **warm seawater itself as the working fluid**. The complete process is as follows:

1. **Warm Water Intake:** Warm surface seawater at about 27 °C is pumped into an **evacuated flash chamber** (pressure ≈ 0.03 atm).
2. **Flash Evaporation:** Due to the low pressure, a portion of the warm water **flash-boils** into low-pressure steam, releasing its latent heat.
3. **Turbine Expansion:** This low-pressure, high-volume steam drives a specially designed **low-pressure turbine** coupled to an electrical generator.
4. **Condensation:** The exhaust steam is condensed by **direct contact** with cold deep-ocean water (≈ 5 °C) pumped up from ~1000 m depth.
5. **By-product:** The condensed steam becomes **fresh, desalinated water** — a valuable co-product for islands.

**Advantages of Open Cycle OTEC:**
- Produces **fresh water** along with electricity.
- No heat exchangers required → lower capital cost.
- Environmentally benign working fluid (just water).

**Disadvantages of Open Cycle OTEC:**
- Must handle **enormous volumes** of low-pressure steam → large turbine dimensions.
- High **vacuum maintenance** cost; risk of air ingress.
- Biofouling and corrosion of equipment.

*(3 marks for process, 2 marks for diagram explanation, 2 marks for advantages/disadvantages.)*

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Tidal Basin Energy (7 Marks)

**Refer to the complete derivation in §3.2 of Section 3 above.** The key steps are:

- Consider an infinitesimal slab of water at height h, thickness dh.
- Its mass: $dm = \rho A \, dh$.
- Its potential energy: $dE = \rho A g h \, dh$.
- Total energy: $E = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$.

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}
$$

Where $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (seawater density), $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $A$ = basin area, $R$ = tidal range. *(7 marks — full breakdown as in §3.2.)*

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the **Closed Cycle OTEC (Anderson Cycle)** with a neat block diagram. Why is ammonia chosen as the working fluid? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A tidal barrage has a basin area of 20 km² and a tidal range of 6 m. Calculate the **average power available** per tidal cycle assuming a turbine efficiency of 0.25 and tidal period of 12.42 hours. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Closed Cycle OTEC (7 Marks)

The **Closed Cycle OTEC** (also called the **Anderson Cycle** after its inventor) uses a **secondary working fluid** (ammonia, NH₃) that is continuously recycled through a closed loop.

**Components and Working:**

1. **Evaporator:** Warm surface seawater (≈ 27 °C) flows around the evaporator tubes containing liquid ammonia, transferring heat to it. Ammonia **boils and vaporizes** at low temperature (-33 °C at 1 atm).
2. **Turbine:** High-pressure ammonia vapor (≈ 9–10 bar) expands through a turbine coupled to a generator.
3. **Condenser:** Cold deep seawater (≈ 5 °C) condenses the ammonia vapor back into liquid form.
4. **Pump:** The liquid ammonia is pressurized and returned to the evaporator — completing the **closed loop**.

**Why Ammonia?**
- Very **low boiling point** (-33 °C) — ideal for low-grade heat sources.
- **High latent heat of vaporization** (~1377 kJ/kg) — extracts more energy per kg.
- Non-corrosive, easy to handle, and inexpensive.

**Block Diagram (See §4.2 of Section 4).**

*(3 marks for working, 2 marks for ammonia properties, 2 marks for diagram.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Basin area: $A = 20 \text{ km}^2 = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$
- Tidal range: $R = 6 \text{ m}$
- Efficiency: $C_{eff} = 0.25$
- Tidal period: $T = 12.42 \text{ hours} = 44,712 \text{ s}$

**Step 1: Calculate the available potential energy per cycle.**

$$
E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2
$$

$$
E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (20 \times 10^6) \times (6)^2
$$

$$
E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 20 \times 10^6 \times 36
$$

$$
E \approx 3.62 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} = 3620 \text{ GJ}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the efficiency factor.**

$$
E_{useful} = 0.25 \times 3.62 \times 10^{12} = 9.05 \times 10^{11} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the average power.**

$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{T} = \dfrac{9.05 \times 10^{11}}{44,712}
$$

$$
P_{avg} \approx 2.02 \times 10^7 \text{ W} = 20.2 \text{ MW}
$$

**Final Answer:** The average power available from the tidal barrage is approximately **20.2 MW** per tidal cycle. *(2 marks for energy, 2 marks for efficiency application, 3 marks for power calculation.)*

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Print This Section Before the Exam**

- [ ] OTEC requires **ΔT ≥ 20 °C** between warm surface and cold deep seawater.
- [ ] **Open Cycle** = Claude = **seawater is the working fluid** + gives **fresh water** as a by-product.
- [ ] **Closed Cycle** = Anderson = **ammonia (NH₃, b.p. = -33 °C)** is the working fluid in a closed loop.
- [ ] **Carnot efficiency** of OTEC at typical ocean temperatures is only **6 % to 8 %**.
- [ ] Cold water for OTEC is drawn from depths of **~1000 m**.
- [ ] Tidal energy is **predictable** and has a high **energy density** (ρ_sea ≈ 1025 kg/m³).
- [ ] **Tidal range formula:** $E = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$.
- [ ] **Tidal stream power:** $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$.
- [ ] **La Rance (France)** = 240 MW, the world's first and largest tidal barrage (1966).
- [ ] **Sihwa Lake (South Korea)** = 254 MW, the world's largest as of 2024.
- [ ] **Advantages of OTEC:** Continuous (24/7) baseload, no fuel cost, byproduct fresh water.
- [ ] **Disadvantages of OTEC:** Low efficiency, high capital cost, suitable only in tropical belts (between 20° N and 20° S).
- [ ] **3 OTEC sites** to memorize: Hawaii (USA), Kume Island (Japan), Sagar (India — proposed).
- [ ] **Formula to remember for KTU exams:** $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - T_c/T_h$ (temperatures in **Kelvin**!).
- [ ] Tidal period = **12 hours 25 minutes** (semi-diurnal) — used in the denominator of power formulas.
- [ ] **Hybrid OTEC** combines the open cycle's fresh water production with the closed cycle's higher efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
