# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Pattern:** 2–3 sentence conceptual answers, definitions, or short derivations.

---

### **Question A1:** Define wind energy and explain why it is considered a form of solar energy. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Wind energy is the kinetic energy possessed by moving air masses that can be converted into electrical or mechanical energy using wind turbines. It is considered a form of solar energy because wind originates from the **uneven heating of the Earth's surface by solar radiation**. Different surfaces (land, water, forests) absorb solar heat at different rates, creating **pressure gradients** in the atmosphere. Air flows from high-pressure to low-pressure regions, producing wind. Approximately **1–2% of incoming solar energy** is converted into wind kinetic energy, making wind energy an indirect form of solar energy.

---

### **Question A2:** State the Betz limit and explain its significance. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Betz limit** states that no wind turbine can extract more than **16/27 (≈ 59.3%)** of the kinetic energy present in the wind. This theoretical maximum efficiency was derived by German physicist **Albert Betz in 1919** using the actuator disc theory. Its significance is that it defines the **upper bound of efficiency** for any wind energy conversion system, regardless of design. Real-world turbines achieve **$C_p$ values of 0.35–0.45** (35–45%), well below the Betz limit, due to mechanical losses, aerodynamic inefficiencies, and wake turbulence.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two sub-parts of 7 marks each, with internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the available wind power passing through a wind turbine rotor. State clearly all assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A wind turbine with rotor diameter 60 m operates at a site where the average wind speed is 14 m/s. The air density is 1.225 kg/m³ and the power coefficient is 0.42. Calculate: (i) swept area, (ii) available wind power, (iii) actual power output, and (iv) annual energy generation assuming 80% plant availability. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation of Available Wind Power (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Definition of Swept Area**

A wind turbine has blades of length $R$ (rotor radius). The disc swept by the rotating blades has area:
$$A = \pi R^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2: Mass of Air Crossing the Rotor in Time $dt$**

In time $dt$, the column of air of cross-section $A$ moves a distance $v \cdot dt$ before reaching the rotor. The mass is:
$$dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot dt$$

where $\rho$ is the air density.

**Step 3: Kinetic Energy of This Mass**
$$dKE = \frac{1}{2} dm \cdot v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 \, dt$$

**Step 4: Power = Rate of Energy Transfer**
$$P_{wind} = \frac{dKE}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Air is **incompressible** (valid for $v < 100$ m/s)
2. Air flow is **uniform** across the rotor disc
3. **No obstacles** upstream (unobstructed wind)
4. Wind speed is **constant** during the analysis
5. The rotor disc is a **thin actuator** with no thickness

**Step 5: Final Expression**
$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = \frac{\pi}{8} \rho D^2 v^3 \text{ (watts)}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 60$ m, $v = 14$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $C_p = 0.42$, plant availability = 80%

**(i) Swept Area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (60)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 3600}{4} = 900\pi \approx 2827.4 \text{ m}^2$$

**(ii) Available Wind Power:**
$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 2827.4 \times (14)^3$$
$$P_{wind} = 0.6125 \times 2827.4 \times 2744 = 4{,}752{,}000 \text{ W} \approx 4.75 \text{ MW}$$

**(iii) Actual Power Output:**
$$P_{actual} = C_p \times P_{wind} = 0.42 \times 4.75 \text{ MW} \approx 1.996 \text{ MW} \approx 2.0 \text{ MW}$$

**(iv) Annual Energy Generation:**

Hours in a year = 365 × 24 = 8760 hours
Effective hours with 80% availability = 0.80 × 8760 = 7008 hours
$$E_{annual} = P_{actual} \times t = 1.996 \text{ MW} \times 7008 \text{ h} \approx 13{,}989 \text{ MWh} \approx 14 \text{ GWh}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the actuator disc theory used to derive the Betz limit. Show that the maximum power coefficient is 16/27. (7 marks)

**(b)** A horizontal-axis wind turbine has a tip speed ratio of 7. The rotor diameter is 90 m and the wind speed is 10 m/s. The turbine rotates at 18 rpm. Calculate: (i) the rotor radius, (ii) the linear tip speed, (iii) verify whether the given TSR matches the given rotational speed, and (iv) the rotational speed in rad/s. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Betz Limit Derivation (7 marks):**

(See the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes.)

**Key Steps to Present:**

**Step 1:** Model the rotor as a porous disc (actuator) that slows the wind from $v_1$ (upstream) to $v_2$ (downstream), with velocity $v$ at the disc.

**Step 2:** Define the **axial induction factor** $a$:
$$v = v_1(1 - a), \quad v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a)$$

**Step 3:** Apply momentum conservation. The thrust force on the disc:
$$F = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2) = 2 \rho A v_1^2 a(1-a)$$

**Step 4:** Power extracted = Force × velocity at disc:
$$P = F \cdot v = 2 \rho A v_1^3 a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 5:** Power coefficient:
$$C_p = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 6:** Differentiate and set to zero:
$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0 \Rightarrow a = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $a = 1/3$:
$$C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum fraction of wind kinetic energy that can be converted to mechanical energy by an ideal wind turbine is **16/27 ≈ 59.3%**, known as the **Betz limit**.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\lambda = 7$, $D = 90$ m, $v = 10$ m/s, $n = 18$ rpm

**(i) Rotor Radius:**
$$R = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{90}{2} = 45 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Linear Tip Speed:**
$$v_{tip} = \lambda \times v = 7 \times 10 = 70 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Verify TSR Match:**

Actual TSR from given rotational speed:
$$\lambda_{actual} = \frac{\pi D n}{60 v} = \frac{\pi \times 90 \times 18}{60 \times 10} = \frac{1620\pi}{600} = 2.7\pi \approx 8.48$$

The given TSR is **7**, but the calculated TSR from $n = 18$ rpm is **8.48**. There is a mismatch — the given TSR of 7 corresponds to a rotational speed of:
$$n_{required} = \frac{60 \lambda v}{\pi D} = \frac{60 \times 7 \times 10}{\pi \times 90} = \frac{4200}{90\pi} \approx 14.85 \text{ rpm}$$

So either the TSR or the rpm value needs to be corrected in the problem statement.

**(iv) Rotational Speed in rad/s:**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi n}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 18}{60} = \frac{36\pi}{60} = 0.6\pi \approx 1.885 \text{ rad/s}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **⚠️ Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cubic power dependence:** Students often write $P \propto v$ instead of $P \propto v^3$. If wind speed changes from 10 m/s to 12 m/s, the power ratio is $(12/10)^3 = 1.728$, not **1.2**. This single error loses **3–4 marks**.
>
> 2. **Using $\pi$ vs. not using $\pi$:** When area is given as $\pi D^2/4$ vs. as a number, students often mix up. Use $A = \pi R^2$ or $A = \pi D^2/4$ **consistently**.
>
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Wind speeds in km/h vs. m/s, areas in cm² vs. m², energy in Wh vs. J. KTU examiners penalize missing unit conversions by **1–2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing power with energy:** Students often calculate $P$ and write it as annual energy. Remember: **Energy = Power × Time**; convert hours correctly (365 days × 24 h = 8760 h/year).
>
> 5. **Stating Betz limit incorrectly:** Some students write 50% or 60% — the **exact value is 16/27 ≈ 0.593**, and you **must show the derivation** to get full marks.
>
> 6. **Skipping assumptions:** In derivation questions, KTU examiners require **explicit assumptions** (incompressible flow, uniform velocity, etc.) before the formula. Skipping them costs **1 mark minimum**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **📋 Last-Minute Revision Before KTU Exam**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- [ ] Wind Energy = Kinetic energy of moving air converted to electrical/mechanical form
- [ ] WECS = Wind Energy Conversion System
- [ ] Power Coefficient $C_p$ = Ratio of extracted power to available wind power
- [ ] Betz Limit = Maximum theoretical $C_p = 16/27 \approx 59.3\%$
- [ ] TSR (Tip Speed Ratio) = $\lambda = \omega R / v$
- [ ] Cut-in, rated, and cut-out wind speeds: **3, 12–15, 25 m/s** (typical values)

### ✅ Essential Formulas
- [ ] Swept Area: $A = \pi D^2 / 4$
- [ ] Available Power: $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$
- [ ] Extracted Power: $P = \frac{1}{2} C_p \rho A v^3 = \frac{\pi}{8} C_p \rho D^2 v^3$
- [ ] Betz Power Coefficient: $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$, max at $a = 1/3$
- [ ] Energy: $E = P \cdot t$
- [ ] Wind Shear Law: $v(h) = v_{ref}(h/h_{ref})^\alpha$

### ✅ Key Numerical Values
- [ ] Air density at sea level (ISA): $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- [ ] Power law exponent (open terrain): $\alpha = 1/7 \approx 0.143$
- [ ] Modern utility turbine capacity: 2–15 MW
- [ ] Blade diameter: 80–220 m
- [ ] Typical capacity factor: **0.20–0.45** (onshore), **0.45–0.60** (offshore)

### ✅ Classification Mastery
- [ ] HAWT vs. VAWT — know examples, pros/cons
- [ ] Micro, Small, Medium, Large turbine categories
- [ ] Onshore vs. Offshore — cost, capacity factor, maintenance differences

### ✅ Conceptual Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Power is **cubic** in wind speed — never linear!
- [ ] Betz is a **theoretical maximum** — real turbines achieve 35–45%
- [ ] Wind energy is **indirect solar energy** — origin matters for exam answers
- [ ] India ranks **4th globally** in installed wind capacity (2024)
- [ ] Standalone vs. Grid-connected applications differ in design

### ✅ Engineering Applications to Remember
- [ ] Electricity generation (utility-scale wind farms)
- [ ] Water pumping (multi-blade slow turbines)
- [ ] Hybrid systems (Wind + Solar + Battery + Diesel)
- [ ] Offshore wind + green hydrogen (emerging frontier)
- [ ] Distributed generation for remote/off-grid areas

### ✅ Frequently Asked KTU Question Topics
- [ ] Derivation of $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ — **asked almost every year**
- [ ] Derivation of Betz limit — **high weightage**
- [ ] Numerical problems on power, energy, and capacity factor
- [ ] Comparison of HAWT vs. VAWT
- [ ] Wind energy potential of India
- [ ] Wind shear and hub-height corrections
- [ ] Cut-in, rated, cut-out speed significance

> [!NOTE] **🎯 Final Exam Strategy for KTU 2024**
> - For **Part A (3 marks):** Focus on precise definitions, Betz limit value, and cubic relationship
> - For **Part B (14 marks):** Always show **all derivation steps with assumptions**; in numericals, carry 4 significant figures and **always state units in the final answer**
> - Use **labeled diagrams** wherever possible — examiners reward visual clarity
> - End with a **boxed final answer** for every numerical problem
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomass Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT632 - RENEWABLE ENERGY > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Biomass Fuels # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biomass Fuel** is any organic, non-fossil biological material of recent origin (plant or animal matter) that is deliberately harvested, processed, or converted to release stored chemical energy in the form of heat, electricity, or liquid/gaseous transportation fuels. In strict thermodynamic terms, biomass is the **stored form of solar energy**, captured by chlorophyll-bearing organisms through the process of **photosynthesis**. The solar radiant energy incident on green plants is converted into chemical energy locked inside carbon-based molecules (carbohydrates, lignocellulose, lipids). The universal photosynthetic reaction acts as the **parent energy equation** for *all* biomass: $$6CO_{2} + 6H_{2}O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight, Chlorophyll}} C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} + 6O_{2}$$ Therefore, every joule of energy liberated by burning biomass is essentially **ancient sunlight** returning to the atmosphere as heat, completing a closed, carbon-neutral cycle on a human timescale. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition Block (Examiner-Ready):** *"Biomass refers to the biodegradable fraction of products, wastes, and residues from agriculture, forestry, and related industries, as well as the biodegradable municipal solid waste, which can be used as a fuel for the production of energy."* ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of a tree as a **biological battery that charges itself from the sun every day**: - The **Sun** acts as the charger (energy source). - The **leaf (chloroplast)** acts as the charging circuit. - The **lignin + cellulose + sugars** inside the wood/fruit act as the stored chemical charge. - When we burn wood, or ferment sugarcane juice, we are simply **discharging this biological battery** to power our stoves, cars, or turbines. Unlike coal or petroleum (which are **non-renewable** "batteries" that take *millions* of years to charge and were sealed away in geological vaults), biomass recharges itself in a single growing season, making it a **renewable** energy source. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics - **Density of dry wood:** 450 – 750 kg/m³ - **Net Calorific Value of dry wood:** ~**17 MJ/kg** (≈ 4050 kcal/kg) - **Carbon content of typical biomass:** 45 – 50 % by mass - **Average biomass-to-energy conversion efficiency** (combustion route): 25 – 35 % - **Density of standard methane (CH₄):** 0.717 kg/m³ at STP - **Global average Photosynthetic Efficiency (η):** only **~1 – 2 %** of incident solar radiation > [!TIP] > **Why is biomass considered carbon-neutral?** > The CO₂ released during combustion of biomass is *reabsorbed* by the next generation of crops/trees during their growth phase. This forms a **closed-loop short carbon cycle**, unlike fossil fuels which release *geologically sequestered* carbon. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Biomass Fuels (KTU High-Yield) Biomass is classified by **physical state** and **source origin**. Examiners frequently ask for tabular comparisons. ### 2.1.1 By Physical State | Category | Examples | Typical State | Energy Density | |----------|----------|---------------|----------------| | **Solid Biomass** | Firewood, sawdust, rice husk, bagasse, cow dung, pellets | Pellets, chips, logs | 14 – 20 MJ/kg | | **Liquid Biofuels** | Bioethanol, biodiesel, methanol, pyrolysis oil | Liquid | 25 – 42 MJ/kg | | **Gaseous Biofuels** | Biogas, producer gas, syngas, hydrogen | Gas | 15 – 55 MJ/m³ | ### 2.1.2 By Generation Tier (KTU-Favoured Classification) 1. **First-Generation:** Sugar/starch crops (sugarcane → ethanol), oilseeds (soybean → biodiesel). Direct food-crop based. 2. **Second-Generation:** Lignocellulosic biomass (wood, agricultural residues, grasses). Non-food based. 3. **Third-Generation:** Algae-based biofuels. High yield, non-arable. 4. **Fourth-Generation:** Genetically engineered cyanobacteria; synthetic biology. ## 2.2 Ultimate (Proximate) Analysis of Biomass Two laboratory tests are universally used to characterize any solid fuel: ### 2.2.1 Proximate Analysis (Mass %) Determines: **Moisture (M), Volatile Matter (VM), Fixed Carbon (FC), Ash Content (A)** - **Moisture Content (M):** Water held within the fuel matrix. **High moisture → poor combustion**. Must be $<20\%$ for efficient furnace operation. - **Volatile Matter (VM):** Gases released upon heating (CO, H₂, CH₄, tars). Typical biomass: 70 – 85 % (much higher than coal, hence biomass burns with long flames). - **Fixed Carbon (FC):** The solid combustible residue left after devolatilization. - **Ash Content (A):** Non-combustible inorganic residue (SiO₂, CaO, K₂O). Biomass ash is typically low (1 – 10 %) but has high alkali content → causes **slagging and fouling** in boilers. ### 2.2.2 Ultimate Analysis (Mass %) Determines elemental composition: **C, H, O, N, S** Biomass is rich in **Oxygen (30 – 45 %)** and low in **Sulphur ($<1\%$)**, making it a "clean" fuel compared to coal. ## 2.3 Conversion Technologies (Process Engineering) The energy locked in biomass is liberated through three primary conversion routes: ### 2.3.1 Thermochemical Conversion Uses heat to break down biomass. | Process | Temperature | Oxygen Supply | Primary Product | |---------|-------------|---------------|-----------------| | **Combustion** | 800 – 1200 °C | Excess air | Heat, flue gas, ash | | **Gasification** | 700 – 900 °C | Sub-stoichiometric | Producer gas (CO + H₂) | | **Pyrolysis** | 300 – 700 °C | Absent / inert | Bio-char, bio-oil, syngas | | **Liquefaction** | 250 – 400 °C | High pressure H₂ | Bio-crude oil | ### 2.3.2 Biochemical Conversion Uses microorganisms/enzymes at ambient conditions. - **Anaerobic Digestion (AD):** Biomass + bacteria (no O₂) → Biogas (60 % CH₄ + 40 % CO₂) + Digestate (fertilizer). - **Fermentation:** Sugars → Bioethanol via yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*). - **Transesterification:** Vegetable oil + Methanol (catalyst: NaOH) → Biodiesel (FAME) + Glycerol. ### 2.3.3 Physico-Chemical Conversion Includes **oil expelling, pelletization, briquetting, torrefaction**. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mastery Warning:** All formulas in this table are exam-essential. Memorize the variables, units, and sign conventions. | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application | |---|--------------|--------------------------|-------------------|-------------| | 1 | **Photosynthesis (Energy Fixation)** | $6CO_{2} + 6H_{2}O \rightarrow C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} + 6O_{2}$ | $\Delta H = +2803 \text{ kJ/mol glucose}$ | Origin of biomass energy | | 2 | **Combustion of Carbon** | $C + O_{2} \rightarrow CO_{2}$ | $\Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Heat from charcoal | | 3 | **Combustion of Hydrogen** | $H_{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} \rightarrow H_{2}O$ | $\Delta H = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Water vapour formation | | 4 | **Dulong's Empirical Formula (GCV)** | $\text{GCV} = 33820\,C + 144000\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9419\,S$ (kJ/kg) | C, H, O, S are mass fractions | Estimating calorific value | | 5 | **Net (Lower) Calorific Value** | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - h_{fg}\!\left(\dfrac{9H + M}{100}\right)$ | $h_{fg} = 2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$, M = moisture % | Realistic boiler heat input | | 6 | **Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric)** | $\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \dfrac{11.6\,C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35\,S}{100}$ | Mass-based ratio | Combustion calculations | | 7 | **Biogas Yield Equation** | $V_{CH_{4}} = 0.35 \times \text{VS}_{loaded} \times \left[1 - \dfrac{e^{-k \cdot HRT}}{1 + \text{recycle ratio}}\right]$ | VS = volatile solids, k = rate constant | Anaerobic digester design | | 8 | **Energy from Combustion** | $E = \dot{m} \times \text{GCV} \times \eta_{comb}$ | $\dot{m}$ = fuel flow (kg/s), $\eta$ = efficiency | Boiler sizing | | 9 | **Transesterification Stoichiometry** | $\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,CH_{3}OH \rightarrow 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$ | 1:3 molar ratio | Biodiesel yield | | 10 | **Energy Density of Biogas** | $\text{CV}_{biogas} = 0.60 \times 35.8 + 0.40 \times 0 = 21.5 \text{ MJ/m}^{3}$ | 60% CH₄, 40% CO₂ typical | Gas engine sizing | | 11 | **Mass Energy Equivalence** | $E = mc^{2}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ | Theoretical limit (not used in biomass problems) | | 12 | **Overall Plant Efficiency** | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{collection} \times \eta_{conversion} \times \eta_{utilization}$ | Dimensionless | System analysis | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder for Examiners:** In Dulong's formula, $\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right)$ accounts for the hydrogen already bound to internal oxygen (i.e., it cannot combust to form H₂O). The "available hydrogen" is the combustible portion only. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Rural Electrification:** 100 % rice-husk-based cogeneration plants in Kerala (e.g., 5 – 10 MW rice mills). - **Transportation:** India’s **E20 ethanol blending** program (20 % ethanol in petrol) is fundamentally a biomass-fuels initiative. - **Climate Mitigation:** Replacing 1 tonne of coal with 1.4 tonnes of wood pellets reduces net CO₂ by ~1.8 tonnes on a life-cycle basis. - **Waste Management:** Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) to energy plants solve two problems simultaneously — sanitation and power. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Biomass Combustion This is a **14-mark favourite** in KTU Module 3. We derive the air needed to completely combust 1 kg of biomass of known ultimate analysis. ### 3.1.1 Step 1 – Write the Fundamental Combustion Equation For a generic solid fuel of mass composition $C, H, O, S, N$ (all in mass fraction, summing to 1), complete combustion yields $CO_2, H_2O, SO_2, N_2$ (and unused $N_2$ from air). $$C + O_{2} \rightarrow CO_{2}$$ $$H_{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2} \rightarrow H_{2}O$$ $$S + O_{2} \rightarrow SO_{2}$$ ### 3.1.2 Step 2 – Calculate Moles of O₂ Required per kg of Fuel Using atomic/molecular masses: C = 12, H = 1, O = 16, S = 32, N = 14, N₂ = 28, O₂ = 32. Oxygen required for C: $\dfrac{C}{12} \times 1 = \dfrac{C}{12}$ kmol of O₂ per kg fuel. Oxygen required for H: $\dfrac{H}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{H}{4}$ kmol of O₂ per kg fuel. Oxygen required for S: $\dfrac{S}{32} \times 1 = \dfrac{S}{32}$ kmol of O₂ per kg fuel. ### 3.1.3 Step 3 – Subtract Internally Available Oxygen The fuel itself contains oxygen, which can be used for partial combustion. Available O₂ from fuel: $\dfrac{O}{32} \times 1 = \dfrac{O}{32}$ kmol per kg. ### 3.1.4 Step 4 – Net O₂ Required (kmol per kg fuel) $$n_{O_{2}} = \dfrac{C}{12} + \dfrac{H}{4} + \dfrac{S}{32} - \dfrac{O}{32}$$ ### 3.1.5 Step 5 – Convert to Mass of O₂ Multiply by molecular weight of O₂ (= 32 kg/kmol): $$m_{O_{2}} = 32 \left(\dfrac{C}{12} + \dfrac{H}{4} + \dfrac{S}{32} - \dfrac{O}{32}\right) \text{ kg per kg fuel}$$ ### 3.1.6 Step 6 – Convert to Mass of Air Air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass (since O₂ = 21 % by volume, and 28.97 ÷ 32 ratio gives ~23.2 %): $$m_{air} = \dfrac{m_{O_{2}}}{0.232}$$ ### 3.1.7 Step 7 – Final Compact Air-Fuel Ratio Expression $$\boxed{\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = 11.6\,C + 34.8\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35\,S \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}\right]}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Derivation Logic Check:** The factors $11.6 = 32/(12 \times 0.232)$, $34.8 = 8/0.232$, $4.35 = 1/0.232$ — all come from converting elemental moles to mass, then dividing by 0.232. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A biomass fuel has the following ultimate analysis (by mass): C = 48 %, H = 6 %, O = 44 %, S = 0.5 %, N = 0.5 %, Ash = 1 %. Calculate the **(a)** stoichiometric air-fuel ratio, **(b)** GCV using Dulong's formula, **(c)** NCV at 20 % moisture. ### 3.2.1 Part (a) – Air-Fuel Ratio $$\begin{aligned} \left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} &= 11.6 \times 0.48 + 34.8 \times \left(0.06 - \dfrac{0.44}{8}\right) + 4.35 \times 0.005 \\ &= 11.6 \times 0.48 + 34.8 \times (0.06 - 0.055) + 0.02175 \\ &= 5.568 + 34.8 \times 0.005 + 0.02175 \\ &= 5.568 + 0.174 + 0.02175 \\ &= 5.764 \quad \dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}} \end{aligned}$$ > *Conversion logic:* Each term represents the air required to burn the respective element. The "−O/8" subtracts the oxygen already chemically combined with hydrogen inside the fuel. ### 3.2.2 Part (b) – Gross Calorific Value (Dulong) $$\begin{aligned} \text{GCV} &= 33820\,C + 144000\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9419\,S \\ &= 33820 \times 0.48 + 144000 \times \left(0.06 - \dfrac{0.44}{8}\right) + 9419 \times 0.005 \\ &= 16233.6 + 144000 \times 0.005 + 47.095 \\ &= 16233.6 + 720 + 47.095 \\ &= 17000.7 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ ### 3.2.3 Part (c) – Net Calorific Value at 20 % Moisture $$\begin{aligned} \text{NCV} &= \text{GCV} - h_{fg}\left(\dfrac{9H + M}{100}\right) \\ &= 17000.7 - 2260 \times \left(\dfrac{9 \times 6 + 20}{100}\right) \\ &= 17000.7 - 2260 \times \left(\dfrac{54 + 20}{100}\right) \\ &= 17000.7 - 2260 \times 0.74 \\ &= 17000.7 - 1672.4 \\ &= 15328.3 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ > *Conversion logic:* The factor 9H comes from the fact that 1 kg of H combines with 8 kg of O to form 9 kg of H₂O, which then absorbs latent heat $h_{fg}$ during vaporization. ## 3.3 Biogas Composition & Energy Calculation Standard anaerobic digester output: 60 % CH₄ + 40 % CO₂ (by volume). Higher Heating Value (HHV) of pure CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/kg = 39.7 MJ/m³ at STP. $$\text{HHV of biogas} = 0.60 \times 39.7 = 23.82 \text{ MJ/m}^{3}$$ > *Conversion logic:* CO₂ is a non-combustible diluent. The energy content is purely from the methane fraction, hence a linear weighted average. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Biomass vs. Fossil Fuels | Property | Wood (Biomass) | Coal | Petroleum | |----------|----------------|------|-----------| | C (%) | 45 – 50 | 65 – 95 | 83 – 87 | | O (%) | 35 – 45 | 2 – 15 | 1 – 3 | | S (%) | $<0.5$ | 1 – 8 | 0.5 – 3 | | Ash (%) | 1 – 5 | 5 – 20 | $<0.5$ | | GCV (MJ/kg) | 14 – 20 | 24 – 35 | 42 – 45 | | CO₂ footprint | Carbon-neutral | High | High | | Renewability | Years | Millions of years | Millions of years | | SO₂ emissions | Negligible | High (acid rain) | Moderate | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Comprehensive Biomass Classification & Conversion Flow The following Mermaid **block-level functional architecture** maps all biomass types to their corresponding conversion technologies and end-use products. This is the kind of schematic a KTU examiner expects in 14-mark answers. ```mermaid graph TD SUN[INCIDENT SOLAR RADIATION] --> PHOTO[PHOTOSYNTHESIS IN CHLOROPLAST] PHOTO --> BM[BIOMASS FEEDSTOCKS] BM --> AGRI[AGRICULTURAL RESIDUES
RICE HUSK BAGASSE COB] BM --> FOREST[FORESTRY RESIDUES
SAWDUST BARK FIREWOOD] BM --> MSW[MUNICIPAL SOLID WASTE
ORGANIC FRACTION] BM --> ENERGY[ENERGY CROPS
SUGARCANE JATROPHA ALGAE] BM --> ANIMAL[ANIMAL WASTE
COW DUNG POULTRY LITTER] AGRI --> THERM[THERMOCHEMICAL ROUTE] FOREST --> THERM MSW --> THERM ENERGY --> BIOCH[BIOCHEMICAL ROUTE] ANIMAL --> BIOCH THERM --> COMB[COMBUSTION
800 TO 1200 DEG C] THERM --> GAS[GASIFICATION
700 TO 900 DEG C] THERM --> PYRO[PYROLYSIS
300 TO 700 DEG C] THERM --> TOR[TORREFACTION
200 TO 300 DEG C] COMB --> H1[HEAT AND STEAM] COMB --> E1[ELECTRICITY VIA BOILER TURBINE] GAS --> PG[PRODUCER GAS
CO PLUS H2 PLUS N2] PG --> GE[GAS ENGINE OR DUAL FUEL ENGINE] PYRO --> BC[BIO CHAR
SOIL AMENDMENT] PYRO --> BO[BIO OIL
FUEL UPGRADE] PYRO --> SG[SYN GAS
FISCHER TROPSCH] TOR --> PEL[UPGRADED PELLETS
EXPORT FUEL] BIOCH --> AD[ANAEROBIC DIGESTION
O2 FREE BIOREACTOR] BIOCH --> FER[FERMENTATION
YEAST MEDIATED] AD --> BG[BIOGAS
60 PERCENT CH4 PLUS 40 PERCENT CO2] BG --> CHE[CHEMICAL ENGINE HEAT] FER --> ETH[BIOETHANOL C2H5OH] ETH --> MIX[E20 OR E100 PETROL BLEND] ENERGY --> TRANS[TRANSESTERIFICATION
OIL PLUS METHANOL PLUS NAOH] TRANS --> BD[BIODIESEL FAME] BD --> VEH[DIESEL ENGINE VEHICLES] E1 --> GRID[POWER GRID DISTRIBUTION] GE --> GRID BG --> GRID H1 --> IND[INDUSTRIAL PROCESS HEAT] CHE --> IND style SUN fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style BM fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style GRID fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 style VEH fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 style IND fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 style BG fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style BD fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style ETH fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow: Anaerobic Digestion Plant ```mermaid graph LR A[BIOMASS FEEDSTOCK
AGRI OR ANIMAL WASTE] --> B[PRE TREATMENT
SIZE REDUCTION] B --> C[DIGESTER
MESOPHILIC 35 DEG C
OR THERMOPHILIC 55 DEG C] C --> D[GAS COLLECTION
H2S SCRUBBING] D --> E[CHP UNIT
COMBINED HEAT AND POWER] E --> F[ELECTRICITY TO GRID] E --> G[HEAT TO DIGESTER RECYCLE] C --> H[DIGESTATE SLURRY] H --> I[DEWATERING] I --> J[LIQUID FERTILIZER] I --> K[SOLID COMPOST] style C fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic: Biodiesel Transesterification ```mermaid graph TD O[VEGETABLE OIL OR ANIMAL FAT
TRIGLYCERIDE] --> R[REACTOR
60 DEG C ATMOSPHERIC] M[METHANOL CH3OH] --> R C[CATALYST NAOH OR KOH
1 PERCENT BY MASS] --> R R --> S[SEPARATION FUNNEL
GRAVITY SETTLING] S --> BD[BIODIESEL FAME LAYER
UPPER PHASE] S --> GL[GLYCEROL LAYER
LOWER PHASE] BD --> W[NEUTRALIZATION
WATER WASHING] W --> DR[DRYING UNDER VACUUM] DR --> OUT[FUEL GRADE BIODIESEL] GL --> SO[SOAP STOCK
SALE OR RECYCLE] style R fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style OUT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each node uses **clean uppercase alphanumeric labels only**. No markdown formatting (no bold/italic) is embedded inside the Mermaid string labels to prevent rendering errors. All process stages (orange) lead to end products (blue) or utilities (green) and finally to end-use (red). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Define biomass. List any four examples of biomass fuels.** **Model Answer:** Biomass is the biodegradable fraction of products, wastes, and residues of biological origin from agriculture, forestry, and related industries, as well as the biodegradable fraction of municipal and industrial waste, which can be used as a fuel for energy generation. (2 marks) Four examples (½ mark each, total 1 mark for any four): 1. Firewood (woody biomass) 2. Rice husk and bagasse (agricultural residues) 3. Cow dung (animal waste) 4. Sugarcane molasses → bioethanol (energy crop) --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **Differentiate between proximate and ultimate analysis of a solid fuel.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Proximate Analysis | Ultimate Analysis | |-----------|-------------------|-------------------| | **Determines** | Moisture, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon, Ash | C, H, O, N, S (elemental form) | | **Application** | Quick combustion behaviour prediction | Stoichiometric and GCV calculations | | **Procedure** | Heating in controlled atmospheres | Combustion + gas chromatography | | **Output (typical biomass)** | M 8 %, VM 75 %, FC 15 %, A 2 % | C 48 %, H 6 %, O 44 %, N 0.5 % | (1½ marks for definition, 1½ marks for the comparison table.) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] > **(a)** Explain in detail the **different thermochemical conversion routes** of biomass with a comparative table of products, temperatures, and air/oxygen requirements. (7 marks) > > **(b)** A biomass sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 50 %, H = 6 %, O = 42 %, S = 0.5 %, N = 0.5 %, Ash = 1.0 %. Calculate: (i) Stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel, (ii) GCV using Dulong's formula, (iii) NCV at 12 % moisture content. (7 marks) #### Solution to A(a): The four major thermochemical routes are: 1. **Combustion (800 – 1200 °C, excess air):** Direct burning of biomass in the presence of excess O₂. Used for steam generation, district heating, and power. Efficiency 25 – 35 %. 2. **Gasification (700 – 900 °C, sub-stoichiometric air):** Partial oxidation produces **producer gas** (CO + H₂ + N₂ + CH₄). Air factor: 0.25 – 0.4 of stoichiometric. Cold gas efficiency 70 – 80 %. 3. **Pyrolysis (300 – 700 °C, no oxygen):** Thermal decomposition in inert atmosphere. Yields three products in varying ratios: - **Slow pyrolysis:** mainly bio-char (35 %) - **Fast pyrolysis:** mainly bio-oil (75 %) - **Flash pyrolysis:** syngas dominance 4. **Torrefaction (200 – 300 °C, inert):** Mild roasting; produces a coal-like hydrophobic solid with higher energy density (~20 – 24 MJ/kg) suitable for export. | Process | Temp (°C) | Atmosphere | Main Product | Yield | |---------|-----------|------------|--------------|-------| | Combustion | 800 – 1200 | Excess air | Heat + ash | n/a | | Gasification | 700 – 900 | Limited air | Producer gas | 70 – 80 % cold gas eff. | | Pyrolysis (fast) | 500 – 700 | Inert (N₂) | Bio-oil | 75 % wt | | Torrefaction | 200 – 300 | Inert (N₂) | Solid pellets | 80 % wt | (7 marks: 4 for descriptions + 3 for table) #### Solution to A(b): **(i) Stoichiometric air:** $$\begin{aligned} \left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} &= 11.6\,C + 34.8\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35\,S \\ &= 11.6 \times 0.50 + 34.8 \times \left(0.06 - \dfrac{0.42}{8}\right) + 4.35 \times 0.005 \\ &= 5.80 + 34.8 \times (0.06 - 0.0525) + 0.02175 \\ &= 5.80 + 34.8 \times 0.0075 + 0.02175 \\ &= 5.80 + 0.261 + 0.02175 \\ &= 6.083 \quad \dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) GCV by Dulong:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{GCV} &= 33820\,C + 144000\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9419\,S \\ &= 33820 \times 0.50 + 144000 \times 0.0075 + 9419 \times 0.005 \\ &= 16910 + 1080 + 47.095 \\ &= 18037.1 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) NCV at M = 12 %:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{NCV} &= \text{GCV} - 2260 \times \left(\dfrac{9H + M}{100}\right) \\ &= 18037.1 - 2260 \times \left(\dfrac{9 \times 6 + 12}{100}\right) \\ &= 18037.1 - 2260 \times 0.66 \\ &= 18037.1 - 1491.6 \\ &= 16545.5 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ (7 marks: 2.5 + 2.5 + 2) --- ### QUESTION B — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] > **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **process of anaerobic digestion** for biogas production. Discuss the factors affecting biogas yield. (7 marks) > > **(b)** What is **transesterification**? Derive the stoichiometry and write the material balance for converting 1000 kg of soybean oil (assumed pure triolein, M = 884 kg/kmol) to biodiesel (methyl oleate, M = 296 kg/kmol) using excess methanol. (7 marks) #### Solution to B(a): Anaerobic digestion (AD) is a biochemical process in which organic matter is decomposed by a consortium of anaerobic bacteria in the **absence of oxygen**, producing a combustible gas called **biogas** (typically 55 – 70 % CH₄, 30 – 45 % CO₂) and a nutrient-rich slurry called **digestate**. **Schematic of an AD plant:** ``` Feedstock → Pre-treatment (size reduction) → Mixing tank ↓ Digester (mesophilic 35 °C, HRT 15 – 30 days) ↓ ↓ Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) Digestate ↓ ↓ H₂S Scrubber Dewatering → Solid compost ↓ ↓ Gas Holder Liquid fertilizer ↓ Gas Engine / Boiler → Electricity + Heat ``` **Four stages of microbial degradation:** 1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers (cellulose, proteins) → monomers (sugars, amino acids). 2. **Acidogenesis** — Monomers → volatile fatty acids (VFAs) + alcohols. 3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs → acetic acid + H₂ + CO₂. 4. **Methanogenesis** — Acetic acid / H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ (by *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus*). **Overall reaction:** $$C_{6}H_{10}O_{4} + H_{2}O \rightarrow 3\,CH_{4} + 3\,CO_{2}$$ **Factors affecting yield:** - **Temperature** (mesophilic 35 °C vs thermophilic 55 °C) - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** 15 – 40 days - **pH:** 6.8 – 7.5 (critical for methanogens) - **C/N ratio:** 20:1 – 30:1 optimal - **Moisture content:** 90 – 95 % in slurry - **Toxicity** from ammonia, heavy metals, antibiotics (7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for stages + reaction, 2 for factors) #### Solution to B(b): **Transesterification** is the chemical reaction in which the **alkyl group of an ester is exchanged** with another alcohol in the presence of a catalyst. In biodiesel production, the triglycerides (vegetable oil/animal fat) react with a short-chain alcohol (methanol/ethanol) in the presence of an alkaline catalyst (NaOH/KOH) to form **fatty acid methyl esters (FAME = biodiesel)** and **glycerol** as a by-product. **Stoichiometric Reaction:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Triglyceride} + 3\,CH_{3}OH &\xrightarrow{\text{NaOH},\, 60^{\circ}C} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol} \\ 1\,\text{mole} \quad + \quad 3\,\text{moles} &\rightarrow 3\,\text{moles} \quad + \quad 1\,\text{mole} \end{aligned}$$ **Molar balance for 1000 kg triolein:** Moles of triolein: $\dfrac{1000}{884} = 1.131$ kmol Methanol required (stoichiometric, 1:3): $3 \times 1.131 = 3.393$ kmol = $3.393 \times 32 = 108.6$ kg With 100 % excess (typical in industry): $108.6 \times 2 = 217.2$ kg methanol Glycerol produced: $1.131 \times 92 = 104.0$ kg Biodiesel (FAME) produced: $3 \times 1.131 \times 296 = 1004.3$ kg **NaOH catalyst:** typically 1 % of oil mass = 10 kg. (7 marks: 2 for definition + reaction, 5 for numerical material balance) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks on Biomass Fuels problems — read carefully!** > > 1. **Dulong's formula units:** Many students forget the "**kJ/kg**" annotation. The formula $33820\,C$ assumes $C$ is a **mass fraction** (0.48, NOT 48). Failing to convert % to fraction costs 1 mark. > 2. **The "−O/8" term:** Examiners **always** check for this. Writing $33820\,C + 144000\,H$ instead of $33820\,C + 144000(H - O/8)$ is the **#1 valuation pitfall**. > 3. **NCV vs GCV:** Students often quote GCV when the question asks for NCV. Always subtract the **latent heat of water vapour** (2260 kJ/kg) for any practical boiler design. > 4. **Moisture "M":** The moisture in NCV formula is in **percent (12)**, not fraction (0.12). The factor 9H assumes H is in **percent too**. Mixing units is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. > 5. **Conversion Technology Definitions:** Pyrolysis = NO oxygen. Gasification = LIMITED oxygen. Combustion = EXCESS oxygen. Confusing these three in a 7-mark descriptive answer is a common 2-mark penalty. > 6. **Biogas Composition:** Don't write "biogas is mostly methane." Be precise: **55 – 70 % CH₄, 30 – 45 % CO₂, traces of H₂S, N₂, H₂O**. > 7. **Sustainability Argument:** "Biomass is carbon-neutral" — be ready to caveat that this is true **only on a life-cycle basis** if the regrowth rate matches the harvest rate. Examiners love this nuance. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Biomass = recently living organic matter** storing solar energy via photosynthesis. - [x] Carbon-neutral on **life-cycle basis** if regeneration matches consumption. - [x] Three physical states: **Solid** (wood, husk), **Liquid** (ethanol, biodiesel), **Gas** (biogas, syngas). - [x] Four generations: food crops → lignocellulose → algae → engineered organisms. - [x] **Proximate analysis** → M, VM, FC, A ; **Ultimate analysis** → C, H, O, N, S. - [x] Biomass has high **VM (70 – 85 %)** and low **ash (1 – 10 %)**. - [x] Conversion routes: **Thermochemical** (combustion, gasification, pyrolysis) and **Biochemical** (AD, fermentation, transesterification). - [x] Combustion: 800 – 1200 °C, excess O₂. Gasification: 700 – 900 °C, limited O₂. Pyrolysis: 300 – 700 °C, no O₂. - [x] Biogas = **60 % CH₄ + 40 % CO₂** ; CV ≈ 21 – 24 MJ/m³. - [x] Biodiesel = **FAME** from transesterification (1 : 3 triglyceride : methanol). - [x] **Dulong's Formula** (must memorize): $GCV = 33820\,C + 144000(H - O/8) + 9419\,S$ kJ/kg - [x] **NCV Formula** (must memorize): $NCV = GCV - 2260 \times (9H + M)/100$ - [x] **Air-Fuel Ratio** (must memorize): $(A/F) = 11.6\,C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35\,S$ kg air/kg fuel - [x] **Anaerobic Digestion** has 4 microbial stages: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. - [x] Optimal **C/N ratio** for AD = 20:1 – 30:1 ; pH 6.8 – 7.5. - [x] India target: **E20 ethanol blending** by 2025 (Ethanol Blending Programme — EBP). - [x] **Photosynthesis equation** = the parent equation of all biomass energy.

topics.title: Biomass Conversion Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT632 - RENEWABLE ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biomass Conversion Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomass Conversion Technologies** refer to the engineered set of physico-chemical, thermochemical, and biochemical processes that transform lignocellulosic, starchy, sugary, or oleaginous organic feedstocks (biomass) into usable forms of energy — including solid fuels (charcoal, pellets, briquettes), liquid fuels (bioethanol, biodiesel, pyrolysis oil), and gaseous fuels (syngas, biogas, producer gas) — together with useful by-products such as biofertilizer and activated carbon.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, biomass conversion is the **bridge between stored solar chemical energy** in plants and the **secondary energy carriers** that satisfy thermodynamic end-use demands (heating, mechanical shaft work, electricity generation, and transportation fuels).

### Classification Overview

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners expect students to classify biomass conversion along **two dominant axes**:
> 1. **Process thermodynamics axis** — Biochemical vs. Thermochemical.
> 2. **Product phase axis** — Solid → Liquid → Gaseous conversions.

| Axis | Category | Principal Routes |
|------|----------|------------------|
| Process Thermodynamics | Biochemical | Anaerobic Digestion, Alcoholic Fermentation, Composting |
| Process Thermodynamics | Thermochemical | Combustion, Gasification, Pyrolysis, Torrefaction, Hydrothermal Liquefaction |
| Product Phase | Solid | Densification (Briquetting, Pelletizing), Torrefaction, Charcoal |
| Product Phase | Liquid | Pyrolysis oil, Bioethanol, Biodiesel, HTL bio-crude |
| Product Phase | Gaseous | Producer gas, Syngas, Biogas, Bio-hydrogen |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen** where raw vegetables (biomass) sit in a fridge. You can:

- **Slow-cook** them for hours in a sealed pot with microbes (anaerobic digestion → biogas).
- **Stir-fry** them on high heat with limited air (pyrolysis → bio-oil).
- **Blast** them in a furnace with abundant air (combustion → heat/flue gas).
- **Boil** them with controlled sub-stoichiometric air (gasification → producer gas).
- **Press** them through a juicer to extract oils (mechanical extraction → biodiesel feedstock).
- **Squash** them into dry pellets for clean burning (densification → solid biofuel).

Each method yields a different "dish" (energy carrier) because the **temperature, oxygen level, residence time, and catalyst (microbe/enzyme)** determine the final product distribution. The art and science of biomass conversion is selecting the right combination of these "recipe" parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constant used extensively in this module:**
> - Higher Heating Value (HHV) of dry biomass ≈ **18–21 MJ/kg**
> - Lower Heating Value (LHV) of dry wood ≈ **17 MJ/kg**
> - Stoichiometric oxygen demand for cellulose $C_6H_{10}O_5$: ≈ **1.18 kg O₂ / kg cellulose**
> - Calorific value of biogas (≈ 60% CH₄): ≈ **22 MJ/m³**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Process Walkthrough

### 2.1.1 Biochemical Conversion Routes

#### (a) Anaerobic Digestion (AD)
A four-stage microbiological cascade operating in **absence of oxygen** that converts wet biomass (dung, sewage, food waste, energy crops) into biogas (≈ 50–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂) and digestate.

**Stage breakdown:**
1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) broken down by extracellular enzymes into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids).
2. **Acidogenesis** — Monomers converted by acidogenic bacteria into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, CO₂.
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs and alcohols oxidized to acetate, H₂, CO₂.
4. **Methanogenesis** — Acetate (acetoclastic) and H₂/CO₂ (hydrogenotrophic) converted into CH₄.

**Operating envelope:**
- Mesophilic: **35–40 °C**
- Thermophilic: **50–55 °C**
- Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT): **15–40 days**
- pH window: **6.8–7.4**
- C/N ratio sweet spot: **20–30 : 1**

#### (b) Alcoholic Fermentation
- Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) converts hexose sugars under anaerobic conditions.
- Overall stoichiometry for glucose:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

- Theoretical yield: **0.51 g ethanol / g glucose** (51% by mass).
- Practical yield: ~90–95% of theoretical.

#### (c) Composting
- Aerobic microbial decomposition of organic matter into humus-rich biofertilizer.
- C/N window: **25–35 : 1**; moisture: **50–60% w/w**.

### 2.1.2 Thermochemical Conversion Routes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners love to ask students to differentiate processes by the **Equivalence Ratio (ER)**, defined as the actual air-to-fuel ratio divided by stoichiometric air-to-fuel ratio.

| Process | Temperature (°C) | ER | Primary Product |
|---------|------------------|-----|-----------------|
| Combustion | 800–1200 | > 1.0 | Heat + flue gas + ash |
| Gasification | 700–900 | 0.25–0.45 | Producer gas / syngas |
| Pyrolysis | 400–700 | 0 | Bio-oil, char, gas |
| Torrefaction | 200–300 | 0 | Solid fuel (bio-coal) |

#### (a) Combustion
Complete oxidation of biomass in excess air; the dominant route for bagasse, rice husk, wood chips in boilers.

$$C_xH_yO_z + n\,O_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O + \text{heat}$$

Stoichiometric oxygen for biomass $CH_{1.4}O_{0.6}$ (typical formula):

$$O_{\text{stoich}} = 1 + \frac{1.4}{4} - \frac{0.6}{2} = 1.07\,\text{kmol O}_2 / \text{kmol biomass}$$

#### (b) Gasification
Partial oxidation; produces **producer gas** (CO, H₂, CO₂, CH₄, N₂). Endothermic–exothermic balance maintained.

**Simplified global reaction (for carbon):**

$$C + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO \quad \Delta H = -111\,\text{MJ/kmol}$$

**Boundary Boudouard reaction (endothermic):**

$$C + CO_2 \leftrightarrow 2\,CO \quad \Delta H = +172\,\text{MJ/kmol}$$

#### (c) Pyrolysis
Thermal decomposition in **complete absence of oxidizer**. Sub-classified as:

| Sub-type | Heating Rate | Temperature | Bio-oil Yield |
|----------|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| Slow (carbonization) | < 1 °C/s | 400–600 °C | ~30% |
| Fast | > 1000 °C/s | 500–800 °C | ~75% |
| Flash | > 10000 °C/s | 700–1000 °C | ~85% |
| Intermediate | 1–1000 °C/s | 500–700 °C | ~50% |

#### (d) Torrefaction (Mild Pyrolysis)
- "Roasting" biomass at **200–300 °C** in inert atmosphere.
- Drives off moisture, hemicellulose decomposition.
- Increases **energy density** by ~30%, makes biomass hydrophobic and brittle.

### 2.1.3 Physico-Chemical Conversion

#### (a) Biodiesel via Transesterification
Vegetable oil + methanol → biodiesel + glycerol, catalyzed by NaOH/KOH.

$$Triglyceride + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} 3\,R-COOCH_3 + Glycerol$$

- Molar ratio methanol:oil = **6:1** (stoichiometric 3:1, but excess drives equilibrium forward).
- Typical yield: **> 95%**.

#### (b) Mechanical Extraction & Densification
- Oilseeds crushed → crude vegetable oil → refining → transesterification.
- Loose biomass compressed at **100–200 MPa** into briquettes/pellets (diameter 6–25 mm).

### 2.1.4 Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL)
- Hot compressed water (**250–374 °C, 5–25 MPa**).
- Converts wet biomass directly into bio-crude — bypasses the costly drying step.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Engineering Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Process:**
> - **Biochemical** works because microbes carry enzymes that lower activation energy barriers for breaking down polymers at near-ambient temperatures. They are **slow but selective**.
> - **Thermochemical** works because heating supplies enough thermal energy to break C–C and C–H bonds directly. It is **fast but less selective**.
> - **Physico-chemical** works because of solubility differences (biodiesel phase separates from glycerol) and mechanical interlocking (lignin softens and binds under pressure).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the table below. KTU questions frequently test these equations through numerical substitution.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Equivalence Ratio | $ER = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | dimensionless | ER $< 1$: sub-stoichiometric |
| 2 | Cold Gas Efficiency (Gasifier) | $\eta_{cg} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{producer gas}}}{Q_{\text{biomass input}}}$ | % | Typical: 60–80% |
| 3 | Thermal Efficiency (Combustor) | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV + \text{losses}}$ | % | |
| 4 | Methane Yield (AD) | $Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \cdot \dfrac{(S_o - S) \cdot V}{1000}$ | m³ CH₄/day | $S_o$, $S$ in g/L COD |
| 5 | Theoretical Biogas Yield | $0.37\,m^3\,CH_4 / kg\,COD$ removed | m³/kg | Standard value |
| 6 | Stoichiometric Air | $A_{stoich} = \dfrac{11.6\,C + 34.8\,(H - O/8) + 4.35\,S}{23.2}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Ultimate analysis basis |
| 7 | Energy Density Ratio (Pellet vs Raw) | $\rho_E = \dfrac{LHV_{\text{pellet}}}{LHV_{\text{raw}}}$ | dimensionless | Torrefied: 1.2–1.4 |
| 8 | Biodiesel Mass Yield | $\eta_{bd} = \dfrac{m_{\text{biodiesel}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100$ | % | Practical > 90% |
| 9 | Pyrolysis Mass Balance | $m_{feed} = m_{char} + m_{oil} + m_{gas}$ | kg | Conservation of mass |
| 10 | Carbon Conversion Efficiency | $\eta_C = \dfrac{C_{\text{gas}}}{C_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$ | % | Gasifier design KPI |
| 11 | Biomass Calorific Value (Dulong) | $GCV = 0.338\,C + 1.428\,(H - O/8) + 0.095\,S$ | MJ/kg | C,H,O,S in % dry basis |
| 12 | Adiabatic Flame Temperature | $T_{ad} = T_{ref} + \dfrac{\sum (n_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i})}{\sum n_j \, C_{p,j}}$ | K | Used for combustor sizing |
| 13 | Higher vs Lower Heating Value | $LHV = HHV - 2.442 \cdot \dfrac{9\,H}{100}$ | MJ/kg | $H$ in % mass; water latent heat ≈ 2.442 MJ/kg |
| 14 | Gasification Stoichiometric O₂ (per kg C) | $O_2^{stoich} = 32/24 = 1.33$ | kg O₂/kg C | For pure carbon |
| 15 | Moisture Effective Calorific Loss | $LHV_{wet} = LHV_{dry} \cdot (1 - w) - 2.442 \cdot w$ | MJ/kg | $w$ = moisture fraction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units reminder:** Use **MJ/kg** for solid/liquid fuels and **MJ/Nm³** for gaseous fuels. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for unit mismatches in energy balance problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Anaerobic Digestion**: Powers rural household lighting in Kerala (Kudumbashree biogas plants); digestate used as organic fertilizer.
- **Combustion**: Bagasse-fired boilers in sugar mills (70% of Kerala's industrial biomass energy).
- **Gasification**: Decentralized 50–500 kWe downdraft gasifiers for rice husk-to-power.
- **Pyrolysis**: Slow-pyrolysis kilns for charcoal in Wayanad; fast-pyrolysis for bio-oil R&D.
- **Biodiesel**: Transesterification units for waste cooking oil in Kochi.
- **Densification**: Coir-pith briquettes in Alappuzha; sawdust pellets in Thrissur.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section presents **complete, line-by-line derivations** of the most examination-relevant equations. No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement from Ultimate Analysis

**Given:**
Ultimate analysis of a biomass fuel (mass % dry basis):
- Carbon = C %
- Hydrogen = H %
- Oxygen = O %
- Sulphur = S %
- Nitrogen = N %
- Ash = A %

**Goal:** Derive the kg of theoretical (stoichiometric) air required per kg of fuel.

### Step 1 — Write the combustion reactions of each combustible element

Carbon combustion:

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$

Mass balance: **12 kg C requires 32 kg O₂**, so per kg C: $32/12 = 2.667\,\text{kg O}_2$.

Hydrogen combustion:

$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$

Mass balance: **4 kg H requires 32 kg O₂**, so per kg H: $32/4 = 8.0\,\text{kg O}_2$.

Sulphur combustion:

$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$$

Per kg S: $32/32 = 1.0\,\text{kg O}_2$.

### Step 2 — Account for intrinsic oxygen in the fuel

Oxygen already present in the fuel partially satisfies the demand. Effective hydrogen for combustion becomes $H - O/8$ (since 8 kg O combines with 1 kg H to form water). Thus per kg O: subtract $1/8\,\text{kg H}$ equivalent.

### Step 3 — Assemble total O₂ demand per kg of dry fuel

$$O_2^{\text{stoich}} = 2.667 \cdot \frac{C}{100} + 8.0 \cdot \left(\frac{H}{100} - \frac{O}{8 \cdot 100}\right) + 1.0 \cdot \frac{S}{100}$$

Simplifying:

$$\boxed{O_2^{\text{stoich}} = \frac{1}{100}\left(2.667\,C + 8\,H - O + S\right)\,\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg fuel}}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to air requirement

Air is 23.2% O₂ by mass, hence:

$$\boxed{A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{O_2^{\text{stoich}}}{0.232} = \frac{1}{23.2}\left(2.667\,C + 8\,H - O + S\right)\,\text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$

Multiplying by 100 to keep percentages clean:

$$\boxed{A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{11.6\,C + 34.8\,(H - O/8) + 4.35\,S}{23.2}\,\text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$

This is the **canonical Dulong-based expression** used universally in KTU problems.

### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example

A bagasse sample has ultimate analysis: C = 44%, H = 6.0%, O = 44%, S = 0%, N = 0.2%, Ash = 5.8%.

$$A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{11.6 \times 44 + 34.8 \times (6.0 - 44/8) + 4.35 \times 0}{23.2}$$

Inner computation of the H term:

$$H_{\text{eff}} = 6.0 - \frac{44}{8} = 6.0 - 5.5 = 0.5$$

$$A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{510.4 + 34.8 \times 0.5 + 0}{23.2} = \frac{510.4 + 17.4}{23.2} = \frac{527.8}{23.2}$$

$$\boxed{A_{\text{stoich}} \approx 22.75\,\text{kg air / kg bagasse}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalence Ratio and Theoretical Air-Fuel Ratio Link

**Given:**
Actual air supplied per kg fuel = $A_{\text{actual}}$.

**Goal:** Show that ER, AFR, and stoichiometric AFR are interrelated.

### Step 1 — Definition of ER

By definition:

$$ER = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$$

### Step 2 — Relate to mass balance

For 1 kg fuel, $A/F$ is just the kg air per kg fuel:

$$ER = \frac{A_{\text{actual}}}{A_{\text{stoich}}}$$

### Step 3 — Gasifier example

A downdraft gasifier operates at ER = 0.30. If $A_{\text{stoich}} = 5.5\,\text{kg air / kg wood}$:

$$A_{\text{actual}} = ER \cdot A_{\text{stoich}} = 0.30 \times 5.5 = 1.65\,\text{kg air / kg wood}$$

This is the air feed rate used in **sizing the gasifier blower** — a common KTU numerical.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cold Gas Efficiency of a Biomass Gasifier

**Given:**
- Biomass feed rate $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/h), LHV = $LHV_f$ (MJ/kg)
- Producer gas flow rate $\dot{V}_g$ (Nm³/h), LHV = $LHV_g$ (MJ/Nm³)

**Goal:** Derive $\eta_{cg}$.

### Step 1 — Energy in biomass input per hour

$$Q_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \cdot LHV_f \quad [\text{MJ/h}]$$

### Step 2 — Energy in producer gas per hour

$$Q_{\text{out}} = \dot{V}_g \cdot LHV_g \quad [\text{MJ/h}]$$

### Step 3 — Efficiency by definition

$$\eta_{cg} = \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{\dot{V}_g \cdot LHV_g}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV_f}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{cg} = \frac{\dot{V}_g \cdot LHV_g}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV_f} \times 100\%}$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

A gasifier consumes 100 kg/h of wood chips (LHV = 17 MJ/kg) and produces 250 Nm³/h of producer gas (LHV = 5.0 MJ/Nm³).

$$\eta_{cg} = \frac{250 \times 5.0}{100 \times 17} = \frac{1250}{1700} = 0.735$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{cg} = 73.5\%}$$

This falls within the textbook range of 60–80% for downdraft gasifiers. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Theoretical Methane Yield from Anaerobic Digestion

**Given:**
- Influent substrate concentration $S_o$ (g COD/L)
- Effluent substrate concentration $S$ (g COD/L)
- Digester volume $V$ (m³)
- Hydraulic retention time $\theta$ (days)

**Goal:** Compute daily methane volume flow.

### Step 1 — COD removal rate per day

Substrate degraded per day:

$$\Delta S = (S_o - S) \cdot V / \theta \quad [\text{kg COD / day}]$$

### Step 2 — Convert COD to CH₄ stoichiometrically

Standard biochemical methane potential at STP:

$$Y_{CH_4}^{stoich} = 0.35\,\text{Nm}^3\,CH_4 / \text{kg COD}$$

### Step 3 — Methane volume produced per day

$$\dot{V}_{CH_4} = Y_{CH_4}^{stoich} \cdot \Delta S$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_{CH_4} = 0.35 \cdot \frac{(S_o - S) \cdot V}{\theta}\,\text{Nm}^3/\text{day}}$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

A digester receives $S_o = 50$ g COD/L, effluent $S = 5$ g COD/L, $V = 100$ m³, $\theta = 20$ days.

$$\Delta S = \frac{(50 - 5) \times 100}{20} = \frac{4500}{20} = 225\,\text{kg COD/day}$$

$$\dot{V}_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times 225 = 78.75\,\text{Nm}^3\,CH_4/\text{day}$$

Assuming CH₄ fraction in biogas = 60%, total biogas:

$$\dot{V}_{biogas} = \frac{78.75}{0.60} = 131.25\,\text{Nm}^3/\text{day}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Conversion Technology

> [!TIP]
> KTU Part B problems often ask "Which biomass conversion route is best suited for a given feedstock?" Use this decision matrix.

| Feedstock | Moisture | Best Route | Rationale |
|-----------|----------|------------|-----------|
| Dry wood (M < 15%) | Low | Combustion / Gasification | High thermal efficiency, no drying cost |
| Rice husk, bagasse | Low | Combustion in boiler | Abundant, low-grade process heat |
| Sawdust, agro-residue | Low | Densification → combustion | Reduces transport cost, improves handling |
| Wet cow dung, sewage | High (>80%) | Anaerobic digestion | Combustion impractical (drying cost prohibitive) |
| Sugarcane, molasses | High sugar | Fermentation → bioethanol | Direct microbial pathway |
| Vegetable oils (used cooking oil) | N/A | Transesterification → biodiesel | Triglyceride-rich |
| Algae, aquatic biomass | High | HTL or AD | Avoids energy-intensive drying |
| Forest residue | Variable | Torrefaction + combustion | Field transport + co-firing in coal plants |

---

## 3.6 Mass & Energy Balance — Worked Example for Pyrolysis

**Given:**
- Feed: 1000 kg/h dry wood
- Yields: bio-oil = 65%, char = 18%, non-condensable gas = 17%
- LHV of wood = 17 MJ/kg
- LHV of bio-oil = 17 MJ/kg, char = 28 MJ/kg, NCG = 6 MJ/Nm³

**Compute:** Overall energy recovery in each product stream.

### Step 1 — Mass flow of each product

$$\dot{m}_{oil} = 0.65 \times 1000 = 650\,\text{kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{char} = 0.18 \times 1000 = 180\,\text{kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{NCG} = 0.17 \times 1000 = 170\,\text{kg/h}$$

### Step 2 — Energy content per stream

$$Q_{oil} = 650 \times 17 = 11050\,\text{MJ/h}$$

$$Q_{char} = 180 \times 28 = 5040\,\text{MJ/h}$$

$$Q_{NCG} = 170 \times 6 = 1020\,\text{MJ/h}$$

### Step 3 — Total energy in

$$Q_{in} = 1000 \times 17 = 17000\,\text{MJ/h}$$

### Step 4 — Sum of product energy

$$Q_{out} = 11050 + 5040 + 1020 = 17110\,\text{MJ/h}$$

### Step 5 — Energy distribution (%)

$$\%\,\text{oil} = \frac{11050}{17000} = 65.0\%$$

$$\%\,\text{char} = \frac{5040}{17000} = 29.6\%$$

$$\%\,\text{NCG} = \frac{1020}{17000} = 6.0\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Bio-oil carries 65% of input energy, char 29.6%, NCG 6\%}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy slightly exceeds 100%** here because char has higher LHV per kg than the parent biomass — this is a feature of slow pyrolysis producing a carbon-rich char.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between combustion, gasification, and pyrolysis of biomass with respect to the equivalence ratio used.**

**Model Answer:**

| Process | Equivalence Ratio | Temperature | Product |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|---------|
| Combustion | ER > 1 (excess air) | 800–1200 °C | Heat + flue gas |
| Gasification | ER = 0.25–0.45 | 700–900 °C | Producer gas |
| Pyrolysis | ER = 0 (no air) | 400–700 °C | Bio-oil + char + gas |

Combustion uses excess oxygen for complete oxidation; gasification uses sub-stoichiometric air producing CO and H₂; pyrolysis uses zero oxidizer, relying solely on thermal decomposition.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the four stages of anaerobic digestion. Name the dominant microbial group in each stage.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hydrolysis** — Hydrolytic bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium*) — convert polymers to monomers.
2. **Acidogenesis** — Acidogenic bacteria (*Bacteroides*, *Clostridium*) — monomers to VFAs.
3. **Acetogenesis** — Acetogenic bacteria (*Syntrophomonas*) — VFAs to acetate + H₂ + CO₂.
4. **Methanogenesis** — Methanogenic archaea (*Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus*) — acetate and H₂/CO₂ to CH₄.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a downdraft biomass gasifier. List the four reaction zones and the dominant chemical reaction in each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A biomass gasifier is fed with 200 kg/h of rice husk with LHV = 14 MJ/kg. The producer gas flow rate is 420 Nm³/h with LHV = 5.2 MJ/Nm³. Calculate (i) Cold gas efficiency, (ii) Hot gas efficiency if the gas exits at 250 °C with $C_p = 1.3\,\text{kJ/(Nm}^3\cdot\text{K)}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Working of downdraft gasifier:

The downdraft gasifier has **four reaction zones** arranged vertically with biomass and air flowing in **co-current downward** direction. The block diagram (refer to SECTION 4.2) shows:

1. **Drying Zone (100–200 °C)** — Moisture evaporated from biomass. Endothermic; dominant reaction: $H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)}$.

2. **Pyrolysis Zone (200–600 °C)** — Volatiles released; biomass converted to char + condensable vapors + gases. Reaction: $Biomass \rightarrow Char + Volatiles + Tar$.

3. **Oxidation/Combustion Zone (1000–1400 °C)** — Char reacts with sub-stoichiometric air exothermically. Dominant: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ and partial $C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO$.

4. **Reduction Zone (800–1000 °C)** — Endothermic gas–solid reactions convert CO₂ and H₂O to CO and H₂:
   - Boudouard: $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO$
   - Water–gas: $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$
   - Methanation: $C + 2\,H_2 \rightarrow CH_4$

Producer gas exits at the bottom carrying sensible heat and particulates, which are removed by cyclone separator before being fed to the engine.

**(b)** Numerical solution:

**Given:**
$\dot{m}_f = 200\,\text{kg/h}$, $LHV_f = 14\,\text{MJ/kg}$, $\dot{V}_g = 420\,\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}$, $LHV_g = 5.2\,\text{MJ/Nm}^3$.

**(i) Cold Gas Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{cg} = \frac{\dot{V}_g \cdot LHV_g}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV_f} = \frac{420 \times 5.2}{200 \times 14}$$

Numerator computation:

$$420 \times 5.2 = 2184\,\text{MJ/h}$$

Denominator computation:

$$200 \times 14 = 2800\,\text{MJ/h}$$

$$\eta_{cg} = \frac{2184}{2800} = 0.78 = \boxed{78\%}$$

**(ii) Hot Gas Efficiency (sensible heat recovery credit):**

Sensible heat in hot producer gas:

$$Q_{sensible} = \dot{V}_g \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{gas} - T_{ref})$$

Using $T_{ref} = 25\,°C = 298\,\text{K}$, $T_{gas} = 250\,°C = 523\,\text{K}$:

$$\Delta T = 523 - 298 = 225\,\text{K}$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 420 \times 1.3 \times 225 = 122850\,\text{kJ/h} = 122.85\,\text{MJ/h}$$

$$\eta_{hg} = \frac{2184 + 122.85}{2800} = \frac{2306.85}{2800} = 0.824 = \boxed{82.4\%}$$

---

### Question B. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain transesterification for biodiesel production with a balanced chemical equation. List three process parameters that affect the yield. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The ultimate analysis of a biomass fuel sample is: C = 48%, H = 6.5%, O = 41%, S = 0.5%, N = 0.5%, Ash = 3.5%. Calculate (i) stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel, (ii) actual air supplied if ER = 1.4, (iii) flue gas composition if combustion is complete. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Transesterification:

Triglycerides in vegetable oil react with a short-chain alcohol (usually methanol) in the presence of a base catalyst (NaOH or KOH) to produce **fatty acid methyl esters (FAME)**, i.e., biodiesel, and glycerol as a byproduct.

Balanced equation:

$$\underbrace{C_3H_5(OCOR)_3}_{\text{Triglyceride}} + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} \underbrace{3\,CH_3OCOR}_{\text{Biodiesel (FAME)}} + \underbrace{C_3H_5(OH)_3}_{\text{Glycerol}}$$

Three critical process parameters:

1. **Molar ratio of alcohol to oil** — Stoichiometric ratio is 3:1, but practical operation uses **6:1** to drive equilibrium toward biodiesel (Le Chatelier's principle).
2. **Reaction temperature** — Optimal at **60–65 °C** (above methanol boiling point risk); atmospheric pressure.
3. **Catalyst concentration** — Typically **1% w/w NaOH** of oil; too much leads to saponification (soap formation), too little yields incomplete conversion.

**(b)** Numerical solution:

**Given:** C = 48, H = 6.5, O = 41, S = 0.5, N = 0.5, Ash = 3.5 (all in % mass, dry basis).

**(i) Stoichiometric air requirement:**

$$A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{11.6\,C + 34.8\,(H - O/8) + 4.35\,S}{23.2}$$

Compute each term:

$$11.6 \times 48 = 556.8$$

$$H_{\text{eff}} = 6.5 - \frac{41}{8} = 6.5 - 5.125 = 1.375$$

$$34.8 \times 1.375 = 47.85$$

$$4.35 \times 0.5 = 2.175$$

Sum:

$$556.8 + 47.85 + 2.175 = 606.825$$

$$A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{606.825}{23.2} = \boxed{26.16\,\text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$

**(ii) Actual air at ER = 1.4:**

$$A_{\text{actual}} = ER \times A_{\text{stoich}} = 1.4 \times 26.16 = \boxed{36.62\,\text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$

**(iii) Flue gas composition (per kg fuel):**

O₂ supplied (actual) = $0.232 \times 36.62 = 8.50\,\text{kg O}_2$.
N₂ supplied = $0.768 \times 36.62 = 28.13\,\text{kg N}_2$.

O₂ required stoichiometrically = $0.232 \times 26.16 = 6.07\,\text{kg O}_2$.
Excess O₂ in flue gas = $8.50 - 6.07 = 2.43\,\text{kg O}_2$.

CO₂ from C combustion:

$$m_{CO_2} = \frac{44}{12} \times 0.48 = 3.52 \times 0.48 = 1.76\,\text{kg CO}_2$$

SO₂ from S combustion:

$$m_{SO_2} = \frac{64}{32} \times 0.005 = 0.01\,\text{kg SO}_2$$

H₂O from H combustion:

$$m_{H_2O} = \frac{18}{2} \times 0.065 = 9 \times 0.065 = 0.585\,\text{kg H}_2O$$

**Flue gas composition (mass):**

| Component | Mass (kg) | Mass % |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| CO₂ | 1.76 | 5.38 |
| SO₂ | 0.01 | 0.03 |
| H₂O | 0.585 | 1.79 |
| O₂ (excess) | 2.43 | 7.43 |
| N₂ | 28.13 | 86.00 |
| **Total** | **32.92** | **100.00** |

The flue gas is dominated by **N₂ (~86%)**, characteristic of air-fired combustion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Marks Are Lost:**
>
> 1. **Equivalence Ratio Confusion**: Students interchange ER with air–fuel ratio. ER is **dimensionless**; AFR has units. Mixing them costs 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **HHV vs LHV Mix-up**: Using LHV where HHV is given (or vice versa) in energy balance problems. Remember: water condensation releases latent heat — HHV > LHV.
> 3. **Missing "per kg fuel" or "per Nm³" units**: Always state units explicitly. KTU examiners actively deduct 0.5–1 mark for each ambiguous unit.
> 4. **Anaerobic Digestion Stages Skipped**: Writing only "biogas is produced by microbes" without naming **hydrolysis → acidogenesis → acetogenesis → methanogenesis** loses up to 3 marks.
> 5. **Stoichiometric Oxygen Errors**: Forgetting the O/8 correction for intrinsic oxygen inflates stoichiometric air by up to 20%.
> 6. **Biodiesel Stoichiometry**: Writing "methanol" instead of balancing to **3 moles** per mole of triglyceride. The 3:1 ratio is the heart of the question.
> 7. **Equilibrium in Transesterification**: Failing to mention that **excess methanol (6:1)** is used to drive equilibrium forward is a frequent omission.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Biomass Conversion Technologies**

- ⭐ **Biomass** = any organic matter of recent biological origin; excludes fossil fuels.
- ⭐ **Three pillars of classification**: biochemical, thermochemical, physico-chemical.
- ⭐ **Anaerobic Digestion stages**: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- ⭐ **AD optimum**: mesophilic 35–40 °C, pH 6.8–7.4, C/N 20–30, HRT 15–40 days.
- ⭐ **Fermentation stoichiometry**: 1 mol glucose → 2 mol ethanol + 2 mol CO₂ (theoretical yield = 51% mass).
- ⭐ **Combustion ER > 1**, **Gasification ER = 0.25–0.45**, **Pyrolysis ER = 0**.
- ⭐ **Downdraft gasifier zones**: Drying → Pyrolysis → Oxidation → Reduction (in vertical co-current flow).
- ⭐ **Boudouard reaction**: $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2\,CO$ (endothermic, drives producer-gas quality in reduction zone).
- ⭐ **Pyrolysis sub-types** distinguished by heating rate: slow (<1 °C/s) → char; fast (>1000 °C/s) → bio-oil; flash (>10000 °C/s) → maximum oil yield.
- ⭐ **Torrefaction**: mild pyrolysis at 200–300 °C; produces hydrophobic bio-coal with ~30% higher energy density.
- ⭐ **Transesterification**: triglyceride + 3 methanol → 3 FAME + glycerol; **molar ratio 6:1** (excess), catalyst 1% NaOH, 60 °C.
- ⭐ **Densification pressure**: 100–200 MPa for pellets/briquettes.
- ⭐ **HTL**: 250–374 °C, 5–25 MPa — converts wet biomass directly to bio-crude.
- ⭐ **Dulong's GCV formula**: $GCV = 0.338\,C + 1.428\,(H - O/8) + 0.095\,S$ (MJ/kg).
- ⭐ **HHV–LHV gap**: $LHV = HHV - 2.442 \cdot \frac{9\,H}{100}$.
- ⭐ **Stoichiometric air**: $A_{stoich} = \frac{11.6\,C + 34.8\,(H - O/8) + 4.35\,S}{23.2}$ kg air/kg fuel.
- ⭐ **Methane yield from AD**: $0.35\,\text{Nm}^3\,CH_4 / \text{kg COD}$ removed.
- ⭐ **Cold gas efficiency**: typical 60–80%; **hot gas efficiency** includes sensible heat credit.
- ⭐ **Equivalence ratio** = $A_{\text{actual}}/A_{\text{stoich}}$ — **dimensionless**.
- ⭐ **Pyrolysis mass balance**: $m_{feed} = m_{char} + m_{oil} + m_{gas}$ (always conserved).
- ⭐ **Biodiesel yield** > 95% in well-controlled transesterification; glycerol is the marketable byproduct.
- ⭐ **KTU must-know diagrams**: Downdraft gasifier with 4 zones; AD 4-stage microbiology; pyrolysis classification tree.
- ⭐ **Kerala context**: Bagasse combustion (sugar mills), coconut-shell charcoal (Wayanad), coir-pith briquettes (Alappuzha), Kudumbashree biogas (statewide).

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Urban Waste to Energy Conversion (WtE)** refers to the systematic process of recovering usable forms of energy—primarily thermal energy (heat), electrical energy (power), and chemical energy (solid, liquid, or gaseous fuels)—from the heterogeneous organic and combustible fraction of solid waste generated by urban municipalities. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, urban waste is classified as **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** and is treated as a renewable bioenergy resource when its biogenic fraction is harnessed through appropriate thermo-chemical or bio-chemical conversion pathways.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMRT632, Module 3):** *Urban waste to energy conversion* encompasses the technologies, characterization methods, and energy-recovery systems applied to municipal solid waste streams. It includes the determination of calorific value, proximate/ultimate analysis, and the operating principles of incineration, gasification, pyrolysis, anaerobic digestion, and landfill gas recovery systems.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The City's Metabolism"

Think of a city as a **living organism**. Just as humans consume food and produce waste that can be composted, a city consumes resources and produces garbage. **Urban Waste to Energy** is essentially the city's "digestive system"—it takes the discarded food, paper, plastics, and yard trimmings, and instead of letting them rot in landfills (releasing uncontrolled methane, a greenhouse gas 25× more potent than CO₂), it **converts the chemical energy locked inside this waste into usable electricity and heat**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A single tonne of MSW with a calorific value of ~10 MJ/kg contains the same energy as roughly **70 litres of gasoline**. WtE plants simply "refine" urban garbage into a usable energy currency, just like an oil refinery converts crude oil into petrol, diesel, and kerosene.

### 1.3 Classification of Urban Waste

Urban waste streams are categorized based on **physical state**, **chemical composition**, and **biodegradability**:

| **Category** | **Examples** | **Preferred WtE Route** |
|---|---|---|
| **Biodegradable / Putrescible** | Food waste, yard trimmings, paper, cardboard | Anaerobic Digestion (AD), Composting |
| **Non-biodegradable Combustible** | Plastics, rubber, textiles, leather | Incineration, Gasification, Pyrolysis, RDF |
| **Non-biodegradable Non-combustible** | Glass, metals, ceramics, construction debris | Mechanical recycling (not WtE) |
| **Hazardous (Domestic)** | Batteries, e-waste, paints, chemicals | Specialized treatment (not conventional WtE) |

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **bold** constants and metrics are essential for KTU-level problem solving:

- **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of dry MSW:** ranges from **8 MJ/kg to 20 MJ/kg** depending on composition.
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV):** typically **6 MJ/kg to 15 MJ/kg** (accounts for water-vapour latent heat loss).
- **Moisture content of fresh MSW:** **20%–60%** by weight (a critical design parameter).
- **Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C/N) ratio for AD feed:** optimum **20:1 to 30:1**.
- **Methane (CH₄) yield via AD:** **0.2–0.5 m³ CH₄ per kg VS (Volatile Solids) destroyed**.
- **Landfill gas composition:** ~**50% CH₄, 50% CO₂** (with trace H₂S, NH₃, VOCs).
- **1 tonne of MSW ≈ 0.45 MWh of electricity** (with ~25% conversion efficiency).

> [!NOTE]
> The **stochiometric air requirement** for complete combustion of MSW is approximately **4.5–5.5 kg air per kg of waste**, which is higher than for pure hydrocarbons due to the oxygen content of the waste matrix itself.
