## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Waste Characterization — The Two Pillars

Energy potential of MSW cannot be estimated without rigorous characterization. KTU 2024 Scheme mandates two standardized analyses:

#### 2.1.1 Proximate Analysis
Determines the distribution of mass fractions when the waste is heated under controlled conditions. It yields four components:

- **Moisture (M):** Water content (lost at ~105°C).
- **Volatile Matter (VM):** Combustible gases released between 105°C and 950°C in inert atmosphere.
- **Fixed Carbon (FC):** Residual combustible solid (mainly carbon) after volatile release.
- **Ash (A):** Incombustible mineral residue remaining after complete combustion.

The identity is:

$$M + VM + FC + A = 100\%$$

#### 2.1.2 Ultimate Analysis
Determines the elemental mass fractions:

- **Carbon (C), Hydrogen (H), Oxygen (O), Nitrogen (N), Sulfur (S), Chlorine (Cl), Ash (A)**

$$C + H + O + N + S + Cl + A = 100\%$$

Ultimate analysis is the **gateway** to computing calorific value using the modified **Dulong's formula** for MSW.

### 2.2 Calorific Value Estimation (Modified Dulong's Equation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For solid waste, the moisture and ash fractions significantly dilute the energy content. The **modified Dulong's equation** accounts for this dilution:

$$\text{HHV} = 0.3389\,C + 1.4428\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.0933\,S \;-\; 0.0239\,M \;-\; 0.0242\,A$$

where all elemental fractions are in **mass %** and HHV is in **MJ/kg**.

**Why each term matters:**
- **0.3389·C:** Energy released per unit mass of carbon.
- **1.4428·(H − O/8):** Net hydrogen available for combustion (oxygen already bonded is subtracted because it cannot combust further).
- **0.0933·S:** Sulfur oxidation energy.
- **−0.0239·M:** Penalty for moisture evaporation.
- **−0.0242·A:** Penalty for inert ash dilution.

### 2.3 Conversion Technologies — Operational Principles

#### A. Incineration (Mass-Burn / Modular)
- **Principle:** Complete combustion of as-received MSW in excess air (λ ≈ 1.5–2.0) at **850°C–1100°C** for a residence time of **≥ 2 seconds**.
- **Products:** Hot flue gases → boiler → steam → turbine → electricity; bottom ash (5–10% by mass) and fly ash.
- **Efficiency:** Electrical ~20–30%; combined heat & power (CHP) ~70–85%.
- **KTU-relevant note:** Two architectures exist—**Mass-Burn** (entire waste incinerated, no preprocessing) and **Modular/RDF-fired** (waste preprocessed into refuse-derived fuel).

#### B. Gasification
- **Principle:** Partial oxidation of waste at sub-stochiometric air ratios (ER ≈ 0.2–0.4) at temperatures of **600°C–1200°C** to produce **synthesis gas (syngas)**—a mixture of CO, H₂, CH₄, CO₂, and N₂.
- **Reactions:** Heterogeneous (C + O₂ → CO₂) and homogeneous (CO₂ + C → 2CO; H₂O + C → CO + H₂).
- **Energy efficiency:** Higher than incineration for syngas applications.

#### C. Pyrolysis
- **Principle:** Thermal decomposition in **complete absence of oxygen** at **300°C–900°C**, yielding three products: **char (solid), pyrolysis oil (liquid), and non-condensable gases**.
- **Temperature regimes:**
  - **Slow pyrolysis:** ~400°C, favours char.
  - **Fast pyrolysis:** ~500°C, high heating rate, favours bio-oil.
  - **Gasification-grade:** >700°C, favours syngas.

#### D. Anaerobic Digestion (AD)
- **Principle:** Microbial breakdown of organic matter in **oxygen-free environments** through four stages: **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis**.
- **Output:** Biogas (60–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂) + digestate (nutrient-rich slurry).
- **Operating conditions:** Mesophilic (35–40°C) or thermophilic (50–55°C); pH 6.8–7.5; HRT 15–40 days.

#### E. Landfill Gas (LFG) Recovery
- **Principle:** Landfills act as large, slow anaerobic digesters. Captured gas is flared or used in gas engines.
- **Generation timeline:** Peak methane production occurs 5–7 years post-closure; recovery can last 20–30 years.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all high-frequency formulas required for KTU board exam problems on this topic.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Application** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{HHV} = 0.3389C + 1.4428(H - O/8) + 0.0933S - 0.0239M - 0.0242A$ | Modified Dulong's equation for MSW | MJ/kg |
| 2 | $M + VM + FC + A = 100\%$ | Proximate analysis identity | mass % |
| 3 | $C + H + O + N + S + Cl + A = 100\%$ | Ultimate analysis identity | mass % |
| 4 | $\text{Energy from waste} = m_{\text{waste}} \times \text{HHV}$ | Total chemical energy in batch | MJ |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{electrical}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{gen}}}{m_{\text{waste}} \times \text{HHV}}$ | Plant electrical efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Stoichiometric O}_2 = \dfrac{32C}{12} + \dfrac{32H}{4} + \dfrac{32S}{32} - O$ | Theoretical O₂ demand (per 100 kg waste) | kg O₂ |
| 7 | $\text{Actual air} = \lambda \times \text{Stoichiometric air}$ | Combustion air with excess air ratio λ | kg air |
| 8 | $\text{CH}_4 \text{ yield} = Y \times m_{VS} \times f_d$ | Biogas production from AD | m³ CH₄ |
| 9 | $\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Hydraulic retention time in digester | days |
| 10 | $E_{\text{CO}_2,\text{avoided}} = m_{\text{waste}} \times EF_{\text{MSW}} - E_{\text{fossil,eq}}$ | Greenhouse gas offset | tonnes CO₂eq |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU exams, students often confuse **HHV** and **LHV**. Always check whether the problem statement specifies whether water vapour in flue gas condenses (HHV) or escapes (LHV). For boiler design and turbine calculations, **LHV is the correct choice**; for thermodynamic balances, **HHV is preferred**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

WtE systems are not merely academic curiosities—they are deployed at industrial scale worldwide:
- **Europe:** Over 500 WtE plants (Germany alone processes ~25 million tonnes/year).
- **India:** Municipalities in Delhi, Bengaluru, and Hyderabad are mandating WtE to address urban landfill crises.
- **Global capacity:** Estimated 130+ GWh of electricity generated annually from MSW.

These plants displace fossil fuel-based power generation, reduce landfill volumes by ~90%, and recover ferrous/non-ferrous metals from bottom ash.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand for MSW Combustion

We derive the theoretical oxygen required for complete combustion of 1 kg of waste with the following ultimate analysis (mass fractions): **C = 0.30, H = 0.04, O = 0.22, N = 0.005, S = 0.001, Ash = 0.435** (moisture absorbed in ash balance).

**Combustion reactions (per unit mass of each element):**

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2$$

**Step 1 — Per-100 kg basis (to avoid decimals).**

Take a 100 kg waste sample:
- Carbon = 30 kg, Hydrogen = 4 kg, Sulfur = 1 kg, Oxygen (intrinsic) = 22 kg.

**Step 2 — Oxygen required for each element.**

- For C: $\dfrac{32}{12} \times 30 = 80.00$ kg O₂
- For H: $\dfrac{16}{2} \times 4 = 32.00$ kg O₂
- For S: $\dfrac{32}{32} \times 1 = 1.00$ kg O₂

**Step 3 — Sum total oxygen required (gross):**

$$O_{2,\text{gross}} = 80.00 + 32.00 + 1.00 = 113.00 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract intrinsic oxygen already present in the waste (it is partly bonded to H and C, and we must avoid double-counting):**

$$O_{2,\text{net}} = 113.00 - \dfrac{22}{2} = 113.00 - 11.00 = 102.00 \text{ kg}$$

> **Logic:** The intrinsic 22 kg of oxygen in the waste is already partially combined with hydrogen (as -OH, -COOH groups in organics) and therefore reduces the external O₂ demand. Empirically, only ~half of the intrinsic O₂ contributes to "self-oxidation," so we deduct O/2.

**Step 5 — Convert to air using the mass ratio of air to oxygen (≈ 100/23 by mass):**

$$m_{\text{air,stoich}} = 102.00 \times \dfrac{100}{23} = 443.48 \text{ kg air per 100 kg waste}$$

**Step 6 — Express per kg of waste:**

$$\boxed{m_{\text{air,stoich}} \approx 4.43 \text{ kg air per kg waste}}$$

**Step 7 — For 30% excess air (λ = 1.3):**

$$m_{\text{air,actual}} = 1.3 \times 4.43 = 5.76 \text{ kg air per kg waste}$$

This matches the empirical range (4.5–5.5 kg air/kg waste) cited in Section 1.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Energy Output from a 500 TPD WtE Plant

A municipal corporation plans a WtE plant processing **500 tonnes/day** of MSW with the following parameters:
- **HHV of waste:** 10 MJ/kg
- **Boiler thermal efficiency:** 80%
- **Steam cycle (Rankine) efficiency:** 30%
- **Generator efficiency:** 95%
- **Plant availability:** 85% (operating days/year)

**Step 1 — Daily waste energy input (chemical):**

$$E_{\text{in}} = 500{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \times 10 \text{ MJ/kg} = 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 2 — Energy absorbed by boiler water/steam:**

$$E_{\text{steam}} = 0.80 \times 5{,}000{,}000 = 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical work at turbine shaft:**

$$W_{\text{shaft}} = 0.30 \times 4{,}000{,}000 = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 4 — Electrical output at generator terminals:**

$$E_{\text{elec}} = 0.95 \times 1{,}200{,}000 = 1{,}140{,}000 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 5 — Convert MJ/day to MWh/day (1 MWh = 3600 MJ):**

$$E_{\text{elec}} = \dfrac{1{,}140{,}000}{3600} = 316.67 \text{ MWh/day}$$

**Step 6 — Apply plant availability to get annual generation:**

$$E_{\text{annual}} = 316.67 \times 365 \times 0.85 = 98{,}214 \text{ MWh/year} \approx 98.2 \text{ GWh/year}$$

**Step 7 — Overall plant electrical efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.80 \times 0.30 \times 0.95 = 0.228 = 22.8\%$$

This 22.8% figure is consistent with real-world WtE plant efficiencies (20–30%).

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Biogas Yield from Anaerobic Digestion

A wet anaerobic digester processes **20 tonnes/day** of food waste with the following specifications:
- **Volatile Solids (VS) content:** 80% of total solids
- **Total Solids (TS) in feed:** 20% (slurry)
- **VS destruction efficiency:** 65%
- **Methane yield coefficient (Y):** 0.35 m³ CH₄/kg VS destroyed

**Step 1 — Total Solids in daily feed:**

$$m_{TS} = 20{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 0.20 = 4000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Volatile Solids (the digestible fraction):**

$$m_{VS} = 4000 \times 0.80 = 3200 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — VS actually destroyed (digested):**

$$m_{VS,\text{des}} = 3200 \times 0.65 = 2080 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Methane produced:**

$$V_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.35 \times 2080 = 728 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4\text{/day}$$

**Step 5 — Biogas volume (assuming 65% CH₄ in biogas):**

$$V_{\text{biogas}} = \dfrac{728}{0.65} = 1120 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}$$

**Step 6 — Energy content (HHV of CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/kg; density = 0.72 kg/m³):**

$$E_{\text{CH}_4} = 728 \times 0.72 \times 55.5 = 29{,}094 \text{ MJ/day} \approx 8.08 \text{ MWh/day}$$

**Step 7 — Electrical output (gas engine η = 35%):**

$$E_{\text{elec}} = 0.35 \times 8.08 = 2.83 \text{ MWh/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The digestion of **20 tonnes/day of food waste produces ~2.83 MWh of electricity** plus heat (CHP) and ~13 tonnes of nutrient-rich digestate usable as fertilizer.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — WtE Technologies

| **Parameter** | **Incineration** | **Gasification** | **Pyrolysis** | **Anaerobic Digestion** | **Landfill Gas** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Oxygen** | Excess (λ > 1) | Sub-stoich (ER 0.2–0.4) | None (inert) | None (anaerobic) | None (passive anaerobic) |
| **Temperature** | 850–1100°C | 600–1200°C | 300–900°C | 35–55°C | Ambient (mesophilic in situ) |
| **Main product** | Heat → electricity | Syngas (CO+H₂) | Bio-oil, char, gas | Biogas (CH₄+CO₂) | LFG (~50% CH₄) |
| **Energy efficiency** | 20–30% elec. | 60–80% (syngas) | 50–70% (bio-oil) | 30–40% (CHP) | 25–35% (engines) |
| **Feedstock** | Raw MSW | Dried/RDF | Dried MSW | Wet organics only | Landfilled waste (delayed) |
| **Emission control** | High (dioxins, HCl, Hg) | Moderate | Moderate-High | Low (biogas scrubbing) | Moderate |
| **Capital cost** | Very High | High | High | Moderate | Low |
| **KTU application** | Large cities | Industrial parks | Pilot scale | Wet-waste zones | Legacy landfills |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Municipal Solid Waste (MSW). List any four major conversion technologies used for energy recovery from MSW.**

**Model Answer:**

Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) refers to the heterogeneous mixture of solid discarded materials generated from residential, commercial, institutional, and small industrial sources within urban municipal limits, excluding hazardous and biomedical waste.

Four major WtE conversion technologies:
1. **Incineration** – Combustion in excess air to produce steam and electricity.
2. **Gasification** – Partial oxidation to produce syngas.
3. **Pyrolysis** – Anaerobic thermal decomposition to produce bio-oil, char, and gas.
4. **Anaerobic Digestion** – Microbial conversion of organic fraction to biogas.

*(Other acceptable: Landfill gas recovery, Refuse Derived Fuel production.)*

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the difference between Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of MSW. Why is LHV used in boiler design calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

- **HHV (Higher Heating Value):** Total heat released when 1 kg of waste is completely combusted and the water vapour in flue gas is condensed, recovering its latent heat.
- **LHV (Lower Heating Value):** Heat released when water vapour leaves as vapour (latent heat NOT recovered).

$$\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - h_{fg} \times \left(\dfrac{9 \times H}{100} + \dfrac{M}{100}\right)$$

where $h_{fg}$ = 2.44 MJ/kg and H, M are hydrogen and moisture percentages.

**Why LHV for boiler design:**
- In a boiler, flue gas temperatures (typically 150–200°C) are well above the dew point (60°C); therefore, water vapour does **not condense**, and its latent heat is **lost to the stack**.
- Hence, the usable energy at the boiler boundary is correctly represented by **LHV**, not HHV.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the proximate and ultimate analysis of MSW. Explain the modified Dulong's formula for estimating the calorific value of MSW. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Mass-Burn Incineration-based WtE plant. Discuss its key environmental control measures. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Proximate and Ultimate Analysis:**

**Proximate Analysis** gives the composition of MSW in four fractions when heated in a controlled manner:
- **Moisture (M)** – determined by drying at 105°C to constant weight.
- **Volatile Matter (VM)** – loss in mass when heated to 950°C in inert atmosphere.
- **Fixed Carbon (FC)** – residual combustible mass after VM removal.
- **Ash (A)** – non-combustible residue after complete combustion.

The closure: $M + VM + FC + A = 100\%$

**Ultimate Analysis** gives the elemental composition: Carbon (C), Hydrogen (H), Oxygen (O), Nitrogen (N), Sulfur (S), Chlorine (Cl), and Ash (A), summing to 100%.

**Modified Dulong's Formula:**

$$\text{HHV} = 0.3389\,C + 1.4428\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.0933\,S - 0.0239\,M - 0.0242\,A \;\; (\text{MJ/kg})$$

**Rationale of each term:**
- **0.3389·C** → Energy from carbon oxidation (12 g C + 32 g O₂ → 44 g CO₂ releases ~32.8 MJ per kg C → 32.8/12 = 0.34).
- **1.4428·(H − O/8)** → Net hydrogen available for combustion; some H is already bonded to O in the waste matrix, so the effective combustible hydrogen is (H − O/8) by mass.
- **0.0933·S** → Energy from sulfur oxidation (9.2 MJ/kg S / 32 g S per mole O₂ ≈ 0.29; mass-weighted gives ~0.0933).
- **−0.0239·M and −0.0242·A** → Penalties due to latent heat loss for moisture and energy dilution by inert ash.

**Worked Numerical Example:**
Consider MSW with C=30%, H=4%, O=22%, N=0.5%, S=0.1%, Cl=0.4%, M=20%, A=23%.

$$\text{HHV} = 0.3389(30) + 1.4428\left(4 - \dfrac{22}{8}\right) + 0.0933(0.1) - 0.0239(20) - 0.0242(23)$$

$$= 10.167 + 1.4428(4 - 2.75) + 0.0093 - 0.478 - 0.557$$

$$= 10.167 + 1.8035 + 0.0093 - 0.478 - 0.557 = 10.945 \;\text{MJ/kg}$$

So the HHV is approximately **10.95 MJ/kg** — a typical value for mixed urban MSW.

**(b) Mass-Burn Incineration Plant:**

**Block Diagram (textual):**
1. **Waste Reception** – Tipping floor and weighbridge.
2. **Bunker Storage** – 3–5 days of storage capacity.
3. **Grab Crane** – Feeds waste to furnace hopper.
4. **Grate Furnace** – Water-cooled or roller grates; combustion at 850–1100°C with residence time ≥ 2 s for dioxin destruction.
5. **Waste Heat Boiler** – Recovers heat to generate steam at 40–60 bar, 400–450°C.
6. **Steam Turbine-Generator** – Converts thermal to electrical energy (η = 20–30%).
7. **Air Pollution Control (APC):**
   - **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) / Bag Filter** – Particulate removal (>99.9%).
   - **Wet Scrubber** – HCl, SO₂, and HF removal.
   - **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** – NOx reduction.
   - **Activated Carbon Injection** – Mercury and dioxin capture.
8. **Stack** – Emits cleaned flue gas (within CPCB/SPCB norms).
9. **Bottom Ash System** – Quenching, ferrous/non-ferrous recovery, ash disposal.

**Key Environmental Controls:**
- Maintaining ≥ 850°C for ≥ 2 s (dioxin destruction).
- Good combustion air mixing to prevent CO spikes.
- Continuous Emission Monitoring System (CEMS).
- Ash leachate testing before disposal.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of Anaerobic Digestion (AD) for urban waste. List the four microbial stages and discuss the effect of operating parameters on biogas yield. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A city generates 800 tonnes/day of MSW with HHV = 9.5 MJ/kg. The city proposes a WtE plant with overall electrical efficiency of 25%. Calculate the daily and annual electrical energy output assuming 90% plant availability. Comment on the environmental significance of the plant. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Anaerobic Digestion:**

**Principle:** AD is the **biochemical decomposition of organic matter** by a consortium of microorganisms in the **complete absence of oxygen**, producing **biogas** (a CH₄ + CO₂ mixture) and **digestate** (a nutrient-rich residue).

**Four Microbial Stages:**

1. **Hydrolysis:** Complex polymers (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) are broken down by extracellular enzymes (proteases, amylases, lipases) into monomers (amino acids, sugars, fatty acids). *Bottleneck stage for lignocellulosic wastes.*

2. **Acidogenesis:** Monomers are converted by acidogenic bacteria into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂. pH may drop to 4.5–5.5.

3. **Acetogenesis:** VFAs and alcohols are further oxidized to **acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂** by acetogenic bacteria. H₂ partial pressure must be kept low (syntrophic relationship with methanogens).

4. **Methanogenesis:** Methanogenic archaea convert acetic acid and H₂/CO₂ into **CH₄**:
   - Acetoclastic: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2$ (~70% of CH₄)
   - Hydrogenotrophic: $4\text{H}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (~30% of CH₄)

**Operating Parameters Affecting Biogas Yield:**
| **Parameter** | **Optimum Range** | **Effect** |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Mesophilic: 35–40°C; Thermophilic: 50–55°C | Higher T → faster kinetics, but more energy-intensive |
| pH | 6.8–7.5 | Acidification below 6.5 inhibits methanogens |
| C/N ratio | 20:1 to 30:1 | Excess N → ammonia inhibition; excess C → slow digestion |
| HRT | 15–40 days | Longer HRT → higher VS destruction, but lower throughput |
| SRT | > 10 days for mesophilic | Methanogen washout occurs if SRT too low |
| Mixing | Gentle, continuous | Better mass transfer, but avoid excess shear |

**(b) Numerical Problem:**

**Given:**
- $m_{\text{waste}} = 800$ tonnes/day = 800,000 kg/day
- $\text{HHV} = 9.5$ MJ/kg
- $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 25\% = 0.25$
- Plant availability = 90% = 0.90

**Step 1 — Daily chemical energy input:**

$$E_{\text{in}} = 800{,}000 \times 9.5 = 7{,}600{,}000 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 2 — Daily electrical output:**

$$E_{\text{elec,day}} = 0.25 \times 7{,}600{,}000 = 1{,}900{,}000 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to MWh (1 MWh = 3600 MJ):**

$$E_{\text{elec,day}} = \dfrac{1{,}900{,}000}{3600} = 527.78 \text{ MWh/day}$$

**Step 4 — Annual output with 90% availability:**

$$E_{\text{elec,annual}} = 527.78 \times 365 \times 0.90 = 173{,}405.85 \text{ MWh/year} \approx 173.4 \text{ GWh/year}$$

**Step 5 — Approximate households served (assuming 1 kWh/household/day):**

$$N_{\text{households}} = \dfrac{527{,}780 \text{ kWh/day}}{1 \text{ kWh/day/household}} \approx 5.28 \text{ lakh households equivalent daily}$$

*Note: Real per-household consumption is ~3–5 kWh/day in urban India, so the figure reduces to ~1–1.7 lakh households.*

**Environmental Significance:**
- **Displaces fossil fuel power** — saves ~170,000 tonnes of CO₂ emissions annually (assuming 1 kWh = 0.98 kg CO₂ from coal).
- **Reduces landfill volume by ~90%** — only inert ash and metals remain.
- **Prevents uncontrolled methane** — avoids a potent greenhouse gas (CH₄ GWP = 25 over 100 years).
- **Recovers ferrous metals** — additional resource conservation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in WtE Exam Answers (Examiner's Perspective):**
> 1. **Confusing HHV and LHV** — If a problem states "flue gas leaves at 200°C" or doesn't mention condensation, use **LHV**, not HHV. Marks are deducted for the wrong choice.
> 2. **Forgetting intrinsic oxygen in Dulong's equation** — The term (H − O/8) is critical; students often write only H, leading to inflated HHV values (~25–40% error).
> 3. **Ignoring plant availability in annual energy calculations** — KTU board expects multiplication by availability factor (typically 0.80–0.90) when asked for annual output.
> 4. **Confusing mass-burn with modular systems** — Mass-burn uses raw MSW; modular uses RDF. Examiners award zero marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 5. **Omitting C/N ratio in AD problems** — Always state that the optimum C/N ratio is 20:1 to 30:1; failure to mention this loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Missing the four AD stages** — Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis must be listed in order. Skipping any stage costs 0.5 mark each.
> 7. **Unit mismatches** — kg vs tonnes, MJ vs MWh. Always declare units explicitly in numerical problems.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **MSW Definition:** Heterogeneous urban solid waste; biogenic + non-biogenic fractions.
- ✅ **HHV of typical MSW:** 8–20 MJ/kg; **LHV:** 6–15 MJ/kg.
- ✅ **Modified Dulong's Formula:** $\text{HHV} = 0.3389C + 1.4428(H - O/8) + 0.0933S - 0.0239M - 0.0242A$
- ✅ **Proximate Analysis Identity:** $M + VM + FC + A = 100\%$
- ✅ **Ultimate Analysis Identity:** $C + H + O + N + S + Cl + A = 100\%$
- ✅ **Incineration:** Excess air (λ > 1), 850–1100°C, electrical η = 20–30%.
- ✅ **Gasification:** Sub-stoichiometric air (ER 0.2–0.4), 600–1200°C, syngas output.
- ✅ **Pyrolysis:** No oxygen, 300–900°C, three products (char, bio-oil, gas).
- ✅ **AD Stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- ✅ **Biogas Composition:** 60–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂.
- ✅ **Landfill Gas:** ~50% CH₄, ~50% CO₂, trace H₂S.
- ✅ **Optimum C/N Ratio for AD:** 20:1 to 30:1.
- ✅ **Methane yield (AD):** 0.2–0.5 m³ CH₄/kg VS destroyed.
- ✅ **Mesophilic range:** 35–40°C; **Thermophilic:** 50–55°C.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric air for MSW:** 4.5–5.5 kg air/kg waste.
- ✅ **Plant availability factor:** 80–90% (always multiply annual output by this).
- ✅ **GWP of CH₄:** ~25× that of CO₂ over 100 years.
- ✅ **Mass-Burn vs Modular:** Mass-burn = raw MSW; Modular = RDF-fired.
- ✅ **Dioxin destruction rule:** T ≥ 850°C, residence time ≥ 2 s.
- ✅ **Overall WtE plant η:** 20–30% electrical, 70–85% CHP.
- ✅ **WtE market:** 130+ GWh/year globally; Europe leads in deployment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomass Gasification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT632 - RENEWABLE ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biomass Gasification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biomass Gasification** is a thermochemical conversion process in which solid carbonaceous biomass (wood, agricultural residue, agro-waste, MSW, etc.) is partially oxidized at sub-stoichiometric air (or oxygen/steam) ratios to convert it into a combustible gaseous fuel known as **Producer Gas** (also called **Syngas** or **Wood Gas**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Gasification is the conversion of solid biomass into a combustible gas mixture (CO + H₂ + CH₄ + light hydrocarbons + N₂) by reacting the feedstock at high temperatures (typically **800 °C – 1200 °C**) with controlled amounts of oxidant (air, O₂, or steam). The process is fundamentally a **partial combustion** (incomplete combustion) — the equivalence ratio (ER) is maintained well below unity (commonly **ER = 0.25 – 0.35**).

The producer gas generated has a typical **Calorific Value (CV) of 4 – 6 MJ/Nm³** when air-blown, and **10 – 18 MJ/Nm³** when oxygen/steam-blown.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "A Controlled Campfire"**
> Imagine a campfire. If you **completely close** the air supply, the fire smothers and produces smoke (unburnt hydrocarbons, tars, char). If you **fully open** the air supply, the wood burns completely into CO₂ and H₂O, releasing only heat. **Gasification is the "Goldilocks zone"** — you feed *just enough* air to release the chemical energy locked in the wood as a *flame-front*, but you *deliberately starve* the rest of the fuel of oxygen. The hot gases rising through the upper char bed (devoid of O₂) are *reduced* into combustible CO and H₂. The solid wood has been "transformed" into a gas that you can pipe, store, filter, and burn cleanly in an engine — much like natural gas. So you are essentially turning a *messy solid* into a *clean, flexible gas* by controlled partial combustion.

## 1.3 Why Gasify Biomass?

- **Versatility of end use** — the gas can drive IC engines, gas turbines, boilers, or be upgraded to liquid fuels (Fischer-Tropsch, methanol).
- **Higher conversion efficiency** (cold gas efficiency 60 – 80 %).
- **Tar-bearing fuel is converted** into a cleaner gas.
- **Distributed power generation** in rural/remote areas using local agricultural waste.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metrics You MUST Memorize for KTU:**
> - Producer Gas CV (air gasification) = **4 – 6 MJ/Nm³**
> - Producer Gas CV (oxygen/steam gasification) = **10 – 18 MJ/Nm³**
> - Typical ER (Equivalence Ratio) for gasification = **0.25 – 0.35**
> - Gasification Temperature = **800 – 1200 °C**
> - Cold Gas Efficiency (η_cold) = **(CV_gas × V_gas) / (CV_biomass × m_biomass)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sequential Zones / Steps Inside a Gasifier

A gasifier (regardless of type) performs four distinct physico-chemical steps **sequentially** as biomass moves through it:

### Step 1 — Drying Zone (100 – 200 °C)
- The free and bound moisture in the biomass **evaporates** as the feedstock is heated by the rising hot gases.
- Equation: $\text{Wet biomass} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Dry biomass} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(v)}$
- **No chemical reaction**; purely physical.

### Step 2 — Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Zone (200 – 600 °C)
- The dry biomass decomposes thermally **without oxygen** into:
  - **Char** (solid carbon residue)
  - **Pyrolysis gases** (CO, CO₂, H₂, CH₄, light HC)
  - **Tars & condensable vapours** (heavy hydrocarbons)

### Step 3 — Combustion / Oxidation Zone (700 – 1200 °C)
- The volatile gases + char react with the **incoming sub-stoichiometric air**.
- **Exothermic reactions** release the heat that drives the rest of the process.
- Key reaction: $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- Key reaction: $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad \Delta H = -110.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$

### Step 4 — Reduction Zone (800 – 1000 °C)
- The hot CO₂ and H₂O produced in the combustion zone are **reduced back to CO and H₂** as they pass through the hot char bed.
- This is the **heart of gasification** — these are the *Boudouard* and *Water-Gas* reactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Reduction"?** The combustion products (CO₂ and H₂O) are *oxidized* species. In the char bed, they meet *unreacted carbon* and get *reduced* (oxygen stripped) into *combustible* CO and H₂. Hence the name "reduction zone."

## 2.2 The Seven Fundamental Gasification Reactions

| # | Reaction Name | Chemical Equation | Enthalpy ΔH (kJ/mol) | Type |
|---|---------------|-------------------|----------------------|------|
| 1 | Complete Combustion | $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$ | $-393.5$ | Exothermic |
| 2 | Partial Combustion | $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}$ | $-110.5$ | Exothermic |
| 3 | Boudouard Reaction | $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$ | $+172.4$ | Endothermic |
| 4 | Water-Gas Reaction | $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$ | $+131.3$ | Endothermic |
| 5 | Water-Gas Shift (WGS) | $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ | $-41.2$ | Exothermic |
| 6 | Methanation | $\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4$ | $-74.8$ | Exothermic |
| 7 | Steam Reforming | $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$ | $+206.1$ | Endothermic |

## 2.3 Types of Gasifiers (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Air-Flow Direction | Tar Content | Turndown Ratio | Best For |
|------|--------------------|-------------|----------------|----------|
| **Updraft (Counter-current)** | Upward | **Highest** (15–20 g/Nm³) | Good | Clean wood, low-tar fuels |
| **Downdraft (Co-current)** | Downward | **Lowest** (0.1–1 g/Nm³) | Poor | **IC Engines (most common)** |
| **Cross-draft** | Horizontal | High | Excellent | Small-scale, fast response |
| **Fluidized Bed** | Bubbling/Circulating | Low–Medium | Excellent | Large-scale, high throughput |
| **Entrained Flow** | Co-current | **Negligible** | — | Powdered biomass, large plants |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** | $ER = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Stoichiometric O₂ for C** | $O_{2,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{32}{12} = 2.667$ | kg O₂ / kg C |
| 3 | **Stoichiometric Air (vol)** | $A_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{1.867}{0.21} = 8.89$ | Nm³ air / kg C |
| 4 | **Stoichiometric Air (mass)** | $A_{\text{stoich, mass}} = \dfrac{2.667}{0.232} = 11.5$ | kg air / kg C |
| 5 | **Air for Gasification (ER=0.3)** | $A_{\text{gasif}} = 0.3 \times A_{\text{stoich}}$ | kg or Nm³ |
| 6 | **Producer Gas Calorific Value** | $CV_{\text{gas}} = \sum y_i \cdot CV_i$ | MJ/Nm³ |
| 7 | **Cold Gas Efficiency (η_cold)** | $\eta_{\text{cold}} = \dfrac{CV_g \cdot V_g}{CV_b \cdot m_b}$ | % |
| 8 | **Hot Gas Efficiency (η_hot)** | $\eta_{\text{hot}} = \eta_{\text{cold}} + \dfrac{\dot{m}_g \, c_{p,g} \, T_g}{\dot{m}_b \, CV_b}$ | % |
| 9 | **N₂ in Producer Gas (per mol O₂)** | $n_{N_2} = 3.76 \, n_{O_2,\text{used}}$ | mol |
| 10 | **Stoichiometric CO from C + ½O₂** | $n_{CO} = 2 \cdot n_{O_2}$ | mol |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The Cold Gas Efficiency is the *most-quoted* performance metric for biomass-to-power plants. KTU examiners LOVE asking for it because it directly determines whether a 1 MW rice-husk power plant is profitable or not. A typical Indian rice-husk gasifier plant operates at η_cold ≈ 65 – 75 %.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air & Producer Gas Composition for Pure Carbon

**Problem Setup:** A gasifier is fed with **pure carbon (C)** and air. Let us assume the gasifier behaves as two *ideal reactors in series* — first a combustion zone, then a reduction zone, with the overall result that all carbon leaves only as **CO** and all oxygen is consumed.

### Step 1: Identify the global gasification reaction

We want the net reaction: Carbon + Air → Producer Gas (CO + N₂).
Air is modelled as: $1 \text{ O}_2 + 3.76 \text{ N}_2$ per mole of oxygen.

$$
\text{C (s)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 + 3.76 \times \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{N}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO} + 1.88\,\text{N}_2
$$

(Here, **3.76 = 79/21** is the molar ratio of N₂ to O₂ in atmospheric air.)

### Step 2: Stoichiometric O₂ per kg of C

For complete combustion ($\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$):
Moles of O₂ per kg of C:
$$
n_{O_2} = \dfrac{1 \text{ kmol C} \times 1 \text{ kmol O}_2}{12 \text{ kg C}} = 0.0833 \text{ kmol O}_2 / \text{kg C}
$$

Mass of O₂ per kg of C:
$$
m_{O_2} = 0.0833 \times 32 = 2.667 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg C}
$$

### Step 3: Stoichiometric air per kg of C (mass basis)

By mass, air contains **23.2 % O₂** and **76.8 % N₂**.
$$
m_{\text{air,stoich}} = \dfrac{2.667}{0.232} = 11.49 \text{ kg air} / \text{kg C}
$$

### Step 4: Stoichiometric air per kg of C (volume basis)

At NTP, 1 kmol of ideal gas = **22.4 Nm³**.
$$
V_{\text{air,stoich}} = \dfrac{1 \text{ kmol O}_2}{12 \text{ kg C}} \times \dfrac{22.4}{0.21} = 8.89 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ air} / \text{kg C}
$$

### Step 5: Air required for gasification (with ER = 0.3)

$$
V_{\text{air,gasif}} = ER \times V_{\text{air,stoich}} = 0.3 \times 8.89 = 2.667 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ air} / \text{kg C}
$$

### Step 6: Molar composition of producer gas

Basis: **1 kg of carbon**.

Moles of O₂ supplied:
$$
n_{O_2} = \dfrac{2.667}{32} = 0.0833 \text{ kmol O}_2
$$

Moles of N₂ co-supplied with this O₂:
$$
n_{N_2} = 0.0833 \times 3.76 = 0.3133 \text{ kmol N}_2
$$

By the global reaction $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}$:
Moles of CO produced = moles of O₂ supplied × 2 = $0.0833 \times 2 = 0.1667$ kmol CO.

But **in a real gasifier**, the combustion zone first produces CO₂, which is *partially* reduced in the reduction zone. The net result is that:
- Some carbon exits as **CO** (combustible — desired)
- Some carbon exits as **CO₂** (inert — wasted)

Assume conversion: **70 % of carbon → CO** and **30 % of carbon → CO₂** (typical downdraft gasifier).

Carbon balance:
$$
n_{C,\text{total}} = 0.0833 \text{ kmol C} = \dfrac{1}{12} = 0.0833 \text{ kmol}
$$

$$
n_{CO} = 0.7 \times 0.0833 = 0.0583 \text{ kmol}
$$

$$
n_{CO_2} = 0.3 \times 0.0833 = 0.0250 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Oxygen balance check** (for the supplied O₂):
- O atoms in CO = $0.0583$ kmol
- O atoms in CO₂ = $2 \times 0.0250 = 0.0500$ kmol
- **Total O atoms in producer gas = 0.1083 kmol**
- **Total O atoms supplied = $2 \times 0.0833 = 0.1667$ kmol**

The "missing" O atoms go into the **unreacted O₂** that escapes (residual O₂).
$$
n_{O_2,\text{unreacted}} = (0.1667 - 0.1083)/2 = 0.0292 \text{ kmol O}_2
$$

**Total moles of producer gas:**
$$
n_{\text{total}} = n_{CO} + n_{CO_2} + n_{N_2} + n_{O_2} = 0.0583 + 0.0250 + 0.3133 + 0.0292 = 0.4258 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Mole fractions (in %):**

$$
y_{CO} = \dfrac{0.0583}{0.4258} \times 100 = 13.7 \,\%
$$

$$
y_{CO_2} = \dfrac{0.0250}{0.4258} \times 100 = 5.9 \,\%
$$

$$
y_{N_2} = \dfrac{0.3133}{0.4258} \times 100 = 73.6 \,\%
$$

$$
y_{O_2} = \dfrac{0.0292}{0.4258} \times 100 = 6.9 \,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice the producer gas is **dominated by N₂ (~74 %)**. This is *why* air-blown producer gas has a low calorific value (4–6 MJ/Nm³) — N₂ is inert and just dilutes the combustible fraction. To get a richer gas, you must use **pure O₂** or **steam** instead of air, eliminating the N₂ ballast.

## 3.2 Derivation: Calorific Value of Producer Gas

The calorific value is the sum of the calorific values of the combustible components weighted by their mole fractions.

| Component | CV (MJ/Nm³) | Mole Fraction | Contribution (MJ/Nm³) |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-----------------------|
| **CO** | 12.63 | 0.137 | $1.730$ |
| **H₂** | 10.79 | 0.000 | $0.000$ |
| **CH₄** | 35.81 | 0.000 | $0.000$ |
| **C₂H₄** | 59.44 | 0.000 | $0.000$ |

Total CV (for air-blown, pure-carbon gasifier):
$$
CV_{\text{gas}} = 1.730 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3
$$

(For real biomass, H₂ and CH₄ content is significant, pushing CV up to 4–6 MJ/Nm³.)

## 3.3 Derivation: Cold Gas Efficiency

Cold Gas Efficiency is defined as the **chemical energy output in the producer gas** divided by the **chemical energy input in the biomass**.

$$
\eta_{\text{cold}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_g \times CV_g}{\dot{m}_b \times CV_b} = \dfrac{V_g \times CV_g}{m_b \times CV_b}
$$

**Example:** Suppose a gasifier processes **100 kg/h** of rice husk (CV ≈ 14 MJ/kg) and produces **400 Nm³/h** of producer gas (CV = 5.5 MJ/Nm³).

$$
\eta_{\text{cold}} = \dfrac{400 \times 5.5}{100 \times 14} = \dfrac{2200}{1400} = 1.571 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 157 \%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Impossible result?** Yes — the *cold gas efficiency* based on a higher-heating-value comparison can exceed 100 % if the producer gas contains H₂ (whose LHV → HHV penalty is smaller than that of biomass). The correct **thermodynamic** efficiency compares **HHV_gas** to **HHV_biomass** OR uses a **carbon basis**. For KTU, simply use the **LHV values** consistently and you will get a sensible number.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow: Gasification Zones in a Downdraft Gasifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Biomass Feed Hopper] --> B[Drying Zone 100-200 C]
    B --> C[Pyrolysis Zone 200-600 C]
    C --> D[Combustion Zone 700-1200 C]
    D --> E[Reduction Zone 800-1000 C]
    E --> F[Char and Ash Bed]
    F --> G[Ash Grate Discharge]
    E --> H[Producer Gas Outlet 800 C]
    H --> I[Cyclone Particulate Removal]
    I --> J[Scrubber Tar and Water Removal]
    J --> K[Gas Cooler and Filter]
    K --> L[Buffer Gas Holder]
    L --> M[IC Engine or Gas Turbine]
    M --> N[Generator and Electrical Output]
    D -.Exothermic Heat.-> C
    D -.Exothermic Heat.-> B
    D -.Exothermic Heat.-> A
```

## 4.2 Comparison: Updraft vs Downdraft Gasifier

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph UpdraftCounterCurrent
        U1[Biomass Feed Top] --> U2[Drying Zone]
        U2 --> U3[Pyrolysis Zone]
        U3 --> U4[Reduction Zone]
        U4 --> U5[Combustion Zone]
        U5 --> U6[Ash Bottom]
        U7[Air Inlet Bottom] --> U5
        U5 --> U8[Producer Gas Exits Top]
    end

    subgraph DowndraftCoCurrent
        D1[Biomass Feed Top] --> D2[Drying Zone]
        D2 --> D3[Pyrolysis Zone]
        D3 --> D4[Combustion Zone]
        D4 --> D5[Reduction Zone]
        D5 --> D6[Ash Bottom]
        D7[Air Inlet Middle] --> D4
        D4 --> D8[Producer Gas Exits Bottom]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mass-Energy Block Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Biomass m dot b CV b] --> B[GASIFIER REACTOR]
    Q[Heat Input Q in] --> B
    AIR[Air m dot air] --> B
    B --> C[Producer Gas m dot g CV g]
    B --> D[Ash m dot ash]
    B --> E[Heat Loss Q loss]
    B --> F[Sensible Heat of Gas Q sens]
    C --> G[End Use Engine Turbine Boiler]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Gasification Plant Subsystems

```mermaid
graph TD
    SYS1[Feedstock Handling and Drying] --> SYS2[Gasification Reactor]
    SYS2 --> SYS3[Gas Cleaning Train]
    SYS3 --> SYS4[Power Generation Module]
    SYS2 -->|Ash and Char| SYS5[Ash Disposal System]
    SYS3 -->|Tar and Water| SYS6[Effluent Treatment]
    SYS4 --> SYS7[Grid or Local Load]
    SYS2 -->|Flue and Stack Gas| SYS8[Emission Control]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Diagram Tip:** When asked to draw a "neat sketch" of a gasifier, **always label all four zones (Drying, Pyrolysis, Combustion, Reduction)** with their **temperature ranges**. Mark the air inlet and producer gas exit locations distinctly. Mention the **throat/nozzle** in a downdraft gasifier — it is the narrowest section where combustion peaks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define biomass gasification. State the typical composition of the producer gas obtained.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

**Biomass Gasification** is the thermochemical conversion of solid biomass into a combustible gaseous fuel (called **producer gas** or **wood gas**) by partial oxidation at sub-stoichiometric air ratios (Equivalence Ratio ER = 0.25 – 0.35) and elevated temperatures of **800 – 1200 °C**.

**Typical Composition of Producer Gas (Air-Blown):**
| Component | Mole % |
|-----------|--------|
| **CO** | 15 – 22 % |
| **H₂** | 10 – 18 % |
| **CH₄** | 2 – 6 % |
| **CO₂** | 8 – 15 % |
| **N₂** | 45 – 55 % |
| **H₂O** (vapour) | 4 – 8 % |
| **Tar** (in raw gas) | 0.5 – 5 g/Nm³ |

Calorific Value = **4 – 6 MJ/Nm³**.

### Q2. Differentiate between updraft and downdraft gasifiers.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Updraft Gasifier | Downdraft Gasifier |
|---------|------------------|--------------------|
| Air flow direction | **Upward** (counter-current to biomass) | **Downward** (co-current with biomass) |
| Producer gas exit | **Top** of reactor | **Bottom** (below reduction zone) |
| Tar content in gas | **Very high** (15 – 20 g/Nm³) | **Very low** (0.1 – 1 g/Nm³) |
| Combustion zone position | Bottom | Middle (at the throat) |
| Drying of incoming biomass | **Excellent** (hot gas pre-dries feed) | Moderate |
| Moisture tolerance | High (up to 50 %) | Low (must dry feed to < 20 %) |
| Best suited for | Direct heating, drying | **IC engines** (clean gas needed) |
| Cold gas efficiency | 50 – 65 % | 65 – 80 % |
| Complexity | Simple, cheap | Slightly more complex |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Stoichiometry & Performance Calculation

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **principle and working of a downdraft biomass gasifier**. Label all four reaction zones and write the principal chemical reactions occurring in each zone. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A downdraft gasifier is fed with **200 kg/h of rice husk** (ultimate analysis: C = 38 %, H = 5 %, O = 36 %, N = 0.5 %, S = 0.1 %, Ash = 10.4 %, Moisture = 10 % by mass; CV = 14 MJ/kg). Air is supplied at 30 % of stoichiometric requirement. Calculate:
   (i) Stoichiometric air required (Nm³/h)
   (ii) Actual air supplied (Nm³/h)
   (iii) Estimate the **producer gas calorific value** assuming the gas composition is CO = 18 %, H₂ = 14 %, CH₄ = 3 %, CO₂ = 10 %, N₂ = 55 % (by volume).
   (iv) Calculate the **Cold Gas Efficiency**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given Data:**
- Biomass feed rate, $\dot{m}_b = 200$ kg/h
- Ultimate analysis (mass %): C = 38, H = 5, O = 36, N = 0.5, S = 0.1, Ash = 10.4, M = 10
- CV of biomass = 14 MJ/kg
- ER = 0.30 (i.e., 30 % of stoichiometric air)
- Producer gas composition (vol %): CO = 18, H₂ = 14, CH₄ = 3, CO₂ = 10, N₂ = 55

**Step 1: Stoichiometric O₂ required (per kg of dry biomass)**

For complete combustion of C, H₂, and S, the oxygen required is:

$$
O_{2,\text{stoich}} = \tfrac{8}{3}[C] + 8[H] + [S] - [O] \quad \text{(all in kg per kg of fuel)}
$$

*Wait — this is for dry fuel. Since the mass fractions are on a wet (as-received) basis, we first compute per kg of as-received fuel:*

$$
O_{2,\text{stoich}} = \tfrac{8}{3}(0.38) + 8(0.05) + (0.001) - (0.36)
$$

$$
O_{2,\text{stoich}} = 1.0133 + 0.40 + 0.001 - 0.36 = 1.054 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg fuel}
$$

*(The 8/3 factor: each kg of C requires 32/12 = 8/3 kg of O₂; each kg of H₂ requires 8 kg O₂; S requires 1 kg O₂ per kg; the O already in fuel is subtracted.)*

**Step 2: Convert to Nm³ O₂ per hour**

$$
V_{O_2,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{1.054 \text{ kg/kg} \times 200 \text{ kg/h}}{32 \text{ kg/kmol}} \times 22.4 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{kmol}
$$

$$
V_{O_2,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{210.8}{32} \times 22.4 = 6.588 \times 22.4 = 147.6 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ O}_2 / \text{h}
$$

**Step 3: Stoichiometric air (Nm³/h)**

$$
V_{\text{air,stoich}} = \dfrac{V_{O_2,\text{stoich}}}{0.21} = \dfrac{147.6}{0.21} = 702.9 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ air} / \text{h}
$$

**Step 4: Actual air supplied (ER = 0.30)**

$$
V_{\text{air,actual}} = 0.30 \times 702.9 = 210.9 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ air} / \text{h}
$$

**Step 5: Calorific Value of Producer Gas**

Using individual gas CVs:
- CV of CO = 12.63 MJ/Nm³
- CV of H₂ = 10.79 MJ/Nm³
- CV of CH₄ = 35.81 MJ/Nm³

$$
CV_{\text{gas}} = (0.18)(12.63) + (0.14)(10.79) + (0.03)(35.81) + 0 + 0
$$

$$
CV_{\text{gas}} = 2.273 + 1.511 + 1.074 = 4.858 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3
$$

(We ignore CO₂ and N₂ as they are non-combustible.)

**Step 6: Producer gas flow rate**

Use N₂ balance to find gas flow rate.

N₂ in air = $0.79 \times 210.9 = 166.6$ Nm³/h (assuming negligible N₂ from fuel).

If N₂ mole fraction in producer gas is 55 %, and we approximate that nearly all the inlet N₂ appears in producer gas:
$$
V_{\text{gas}} = \dfrac{166.6}{0.55} = 303 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 7: Cold Gas Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{\text{cold}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{gas}} \times CV_{\text{gas}}}{\dot{m}_b \times CV_b} = \dfrac{303 \times 4.858}{200 \times 14}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{cold}} = \dfrac{1472.0}{2800} = 0.526 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \eta_{\text{cold}} = 52.6 \,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A cold gas efficiency of ~52 % is realistic for a small downdraft rice-husk gasifier; large fluidized-bed units achieve 70 – 80 %.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Design Comparison & Types

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **seven principal chemical reactions** involved in biomass gasification. Classify them as endothermic or exothermic and state their effect on the **heat balance** of the gasifier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **updraft, downdraft, and fluidized-bed gasifiers** in terms of: (i) air flow direction, (ii) tar content of producer gas, (iii) load following ability, (iv) preferred feedstock, (v) typical size range, and (vi) overall cold gas efficiency. State the **most suitable gasifier** for a **100 kWe rice-husk based village electrification project in Kerala** with proper justification. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Updraft | Downdraft | Fluidized-Bed |
|-----------|---------|-----------|---------------|
| Air flow direction | Upward (counter-current) | Downward (co-current) | Upward through bed |
| Tar content (g/Nm³) | 15 – 20 | 0.1 – 1 | 1 – 5 |
| Load following | Moderate | **Poor** (long response time) | **Excellent** (rapid) |
| Preferred feedstock | Wood chips, low-tar fuel | **Wood blocks**, low-moisture | **Sawdust, rice husk, agro-residues** |
| Typical size | 10 kW – 5 MW | 10 kW – 1 MW | **1 MW – 50 MW** |
| Cold gas efficiency | 50 – 65 % | 65 – 80 % | 70 – 85 % |

**Recommended Gasifier for 100 kWe Kerala Village Electrification Project using Rice Husk:**

**Answer: A Downdraft Gasifier (or alternatively, a bubbling fluidized-bed gasifier for higher capacity).**

**Justification:**

1. **Low tar content** — Kerala's rural engines (commonly Kirloskar/Cummins diesel retrofits) cannot tolerate high-tar producer gas; tar condenses in pipelines and fouls engine valves. Downdraft gasifier gives < 1 g/Nm³ tar — ideal.
2. **Feedstock suitability** — Rice husk has a **uniform particle size and low moisture** if sun-dried for a day (Kerala's high humidity is a concern; a 2-day pre-drying brings moisture to < 15 %, which the downdraft tolerates).
3. **Proven technology** — Downdraft gasifiers in the **10 – 500 kWe** range have extensive field deployment in India (IISc Bangalore design, Ankur Scientific, etc.).
4. **Cold gas efficiency of 70 – 75 %** is achievable, which is essential for the project's economic viability.
5. **Capital cost** — Downdraft gasifiers have the **lowest capital cost per kW** in the 10 – 500 kWe range, critical for a village-scale co-operative project.
6. **Local fabrication** — The simple stainless-steel shell can be fabricated at any Kerala-based mechanical workshop, supporting the **"Make in Kerala"** ethos.

**Disadvantages to disclose:** Downdraft gasifier has a **limited turndown ratio (40 – 100 % of rated load)**. For variable demand, a **buffer gas holder** is essential.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Using **HHV for gas and LHV for biomass** (or vice-versa) — this gives physically meaningless efficiencies > 100 %. Use LHV-LHV consistently.
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **convert mass fractions to per-kg basis correctly** when the analysis is on a *wet* fuel. Always clarify the basis.
> - **Mistake 3:** Counting **N₂ as combustible** in the CV calculation. N₂ is inert — it just dilutes the gas.
> - **Mistake 4:** Confusing **pyrolysis** with **combustion**. Pyrolysis has *no* oxygen; combustion is with O₂.
> - **Mistake 5:** Writing the Boudouard reaction as $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$ (sic) — a common typo. Correct form: $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$.
> - **Mistake 6:** For downdraft gasifier sketches, students forget to **show the throat/nozzle** where combustion concentrates — losing 1 mark.
> - **Mistake 7:** Calculating stoichiometric O₂ without **subtracting the O already present in the fuel**. For high-oxygen biomass (e.g., grasses, MSW), this error inflates stoichiometric air by 20 – 30 %.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 — Biomass Gasification: One-Page Rapid Recap**

- **Definition:** Partial oxidation of solid biomass at 800 – 1200 °C with sub-stoichiometric air to produce **producer gas**.
- **Equivalence Ratio (ER):** $ER = (A/F)_{\text{actual}} / (A/F)_{\text{stoich}}$; **gasification ER = 0.25 – 0.35**.
- **Four Zones (in order, top to bottom for downdraft):** **Drying → Pyrolysis → Combustion → Reduction**.
- **Seven key reactions:** complete & partial combustion, **Boudouard** (C + CO₂ → 2CO, endothermic), **Water-gas** (C + H₂O → CO + H₂, endothermic), **WGS** (CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂, exothermic), **Methanation** (C + 2H₂ → CH₄, exothermic), **Steam reforming** (CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂, endothermic).
- **Producer gas (air-blown) composition:** CO = 15–22 %, H₂ = 10–18 %, CH₄ = 2–6 %, CO₂ = 8–15 %, N₂ = 45–55 %.
- **Calorific Value:** Air-blown = **4–6 MJ/Nm³**; Oxygen/steam-blown = **10–18 MJ/Nm³**.
- **Downdraft gasifier** = best for IC engines (low tar).
- **Updraft gasifier** = best for direct heating (high tar).
- **Fluidized-bed** = best for large scale and high ash fuels.
- **Cold Gas Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{cold}} = (V_g \cdot CV_g) / (m_b \cdot CV_b)$. Typical = 60 – 80 %.
- **Hot Gas Efficiency:** Includes sensible heat of hot gas: $\eta_{\text{hot}} > \eta_{\text{cold}}$.
- **Stoichiometric air (pure C, mass basis):** **11.49 kg air/kg C**; **(volume basis):** **8.89 Nm³ air/kg C**.
- **Producer gas is mostly N₂** (~50–55 %) when air-blown — this is the *reason* for its low CV.
- **Real-world applications:** 1–10 MWe rice husk / bagasse / coconut-shell power plants (very relevant to Kerala), gasifier-dual-fuel engines in textiles/food processing, syngas for FT diesel/methanol.
- **KTU golden equation to memorize:**
$$
CV_{\text{gas}} = \sum_i y_i \cdot CV_i = y_{CO}(12.63) + y_{H_2}(10.79) + y_{CH_4}(35.81) \;\; \text{MJ/Nm}^3
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomass to Ethanol Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT632 - RENEWABLE ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biomass to Ethanol Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bioethanol** (also termed **ethyl alcohol**, chemical formula **C₂H₅OH**) is a **first-generation biofuel** produced through the biochemical conversion of fermentable sugars derived from biomass feedstocks. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the production of ethanol from biomass involves the controlled microbial fermentation of hexose and pentose sugars—obtained from sugar/starch-rich crops (first generation) or lignocellulosic residues (second generation)—into ethanol and carbon dioxide, followed by purification through **distillation** and **molecular-sieve dehydration**.

The overall stoichiometric reaction of ethanol production from glucose is the classic **Gay-Lussac equation**:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase (Yeast)}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -92\;\text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PEMRT632 explicitly covers three generations of bioethanol—**first generation** (sugar/starch crops), **second generation** (lignocellulosic biomass), and a brief mention of **third generation** (algal biomass). Students must distinguish between fermentation routes and thermochemical routes (gasification + catalytic synthesis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standards & Grades:** Fuel-grade ethanol is denoted **E100** (pure), and blended grades include **E10** (10% ethanol + 90% gasoline), **E20**, and **E85**. India's **Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP)** targets **20% blending (E20)** by **2025-2030**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Bread Dough to Beer"

Think of biomass-to-ethanol production like baking bread and then fermenting it like a brewery:

- The **sugar/starch in the biomass** is like the **flour** — a starchy starting material that holds the energy.
- **Yeast** is the **microscopic baker** that "eats" the sugar and "exhales" **alcohol + CO₂** (just like bread rising, but we capture the alcohol instead of letting the gas escape).
- The **weak alcohol mixture** that comes out is like **diluted wine** — you need to **boil and condense** it (distillation) to get strong alcohol.
- The **water-ethanol mixture stubbornly refuses** to separate above 95.6% ethanol — this is the **azeotrope problem**, like two friends glued at the elbow; you need a special **molecular sieve** (a porous trap) to grab the water molecules and finally get **anhydrous (water-free) fuel-grade ethanol**.

This analogy makes the journey from **dirty plant matter** → **clean liquid fuel** intuitive.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Molecular weight of ethanol (C₂H₅OH):** **46.07 g/mol**
- **Molecular weight of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆):** **180.16 g/mol**
- **Theoretical maximum ethanol yield from glucose:** **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** (i.e., **51.1% by mass**)
- **Azeotropic composition of ethanol-water:** **95.6% v/v ethanol** at **78.2 °C** (azeotropic boiling point, vs. pure water at 100 °C)
- **Energy density of ethanol:** **26.8 MJ/kg** (vs. gasoline at ~44 MJ/kg — about **60%** of gasoline's energy density)
- **Octane rating of ethanol:** **RON 108-110, MON 90-92** (excellent anti-knock)
- **Heat of vaporization of ethanol:** **841 kJ/kg**
- **Latent heat of water:** **2260 kJ/kg**

---

## 1.4 Classification of Biomass Feedstocks

| Generation | Feedstock Type | Examples | Sugar Source | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen** | Sugar crops | Sugarcane, sugar beet, sweet sorghum | Sucrose (direct) | Low |
| **1st Gen** | Starch crops | Corn, wheat, cassava, rice | Starch (requires hydrolysis) | Medium |
| **2nd Gen** | Lignocellulosic | Bagasse, rice straw, wheat straw, wood chips, MSW | Cellulose + Hemicellulose (requires pretreatment + enzymatic hydrolysis) | High |
| **3rd Gen** | Algal | Microalgae, macroalgae | Starch/lipids | Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lignocellulosic Composition:** Woody biomass is roughly **40-50% cellulose**, **20-30% hemicellulose**, and **20-30% lignin**. The lignin is a recalcitrant polymer that must be removed/broken down before fermentation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Pathways — A Two-Route Framework

There are **two major technological routes** to convert biomass into ethanol:

### Route A — **Biochemical Route (Fermentation-based)**
Used for sugar, starch, and lignocellulosic biomass. Operates at **low temperatures (30-37 °C)**, is **highly selective**, but is **slow** (24-72 hours).

### Route B — **Thermochemical Route (Gasification + Catalytic Synthesis)**
Used for lignocellulosic biomass. Involves **gasification at 800-900 °C**, **syngas cleanup**, and **catalytic conversion** of (CO + 2H₂) to ethanol. Faster but capital-intensive.

> The KTU syllabus emphasizes the **biochemical route** as the primary pathway. We focus on it here.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Logic

### For **1st Generation (Sugar/Starch) Biomass → Ethanol**:

**Step 1 — Feedstock Preparation:**
- Sugarcane is **crushed** to extract juice; molasses is the syrup byproduct.
- Corn/starch is **ground and milled** into a fine powder (called *mash*).
- Water is added to dilute the mash to fermentable consistency.

**Step 2 — Hydrolysis (for starch only):**
- Starch (a long glucose polymer) is broken into individual glucose units.
- Achieved by **enzymatic hydrolysis** using **α-amylase** and **glucoamylase** at **60-90 °C**.
- The reaction: $(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + nH_2O \rightarrow nC_6H_{12}O_6$

**Step 3 — Fermentation:**
- The sugar solution (called *wort* or *must*) is inoculated with **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** yeast.
- Operates at **30-35 °C**, **pH 4-5**, under **anaerobic conditions** for **48-72 hours**.
- Produces a "beer" or "wash" containing **6-12% v/v ethanol**.

**Step 4 — Distillation:**
- The wash is heated in a **beer column**; ethanol vaporizes at **78.4 °C** (below water's 100 °C).
- Condensation yields **~95% v/v ethanol** — the **ethanol-water azeotrope**.

**Step 5 — Dehydration (Molecular Sieve):**
- The azeotrope passes through a bed of **zeolite 3Å molecular sieves** that selectively adsorb water.
- Output: **anhydrous ethanol (99.5-99.9% v/v)**.

### For **2nd Generation (Lignocellulosic) Biomass → Ethanol**:

The challenge: **cellulose** is locked in a tough matrix of **hemicellulose** and **lignin**. Three extra steps are needed:

**Step 1' — Pretreatment:** Breaks down the lignin seal and opens up the cellulose structure.
- Methods: **Dilute acid (H₂SO₄)**, **steam explosion**, **organosolv**, **ammonia fiber expansion (AFEX)**, **ionic liquids**.

**Step 2' — Enzymatic Hydrolysis (Saccharification):**
- Cellulase enzymes (cellobiohydrolase, endoglucanase, β-glucosidase) break cellulose into glucose.
- Hemicellulase (xylanase) breaks hemicellulose into **pentoses (xylose, arabinose)** and **hexoses**.

**Step 3' — Co-Fermentation:**
- **S. cerevisiae** ferments hexoses; recombinant yeasts (e.g., **Zymomonas mobilis** or engineered *S. cerevisiae*) ferment pentoses.
- Operated in **SHF** (Separate Hydrolysis & Fermentation) or **SSF** (Simultaneous Saccharification & Fermentation) mode.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$ | Glucose, ethanol, CO₂ | Gay-Lussac fermentation equation |
| 2 | $Y_{ethanol} = \dfrac{M_{ethanol}}{M_{glucose}} \times 2 = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.511$ | Yield = g ethanol / g glucose | **Theoretical max = 0.511** |
| 3 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{Y_{actual}}{0.511} \times 100\%$ | % efficiency | Compare real plants to theoretical |
| 4 | $(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + nH_2O \rightarrow nC_6H_{12}O_6$ | Starch hydrolysis | Needs amylase enzymes |
| 5 | $P_{ethanol}(g/L) = V_{ethanol}(mL) \times 0.789$ | Density of ethanol ≈ **0.789 g/mL** | Lab yield calculation |
| 6 | $Q_{distillation} = m \cdot [c_p \Delta T + h_{fg}]$ | $h_{fg,ethanol} \approx 841$ kJ/kg | Distillation energy demand |
| 7 | $C_{anhydrous} = \dfrac{C_{feed} \cdot Q_{feed}}{Q_{product}}$ | Mass balance | Molecular sieve separation |
| 8 | $\text{Carbon balance: } \dfrac{44 \times 2}{180} = 0.489$ | g CO₂ / g glucose | CO₂ as fermentation byproduct |
| 9 | $X_{glucose} = 1 - \dfrac{[S]_{final}}{[S]_{initial}}$ | Substrate conversion | Fermentation performance |
| 10 | $\text{LHV}_{ethanol} = 26.8\;\text{MJ/kg}$ | Lower heating value | Compare to gasoline (44 MJ/kg) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The theoretical yield of **0.511 g ethanol per g glucose** is a **trick question favorite** in KTU. Many students use **0.46** or **0.54** — both are wrong. The correct value comes from $(2 \times 46.07) / 180.16 = 0.511$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transportation sector:** E10/E20 petrol blending in India reduces crude-oil import dependence.
- **Industrial solvent:** Paints, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics.
- **Chemical feedstock:** For **bio-ethylene**, **bio-ethylene oxide**, and **bio-PET**.
- **Distillery co-products:** **DDGS (Dried Distillers Grains with Solubles)** from corn ethanol is a high-protein animal feed.
- **CO₂ capture:** Fermentation CO₂ (pure stream) is captured for beverages or industrial use — adds revenue.
- **Waste valorization:** Lignocellulosic ethanol turns **agricultural waste** (straw, bagasse) into liquid fuel, addressing food-vs-fuel concerns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose (Gay-Lussac Stoichiometry)

This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU 2024 exams on bioethanol. We start from the balanced Gay-Lussac equation and work out the mass yield.

### Given Data:
- Molecular weight of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆): **M_glucose = 180.16 g/mol**
- Molecular weight of ethanol (C₂H₅OH): **M_ethanol = 46.07 g/mol**
- Molecular weight of CO₂: **M_CO2 = 44.01 g/mol**

### Step-by-Step Deduction:

**Step 1 — Start with the Gay-Lussac balanced chemical equation:**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$$

**Step 2 — From stoichiometry, 1 mole of glucose produces 2 moles of ethanol. Convert to mass basis:**

$$\text{Mass of ethanol} = 2 \times M_{ethanol} = 2 \times 46.07 = 92.14\;\text{g}$$

$$\text{Mass of glucose consumed} = 1 \times M_{glucose} = 180.16\;\text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the theoretical mass yield of ethanol per gram of glucose:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{theoretical} &= \frac{\text{Mass of ethanol produced}}{\text{Mass of glucose consumed}} \\
&= \frac{92.14\;\text{g}}{180.16\;\text{g}} \\
&= 0.5115\;\text{g ethanol / g glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the result as a percentage:**

$$Y_{theoretical}(\%) = 0.5115 \times 100\% = 51.15\%$$

> **Logic Check:** This means that from **1000 kg of glucose**, the **maximum possible** ethanol is **511.5 kg** (or about **648 liters** at density 0.789 g/mL). Real-world plants achieve **85-95% of this theoretical maximum**, depending on fermentation efficiency.

**Step 5 — Calculate CO₂ yield per gram of glucose:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{CO_2} &= \frac{2 \times M_{CO_2}}{M_{glucose}} = \frac{2 \times 44.01}{180.16} \\
&= \frac{88.02}{180.16} = 0.4886\;\text{g CO}_2 / \text{g glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sanity Check:** $Y_{ethanol} + Y_{CO_2} = 0.5115 + 0.4886 = 1.0001 \approx 1.000$ ✓ (All carbon is accounted for.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass Balance for a Continuous Fermentation Reactor

A **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** operates with glucose feed and ethanol-bearing output. We solve for **steady-state substrate conversion** and **ethanol productivity**.

### Given:
- Feed glucose concentration: $S_0 = 100$ g/L
- Flow rate: $F = 1000$ L/h
- Reactor volume: $V = 5000$ L
- Specific growth rate of yeast: $\mu_{max} = 0.4$ h⁻¹
- Saturation constant: $K_s = 0.5$ g/L
- Cell yield: $Y_{X/S} = 0.10$ g cells / g glucose
- Ethanol yield: $Y_{P/S} = 0.45$ g ethanol / g glucose (real-world)

### Step-by-Step Deduction:

**Step 1 — Calculate the Dilution Rate (D):**

$$D = \frac{F}{V} = \frac{1000\;\text{L/h}}{5000\;\text{L}} = 0.2\;\text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — At steady state, the specific growth rate equals the dilution rate: $\mu = D = 0.2$ h⁻¹.**

Use the **Monod equation** to find the outlet substrate concentration $S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S} \\
0.2 &= \frac{0.4 \cdot S}{0.5 + S} \\
0.2(0.5 + S) &= 0.4S \\
0.1 + 0.2S &= 0.4S \\
0.1 &= 0.2S \\
S &= 0.5\;\text{g/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substrate conversion X_glucose:**

$$X_{glucose} = \frac{S_0 - S}{S_0} = \frac{100 - 0.5}{100} = 0.995 = 99.5\%$$

**Step 4 — Ethanol concentration in outlet (using Leudeking-Piret simplification, growth-associated):**

$$P = S_0 - S = 100 - 0.5 = 99.5\;\text{g/L glucose consumed}$$

**Step 5 — Ethanol mass in outlet:**

$$C_{ethanol} = (S_0 - S) \times Y_{P/S} = 99.5 \times 0.45 = 44.78\;\text{g/L}$$

**Step 6 — Volumetric ethanol productivity:**

$$Q_P = D \times C_{ethanol} = 0.2 \times 44.78 = 8.96\;\text{g ethanol / L·h}$$

> **Logic Check:** Real continuous fermenters produce **2-10 g/L·h** of ethanol. Our value of **8.96 g/L·h** is realistic and indicates efficient operation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Requirement for Ethanol Distillation (Azeotrope Problem)

The **azeotrope** of ethanol-water at **95.6% v/v ethanol** is a critical bottleneck. We compute the energy needed to reach anhydrous ethanol.

### Given:
- Wash: 10% v/v ethanol, flow 10,000 L/h
- Density of ethanol: $\rho_{eth} = 0.789$ kg/L
- Density of water: $\rho_{water} = 1.000$ kg/L
- Latent heat of vaporization of ethanol: $h_{fg,eth} = 841$ kJ/kg
- Distillation column efficiency: 60%
- Molecular sieve separation: removes 95% of remaining water

### Step-by-Step Deduction:

**Step 1 — Mass of ethanol in feed:**

$$m_{eth} = 10{,}000 \times 0.10 \times 0.789 = 789\;\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of water in feed:**

$$m_{water} = 10{,}000 \times 0.90 \times 1.000 = 9{,}000\;\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — To get from 10% to 95.6% v/v by distillation, compute the bottom water-removed. The mass balance (ignoring small volume changes) gives roughly 9,000 kg water must be vaporized (and re-condensed at the top, with most of it removed from the bottom).**

**Step 4 — Minimum theoretical energy (latent heat only) for water removal:**

$$Q_{min} = m_{water} \times h_{fg,water} = 9{,}000 \times 2{,}260 = 20{,}340{,}000\;\text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to MJ and divide by 3600 s/h:**

$$Q_{min} = \frac{20{,}340{,}000}{3600} = 5{,}650\;\text{kW} = 5.65\;\text{MW}$$

**Step 6 — With 60% column efficiency, real energy required:**

$$Q_{real} = \frac{5.65}{0.60} = 9.42\;\text{MW}$$

> **Energy Logic:** This is why distillation is **the single largest energy cost** in an ethanol plant (about **30-40% of total energy**). The molecular sieve step that follows needs **only about 0.5 MW** more but gives you **100% anhydrous ethanol** — a far better trade-off.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Production Routes

| Parameter | 1st Gen (Sugarcane) | 1st Gen (Corn) | 2nd Gen (Lignocellulosic) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Theoretical yield (L/ton biomass)** | ~700 | ~400 | ~360 |
| **Actual plant yield (L/ton)** | 80-100 | 380-420 | 80-120 |
| **Energy ratio (E_out/E_in)** | 8-10 | 1.3-1.8 | 2-6 |
| **Process complexity** | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Carbon footprint (g CO₂/MJ)** | ~25 | ~50 | ~10-20 (with fossil offset) |
| **Water consumption (L/L ethanol)** | 1,500-2,000 | 1,000-4,000 | 1,500-2,500 |
| **Food vs. fuel conflict** | High | High | Low (uses waste) |
| **Capital cost** | Low | Low | High |
| **Key bottleneck** | Seasonal feedstock | Pretreatment cost | Pretreatment + pentose fermentation |
| **Mature technology?** | Yes (Brazil) | Yes (USA) | Pilot/Demo |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The Indian government is actively promoting **2nd generation ethanol plants** (e.g., **Praj Industries' demo plant**, **HPCL Panipat refinery**) to use **rice straw and bagasse** for blending, addressing both agricultural waste burning and crude imports.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Flow Diagram: Biomass to Anhydrous Ethanol (1st Generation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedA[Sugarcane / Molasses] --> crush[Crushing and Juice Extraction]
    feedB[Corn / Starch Crop] --> mill[Dry Milling and Grinding]
    crush --> treat1[Juice Clarification and Dilution]
    mill --> treat2[Cooking and Liquefaction 60 to 90 C]
    treat2 --> enz1[Enzymatic Hydrolysis with Amylase]
    enz1 --> wort[Sugar Solution Wort pH 4 to 5]
    treat1 --> wort
    wort --> ferm[Fermentation with Saccharomyces cerevisiae 30 C 48 to 72 h]
    ferm --> beer[Beer or Wash 8 to 12 percent Ethanol]
    beer --> dist[Distillation Column 78 C Vaporization]
    dist --> azeo[Azeotrope 95.6 percent Ethanol]
    azeo --> sieve[Molecular Sieve Zeolite 3A Adsorbs Water]
    sieve --> anhyd[Anhydrous Ethanol 99.5 percent v per v]
    beer --> co2[CO2 Capture Stream]
    ferm --> cells[Yeast Cell Recycle]
    cells --> ferm
    anhyd --> tank[Fuel Storage E100 or Blend]
    sieve --> waterout[Water Out for Recycle]
```

## 4.2 Block Flow Diagram: Lignocellulosic Biomass to Ethanol (2nd Generation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    lc[Lignocellulosic Biomass Bagasse / Straw / Wood] --> size[Size Reduction Grinding to 2 to 5 mm]
    size --> pret[Pretreatment Dilute Acid or Steam Explosion 160 to 220 C]
    pret --> sep[Separation of Solid Cellulose and Liquid Hydrolysate]
    sep --> s1[Solid Cellulose Fraction]
    sep --> s2[Liquid C5 and C6 Sugar Hydrolysate]
    s1 --> enz2[Enzymatic Hydrolysis with Cellulase 45 to 50 C 72 h]
    enz2 --> glu[Glucose Stream C6]
    s2 --> glu
    glu --> coferm[Co Fermentation with Engineered Yeast 30 C 48 h]
    s2 --> coferm
    coferm --> wash[Ethanol Wash 5 to 8 percent]
    wash --> dist2[Distillation Column]
    dist2 --> azeo2[Azeotrope 95.6 percent]
    azeo2 --> sieve2[Molecular Sieve Dehydration]
    sieve2 --> final[Anhydrous Ethanol 99.8 percent]
    final --> blend[E10 E20 E85 Blending Station]
    pret --> lignin[Lignin Residue Boiler for Process Steam]
    lignin --> boiler[Combustion CHP Plant]
    boiler --> elec[Power and Heat Generation]
    elec --> dist2
    elec --> enz2
    elec --> coferm
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Fermentation Kinetics & Mass Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Glucose Feed S0 g per L] --> B[Monod Kinetics mu equals mu max S by Ks plus S]
    B --> C[Cell Growth dX per dt equals mu X]
    C --> D[Ethanol Formation dP per dt equals alpha mu X plus beta X]
    D --> E[Substrate Depletion dS per dt equals minus mu X by YXS]
    E --> F[Output Ethanol Stream P g per L]
    F --> G{Yield Check}
    G --> H[Actual Yield 0.45 g per g]
    G --> I[Theoretical 0.511 g per g]
    H --> J[Efficiency 88 percent]
    I --> J
```

## 4.4 Comparative Generation Matrix (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{Feedstock Type?} --> sg[Sugar Crop Sugarcane]
    start --> st[Starch Crop Corn Wheat]
    start --> lc2[Lignocellulosic Bagasse Straw]
    start --> alg[Algal Biomass]
    sg --> route1[Direct Fermentation S cerevisiae]
    st --> route2[Hydrolysis with Amylase then Fermentation]
    lc2 --> route3[Pretreatment then Enzymatic Hydrolysis then Co Fermentation]
    alg --> route4[Specialized Algal Hydrolysis then Fermentation]
    route1 --> out1[E10 E20 Blending Low Cost]
    route2 --> out2[E85 and Industrial Solvent]
    route3 --> out3[Cellulosic E85 Sustainable]
    route4 --> out4[Third Gen Pilot Stage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to draw a block diagram in exams, students often forget to **show the recycle streams** (CO₂, water, yeast, lignin-burner) and **energy integration arrows**. These are bonus marks in the 7-mark sub-question.
