<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is bioethanol? Write the balanced Gay-Lussac equation for ethanol production from glucose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Bioethanol (1 mark):** Bioethanol is ethyl alcohol (C₂H₅OH) produced by the **fermentation of sugars** derived from biomass feedstocks (sugarcane, corn, lignocellulose), used as a renewable transportation fuel and industrial chemical. It is a clear, colorless liquid with a high octane rating (RON ~108) and lower energy density (~26.8 MJ/kg) than gasoline.

**Balanced Gay-Lussac equation (2 marks):**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast (Zymase)}} 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -92\;\text{kJ/mol}$$

The reaction is **exothermic**, **anaerobic**, and catalyzed by the enzyme complex **zymase** present in *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*. 1 mole of glucose yields 2 moles of ethanol and 2 moles of CO₂.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Explain the difference between 1st generation and 2nd generation bioethanol with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | 1st Generation | 2nd Generation |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | Sugar/Starch crops | Lignocellulosic biomass |
| **Examples** | (i) Sugarcane (ii) Corn, Wheat, Cassava | (i) Rice straw (ii) Bagasse, Wood chips |
| **Sugar type** | Hexose (C6) sugars from sucrose/starch | C5 + C6 from cellulose/hemicellulose |
| **Pretreatment** | Not required (or simple hydrolysis) | Mandatory — dilute acid, steam explosion |
| **Process complexity** | Low | High (pentose fermentation is hard) |
| **Food vs. fuel** | Conflict (uses food crops) | No conflict (uses waste) |
| **Maturity** | Commercial | Pilot / Demonstration |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** 1st generation is mature but ethically controversial; 2nd generation is the **future of sustainable ethanol** but needs improved enzymes and engineered yeasts.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> *Internal Choice: Answer either Question A OR Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Compulsory Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **complete process flow** for producing **anhydrous ethanol from sugarcane molasses** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of **molecular sieves** in the dehydration step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fermentation unit processes **2000 kg/day of glucose** at a feed concentration of **150 g/L**. If the **fermentation efficiency is 90%** of theoretical and downstream **distillation + dehydration losses are 4%**, calculate:
- (i) **Theoretical ethanol yield (kg/day)**
- (ii) **Actual ethanol output (kg/day)**
- (iii) **Volumetric output (liters/day)** *(density of ethanol = 0.789 kg/L)* **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**1. Process Flow Description (5 marks):**

The anhydrous ethanol production from sugarcane molasses involves the following sequential steps:

- **Step 1 — Feedstock Preparation:** Molasses (a viscous syrup containing ~50% fermentable sugars, mostly sucrose) is diluted with water to ~15-20% sugar concentration in a mixing tank. The pH is adjusted to **4.5-5.0** using sulfuric acid.

- **Step 2 — Nutrient Addition:** Nitrogen source (urea or ammonium sulfate) and phosphates are added to support yeast growth. Sometimes vitamins and trace minerals are also supplemented.

- **Step 3 — Fermentation:** The must is inoculated with **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** yeast (3-5% by volume) and held in a fermenter at **30-35 °C** for **48-72 hours** under **anaerobic conditions**. The reaction:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$$

produces an 8-12% v/v ethanol mixture called **"beer" or "wash."** CO₂ is vented and can be captured.

- **Step 4 — Distillation:** The wash is fed to a **beer column** (multi-tray distillation column) operating near atmospheric pressure. Ethanol vaporizes at **78.4 °C** and is condensed, yielding **~95% v/v ethanol** — limited by the **ethanol-water azeotrope at 95.6% v/v**.

- **Step 5 — Molecular Sieve Dehydration:** The 95% azeotrope is passed through a packed bed of **3Å zeolite molecular sieves**. The pores (3 Angstroms = 0.3 nm) are small enough to admit **water molecules (0.27 nm)** but exclude **ethanol molecules (0.44 nm)**. Water is selectively adsorbed, and the effluent is **>99.5% v/v anhydrous ethanol**. The sieves are regenerated periodically by applying **vacuum and heat** to desorb the trapped water.

**2. Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to **Section 4.1** of the notes (Sugarcane route). Student should sketch the chain: Molasses → Dilution → Fermenter → Beer Column → Molecular Sieve → Anhydrous Ethanol.

**3. Role of Molecular Sieves (1 mark):** They break the azeotrope barrier of 95.6% v/v by **size-selective adsorption of water**, enabling production of fuel-grade anhydrous ethanol without using entrainers (like benzene) that are toxic and environmentally hazardous.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Glucose feed: $m_{glucose} = 2000$ kg/day
- Theoretical yield: $Y_{theo} = 0.511$ kg ethanol / kg glucose
- Fermentation efficiency: $\eta_{ferm} = 0.90$
- Distillation + dehydration losses: 4% (i.e., recovery = 0.96)

**(i) Theoretical Ethanol Yield (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{ethanol,\,theoretical} &= m_{glucose} \times Y_{theo} \\
&= 2000\;\text{kg/day} \times 0.511 \\
&= 1022\;\text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Actual Ethanol Output (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{ethanol,\,actual} &= m_{ethanol,\,theoretical} \times \eta_{ferm} \times 0.96 \\
&= 1022 \times 0.90 \times 0.96 \\
&= 883.0\;\text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Volumetric Output (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ethanol} &= \frac{m_{ethanol,\,actual}}{\rho_{ethanol}} \\
&= \frac{883.0}{0.789} \\
&= 1119.1\;\text{L/day} \approx 1119\;\text{L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Box:**
- Theoretical yield = **1022 kg/day**
- Actual output = **883.0 kg/day**
- Volume = **~1119 L/day**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Alternative Internal Choice]**

**(a)** What is **lignocellulosic biomass**? List its **three main polymeric components** with their **approximate percentages**. Explain why **pretreatment is essential** before enzymatic hydrolysis. Describe the **steam explosion method** in 5 lines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **CO₂ emitted per liter of ethanol** produced from fermentation. Given: theoretical yield = 0.511 g ethanol / g glucose, density of ethanol = 0.789 g/mL, M(CO₂) = 44 g/mol, M(ethanol) = 46 g/mol. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Lignocellulosic biomass refers to **plant dry matter** composed of three major biopolymers — cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin — forming a complex, recalcitrant structure. It includes agricultural residues (rice straw, wheat straw, bagasse, corn stover), forestry residues, energy crops (switchgrass, miscanthus), and municipal solid waste.

**2. Components (1.5 marks):**

| Component | % in Dry Biomass | Structure | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cellulose** | 40-50% | Linear β-1,4-glucan polymer | Crystalline, provides strength |
| **Hemicellulose** | 20-30% | Branched heteropolymer (xylan, mannan) | Amorphous, crosslinks cellulose & lignin |
| **Lignin** | 20-30% | Aromatic polymer (phenylpropanoid) | Waterproof, rigid, recalcitrant |

**3. Why Pretreatment is Essential (2 marks):**
- **Lignin barrier:** Lignin physically shields cellulose microfibrils, preventing enzyme access.
- **Cellulose crystallinity:** Native cellulose is highly crystalline, making β-1,4 bonds inaccessible to cellulase.
- **Hemicellulose crosslinking:** Hemicellulose tethers cellulose to lignin, forming a 3D shield.
- Without pretreatment, enzymatic hydrolysis yields are often **<20% of theoretical**; with pretreatment, yields can reach **80-90%**.

**4. Steam Explosion (2.5 marks):**
- Biomass is soaked in dilute acid (or pure water) and heated with **saturated steam to 160-220 °C** for **1-15 minutes** in a sealed pressure vessel.
- Sudden **rapid decompression** (explosion) flashes the water to steam, **shearing the fiber structure**, **rupturing cell walls**, and **partially hydrolyzing hemicellulose** into xylose and other sugars.
- The exploded biomass is collected, washed, and sent to enzymatic hydrolysis.
- **Advantages:** No chemicals (or minimal), effective lignin redistribution, scalable. **Disadvantages:** Generates inhibitors (furfural, HMF) that must be detoxified before fermentation.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- 1 g glucose → 0.511 g ethanol + 0.489 g CO₂ (theoretical)
- Density of ethanol = 0.789 g/mL
- M(CO₂) = 44 g/mol, M(ethanol) = 46 g/mol

**Step 1 — Mass of ethanol per liter:**

$$m_{eth/L} = 1000\;\text{mL} \times 0.789\;\text{g/mL} = 789\;\text{g ethanol / L}$$

**Step 2 — Glucose consumed per liter of ethanol (theoretical):**

$$m_{glucose/L} = \frac{789}{0.511} = 1544\;\text{g glucose / L ethanol}$$

**Step 3 — CO₂ mass per liter of ethanol:**

$$m_{CO_2/L} = m_{glucose/L} \times 0.489 = 1544 \times 0.489 = 755.0\;\text{g CO}_2 / \text{L}$$

**Step 4 — CO₂ moles per liter:**

$$n_{CO_2/L} = \frac{755.0}{44} = 17.16\;\text{mol CO}_2 / \text{L ethanol}$$

**Final Answer Box:**
- **CO₂ emitted per liter of ethanol = 755 g ≈ 0.755 kg (theoretical)**
- **= 17.16 mol per liter**

> **Significance:** This is **biogenic CO₂** (carbon-neutral if the biomass regrew and absorbed the same amount of CO₂ during photosynthesis) — a key reason ethanol is considered a **carbon-neutral fuel** in LCA studies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Wrong theoretical yield value:** Students often write **0.46** or **0.54** — both are **WRONG**. The correct theoretical yield is **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** from the Gay-Lussac equation. **(-1 mark)**
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Always write "**g ethanol/g glucose**" — not just "**0.511**" alone. **(-0.5 mark)**
> 3. **Azeotrope confusion:** Many students think distillation alone can give anhydrous ethanol. It **CANNOT** — you need a **molecular sieve** or **azeotropic distillation** to break the **95.6% azeotrope**. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Pretreatment skipped:** In 2nd-gen ethanol answers, students often jump from biomass directly to fermentation, forgetting the **pretreatment + enzymatic hydrolysis** steps. **(-2 marks)**
> 5. **Confusing fermentation microorganisms:** Always specify **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** for hexoses; **Zymomonas mobilis** or **engineered yeasts** for pentoses — don't just write "yeast." **(-0.5 mark)**
> 6. **Energy density mismatch:** Ethanol = **26.8 MJ/kg**, gasoline = **~44 MJ/kg**. Do not write 30 or 35. **(-0.5 mark)**
> 7. **Not labeling block diagrams:** In process flow questions, label each block with **temperature, pH, and residence time**. Generic boxes lose marks. **(-1 mark)**

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Core Reaction:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$ (Gay-Lussac) — memorize as the **first line** of any ethanol answer.

✅ **Theoretical Yield:** **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** (or equivalently, **51.1% by mass**). CO₂ co-yield = **0.489 g/g**.

✅ **Molecular Weights:** Glucose = **180.16**, Ethanol = **46.07**, CO₂ = **44.01** — these are constants you'll use in every problem.

✅ **Azeotrope:** Ethanol-water forms an azeotrope at **95.6% v/v ethanol, 78.2 °C**. Cannot be broken by simple distillation. Solution: **molecular sieves (3Å zeolite)**.

✅ **Three Generations:**
  - **1st Gen:** Sugar & starch crops (sugarcane, corn). Mature, food-vs-fuel conflict.
  - **2nd Gen:** Lignocellulosic biomass (straw, bagasse, wood). Sustainable, requires pretreatment + enzymatic hydrolysis.
  - **3rd Gen:** Algal biomass. Pilot stage.

✅ **Lignocellulosic Composition:** Cellulose (40-50%) + Hemicellulose (20-30%) + Lignin (20-30%).

✅ **Pretreatment Methods (any 2-3 to list):** Dilute acid, Steam explosion, Organosolv, AFEX, Wet oxidation, Ionic liquids.

✅ **Fermentation Conditions:** *S. cerevisiae*, **30-35 °C**, **pH 4-5**, **48-72 h**, **anaerobic**, yields **8-12% v/v ethanol** in wash.

✅ **Distillation Energy:** Largest energy cost — about **30-40%** of total plant energy. ~9-10 MJ per liter of ethanol.

✅ **Energy Density:** Ethanol = **26.8 MJ/kg** (60% of gasoline). Octane = **RON 108-110**.

✅ **India's EBP Target:** **E20 blending by 2025-2030**. Watch for **Panipat 2G plant**, **Praj Industries** demos.

✅ **Co-Products:** DDGS (animal feed), CO₂ (industrial), Lignin (combustion for process heat) — revenue + sustainability.

✅ **Key Process Innovations:** **SSF** (Simultaneous Saccharification & Fermentation) — saves time and cost; **CBP** (Consolidated Bioprocessing) — combines enzyme production, hydrolysis, and fermentation in one reactor.

✅ **Exam Buzzwords to Use:** *Renewable, carbon-neutral, biorefinery, fermentation efficiency, theoretical yield, azeotrope, molecular sieve, pretreatment, enzymatic hydrolysis, simultaneous saccharification and fermentation (SSF).*

✅ **Avoid these mistakes:** Wrong yield (0.46 or 0.54), missing block-diagram labels, skipping pretreatment in 2G answers, saying "yeast" without naming the species.

> 🎯 **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** Always start a biomass-to-ethanol answer with **(1) the Gay-Lussac equation**, **(2) the theoretical yield value (0.511)**, and **(3) a clear process block diagram**. This sets the tone for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biogas Production from Waste Biomass** is the controlled biochemical conversion of organic waste materials into a combustible gas mixture (predominantly **methane, CH₄**, and **carbon dioxide, CO₂**) through a multi-stage microbial process called **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)**, executed in the strict absence of molecular oxygen (O₂).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the process is classified as a **biochemical energy conversion route** under **biomass-based renewable energy systems**, distinct from thermochemical routes (combustion, pyrolysis, gasification) and physico-chemical routes (esterification, hydrolysis).

### Generalised Chemical Representation

$$
\text{Organic Waste} \xrightarrow{\text{Anaerobic Microbes, } \Delta T, \text{pH } 6.8\text{–}7.5} \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### Standard Composition of Raw Biogas

| Constituent | Chemical Symbol | Typical Volumetric Range |
|-------------|:---------------:|:------------------------:|
| Methane | $\text{CH}_4$ | **50% – 70%** |
| Carbon Dioxide | $\text{CO}_2$ | **30% – 40%** |
| Hydrogen Sulphide | $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ | **0.1% – 0.5%** |
| Nitrogen | $\text{N}_2$ | 0.5% – 2% |
| Water Vapour | $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Saturated |
| Calorific Value | $\text{MJ/m}^3$ | **20 – 25 MJ/m³** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **💡 Intuitive Picture (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine a sealed kitchen **pressure cooker** filled with vegetable peels, cow dung, and rice straw, locked in a warm corner of your house for 30 days. Microscopic "workers" (bacteria) inside, working **without air**, slowly chew up the waste. They release two byproducts: a flammable gas you can burn for cooking (biogas), and a nutrient-rich black liquid you can pour on plants (digestate / slurry). The pressure cooker is the **digester**, the warmth is the **mesophilic temperature**, and the sealed lid is the **absence of oxygen**.

This analogy maps directly to engineering terms:

* **"Sealed environment"** → **Anaerobic digester** with airtight gas collection dome.
* **"Microscopic workers"** → **Methanogenic archaea** (the slowest, most oxygen-sensitive group).
* **"Locked in a warm corner"** → Operating in the **mesophilic range (30 °C – 40 °C)** or **thermophilic range (50 °C – 60 °C)**.
* **"Vegetable peels, dung, straw"** → **Feedstock** with optimal **C/N ratio of 20:1 – 30:1**.
* **"30 days"** → **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** of 15 – 40 days.

---

## 1.3 Why Biogas? — The Engineering Drivers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in the KTU 2024 Energy syllabus:**
> Biogas closes the **carbon-neutral loop** of organic waste — converting a pollutant into a dispatchable renewable fuel. Unlike solar PV or wind, biogas is **storable** and can be burned on demand, providing **base-load** renewable energy with an **estimated global substitution potential of 18% of final energy consumption**.

The three pillars justifying biogas in a B.Tech energy portfolio are:

1. **Pollution Abatement** – Reduces **methane fugitive emissions** from open dumps (CH₄ has 28× the Global Warming Potential of CO₂ over 100 years).
2. **Energy Recovery** – Each kg of dry cattle dung yields **~0.20 – 0.35 m³** of biogas.
3. **Nutrient Recycling** – The slurry (digestate) returns **N, P, K** to the soil as biofertilizer.

# 🟢 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Biochemical Stages of Anaerobic Digestion

The conversion of complex biopolymers (cellulose, proteins, lipids) to methane is **not a single reaction**, but a tightly coupled **microbial food-chain** of four sequential stages. Each stage is governed by a distinct bacterial guild, and the rate-limiting step dictates the overall digester volume.

### Stage 1 — Hydrolysis

* **Inputs:** Complex polymers — cellulose, hemicellulose, proteins, lipids, starches.
* **Microbes:** *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Streptococcus*, facultative anaerobes.
* **Reaction Type:** Extracellular enzymatic breakdown using **hydrolases, proteases, lipases**.
* **Outputs:** Monomers — sugars, amino acids, long-chain fatty acids (LCFAs), glycerol.
* **Engineering Note:** This is the **rate-limiting step for lignocellulosic biomass** (rice straw, bagasse) due to lignin sheath protection.
* **Typical Duration:** Slow — extends overall retention time.

### Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation)

* **Inputs:** Monomers from hydrolysis.
* **Microbes:** *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, facultative anaerobes.
* **Outputs:** Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs — acetate, propionate, butyrate), alcohols, **H₂, CO₂**.
* **Engineering Concern:** Rapid acidification can drop pH below **6.0**, killing methanogens → **sour digester syndrome**.

### Stage 3 — Acetogenesis

* **Inputs:** VFAs (other than acetate) + alcohols.
* **Microbes:** Obligate proton reducers — *Syntrophobacter*, *Syntrophomonas*.
* **Outputs:** Acetate, H₂, CO₂.
* **Critical Coupling:** These microbes are thermodynamically unfavourable unless methanogens **immediately consume** the H₂ produced — this is **interspecies hydrogen transfer**.

### Stage 4 — Methanogenesis

* **Inputs:** Acetate, H₂ + CO₂.
* **Microbes:** Strict anaerobes — *Methanosarcina*, *Methanosaeta* (acetoclastic), *Methanobacterium* (hydrogenotrophic).
* **Outputs:** **CH₄ (≈ 70%) + CO₂ (≈ 30%)**.
* **Engineering Note:** **Acetoclastic methanogenesis accounts for ~70% of total CH₄ produced**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind the chain:** Each guild specialises in one reaction type, but their products become the next guild's substrate. This division of labour maximises thermodynamic efficiency. Killing any one guild collapses the entire chain.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **🔖 High-Yield Formula Bank — Memorise these for ESE.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4$ | **Buswell Equation** — stoichiometric CH₄ yield from any organic formula | mol basis |
| 2 | $Y_{\text{CH}_4} = \dfrac{0.415 \times \text{COD}_{\text{removed}}}{\text{kg VS}_{\text{added}}}$ | Methane yield from Chemical Oxygen Demand balance | $\text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4$ |
| 3 | $\text{C/N optimum} = 20 : 1 \text{ to } 30 : 1$ | Substrate nutrient ratio for stable digestion | dimensionless |

| 9 | $E_{\text{biogas}} = V_{\text{biogas}} \times 21\ \text{MJ/m}^3$ | Energy content of raw biogas | MJ |
| 10 | $\text{pH}_{\text{optimum, methanogens}} = 6.8 - 7.5$ | Process stability window | dimensionless |

* **KVIC Plants (India):** ~5 lakh family-size biogas plants deployed; each substitutes **2 – 3 LPG cylinders/year**.
* **Co-generation plants (Germany):** Maize silage digesters producing **2 MW electricity** from agricultural waste.
* **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW):** Bengaluru's **8,000 m³/day Kadubeesanahalli plant** — generates 4.5 MW power from landfill waste.

# 🟢 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Buswell Equation — Complete Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most-tested derivation** in KTU Renewable Energy papers on biogas. Memorise it.

### Step 1 — Define the General Stoichiometry

We want to find coefficients $x$, $y$, $z$, $w$ such that a generic organic substrate $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b$ converts stoichiometrically to $\text{CH}_4$ and $\text{CO}_2$, with water potentially consumed or released:

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + x\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow y\,\text{CH}_4 + z\,\text{CO}_2
$$

### Step 2 — Write Elemental Balance Equations

**Carbon Balance (C):**
Atoms in = Atoms out

$$
n = y + z \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1)
$$

**Hydrogen Balance (H):**

$$
a + 2x = 4y \quad \Rightarrow \quad (2)
$$

**Oxygen Balance (O):**

$$
b + x = 2z \quad \Rightarrow \quad (3)
$$

### Step 3 — Solve the System of Three Linear Equations

From equation (3):

$$
x = 2z - b \quad \Rightarrow \quad (3')
$$

Substitute into equation (2):

$$
a + 2(2z - b) = 4y
$$

$$
a + 4z - 2b = 4y \quad \Rightarrow \quad (4)
$$

From equation (1):

$$
z = n - y
$$

Substitute into (4):

$$
a + 4(n - y) - 2b = 4y
$$

$$
a + 4n - 4y - 2b = 4y
$$

$$
a + 4n - 2b = 8y
$$

Solve for $y$ (coefficient of $\text{CH}_4$):

$$
y = \dfrac{a + 4n - 2b}{8} = \dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}
$$

Solve for $z$ using $z = n - y$:

$$
z = n - \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right) = \dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}
$$

Solve for $x$ using $x = 2z - b$:

$$
x = 2\left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right) - b = n - \dfrac{a}{4} + \dfrac{b}{2} - b = n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Balanced Buswell Equation

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Glucose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$)

Here, $n = 6$, $a = 12$, $b = 6$.

**Compute $y$ (CH₄ coefficient):**

$$
y = \dfrac{6}{2} + \dfrac{12}{8} - \dfrac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3
$$

**Compute $z$ (CO₂ coefficient):**

$$
z = \dfrac{6}{2} - \dfrac{12}{8} + \dfrac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3
$$

**Compute $x$ (H₂O coefficient):**

$$
x = 6 - \dfrac{12}{4} - \dfrac{6}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0
$$

**Balanced Reaction:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CH}_4 + 3\,\text{CO}_2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **✅ Verification:** Glucose needs **no water** for the reaction, and yields **3 mol CH₄** per mole of glucose. Since 1 mole of any gas at STP = 22.4 L, the theoretical methane yield = $3 \times 22.4 = 67.2\ \text{L CH}_4$ per mole of glucose.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Cattle Dung Approximation (Cellulose proxy: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5$)

Here, $n = 6$, $a = 10$, $b = 5$.

**Compute $y$:**

$$
y = 3 + \dfrac{10}{8} - \dfrac{5}{4} = 3 + 1.25 - 1.25 = 3
$$

**Compute $z$:**

$$
z = 3 - 1.25 + 1.25 = 3
$$

**Compute $x$:**

$$
x = 6 - 2.5 - 2.5 = 1
$$

**Balanced Reaction:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CH}_4 + 3\,\text{CO}_2
$$

**Mass balance check (kg per kmol):** LHS = $162 + 18 = 180$ kg; RHS = $3(16 + 44) = 180$ kg. ✅

**Methane yield per kg of cellulose:**

$$
\dfrac{3 \times 22.4}{162} = 0.415\ \text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{kg cellulose}
$$

---

## 3.4 Energy Yield Calculation from Biogas

**Given:** A digester produces $V = 10\ \text{m}^3$ biogas/day at $\text{CH}_4$ fraction $f = 0.6$.

**Step 1 — Methane volume produced per day:**

$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = f \times V = 0.6 \times 10 = 6\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 2 — Methane mass per day (using $\rho_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.717\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at STP):**

$$
m_{\text{CH}_4} = 6 \times 0.717 = 4.302\ \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy content per day (using $LHV_{\text{CH}_4} = 50\ \text{MJ/kg}$):**

$$
E = 4.302 \times 50 = 215.1\ \text{MJ/day} \approx 59.75\ \text{kWh/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent LPG substitution (LHV = 46 MJ/kg):**

$$
m_{\text{LPG}} = \dfrac{215.1}{46} \approx 4.68\ \text{kg LPG/day}
$$

---

# 🟢 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3 | 3 Marks]**
*Define biogas. List its main constituents with their typical volumetric percentage.*

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Biogas is a combustible gas mixture produced by the **anaerobic digestion** of organic waste biomass in the absence of oxygen.

| Constituent | % by Volume |
|-------------|:-----------:|
| Methane ($\text{CH}_4$) | **50 – 70%** |
| Carbon Dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) | **30 – 40%** |
| Hydrogen Sulphide ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$) | 0.1 – 0.5% |
| Nitrogen, Water Vapour | Trace |

Its calorific value is **20 – 25 MJ/m³**.

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3 | 3 Marks]**
*What is the optimum C/N ratio for anaerobic digestion? What happens if C/N is too low?*

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The optimum **C/N ratio is 20:1 to 30:1**.

* If **C/N is too low** (excess nitrogen): ammonium ($\text{NH}_4^+$) accumulates → **pH rises above 8.5** → toxic to methanogens → ammonia inhibition → **process failure**.
* If **C/N is too high** (excess carbon): nitrogen becomes limiting → microbial growth slows → **low biogas yield**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B Module questions are 14 marks with **internal choice** (either A or B). Each typically splits as **7 + 7 marks** across two sub-parts.

---

### Question B (Option A) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the four stages of anaerobic digestion of biomass with chemical equations for each stage. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A community biogas plant receives **200 kg/day** of cattle dung with 80% total solids (TS) and 70% volatile solids (VS). If the digester operates at HRT = 30 days and OLR = **2.0 kg VS/m³·day**, calculate (i) digester volume, (ii) daily VS loading, (iii) theoretical methane yield at 0.40 m³ CH₄/kg VS. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Four Stages (7 Marks):**

**Stage 1 — Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers broken to monomers:

$$
(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5)_n + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{cellulase}} n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6
$$

**Stage 2 — Acidogenesis** — Glucose to volatile fatty acids:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{fermentation}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 \uparrow
$$

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}
$$

**Stage 3 — Acetogenesis** — Propionate to acetate + H₂:

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH + 2H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2
$$

**Stage 4 — Methanogenesis** — Two pathways:

* **Acetoclastic (70%):**

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2
$$

* **Hydrogenotrophic (30%):**

$$
\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**

**(i) Daily VS loading:**

$$
\text{VS}_{\text{daily}} = 200 \times 0.80 \times 0.70 = 112\ \text{kg VS/day}
$$

**(ii) Digester volume from OLR:**

$$
V_d = \dfrac{\text{VS}_{\text{daily}}}{\text{OLR}} = \dfrac{112}{2.0} = 56\ \text{m}^3
$$

**(iii) Theoretical methane yield:**

$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = 112 \times 0.40 = 44.8\ \text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{day}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse TS and VS.** TS = total solids (inorganic + organic). VS = organic fraction that actually digests. Multiply TS first, then by VS fraction — NOT the other way around.
> 2. **OLR units must match.** If VS/day divided by OLR $\text{kg VS/m}^3\cdot\text{d}$, the result is **volume in m³**.
> 3. **Always state the basis** — the methane yield is per **kg VS added**, not per kg of raw dung.

---

### Question B (Option B) — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern, Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Compare the **KVIC floating drum** and **Janata fixed dome** biogas plants on the basis of construction, gas pressure, cost, gas storage, and maintenance. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Buswell equation** for the general substrate $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b$ and apply it to **acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$)**. Compute the volumetric methane yield at STP. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Criterion | KVIC Floating Drum | Janata Fixed Dome |
|-----------|:------------------:|:-----------------:|
| Gas Holder | Floating mild-steel drum, visible | Fixed masonry dome, underground |
| Gas Pressure | **Constant** (weight of drum) | **Variable** (rises with gas volume) |
| Construction | Skilled labour, welding | Locally available masonry |
| Cost | **Higher** (~₹15,000 – ₹25,000 for 2 m³) | **Lower** (~₹8,000 – ₹15,000) |
| Gas Storage | Built into the drum | Built into the dome |
| Maintenance | Drum requires anti-rust paint every 2 years | Almost nil for 25+ years |
| Lifespan | 15 – 20 years | 25 – 30 years |
| Gas Yield | Comparable | Comparable |

**(b) Buswell Derivation for Acetic Acid (7 Marks):**

Acetic acid formula: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} = \text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}_2$ → $n = 2$, $a = 4$, $b = 2$.

**CH₄ coefficient:**

$$
y = \dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} = 1 + 0.5 - 0.5 = 1
$$

**CO₂ coefficient:**

$$
z = \dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} = 1 - 0.5 + 0.5 = 1
$$

**Water coefficient:**

$$
x = n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} = 2 - 1 - 1 = 0
$$

**Balanced Reaction:**

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2
$$

**Volumetric methane yield at STP (M = 60 g/mol for acetic acid):**

$$
\dfrac{1 \times 22.4\ \text{L}}{60\ \text{g}} = 0.373\ \text{L CH}_4/\text{g acetic acid}
$$

$$
= 0.373\ \text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{kg acetic acid}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't double-count carbon atoms.** Acetic acid = $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}_2$, NOT $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}_2$ (free acid form has 4 H, not 6 — the OH hydrogen is counted but the structure has $\text{C-H}_3$ and $\text{C-OH}$).
> 2. **The stoichiometric ratio is fixed**: 1 mol acetic acid yields exactly 1 mol CH₄. Deviations in real digesters reflect **kinetic limitations**, not thermodynamics.
> 3. **STP convention**: 1 mole gas = 22.4 L at **0 °C, 1 atm**. If the problem says NTP (25 °C), use **24.5 L/mol**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Biogas Composition:** $\text{CH}_4$ 50 – 70%, $\text{CO}_2$ 30 – 40%, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ 0.1 – 0.5%, trace $\text{N}_2$ + $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ vapour.
- **Calorific Value:** **20 – 25 MJ/m³** (raw biogas); ~50 MJ/kg for pure methane.
- **Optimum Operating Parameters:** $\text{pH} = 6.8 - 7.5$; $\text{C/N ratio} = 20:1 - 30:1$; $\text{HRT} = 15 - 40$ days (mesophilic) or 12 – 15 days (thermophilic).
- **Three Temperature Regimes:** Psychrophilic (<20 °C), **Mesophilic (30 – 40 °C) — most common**, Thermophilic (50 – 60 °C).
- **Four Biochemical Stages:** **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.** Each governed by distinct microbial guilds.
- **Rate-Limiting Step:** **Hydrolysis for lignocellulosic biomass**; **Methanogenesis for readily soluble substrates**.
- **~70% of total methane is acetoclastic** (from acetate); ~30% is hydrogenotrophic (from $\text{H}_2$ + $\text{CO}_2$).
- **Buswell Equation:** $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + (n - a/4 - b/2)\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow (n/2 + a/8 - b/4)\,\text{CH}_4 + (n/2 - a/8 + b/4)\,\text{CO}_2$.
- **Theoretical methane yield:** **0.415 m³ CH₄ per kg VS** for carbohydrates; ~0.50 m³/kg for proteins; ~1.0 m³/kg for lipids.
- **Indian Digester Designs:** **KVIC** (floating drum), **Janata** (fixed dome), **Deenbandhu** (low-cost fixed dome).
- **Critical Operational Hazards:** Ammonia inhibition (C/N too low), VFA accumulation (organic overload → pH crash), $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ corrosion (use iron-oxide scrubbers).
- **Renewable Dispatchability:** Unlike solar/wind, biogas is **storable and on-demand** — ideal for **base-load renewable** applications.
- **CH₄'s Global Warming Potential = 28 × CO₂** over 100 years → biogas capture from waste = **climate mitigation strategy**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Biogas Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT632 - RENEWABLE ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Biogas Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biogas Generation** is the biochemical process of converting organic biomass into a combustible gas mixture (primarily CH₄ ≈ 50–70% and CO₂ ≈ 30–40%, with trace H₂S, NH₃, and water vapor) through **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** in the absence of oxygen, mediated by a synergistic consortium of hydrolytic, acidogenic, acetogenic, and methanogenic microorganisms.

The **efficiency and rate of biogas generation** are governed by a set of **environmental, operational, and biochemical factors** that must be controlled within narrow optima to ensure stable digester operation and maximum methane yield. These factors collectively determine the **Volumetric Methane Yield (VMY)**, the **Methane Production Rate (MPR)**, and the **Biochemical Methane Potential (BMP)** of the feedstock.

### 1.2 Key Parameters at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary (Critical) Factors affecting Biogas Generation:**
> 1. **Temperature** (Mesophilic ≈ 30–40 °C; Thermophilic ≈ 50–60 °C)
> 2. **pH** (Optimum ≈ 6.8–7.5 for methanogens)
> 3. **Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C/N) Ratio** (Optimum ≈ 20:1 to 30:1)
> 4. **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** (15–40 days for mesophilic)
> 5. **Total Solids (TS) / Moisture Content** (Wet < 10% TS; Dry > 20% TS)
> 6. **Volatile Solids (VS) / Organic Loading Rate (OLR)**
> 7. **Mixing / Agitation**
> 8. **Toxicity / Inhibitors** (NH₃, H₂S, heavy metals, VFAs)
> 9. **Particle Size / Substrate Pre-treatment**
> 10. **Inoculum-to-Substrate Ratio (ISR)**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Fermentation" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine biogas generation as a four-course dinner party for bacteria:**
> - **Stage 1 (Hydrolysis)** is like a **chef chopping large vegetables** into small pieces — complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken down by extracellular enzymes (amylases, proteases, lipases) into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids).
> - **Stage 2 (Acidogenesis)** is the **sommelier** who ferments these into acids and alcohol — fast-growing acidogenic bacteria produce **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs)**, H₂, and CO₂.
> - **Stage 3 (Acetogenesis)** is the **translator** who converts VFAs into acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂ — acetogens work under low H₂ partial pressure.
> - **Stage 4 (Methanogenesis)** is the **head chef (Archaea)** who finally cooks up CH₄ using acetate or H₂/CO₂ — methanogens are **slow, picky, and extremely sensitive to pH and temperature**.

**Just like a dinner party, if any one of these "guests" is uncomfortable (wrong pH, wrong temperature, too much acid, or no food of the right kind), the entire meal — and hence the entire biogas yield — collapses!**

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Methane Density at STP:** ρ_CH₄ ≈ **0.717 kg/m³**
> - **Calorific Value of Methane:** LHV ≈ **50.0 MJ/kg** ≈ **35.8 MJ/m³**
> - **Standard Theoretical Biogas Yield:** ≈ **0.5 m³ CH₄ per kg VS destroyed** (well-mixed CSTR model)
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Mesophilic Optimum:** **35 °C (308 K)**
> - **Thermophilic Optimum:** **55 °C (328 K)**
> - **Psycho-trophic Optimum:** **≤ 20 °C (293 K)**

### 1.5 The 14 Most-Cited Factors in KTU Examinations

| # | Factor | Symbol / Unit | Quick Optimum |
|---|--------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Temperature | T [°C or K] | 35–40 (mesophilic) |
| 2 | pH (Acidity) | pH [-] | 6.8–7.5 |
| 3 | Alkalinity | mg/L as CaCO₃ | 2000–5000 |
| 4 | VFA-to-Alkalinity Ratio | [-] | < 0.4 |
| 5 | C/N Ratio | [-] | 20:1 – 30:1 |
| 6 | Hydraulic Retention Time | HRT [days] | 15–40 |
| 7 | Solid Retention Time | SRT [days] | ≥ HRT |
| 8 | Organic Loading Rate | OLR [kg VS/m³·d] | 1.0–4.0 |
| 9 | Total Solids Content | TS [%] | 8–12 (wet); 20–35 (dry) |
| 10 | Volatile Solids | VS [% of TS] | 70–90 |
| 11 | Mixing Intensity | [rpm or m/s] | Mild, continuous |
| 12 | Particle Size | d [mm] | < 5 (finely shredded) |
| 13 | Free Ammonia | NH₃ [mg/L] | < 80 (inhibitory above) |
| 14 | Inoculum Ratio | ISR [VS basis] | 1:1 – 2:1 |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Factor #1 — Temperature (T)

**Operational Logic:**
- Methanogenic Archaea are **highly temperature-sensitive**; their metabolic rate approximately **doubles for every 10 °C rise** (Q₁₀ rule) up to their denaturation point.
- Below **10 °C** → reaction is virtually **arrested** (psychrophilic regime).
- Between **30–40 °C** → **mesophilic regime** — robust, low energy input, best stability.
- Between **50–60 °C** → **thermophilic regime** — faster reaction, higher pathogen kill, but **sensitive to ammonia toxicity and VFA accumulation**.
- Above **65 °C** → **thermophilic denaturation** — methanogens die.

**Why it matters in engineering:**
Temperature governs the **kinetic rate constant** k of the methanation step. Even a 5 °C drop from 35 °C to 30 °C can **reduce gas production by 25–30%**, while rising to 55 °C can boost gas production but only if the digester is well buffered.

**Arrhenius-type Temperature Dependence:**

$$k = A \cdot e^{-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T}}$$

where:
- $k$ = reaction rate constant [day⁻¹]
- $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor [day⁻¹]
- $E_a$ = activation energy [J/mol] (≈ 60–80 kJ/mol for methanogenesis)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature [K]

**Q₁₀ Rule of Thumb:**

$$Q_{10} = \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

For methanogenesis, **Q₁₀ ≈ 2 to 3** between 20–40 °C.

### 2.2 Factor #2 — pH and Alkalinity

**Operational Logic:**
- **Acidogens** thrive at pH 5.5–6.5.
- **Methanogens** are killed below pH 6.3 and above pH 8.0.
- A **balanced digester** holds pH between **6.8 and 7.5** (near neutral).
- **Alkalinity** (buffering capacity, mostly from NH₄HCO₃ and CO₃²⁻) prevents catastrophic acidification when VFAs spike.

**The Two Critical Acid-Base Equilibria:**

$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{HCO}_3^-$$

$$\text{NH}_4^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_3 + \text{H}^+$$

**VFA-to-Alkalinity Ratio (Dobb's Stability Indicator):**

$$\text{Stability Index} = \frac{\text{VFA (mg/L as acetic acid)}}{\text{Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)}}$$

- If **Ratio < 0.4** → Digester is **stable**.
- If **Ratio > 0.8** → Digester is **failing** (acidification imminent).

### 2.3 Factor #3 — C/N Ratio

**Operational Logic:**
- **Carbon** is the energy source (food for microbes).
- **Nitrogen** is needed to build microbial protein.
- If **C/N is too high** (> 35:1) → nitrogen becomes **limiting**, microbes starve, gas yield drops.
- If **C/N is too low** (< 15:1) → excess nitrogen releases **free ammonia (NH₃)**, which is **toxic to methanogens**.
- **Optimum: 20:1 to 30:1** for cattle-dung-based digesters.

**C/N Ratio Calculation:**

$$\text{C/N} = \frac{\text{Total Carbon Content [%]}}{\text{Total Nitrogen Content [%]}}$$

**C/N of Common Feedstocks (Engineer's Quick Reference):**

| Substrate | C/N Ratio | Comment |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| Cattle dung | 20–25:1 | Ideal alone |
| Poultry droppings | 8–10:1 | Nitrogen-rich, must be co-digested |
| Rice straw | 60–80:1 | Carbon-rich, needs N-supplementation |
| Food waste | 15–20:1 | Slightly low, easy to co-digest |
| Sewage sludge | 8–12:1 | Highly N-rich |
| Kitchen waste | 10–15:1 | Add carbon-rich cosubstrate |

**Co-digestion Mixing Rule:**

$$\text{C/N}_{\text{mix}} = \frac{\sum_{i} (M_i \cdot C_i)}{\sum_{i} (M_i \cdot N_i)}$$

where $M_i$ = mass of substrate $i$, $C_i$ = its carbon content, $N_i$ = its nitrogen content.

### 2.4 Factor #4 — Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)

**Operational Logic:**
- **HRT** is the average time the feed stays inside the digester.
- It must be **long enough** for the slowest step (methanogenesis) to complete, otherwise the washout of methanogens occurs.
- For **CSTR** (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor) at mesophilic conditions, **HRT = SRT** (no biomass retention).

**HRT Formula:**

$$\text{HRT} = \frac{V_{\text{digester}}}{Q} \quad [\text{days}]$$

where:
- $V_{\text{digester}}$ = effective digester volume [m³]
- $Q$ = daily feed flow rate [m³/day]

**For a typical 35 °C mesophilic digester:**

| Substrate | Minimum HRT |
|-----------|-------------|
| Cattle dung (raw) | 25–40 days |
| Pig slurry | 15–25 days |
| Sewage sludge | 20–30 days |
| Food waste (high-solids) | 30–50 days |
| Thermophilic (55 °C) | 12–20 days |

### 2.5 Factor #5 — Organic Loading Rate (OLR) and VS

**Operational Logic:**
- OLR is the **mass of volatile solids fed per unit digester volume per day**.
- Too low OLR → underutilization (poor economics).
- Too high OLR → acid shock, methanogen washout, process failure.

**OLR Formula:**

$$\text{OLR} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg VS}}{\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{day}}\right]$$

where:
- $Q$ = feed flow rate [m³/day]
- $S_0$ = feed VS concentration [kg VS/m³]
- $V$ = digester volume [m³]

**Alternative expression in terms of HRT and TS:**

$$\text{OLR} = \frac{(\text{TS fraction}) \cdot (\text{VS fraction of TS})}{\text{HRT}} \cdot \rho_{\text{slurry}}$$

### 2.6 Factor #6 — Mixing and Agitation

**Operational Logic:**
- Mixing ensures **uniform temperature, pH, and substrate distribution**.
- It also breaks the **scum layer** and prevents **dead zones**.
- **Optimal mixing intensity** is **mild and intermittent** — vigorous mixing can **disrupt syntrophic acetogen-methanogen partnerships** (high shear = detachment of H₂ scavengers).

**Typical design rule:**

$$P_{\text{mixer}} \approx 5 \text{ to } 10 \text{ W per m}^3 \text{ of digester volume}$$

### 2.7 Factor #7 — Toxicity and Inhibitors

| Inhibitor | Toxic Threshold | Mechanism |
|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| Free Ammonia (NH₃) | > 80–150 mg/L | Cell-membrane disruption of Archaea |
| Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S) | > 200 mg/L | Enzyme inhibition (cytochrome oxidase) |
| Sodium (Na⁺) | > 3500 mg/L | Osmotic stress |
| Potassium (K⁺) | > 2500 mg/L | Osmotic stress |
| Heavy metals (Cu, Zn, Cr) | > 100 mg/L | Enzyme denaturation |
| Long-chain fatty acids | > 1000 mg/L | Granule flotation |
| Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs) | > 5000 mg/L as acetate | pH crash |

**Free Ammonia Fraction (Temperature-Dependent):**

$$\frac{[\text{NH}_3]}{[\text{NH}_4^+ + \text{NH}_3]} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{10^{-pK_a}}{10^{-pH}}}$$

with $pK_a$ depending on temperature:

$$pK_a = 0.09018 + \frac{2729.92}{T [K]}$$

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the consolidated formula table for the topic **"Factors Affecting Biogas Generation"**. Master every row — these are the most-tested equations in KTU Module-3 questions.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|--------------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Methane Yield (per kg VS destroyed) | $Y_{CH_4} = 0.5 \cdot f_d$ | m³ CH₄/kg VS | Gas-yield estimate |
| 2 | Energy in Biogas | $E = V_{CH_4} \cdot 35.8$ | MJ | Calorific value |
| 3 | Hydraulic Retention Time | $\text{HRT} = V/Q$ | days | Reactor sizing |
| 4 | Organic Loading Rate | $\text{OLR} = Q \cdot S_0 / V$ | kg VS/(m³·d) | Feed design |
| 5 | Volumetric Biogas Production | $G_v = Y \cdot \text{OLR}$ | m³/(m³·d) | Specific productivity |
| 6 | C/N Ratio | $\text{C/N} = \sum M_i C_i \,/\, \sum M_i N_i$ | dimensionless | Feedstock design |
| 7 | Arrhenius rate | $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | day⁻¹ | Temperature effect |
| 8 | Q₁₀ Rule | $Q_{10} = (k_2/k_1)^{10/(T_2-T_1)}$ | dimensionless | Thermal sensitivity |
| 9 | Free NH₃ fraction | $f = 1/(1+10^{pK_a-pH})$ | dimensionless | Toxicity check |
| 10 | VFA / Alkalinity ratio | $r = \text{VFA}/\text{Alk.}$ | dimensionless | Process stability |
| 11 | Methane Fraction (Buswell Eq.) | $f_{CH_4} = (0.5 \cdot n - 0.25 \cdot a + 0.375 \cdot b - 0.5 \cdot c)/(n + a + 3.75 b + 2 c)$ | dimensionless | Theoretical maximum |
| 12 | Gas Density at STP | $\rho = PM/(R T)$ | kg/m³ | Mass↔Volume |
| 13 | BMP (Buswell Formula) | $\text{BMP} = 0.5 \cdot (V_S/1000) \cdot 22.4$ | L CH₄/g VS added | Substrate potential |
| 14 | Methane Mass from Volume | $m_{CH_4} = 0.717 \cdot V_{CH_4}$ | kg | Yield conversion |
| 15 | Power from Biogas | $P = \eta \cdot \dot{V}_{CH_4} \cdot 35.8$ | kW | CHP design |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why do KTU examiners love this topic?**
> Because **every operating digester in India** (e.g., the **DEWATS systems in Kerala**, the **NARDF plant in Rajasthan**, the **MST-RAU biogas plants in Maharashtra**) is sized and controlled using these very factors. Mastering these parameters enables a renewable-energy engineer to:
> - **Size digester volume** from feedstock quantity and desired HRT.
> - **Co-digest** multiple waste streams to hit the **optimum C/N of 25:1**.
> - **Diagnose failures** (acid crash, ammonia toxicity) using the VFA/Alkalinity ratio and free-NH₃ check.
> - **Predict methane yield** using the Buswell equation for a given VS feed.
> - **Design CHP systems** by converting the predicted methane volume into electrical kW.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation #1 — Volumetric Methane Yield (Buswell Equation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Buswell Equation (1932)** gives the theoretical maximum methane yield from the elemental composition of a substrate, assuming complete digestion. It is the single most-tested derivation in KTU module-3 problems.

**Starting Stoichiometry:**

For a substrate with empirical formula $\text{C}_n \text{H}_a \text{O}_b \text{N}_c$, the complete anaerobic digestion gives:

$$\text{C}_n \text{H}_a \text{O}_b \text{N}_c + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2} + \frac{3c}{4}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} - \frac{3c}{8}\right)\text{CH}_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} - \frac{3c}{8}\right)\text{CO}_2 + c \cdot \text{NH}_3$$

**Methane coefficient derivation by balancing atoms:**

- **Carbon balance:** $n = m_{CH_4} + m_{CO_2}$ (no. of moles in products)
- **Hydrogen balance:** $a + 2 \cdot n_{H_2O} = 4 \cdot m_{CH_4} + 3 \cdot m_{NH_3}$
- **Oxygen balance:** $b + n_{H_2O} = 2 \cdot m_{CO_2}$
- **Nitrogen balance:** $c = m_{NH_3}$

Let $x = m_{CH_4}$ (moles of CH₄ produced per mole of substrate). Solving the simultaneous atom balances:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{From O balance:} \quad & n_{H_2O} = 2 m_{CO_2} - b \\
& \text{Substitute into H balance:} \quad a + 2(2 m_{CO_2} - b) = 4 x + 3 c \\
& \text{Substitute } m_{CO_2} = n - x: \quad a + 4(n - x) - 2 b = 4 x + 3 c \\
& \Rightarrow a + 4n - 4 x - 2 b = 4 x + 3 c \\
& \Rightarrow 8 x = a + 4n - 2b - 3 c \\
& \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{8} + \frac{n}{2} - \frac{b}{4} - \frac{3c}{8}
\end{aligned}$$

**Therefore the Methane Fraction in the Biogas:**

$$f_{CH_4} = \frac{x}{x + m_{CO_2}} = \frac{\frac{a}{8} + \frac{n}{2} - \frac{b}{4} - \frac{3c}{8}}{\frac{a}{8} + \frac{n}{2} - \frac{b}{4} - \frac{3c}{8} + n - \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} + \frac{3c}{8}} = \frac{4n + a - 2b - 3c}{4n + 3a - 2b - 3c}$$

This simplifies to the classical Buswell formula for a generic organic substrate containing C, H, O, N:

$$\boxed{f_{CH_4} = \frac{4n + a - 2b - 3c}{4n + 3a - 2b - 3c}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For pure **carbohydrate** (e.g., glucose C₆H₁₂O₆ → n=6, a=12, b=6, c=0):
> $f_{CH_4} = (24+12-12-0)/(24+36-12-0) = 24/48 = \mathbf{0.50}$ → 50% CH₄ ✓
> For pure **lipid** (e.g., tripalmitin C₅₁H₉₈O₆ → n=51, a=98, b=6, c=0):
> $f_{CH_4} = (204+98-12-0)/(204+294-12-0) = 290/486 \approx \mathbf{0.597}$ → 60% CH₄ ✓
> For pure **protein** (C₅H₇NO₂ → n=5, a=7, b=2, c=1):
> $f_{CH_4} = (20+7-4-3)/(20+21-4-3) = 20/34 \approx \mathbf{0.588}$ → 59% CH₄ ✓
> These match experimental data within ±5%.

---

### 3.2 Derivation #2 — Quantification of the Temperature Effect (Arrhenius)

**Step 1 — Write Arrhenius Equation at two temperatures:**

$$k_1 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_1)} \quad \text{and} \quad k_2 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_2)}$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio (A cancels):**

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Eₐ (a classic KTU question type):**

$$\boxed{E_a = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Q₁₀ (a special case where T₂ − T₁ = 10 K):**

$$Q_{10} = e^{\frac{10 \cdot E_a}{R \cdot T_1 (T_1 + 10)}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad Q_{10} = \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

> A mesophilic digester runs at 35 °C (308 K) with k₁ = 0.25 day⁻¹. What is the new rate constant at 55 °C (328 K) if Eₐ = 68 kJ/mol?

Given: T₁ = 308 K, T₂ = 328 K, k₁ = 0.25 day⁻¹, Eₐ = 68 000 J/mol, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K).

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} &= \frac{1}{308} - \frac{1}{328} \\
&= \frac{328 - 308}{308 \cdot 328} \\
&= \frac{20}{101{,}024} \\
&\approx 1.9796 \times 10^{-4} \quad [\text{K}^{-1}]
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) &= \frac{68{,}000}{8.314} \times 1.9796 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 8178.0 \times 1.9796 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\approx 1.6191
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{k_2}{k_1} &= e^{1.6191} \approx 5.05 \\
k_2 &\approx 5.05 \times 0.25 = 1.26 \quad \text{day}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result:** Doubling the temperature from 35 °C to 55 °C **multiplies the methanogenic rate by ~5×**, but the practical yield gain is only **20–40%** due to ammonia inhibition and ammonia escape.

---

### 3.3 Derivation #3 — Relationship Between HRT, OLR, and Daily Biogas Production

**Step 1 — Define Volumetric Productivity:**

$$G_v = \text{OLR} \cdot Y_{CH_4} \quad \left[\frac{\text{m}^3_{CH_4}}{\text{m}^3_{\text{digester}} \cdot \text{day}}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Express OLR in terms of TS, VS, HRT:**

For a slurry of total mass flow $\dot{M}$ [kg/day], with solids fraction $x$ and volatile fraction $f_V$:

$$\text{OLR} = \frac{\dot{M} \cdot x \cdot f_V}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Relate HRT to volume and feed:**

$$\text{HRT} = \frac{V}{Q_{\text{slurry}}}$$

For a slurry of density $\rho$ and solids fraction $x$: $Q_{\text{slurry}} = \dot{M}/\rho$. Hence:

$$\text{HRT} = \frac{V \cdot \rho}{\dot{M}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute to get:**

$$\text{OLR} = \frac{x \cdot f_V \cdot \rho}{\text{HRT}}$$

**Step 5 — Daily Biogas Production:**

$$\dot{V}_{CH_4} = G_v \cdot V = \text{OLR} \cdot Y_{CH_4} \cdot V = \frac{Y_{CH_4} \cdot x \cdot f_V \cdot \rho \cdot V}{\text{HRT}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $V = \text{HRT} \cdot Q$:**

$$\dot{V}_{CH_4} = Y_{CH_4} \cdot x \cdot f_V \cdot \rho \cdot Q = Y_{CH_4} \cdot \dot{M}_{\text{VS}}$$

> **Conclusion (key insight):** *In a CSTR with a given methane yield coefficient Y, the daily CH₄ output is governed purely by the VS mass feed rate, not the digester volume! The volume only matters insofar as it determines the HRT and hence the allowable OLR.*

---

### 3.4 Derivation #4 — Optimum C/N by Co-digestion

**Problem:** Co-digest cattle dung (C/N = 25, mass M₁) and poultry droppings (C/N = 8, mass M₂). Find the mass ratio M₁/M₂ that gives overall C/N = 22.

**Step 1 — Assume fixed carbon and nitrogen percentages:**

Let carbon% in cattle dung be C₁, nitrogen% be N₁. For cattle dung C/N = C₁/N₁ = 25.

Similarly C₂/N₂ = 8 for poultry.

**Step 2 — Total Carbon = M₁C₁ + M₂C₂; Total Nitrogen = M₁N₁ + M₂N₂:**

$$\text{C/N}_{\text{mix}} = \frac{M_1 C_1 + M_2 C_2}{M_1 N_1 + M_2 N_2} = 22$$

**Step 3 — Divide numerator and denominator by M₁, let x = M₂/M₁:**

$$22 = \frac{C_1 + x C_2}{N_1 + x N_2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $N_1 = C_1/25$ and $N_2 = C_2/8$:**

$$22 = \frac{C_1 + x C_2}{C_1/25 + x C_2/8} = \frac{25 C_1 + 25 x C_2}{C_1 + 25 x C_2/8} \cdot \frac{1}{?}$$

A simpler approach: define **C per unit mass** as $C_1$ and **N per unit mass** as $N_1$, $N_2$. Then:

$$22 = \frac{C_1 + x C_2}{N_1 + x N_2}$$

**Step 5 — Solving for x:**

$$\begin{aligned}
22(N_1 + x N_2) &= C_1 + x C_2 \\
22 N_1 + 22 x N_2 &= C_1 + x C_2 \\
x (22 N_2 - C_2) &= C_1 - 22 N_1 \\
x &= \frac{C_1 - 22 N_1}{22 N_2 - C_2} = \frac{N_1(C_1/N_1 - 22)}{N_2(22 - C_2/N_2)} = \frac{N_1(25 - 22)}{N_2(22 - 8)} = \frac{3 N_1}{14 N_2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mass ratio assuming equal N% (or using empirical N values):**

If $N_1 \approx 1.5\%$ and $N_2 \approx 4.0\%$ (typical), then $x = (3 \times 1.5)/(14 \times 4.0) = 4.5/56 = 0.080$.

So **M₁/M₂ ≈ 12.5 : 1** (i.e., 12.5 kg cattle dung per kg poultry droppings).

> **Engineering Implication:** This is the kind of calculation that **KVIC and IIT-Bombay biogas pilots** use to size feedstock blends. Students are expected to **show this entire algebraic chain in the answer key.**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Biogas Yield to Each Factor

| Factor | Direction of Effect | Magnitude of Effect | Reversibility |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| ↑ T (up to optimum) | ↑ CH₄ rate | 2–3× per 10 °C (Q₁₀) | Reversible within range |
| ↑ T (above optimum) | ↓ CH₄ yield, ↑ NH₃ | Cell death, irreversible | **Irreversible** |
| ↑ pH (above 8) | ↓ Methanogen activity | 50% drop at pH 8.5 | Slowly reversible |
| ↓ pH (below 6.5) | Acid crash | Total failure | Slow recovery, ~1 month |
| ↑ C/N (above 35) | Nutrient limitation | 30% drop in yield | Reversible on blending |
| ↓ C/N (below 15) | NH₃ toxicity | Yield falls 50% | Slow recovery |
| ↑ OLR (above optimum) | Acid accumulation | Process crash | Recovery very slow |
| ↓ HRT (below critical) | Methanogen washout | Sudden failure | **Irreversible** |
| ↑ Mixing intensity | ↓ Boundary layer | Optimum 5–10 W/m³ | Reversible |
| ↑ Particle size | ↓ Hydrolysis rate | 30% drop at 10 mm | Reversible (by grinding) |
| ↑ Free NH₃ (>150 ppm) | Enzyme inhibition | Linear decline | Slowly reversible |
| ↑ VS destruction (better mixing) | ↑ Gas yield | Theoretical max ≈ 0.5 m³/kg VS | — |

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse HRT and SRT.** In a CSTR they are equal, but in a UASB or anaerobic filter, SRT > HRT (biomass attached). This is a 2–3 mark question hidden inside 14-mark problems.
> 2. **Units trap:** Always write HRP in **days**, OLR in **kg VS/(m³·day)**, and yield in **m³ CH₄/kg VS**. The examiner deducts 0.5 marks per unit mismatch.
> 3. **Free NH₃ vs Total NH₃:** A common trap: students report *total* ammonia and conclude "within limits", but the *free* (un-ionized) NH₃ is what is toxic. Always apply the temperature-pH correction.
> 4. **Methane yield number:** Using **0.5 m³/kg VS** without qualifying it (i.e., stating it is for a well-mixed CSTR at mesophilic, complete digestion) loses credit.
> 5. **C/N ratio wording:** Never just say "C/N should be 25:1" — *justify* by referencing microbial protein synthesis requirement.
> 6. **Temperature extremes:** "Higher temperature = more gas" is **wrong above 60 °C**. Always state the optimum and the upper limit.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEMRT632]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *List and briefly explain any THREE critical factors that affect biogas generation in an anaerobic digester.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Each factor 1 Mark):**

Three critical factors are:

**(i) Temperature:** Biogas-producing methanogens operate optimally in the **mesophilic range of 30–40 °C** or the **thermophilic range of 50–60 °C**. Below 10 °C the reaction virtually stops; above 60 °C enzyme denaturation occurs. The kinetic rate roughly **doubles for every 10 °C rise** (Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3).

**(ii) pH and Alkalinity:** Methanogens require a **near-neutral pH of 6.8–7.5**. Acidogenic bacteria produce volatile fatty acids (VFAs); without sufficient alkalinity (typically 2000–5000 mg/L as CaCO₃), the digester "crashes" to acidic pH and methane production halts.

**(iii) C/N Ratio:** Microbes need a balanced supply of **carbon (energy)** and **nitrogen (protein synthesis)**. The **optimum C/N ratio is 20:1 to 30:1**. A high C/N (e.g., rice straw 60:1) starves microbes; a low C/N (e.g., poultry manure 8:1) releases toxic free ammonia.

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *Explain the role of Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) in biogas generation. What happens if HRT is too short?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** HRT is the average time the feed slurry spends inside the digester:

$$\text{HRT} = \frac{V_{\text{digester}}}{Q_{\text{feed flow}}} \quad [\text{days}]$$

**Role (1 Mark):** HRT must be **long enough to complete the slowest step — methanogenesis** — so that a high fraction of the volatile solids (VS) is converted to biogas. Typical values are 15–40 days for mesophilic digesters.

**Effect of too-short HRT (1 Mark):** If HRT falls below the **critical washout time** (≈ 10 days for mesophilic cattle-dung digesters), the slow-growing methanogens are **flushed out faster than they can reproduce**, leading to **total process failure**. The digester then "sours" to acid pH and biogas production ceases.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEMRT632, Module 3]** — *Solve ANY ONE of the following:*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Derive the Buswell equation for methane yield from a generic organic substrate CₙHₐOᵦN_c. Hence calculate the methane fraction in biogas produced from glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) and a protein of formula C₅H₇NO₂.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A farmer co-digests 1000 kg/day cattle dung (C/N = 25) with 200 kg/day poultry droppings (C/N = 8). If the methane yield coefficient is 0.30 m³ CH₄/kg VS added, VS content is 80% of wet mass, and the digester volume is 250 m³ at 35 °C, find: (i) the overall C/N ratio of the feed, (ii) the daily methane volume at STP, and (iii) the HRT.*

---

**Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 [2 Marks]:** Write the generalized stoichiometry and balance the elements C, H, O, N. Let $x$ be the moles of CH₄ formed per mole of substrate. Solving the simultaneous atom balances (as shown in Section 3.1) gives:

$$\boxed{x = \frac{a}{8} + \frac{n}{2} - \frac{b}{4} - \frac{3c}{8}}$$

The CO₂ produced is $n - x$ moles, so the **methane fraction** is:

$$f_{CH_4} = \frac{4n + a - 2b - 3c}{4n + 3a - 2b - 3c}$$

**Step 2 [2 Marks]:** For **glucose C₆H₁₂O₆** → $n=6, a=12, b=6, c=0$:

$$f_{CH_4} = \frac{4(6) + 12 - 2(6) - 0}{4(6) + 3(12) - 2(6) - 0} = \frac{24+12-12}{24+36-12} = \frac{24}{48} = 0.50$$

So biogas from glucose contains **50% CH₄ and 50% CO₂** ✓

**Step 3 [2 Marks]:** For **protein C₅H₇NO₂** → $n=5, a=7, b=2, c=1$:

$$f_{CH_4} = \frac{4(5) + 7 - 2(2) - 3(1)}{4(5) + 3(7) - 2(2) - 3(1)} = \frac{20+7-4-3}{20+21-4-3} = \frac{20}{34} \approx 0.588$$

So biogas from this protein contains **≈ 58.8% CH₄** ✓

**Step 4 [1 Mark]:** *Comment:* Protein is more methanogenic per unit mass than carbohydrate because of its higher hydrogen content and lower oxygen content, leading to a higher H/C ratio in the digestion products.

---

**Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $M_1 = 1000$ kg/day cattle dung, C/N₁ = 25
- $M_2 = 200$ kg/day poultry, C/N₂ = 8
- $Y_{CH_4} = 0.30$ m³/kg VS
- VS = 80% of wet mass
- $V = 250$ m³, $T = 35$ °C

**Part (i) — Overall C/N ratio [2 Marks]:**

Let C₁, N₁ be carbon% and nitrogen% of cattle dung; C₂, N₂ for poultry.

Using the **C/N blending formula:**

$$\text{C/N}_{\text{mix}} = \frac{M_1 C_1 + M_2 C_2}{M_1 N_1 + M_2 N_2}$$

Substituting $C_1 = 25 N_1$ and $C_2 = 8 N_2$:

$$\text{C/N}_{\text{mix}} = \frac{M_1 (25 N_1) + M_2 (8 N_2)}{M_1 N_1 + M_2 N_2}$$

Assuming the *nitrogen content per kg* is approximately the same (≈ 1.5% for cattle dung, ≈ 4% for poultry — but ratios dominate), we can simplify by using empirical N values:

Total N ≈ $1000 \times 0.015 + 200 \times 0.04 = 15 + 8 = 23$ kg N/day
Total C ≈ $25 \times 15 + 8 \times 8 = 375 + 64 = 439$ kg C/day

$$\boxed{\text{C/N}_{\text{mix}} = \frac{439}{23} \approx 19.1 : 1}$$

This is **slightly below the optimum 20:1–30:1 range**, indicating mild nitrogen excess. Recovery: add a carbon-rich cosubstrate (e.g., rice straw) to push C/N closer to 25.

**Part (ii) — Daily methane volume [2 Marks]:**

Total VS feed = $(1000 + 200) \times 0.80 = 1200 \times 0.80 = 960$ kg VS/day

Daily CH₄ volume:

$$V_{CH_4} = Y \cdot \dot{M}_{VS} = 0.30 \times 960 = 288 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{day}$$

At STP, this is **288 m³/day** of methane (≈ 50% of biogas = 576 m³/day total biogas).

**Part (iii) — HRT [2 Marks]:**

VS concentration in feed (assuming water + solids slurry of density 1000 kg/m³): for liquid slurry $Q \approx (1000+200)/1000 = 1.2$ m³/day.

$$\text{HRT} = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{250}{1.2} \approx 208 \text{ days}$$

[1 Mark for correct unit] This is unrealistically long — so for this 250 m³ digester, the actual feeding should be **larger** to bring HRT to the typical 30–40 day range. *Conclusion (1 Mark):* The digester is **oversized**; the farmer could increase feed rate to ~6–8 m³/day to bring HRT to the optimum range, OR use this digester for additional co-substrates.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With the help of the Arrhenius equation, derive the temperature dependence of the methanogenic rate constant. A digester operates at 35 °C with $k_1 = 0.20$ day⁻¹. Estimate the new rate constant at 55 °C if the activation energy is 70 kJ/mol. Discuss why the practical gas yield does not increase by the same factor.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Explain in detail: (i) the effect of pH, alkalinity, and the VFA/alkalinity ratio on digester stability; (ii) the effect of C/N ratio and the principles of co-digestion to achieve an optimum C/N.*

---

**Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 [2 Marks]:** The Arrhenius equation:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T)}$$

At $T_1 = 308$ K: $k_1 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_1)}$
At $T_2 = 328$ K: $k_2 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_2)}$

Dividing:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

**Step 2 [2 Marks]:** Substituting the values $E_a = 70{,}000$ J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T_1 = 308$ K, $T_2 = 328$ K:

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{308} - \frac{1}{328} = \frac{20}{101{,}024} \approx 1.98 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{E_a}{R} \times 1.98 \times 10^{-4} = \frac{70{,}000}{8.314} \times 1.98 \times 10^{-4} \approx 8419 \times 1.98 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1.667$$

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{1.667} \approx 5.30$$

**Step 3 [1 Mark]:** Therefore:

$$k_2 = 5.30 \times 0.20 = 1.06 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 [2 Marks]:** *Discussion — why practical yield does not scale 5×:*

(i) **Ammonia toxicity** rises sharply with temperature: free NH₃ concentration approximately **doubles per 10 °C** rise. At 55 °C, even moderate N-feed leads to NH₃ > 200 mg/L, inhibiting methanogens.

(ii) **VFA accumulation**: Higher temperatures accelerate acidogenesis faster than methanogenesis, causing the VFA pool to spike and **pH to crash**.

(iii) **Cell death and granule breakup**: Thermophilic archaea are more fragile; sudden temperature changes can wipe out biomass.

(iv) **Heat-input energy penalty**: Maintaining 55 °C in a tropical climate costs more than at 35 °C (cooling may even be needed), eroding the energy gain.

> **Net practical yield gain at 55 °C is only 20–40%** (and sometimes negative), not the 5× predicted by Arrhenius kinetics.

---

**Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 Marks]**

**(i) pH, Alkalinity, VFA/Alkalinity Ratio [4 Marks]:**

- **pH Optimum:** Methanogenic archaea require a pH window of **6.8–7.5**. Below 6.3, they cease activity; above 8.0, NH₃ toxicity rises.
- **Alkalinity:** A buffered system needs **2000–5000 mg/L as CaCO₃**. The dominant buffering species are **bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻)** from CO₂ dissolution and **ammonia (NH₄⁺)** from protein breakdown.
- **VFA/alkalinity ratio (Dobb's stability index):**
  $$\text{Index} = \frac{\text{VFA (mg/L as acetic)}}{\text{Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)}}$$
  - **Index < 0.4 → Stable digester** (with high reserve buffering capacity).
  - **Index 0.4–0.8 → Warning zone**, take corrective action (reduce OLR, add alkalinity).
  - **Index > 0.8 → Process failure imminent**; immediate feeding reduction required.

- **Action plan for high VFA/alk alkalinity:** (1) Stop or reduce feeding; (2) Recirculate digester supernatant; (3) Add lime/bicarbonate cautiously; (4) Increase mixing to release CO₂ and re-equilibrate pH.

**(ii) C/N Ratio and Co-digestion [3 Marks]:**

- **Microbial requirement:** Microbial cells need ~25 parts C for every 1 part N to synthesize protein. Hence **C/N optimum = 20:1 to 30:1**.
- **High C/N (e.g., rice straw 80:1):** Nitrogen becomes the limiting nutrient; microbes cannot multiply, hydrolysis slows, and the digestion is "nitrogen-starved".
- **Low C/N (e.g., poultry 8:1):** Excess nitrogen is released as **free NH₃**, which is bactericidal to methanogens.
- **Co-digestion principle:** Blending two substrates to land at optimum C/N.

$$\text{C/N}_{\text{mix}} = \frac{\sum M_i C_i}{\sum M_i N_i}$$

- **Example:** 80% cattle dung (C/N = 25) + 20% poultry (C/N = 8) → mixed C/N ≈ 21.5 ✓
- **Benefit:** Co-digestion also balances moisture, supplies trace micronutrients, and increases per-unit VS methane yield by 20–40% — a phenomenon called the **"synergistic effect"**.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Cheat Sheet — Print This Before the Exam!**

**🟢 CORE KNOWLEDGE:**
- Biogas = ~50–70% CH₄ + 30–40% CO₂ + trace H₂S, NH₃, H₂O
- Buswell equation: $f_{CH_4} = (4n + a - 2b - 3c)/(4n + 3a - 2b - 3c)$
- Methane yield typical value: **0.30–0.50 m³ CH₄/kg VS added**
- Methane LHV ≈ **35.8 MJ/m³**

**🟡 MUST-KNOW PARAMETERS:**
- **Temperature:** Mesophilic 30–40 °C (best 35 °C); Thermophilic 50–60 °C (best 55 °C); Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3
- **pH:** 6.8–7.5 (best 7.0)
- **Alkalinity:** 2000–5000 mg/L as CaCO₃
- **VFA/Alkalinity:** Stable < 0.4; Failure > 0.8
- **C/N ratio:** 20:1 to 30:1 (best 25:1)
- **HRT:** 15–40 days mesophilic; 12–20 days thermophilic
- **OLR:** 1.0–4.0 kg VS/(m³·day)
- **Total Solids:** 8–12% (wet); 20–35% (dry)
- **Mixing:** 5–10 W/m³, mild and continuous

**🔴 DON'T FORGET — TOXIC THRESHOLDS:**
- Free NH₃ > 80–150 mg/L → Methanogen inhibition
- H₂S > 200 mg/L → Enzyme inhibition
- Na⁺ > 3500 mg/L; K⁺ > 2500 mg/L
- VFAs > 5000 mg/L → pH crash

**🟣 KEY EQUATIONS TO MEMORIZE:**
1. $\text{HRT} = V/Q$
2. $\text{OLR} = Q S_0 / V$
3. $k = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$
4. $Q_{10} = (k_2/k_1)^{10/(T_2-T_1)}$
5. $\text{C/N}_{\text{mix}} = \sum M_i C_i / \sum M_i N_i$
6. $\text{Free NH}_3 = \text{Total} / (1 + 10^{pK_a - pH})$
7. $\dot{V}_{CH_4} = Y_{CH_4} \cdot \dot{M}_{VS}$

**⚫ COMMON EXAM MISTAKES TO AVOID:**
- ❌ Writing HRT in hours (must be in days for KTU biogas problems)
- ❌ Confusing **SRT** (Solid Retention Time) with **HRT**
- ❌ Using **total** NH₃ instead of **free** NH₃ for toxicity
- ❌ Reporting Y = 0.5 m³/kg VS as exact (it is *theoretical maximum* for CSTR)
- ❌ Stating C/N = 25 without units ("25:1 dimensionless")
- ❌ Forgetting to convert °C to K in Arrhenius calculation

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Biogas Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT632 - RENEWABLE ENERGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types of Biogas Plants # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biogas Plant (Anaerobic Digester):** A closed, airtight vessel (reactor) in which organic biomass (cattle dung, agricultural residue, municipal waste, kitchen waste, etc.) is decomposed by **methanogenic bacteria** under **anaerobic (oxygen-free) conditions** through a four-stage biochemical process (Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis), producing a combustible gas mixture called **biogas** (≈ 55–65 % CH₄ + 30–40 % CO₂ + trace H₂S, N₂, water vapour) and a nutrient-rich slurry called **digestate / bio-slurry**. A **biogas plant** is therefore a controlled bio-reactor that converts **putrescible organic matter** into a clean fuel and a high-value organic fertilizer, simultaneously addressing **waste management**, **rural energy supply**, and **carbon-neutral energy generation**. ## 1.2 Classification of Biogas Plants (As per KTU Module 3) Biogas plants are classified based on **construction geometry**, **mode of feeding**, and **gas storage mechanism**: | S.No | Classification Basis | Type | |---|---|---| | 1 | By Feeding Mode | **Batch Type** and **Continuous Type** | | 2 | By Gas Storage / Geometry | **Fixed Dome Type (Chinese Model)** and **Floating Dome Type (Indian Model)** | | 3 | By Reactor Hydraulics | **Plug Flow**, **CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor)**, **UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket)**, **Anaerobic Filter (AF)** | | 4 | By Operating Temperature | **Mesophilic (25–40 °C)** and **Thermophilic (45–60 °C)** | | 5 | By Construction | **Brick/Masonry**, **Prefab Concrete**, **Steel/Fibreglass**, **HDPE/PVC Bag Digesters** | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Stomach Analogy"** — Think of a biogas plant exactly like the **stomach of a cow** (the cow is, in fact, the world's most efficient mobile biogas reactor! 🐄). The cow chews (substrate preparation), swallows slurry into its four-chambered rumen (digester), microbes ferment it in oxygen-free conditions, methane is produced (cow belches it out!), and the nutrient-rich digested grass is excreted (bio-slurry / digested cow-dung). A biogas plant simply puts this natural process inside a **sealed steel/brick vessel** so we can **capture the methane instead of letting it escape into the atmosphere** — turning a polluting greenhouse gas into usable cooking fuel and electricity. - The **fixed dome** is like a **closed pressure cooker** that holds gas at variable pressure as its volume remains constant. - The **floating dome** is like a **bell jar floating on water** — gas pressure stays nearly constant as the dome simply rises and falls with the amount of gas produced. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants - **Calorific value of biogas:** **≈ 20–26 MJ/m³** (≈ 5,500–7,000 kcal/m³) - **Specific gravity of methane:** **0.554** (relative to air) - **Ignition temperature of methane:** **≈ 650 °C** - **Explosive limit of methane in air:** **5–15 %** by volume - **Methane yield from cattle dung:** **≈ 0.04–0.05 m³ CH₄ / kg of dung per day** under mesophilic conditions - **Optimum C:N ratio of feed:** **25 : 1 to 30 : 1** (Cattle dung ≈ 25:1, ideal as baseline) - **pH range for methanogenesis:** **6.8 – 7.4** (strictly neutral) - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** **30–60 days** for cattle-dung-based plants # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Major Types — Operational Walk-Through ### TYPE 1: Fixed Dome Type Biogas Plant (Chinese Model / KVIC Improved Model) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **fixed dome plant** has a **rigid, non-movable dome** constructed of brick, masonry, or reinforced concrete. Gas is stored in the upper portion of the same digester chamber at **variable pressure** (typically 0.05–0.15 bar gauge). As gas production increases, the slurry is pushed down into the **displacement chamber (compensation tank)**. **Constructional Components:** 1. **Inlet / Mixing Tank** — receives fresh dung + water slurry (≈ 1:1 ratio) 2. **Digester Chamber** — sealed underground dome where fermentation occurs 3. **Outlet / Displacement Chamber** — receives displaced digested slurry 4. **Gas Outlet Pipe** — takes biogas to the point of use 5. **Gas Scrubber / Filter** — removes H₂S and moisture (typically iron-oxide based or water scrubber) 6. **Pressure Gauge & Safety Valve** — to prevent dome bursting **Operational Logic — Step-by-Step:** - Fresh slurry enters inlet → flows into digester → microbes break it down anaerobically for 30–60 days. - Biogas collects in the **top void of the fixed dome** (gas space is not separate from digester). - As gas pressure rises, slurry is forced **downward and sideways** into the outlet/expansion chamber. - The **height difference** between slurry level in the digester and the outlet chamber × slurry density × g = gas pressure. - The plant works best under **near-constant gas draw-off**; if no gas is used, pressure builds up and slurry overflows harmlessly into the expansion chamber. **Advantages:** - Long service life (≈ 20–25 years for masonry domes) - No moving/rotating parts → very low maintenance - No corrosion problems (no steel parts in contact with gas) - Underground → aesthetic, space-saving - Suited to **cold climates** (subsurface insulation) - **Disadvantages:** Variable gas pressure (problem for burners), gas leak risk in masonry joints, initial cost high, requires skilled masonry labour. ### TYPE 2: Floating Gas Holder Type (Indian / Gobar Gas Model) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **floating dome (gas holder)** is an **inverted mild-steel drum** that floats on the slurry inside the digester, rising and falling like a **piston** as biogas is produced or consumed. Gas is therefore stored at **nearly constant pressure** (≈ 8–10 cm water column, 80–100 mm WC). **Constructional Components:** 1. **Brick/Masonry Digester Well** — cylindrical underground tank 2. **Floating Steel Dome (Gobar Gas Type)** — fabricated M.S. sheet, top-rim guide frame, central guide rod 3. **Inlet Pipe** — delivers fresh slurry 4. **Outlet Pipe** — overflows displaced digested slurry 5. **Gas Outlet Pipe with valve** — to burner/lamps 6. **Central Guide Rod & Frame** — keeps dome vertical, prevents tilting **Operational Logic — Step-by-Step:** - Slurry is fed through inlet → enters digester → digestion produces biogas. - Gas bubbles rise, accumulate under the **floating drum**, increasing buoyancy → drum rises. - The weight of the drum sets the **constant gas pressure** (pressure = W_dome / A_dome + atmospheric). - As gas is consumed, the drum descends, displacing slurry out through the outlet. - The **dome never goes to the bottom**; a minimum 15–20 cm of slurry is always retained as a gas seal. **Advantages:** - **Constant gas pressure** → stable burner performance, no regulator needed - Visual indication of gas stored (dome height) - Easy to install in regions with poor masonry skills - Short construction time - **Disadvantages:** Steel dome corrodes (life 8–12 years), needs repainting, not suitable for high-pressure storage, above-ground dome is aesthetically less appealing. ### TYPE 3: Continuous-Flow vs Batch-Type Plants | Feature | Batch Type | Continuous Type | |---|---|---| | Feeding | Once, then sealed | Daily/regular feeding | | Gas production | Peaks then declines | Steady, long-term | | Typical use | Festive/seasonal waste, leaf litter, FYM | Cattle dung, kitchen waste, sewage | | Examples | Drum digesters, Lakshmi-type | KVIC, Gobar Gas, UASB | | HRT control | Not flexible | Precisely controlled | ### TYPE 4: High-Rate / Industrial Anaerobic Reactors (Brief) > [!NOTE] > Used in **large commercial / industrial plants** for sewage sludge, distillery effluent, food processing waste: > - **CSTR** — Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor, fully mixed > - **UASB** — Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket, very high biomass retention > - **Anaerobic Filter (AF)** — biofilm on packed media > - **Plug Flow Reactor** — for high-solid substrates (≈ 10–15 % TS) > - **EGSB** — Expanded Granular Sludge Bed (variant of UASB) ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Daily gas production | $G_d = Y \cdot m_d$ | m³/day | $Y$ = yield, $m_d$ = daily substrate loading | | 2 | Required digester volume | $V = Q \cdot \theta$ | m³ | $Q$ = slurry flow rate (m³/day), $\theta$ = HRT (days) | | 3 | Slurry density (typical dung-water) | $\rho_s \approx 1010$ | kg/m³ | For 8–10 % TS slurry | | 4 | Mass of substrate per day | $m_d = \rho_s \cdot Q$ | kg/day | If flow is in m³/day | | 5 | Biogas yield per kg VS | $G_{VS} = 0.25 \text{ to } 0.50$ | m³/kg VS | Depends on substrate & temperature | | 6 | Methane fraction | $f_{CH_4} = 0.55 \text{ to } 0.65$ | — | Volume fraction | | 7 | Daily water + dung mix | $V_{mix} = \frac{m_{dung}}{\rho_{mix}}$ | m³ | $\rho_{mix} \approx 1090$ kg/m³ | | 8 | Gas pressure (floating dome) | $P = \dfrac{W_{dome}}{A_{dome}} + P_{atm}$ | Pa | $W_{dome}$ = dome weight (N) | | 9 | Gas pressure (fixed dome) | $P = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa | $h$ = height difference of slurry | | 10 | Calorific value of biogas | $CV = f_{CH_4} \times CV_{CH_4}$ | MJ/m³ | $CV_{CH_4} \approx 37.5$ MJ/m³ | | 11 | Power equivalent | $P_e = \dfrac{G_d \times CV}{\eta \times 3600}$ | kW | $\eta \approx 0.30$ (gen-set) | | 12 | HRT formula | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | days | Critical design parameter | > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** KTU examiners **always test** the difference between **fixed dome vs floating dome** pressure characteristics.
