Memorize: **Fixed = Variable Pressure + No moving parts**, **Floating = Constant Pressure + Moving dome**. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Biogas plants are used by: - **Domestic cooking** (1–6 m³ plant for a family of 4–6) - **Rural electrification** (gas engines, dual-fuel systems) - **Industrial combined heat & power (CHP)** - **Municipal sewage treatment** (digesters in STP plants) - **Compressed Biogas (CBG)** for vehicle fuel under India's SATAT scheme - **Carbon credit projects** under CDM / Verra standards # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Design Problem 1 — Sizing a Floating-Dome KVIC Plant for a Farm > **Given:** A dairy farm has **20 adult cows** producing dung at an average rate of **10 kg/cow/day**. Design a **floating-dome (Gobar Gas) biogas plant** for the following requirements: > - Slurry concentration to be fed: **9 % Total Solids (TS)** > - Water-to-dung ratio by mass: **1 : 1** (i.e., equal water and dung) > - Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT): **40 days** > - Methane yield: **0.040 m³ CH₄ / kg of fresh dung** > - Methane fraction in biogas: **60 %** > - Density of slurry (≈ 9 % TS): **ρ_s = 1090 kg/m³** > - Density of fresh dung: **ρ_d ≈ 1050 kg/m³** ### Part (a) — Daily Dung, Slurry Volume & Methane Production **Step 1: Daily dung mass available** $$ m_{dung} = N_{cows} \times r_{dung} $$ $$ m_{dung} = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ kg/day} $$ **Step 2: Total slurry mass (dung + water, 1:1 ratio)** $$ m_{slurry} = m_{dung} + m_{water} = m_{dung} \times 2 $$ $$ m_{slurry} = 200 \times 2 = 400 \text{ kg/day} $$ **Step 3: Daily slurry volume** $$ Q = \frac{m_{slurry}}{\rho_s} = \frac{400}{1090} $$ $$ Q = 0.367 \text{ m³/day} $$ **Step 4: Methane produced per day** $$ Q_{CH_4} = Y_{CH_4} \times m_{dung} $$ $$ Q_{CH_4} = 0.040 \times 200 = 8.00 \text{ m³ CH}_4/\text{day} $$ **Step 5: Total biogas produced per day (since CH₄ = 60 % of biogas)** $$ Q_{biogas} = \frac{Q_{CH_4}}{f_{CH_4}} = \frac{8.00}{0.60} $$ $$ Q_{biogas} = 13.33 \text{ m³ biogas/day} $$ ### Part (b) — Digester Volume, Dome Sizing & Power Equivalent **Step 6: Required digester volume (based on HRT)** $$ V_{digester} = Q \times \theta $$ $$ V_{digester} = 0.367 \times 40 = 14.68 \text{ m³} $$ Round up to a **standard KVIC plant size of 15 m³** (these come in 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 … m³ sizes). **Step 7: Power equivalent of produced biogas** Using calorific value of methane = **37.5 MJ/m³** and generator efficiency **η = 0.30**: $$ P_e = \frac{Q_{biogas} \times f_{CH_4} \times CV_{CH_4} \times \eta_{overall}}{3600} $$ Here we use the **calorific value of biogas directly** ≈ **22 MJ/m³** for a 60 %-methane mix. $$ P_e = \frac{13.33 \times 22 \times 0.30}{3.6} $$ $$ P_e = \frac{88.0}{3.6} = 24.4 \text{ kW} $$ > This is the **continuous electrical power** available — enough to run a **25 kVA genset** (after adding ~10 % auxiliary load margin) for **24 × 7 operation** if slurry is supplied continuously. **Step 8: Cooking-equivalent** - One domestic LPG cylinder = 14.2 kg ≈ 47.5 m³ of biogas equivalent - Daily biogas = 13.33 m³ → **2.8 cylinders/month** equivalent cooking gas (but free!). ## 3.2 Worked Design Problem 2 — Gas Pressure in a Fixed-Dome Plant > **Given:** A fixed-dome Chinese-type plant. When fully gas-filled, the slurry level inside the digester is **0.80 m below** the top of the dome. The slurry in the outlet/expansion chamber rises to a height of **1.50 m above** the dome-base reference line. Slurry density ρ_s = 1090 kg/m³. Find the gas pressure. **Step 1: Effective height difference between slurry in digester and outlet chamber** $$ h = h_{outlet} - (-h_{digester}) = 1.50 - (-0.80) = 2.30 \text{ m} $$ Wait — the digester slurry is **pushed down by 0.80 m**; the outlet chamber slurry is **risen by 1.50 m**. The effective head pushing the gas is the **total height difference** between the slurry surface in the outlet chamber and the slurry surface in the digester. $$ h = 1.50 + 0.80 = 2.30 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2: Hydrostatic pressure head** $$ P_{gauge} = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot h $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 1090 \times 9.81 \times 2.30 $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 24{,}594 \text{ Pa} \approx 24.6 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 3: Convert to convenient units** $$ P_{gauge} = 0.246 \text{ bar} \approx 0.243 \text{ atm} \approx 2507 \text{ mm WC (water column)} $$ This is the **maximum design pressure** — hence the **safety valve** must be set at ≈ 0.20 bar to release excess gas and protect the masonry dome. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fixed Dome vs Floating Dome | Design / Operational Parameter | Fixed Dome (Chinese) | Floating Dome (Indian) | |---|---|---| | **Gas storage location** | Top of same digester | Separate inverted drum | | **Gas pressure** | **Variable** (rises with stored volume) | **Constant** (≈ 80–100 mm WC) | | **Pressure determination** | $\rho_s g h$ (slurry height) | $W_{dome}/A_{dome}$ (dome weight/area) | | **Regulator required?** | Yes (essential) | Usually not required | | **Gas leakage risk** | Higher (masonry joints) | Lower (welded steel) | | **Dome material** | Brick / RCC / Ferrocement | M.S. steel drum | | **Lifespan** | 20–25 years | 8–12 years (corrosion) | | **Maintenance** | Low | Periodic painting, anti-corrosion | | **Visual gas-level indication** | None | Yes (dome height) | | **Initial cost (per m³)** | Higher (skilled masonry) | Lower (faster installation) | | **Best suited for** | Cold climate, large community plants, deep underground | Warm climate, individual households, semi-skilled regions | | **Volumetric capacity range** | 5–200 m³ | 1–30 m³ typical | | **HRT (dung-based)** | 30–55 days | 30–50 days | ## 3.4 Energy & Carbon Audit Snapshot > [!NOTE] > - 1 m³ biogas displaces ≈ **0.7 kg LPG** or **1.5 kg firewood** > - Avoids **≈ 2.0 kg CO₂ emission per m³** vs firewood > - A **1 m³/day biogas plant** offsets about **0.7 tonne CO₂/year** > - A 4 m³ family plant saves about **₹ 1,200–1,500/month** in fuel costs (2024 India) # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Biogas Plant Working ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Substrate Storage
Dung / Waste] --> B[Mixing Tank
Dung + Water 1:1] B --> C[Inlet Pipe] C --> D[Anaerobic Digester
Closed Vessel
30 to 60 days HRT] D --> E[Microbial Action
4 stage AD process] E --> F[Biogas Collection
Top of Digester] F --> G[Gas Scrubber
H2S and Moisture Removal] G --> H[Gas Storage
Fixed Dome or Floating Holder] H --> I[Distribution
Pipeline to Burner or Engine] D --> J[Slurry Displacement
Outlet Chamber] J --> K[Bio slurry Storage
Organic Fertilizer] K --> L[Application
To Fields as Manure] ``` ## 4.2 Fixed Dome Type — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB IN1[Inlet Tank
Fresh Slurry] --> DIG[Digester Dome
Masonry or RCC
Underground] DIG --> GAS[Gas Space
Top of Dome
Variable Pressure] GAS --> OUT1[Gas Outlet Pipe
To Scrubber] OUT1 --> USE[Cooking Stove or Engine] DIG --> DISP[Displacement
Chamber] DISP --> OUT2[Slurry Outlet
To Compost Pit] GAS -. pressure pushes .-> DISP ``` ## 4.3 Floating Dome (Gobar Gas) — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB IN1[Inlet Pipe
Fresh Slurry] --> WELL[Brick Digester Well
Underground] WELL --> MICRO[Microbial Digestion
30 to 50 days] MICRO --> BUB[Biogas Bubbles
Rise and Accumulate] BUB --> DOME[Floating Steel Dome
Inverted MS Drum
Rises and Falls] DOME --> VALVE[Gas Outlet Valve
Constant Pressure] VALVE --> BURNER[Gas Burner or Genset] DOME -. buoyancy pushes .-> OUTLET[Slurry Overflow Outlet] OUTLET --> SLURRY[Bio slurry Storage] SLURRY --> FIELD[Field Application
Organic Manure] ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Two Types Side-by-Side ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FIXED[FIXED DOME TYPE] A1[Slurry In] --> A2[Rigid Masonry Dome] A2 --> A3[Gas Stored at Variable P] A3 --> A4[Pressure Regulator Needed] A4 --> A5[Slurry to Expansion Chamber] end subgraph FLOATING[FLOATING DOME TYPE] B1[Slurry In] --> B2[Underground Brick Well] B2 --> B3[Steel Drum Floats on Slurry] B3 --> B4[Gas Stored at Constant P] B4 --> B5[Direct Burner Connection] B5 --> B6[Slurry Overflow] end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer) ### **Q1. Differentiate between a fixed-dome and a floating-dome biogas plant in terms of (i) gas storage mechanism, (ii) gas pressure, and (iii) lifespan of the gas-holding structure.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 | Module 3 | 3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Fixed Dome (Chinese) | Floating Dome (Indian) | |---|---|---| | Gas storage | Top portion of the same underground digester dome | Separate inverted steel drum floating on slurry | | Gas pressure | **Variable** — increases as more gas is stored (driven by slurry displacement) | **Constant** — set by weight-to-area ratio of the steel dome | | Lifespan | **20–25 years** (masonry/RCC dome, no corrosion) | **8–12 years** (steel drum corrodes, needs repainting) | *(Plus any 1 more valid difference: e.g. regulator needed vs not needed, visual indication of gas present, construction cost.)* --- ### **Q2. State any three ideal conditions maintained inside an anaerobic digester for efficient biogas production.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 3 | 3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** 1. **Anaerobic (oxygen-free) environment** — must be airtight to prevent entry of O₂ which is toxic to methanogens. 2. **Mesophilic temperature range 25–40 °C** (or thermophilic 45–60 °C) — for optimal microbial activity. 3. **pH range 6.8 – 7.4** — neutral, since methanogens are highly pH-sensitive; acidification below 6.5 stalls the process. 4. **C:N ratio of feed ≈ 25 : 1 to 30 : 1** — to ensure microbes have balanced nutrition. 5. **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) of 30–60 days** — sufficient for complete digestion. *(Any three with brief justification → 3 marks.)* --- ## 📗 PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Module 3 Choice Pattern:** Answer any **ONE FULL QUESTION** (Q-A or Q-B) from the module, each carrying **14 marks** divided as 7 + 7 sub-parts. --- ### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[A(a)] With a neat functional diagram, explain the construction and working of a fixed-dome (Chinese-type) biogas plant. List any two of its advantages and two limitations.** `7 Marks` `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 3]` **Model Answer:** **Construction (3 marks):** A fixed-dome biogas plant consists of: - An **inlet/mixing tank** where dung is mixed with water in 1:1 ratio. - An **underground digester chamber** with a rigid, hemispherical masonry/RCC **dome** at the top. - An **outlet/expansion (displacement) chamber** connected by pipe to the digester. - A **gas outlet pipe** at the top of the dome leading to a **gas scrubber** (for H₂S removal) and then to the point of use. - A **safety valve / pressure release device** to protect the dome from over-pressure. - A **manhole** for periodic cleaning. **Working (3 marks):** - Fresh slurry enters the digester via the inlet and is sealed in. - Under anaerobic conditions, **four-stage microbial digestion** (hydrolysis, acidogenesis, acetogenesis, methanogenesis) takes 30–55 days. - Biogas (≈ 60 % CH₄, 38 % CO₂) collects in the **top void of the fixed dome**. - As gas accumulates, it pushes the slurry down and sideways into the **outlet/expansion chamber**. - The **height difference** between slurry levels in the digester and expansion chamber produces a **variable gas pressure** $P = \rho_s g h$, typically 0.05–0.15 bar gauge. - Gas is drawn off continuously for cooking/lighting/engine use; over-pressure releases through the safety valve. **Advantages (0.5 mark each, any 2):** - Long service life (20–25 years). - No moving parts → minimum maintenance. - Underground → no corrosion, aesthetic, space-saving. - No gas pressure regulator needed for low-pressure use. **Limitations (0.5 mark each, any 2):** - Variable gas pressure requires a **regulator** for consistent burner/engine operation. - Skilled masonry labour required → higher initial cost. - Difficult to detect gas leaks in masonry joints. - Gas storage capacity is limited (cannot expand beyond the dome's empty volume). --- **[A(b)] A community of 50 families wishes to install a floating-dome (Gobar Gas) plant. Each family supplies 8 kg of dung/day. Dung-to-water ratio = 1:1. Slurry density = 1090 kg/m³. HRT = 40 days. Biogas yield = 0.040 m³ CH₄/kg dung. CH₄ fraction = 60 %. Calculate: (i) daily slurry volume, (ii) digester volume, (iii) total daily biogas volume, (iv) daily energy in MJ.** `7 Marks` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Total daily dung mass:** $$ m_{dung} = 50 \times 8 = 400 \text{ kg/day} $$ **Step 2 — Total slurry mass (1:1 with water):** $$ m_{slurry} = 2 \times 400 = 800 \text{ kg/day} $$ **Step 3 — Daily slurry volume:** $$ Q = \frac{m_{slurry}}{\rho_s} = \frac{800}{1090} = 0.734 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 4 — Required digester volume:** $$ V = Q \times \theta = 0.734 \times 40 = 29.36 \approx 30 \text{ m}^3 $$ (Standard KVIC plant size = **30 m³**) **Step 5 — Daily methane volume:** $$ Q_{CH_4} = 0.040 \times 400 = 16.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 6 — Daily total biogas volume:** $$ Q_{biogas} = \frac{Q_{CH_4}}{f_{CH_4}} = \frac{16.0}{0.60} = 26.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 7 — Daily energy content (CV of biogas ≈ 22 MJ/m³):** $$ E_{daily} = 26.67 \times 22 = 586.7 \text{ MJ/day} $$ **Per-family per day:** $$ E_{family} = \frac{586.7}{50} = 11.7 \text{ MJ/family/day} $$ (Equivalent to ≈ 0.9 LPG cylinder/month per family.) --- ### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[B(a)] Explain the working of a floating-gas-holder type biogas plant with a functional diagram. State its four key advantages over the fixed-dome type.** `7 Marks` `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 3]` **Model Answer:** **Construction (2 marks):** A floating-gas-holder plant consists of an **underground cylindrical brick/masonry digester well** with an **inverted mild-steel drum (floating dome)** placed on top of the slurry. A **central guide rod with a top guide frame** prevents the drum from tilting. An **inlet pipe** feeds fresh slurry, an **outlet/overflow pipe** removes displaced slurry, and a **gas outlet pipe** with valve carries the biogas to the point of use. **Working (3 marks):** - Slurry is fed into the digester and undergoes **anaerobic fermentation** for 30–50 days. - Biogas bubbles rise, accumulate beneath the **floating drum**, and **lift the drum upward** by buoyancy. - As gas is consumed, the drum **descends**, pushing slurry out through the overflow pipe. - Gas pressure is **constant** because the downward force (weight of the drum) is divided by the constant area of the drum — pressure does not depend on gas volume. - A **minimum 15–20 cm of slurry** is always retained at the bottom of the drum as a gas seal. **Four Key Advantages over Fixed-Dome (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Constant gas pressure** → stable burner/engine operation, no regulator needed. 2. **Visual gas-level indication** → the height of the floating drum tells how much gas is stored. 3. **Shorter construction time** and **lower initial cost** — no need for skilled masonry dome-casting. 4. **Easy to maintain and relocate** — the steel dome can be lifted for digester cleaning; the entire drum can be replaced at end of life. --- **[B(b)] A fixed-dome Chinese-type biogas plant has its outlet/expansion chamber slurry surface **1.2 m** above the digester-base reference line. When the dome is full of gas, the slurry inside the digester is depressed by **0.6 m** below the reference line. Slurry density = 1090 kg/m³. Determine: (i) gauge gas pressure in Pa and bar, (ii) corresponding pressure in mm of water column (WC), and (iii) comment on the need for a pressure regulator.** `7 Marks` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 3]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Effective height difference (head pushing gas):** $$ h = h_{outlet} + h_{depression} = 1.2 + 0.6 = 1.8 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Gauge pressure (Pa):** $$ P_{gauge} = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot h $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 1090 \times 9.81 \times 1.8 = 19{,}242 \text{ Pa} \approx 19.24 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to bar:** $$ P_{gauge} = \frac{19{,}242}{10^5} = 0.1924 \text{ bar} \approx 0.192 \text{ bar} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to mm of water column (WC):** $$ P_{gauge} \text{ in mm WC} = h \times 1000 = 1.8 \times 1000 = 1800 \text{ mm WC} $$ (More accurately: $1.924 \times 10^4 / 9.81 = 1961$ mm WC — both interpretations accepted in valuation if the logic is clear.) **Step 5 — Comment on regulator need:** > Since the gas pressure **varies with the amount of gas stored** (drops as gas is consumed and displaced slurry returns to the digester), a **pressure regulator is mandatory** for stable operation of biogas burners and gas engines. A typical biogas regulator should be set to deliver ≈ **65–100 mm WC** at the appliance, irrespective of the dome's instantaneous pressure of 0–2000 mm WC. --- > [!WARNING] > ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Marks Are Lost** > 1. **Conflating "fixed dome" and "floating dome"** — Examiners deduct full marks if you describe the wrong dome geometry for the asked plant type. Always state: *fixed = masonry rigid, floating = movable steel drum*. > 2. **Skipping the gas pressure derivation** — In Part B numericals, you **must** show the formula $P = \rho_s g h$ and unit conversion to **bar AND mm WC**; just writing the final number in Pa loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting to add the depression height** to the outlet height in fixed-dome pressure problems — this is the single most common mistake. > 4. **Not standardising plant size** — After computing $V$, always round to a **standard KVIC size** (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 60 m³ etc.) and **mention the chosen size explicitly**. > 5. **Missing the H₂S scrubber and safety valve** in the construction diagram — these are routinely asked in 3-mark short questions. > 6. **Forgetting the 9–10 % TS slurry density** — never use water density (1000 kg/m³) for the slurry pressure column; it must be **1090 kg/m³**. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Two classical types** for KTU Module 3: **Fixed Dome (Chinese)** and **Floating Dome (Indian/Gobar Gas)**. - ✅ Fixed dome = **variable pressure, no moving parts, long life, masonry**. - ✅ Floating dome = **constant pressure, moving drum, shorter life, steel, lower cost**. - ✅ Both require a **gas scrubber** (H₂S removal) and **safety valve**. - ✅ Biogas composition: **55–65 % CH₄, 30–40 % CO₂, trace H₂S, N₂, H₂O**. - ✅ Calorific value of biogas: **20–26 MJ/m³** (methane itself = 37.5 MJ/m³). - ✅ Standard dung-to-water ratio: **1 : 1** by mass; slurry ≈ **9 % TS**, ρ ≈ **1090 kg/m³**. - ✅ Optimum **C:N ratio = 25 : 1 to 30 : 1**; pH **6.8–7.4**; HRT **30–60 days**; T **25–40 °C mesophilic**. - ✅ Digester volume formula: $V = Q \times \theta$. - ✅ Biogas generation formula: $Q_{biogas} = (Y_{CH_4} \times m_{dung}) / f_{CH_4}$. - ✅ Fixed-dome pressure: $P_{gauge} = \rho_s g h$ (variable with stored volume). - ✅ Floating-dome pressure: $P = W_{dome}/A_{dome}$ (constant by design). - ✅ KVIC standard plant sizes: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 60, 85, 140 m³. - ✅ 1 m³ biogas ≈ 0.7 kg LPG ≈ 1.5 kg firewood; **offset ≈ 2 kg CO₂/m³**. - ✅ Industrial / high-rate types: **CSTR, UASB, Anaerobic Filter, Plug Flow** — high-rate, low HRT. - ✅ Explosive limit of CH₄ in air: **5–15 %** — critical safety fact for design. - ✅ **Cattle dung** is the **gold-standard substrate** because of its near-ideal C:N ratio (~25:1).

topics.title: KVIC and Janata Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT632 - RENEWABLE ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** KVIC and Janata Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# KVIC and Janata Model Biogas Plants

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Biogas Plant — Formal Definition
A **biogas plant** is an anaerobic bio-digester that converts biodegradable organic substrates (cattle dung, kitchen waste, agricultural residue, sewage sludge, etc.) into a combustible biogas (typically **55–65% CH₄**, **30–40% CO₂**, trace H₂S, N₂, water vapor) and a nutrient-rich bio-slurry, through microbial action in the absence of oxygen.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KVIC and Janata models are the **two most widely deployed family-size biogas plants in India** and form the *de-facto standard* for the Decentralized Biogas Development Programme under MNRE (Ministry of New & Renewable Energy, Govt. of India).

### KVIC Model — Formal Definition
The **KVIC (Khadi and Village Industries Commission) Model** is a **floating-drum / constant-pressure biogas plant**, in which biogas is stored in a movable steel gas-holder (the *floating drum*) that rises and falls with the volume of gas produced. It was standardized by KVIC in the 1960s and remains the most popular biogas technology in rural India.

### Janata Model — Formal Definition
The **Janata Model** is a **fixed-dome / variable-pressure biogas plant** introduced by the Planning Commission (later propagated by KVIC) as a low-cost alternative using masonry construction. Here, biogas accumulates inside the *rigid upper dome*, displacing slurry into a compensation/displacement chamber; gas pressure varies with production rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic to Remember:**
> **KVIC** → **K**inetic (floating) drum = **K**hulega (Hindi: opens/moves)
> **Janata** → **J**hukega Nahi (Hindi: rigid/fixed dome) — the dome doesn't move.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **kitchen pressure cooker with a movable weight on the whistle**:
- The **KVIC model** is like a *pressure cooker with a floating piston* — as gas is produced, the piston (drum) lifts; gas pressure stays nearly constant because the weight of the drum is fixed.
- The **Janata model** is like a *rigid pressure cooker* — gas accumulates inside a fixed chamber; pressure rises until it is high enough to push slurry out into a side chamber (displacement tank), then equilibrates.

In both cases, the *slurry is the food* and the *microbes are the cooks*. The structure is just how we collect and store the *biogas "steam"* they produce.

### Standard Metrics and Physical Constants
- **Calorific value of biogas:** **18–25 MJ/m³** (≈ **5–7 kWh/m³**).
- **Gas yield from cattle dung:** ~**0.04 m³/kg** of fresh dung (at **HRT ≈ 30–40 days**, mesophilic temperature **30–40 °C**).
- **Daily dung requirement** per m³ of digester capacity: ≈ **25–30 kg** (cattle).
- **Recommended standard sizes:** **1 m³/day, 2 m³/day, 3 m³/day, 4 m³/day, 6 m³/day** for KVIC; Janata typically **1–4 m³/day**.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 KVIC Model — Operational Concept (Step-by-Step)

| Step | Process | Engineering Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Feed** | Cattle dung mixed with water in **1:1** ratio is fed into the inlet | Achieves Total Solids (TS) ≈ **8–10%**, ideal for methanogens |
| **2. Digestion** | Slurry flows into the underground masonry digester where anaerobic microbes act | **HRT = 30–40 days**, mesophilic range |
| **3. Gas collection** | Biogas bubbles up and collects under the **floating mild-steel drum** inverted over the slurry | Drum is coated with anti-corrosive paint; floats up/down freely on guide rails |
| **4. Gas usage** | Biogas exits via the outlet pipe connected to the top of the drum | Pressure ≈ constant ≈ **8–10 cm water column** (weight of drum) |
| **5. Effluent** | Spent slurry overflows into the outlet/expansion chamber | Nutrient-rich biofertilizer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why floating drum?** It self-indicates gas level (visual gauge), gives **constant pressure**, and avoids expensive gas compressors for small loads (lamps, stoves, small engines).

### 2.2 Janata Model — Operational Concept (Step-by-Step)

| Step | Process | Engineering Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Feed** | Dung + water slurry fed through the inlet pipe | Same dilution ratio as KVIC |
| **2. Digestion** | Slurry enters the **fixed dome-shaped masonry digester** | Brick/cement dome cast over a sand dome mould |
| **3. Gas accumulation** | Gas rises into the **rigid top dome** | Dome acts as both digester roof *and* gas storage |
| **4. Displacement** | As gas volume grows, it pushes slurry down into the **displacement/compensation chamber** connected via the outlet | Pressure rises until equilibrium with slurry head difference |
| **5. Gas usage** | Gas drawn from the top of the fixed dome via outlet pipe | Pressure ≈ **60–100 cm water column** — much higher than KVIC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Janata is "cheaper"?** No steel drum required, all masonry construction, locally buildable — but gas pressure is variable, requiring a pressure regulator for burners.

### 2.3 Comparative Analysis — Engineering Trade-off

| Parameter | KVIC Model | Janata Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Floating drum (movable) | Fixed dome (rigid) |
| **Gas pressure** | **Constant** (~8–10 cm WC) | **Variable** (~60–100 cm WC) |
| **Construction material** | Masonry digester + **steel gas holder** | Brick + cement plaster throughout |
| **Capital cost (1 m³/day)** | Higher (steel drum cost) | **30–40% lower** |
| **Gas leakage** | Lower (welded steel drum) | Higher (masonry cracks possible) |
| **Corrosion / maintenance** | Steel drum needs repainting every 2–3 yrs | Minimal structural maintenance |
| **Scrubber required?** | Usually not (low pressure) | **Mandatory** (H₂S removal at high pressure) |
| **Gas holder capacity** | Externally visible (floating drum height) | Hidden; must use level gauge |
| **Standardization** | KVIC standardized sizes (1–6 m³) | Less rigid standardization |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Bio-gas yield} = G_y \times M_d$ | Daily gas volume = yield per kg × dung mass | $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ |
| $V_d = Q \times \theta$ | Digester volume = daily input × Hydraulic Retention Time | $\text{m}^3$ |
| $\theta = \dfrac{V_d}{Q}$ | HRT (mesophilic design: **30–40 days**) | days |
| $G_y \approx 0.04 \, M_d$ | Empirical yield for cattle dung at **HRT = 40 d** | $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ |
| $V_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.6 \times V_{\text{biogas}}$ | CH₄ fraction (~60% in well-designed plant) | $\text{m}^3$ |
| $E_{\text{thermal}} = V_{\text{biogas}} \times 20 \, \text{MJ/m}^3$ | Thermal energy content (avg.) | MJ |
| $\eta_{\text{stove}} \approx 0.6$ | Biogas stove efficiency | dimensionless |
| $d \approx \left(\dfrac{4V_d}{\pi h}\right)^{0.5}$ | Digester diameter from cylindrical geometry | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Design Parameters (KTU Exam Favourite):**
> - Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) $\theta = 30\text{–}40$ days.
> - TS (Total Solids) in slurry = **8–10%**.
> - Digester temperature (mesophilic) = **30–40 °C**.
> - Biogas calorific value ≈ **20 MJ/m³** (design mean).
> - pH in digester = **6.8–7.4** (optimum **7.0–7.2**).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **KVIC plants** (1–6 m³/day) typically supply 2–4 hours of cooking gas + lighting for 1–2 rural families + organic fertilizer (~**25–40 tonnes/year** of slurry per 1 m³ plant).
- **Janata plants** are preferred in flood-prone or coastal areas (no floating metal to corrode or wash away).
- Both feed the **MNRE's National Biogas Programme** — India has over **5 million installed family-size biogas plants** (as of recent MNRE census).
- Industrial scale-ups of the Janata design form the basis of **500–5000 m³ community/industrial digesters**.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Daily Biogas Volume from Cattle Dung

Let:
- $M_d$ = Mass of fresh cattle dung fed per day (kg/day)
- $G_y$ = Specific biogas yield (m³/kg)
- $V_g$ = Daily biogas volume (m³/day)

**Step 1: Empirical yield relation.**
For cattle dung under mesophilic conditions and HRT $\theta = 40$ days:

$$G_y = k \cdot \theta$$

where $k$ is a degradation rate constant. Using standard KVIC field data: $k \approx 0.001 \, \text{m}^3/(\text{kg·day})$, thus:

$$\begin{aligned}
G_y &= 0.001 \times 40 \\
G_y &= 0.040 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* At HRT 40 d, each kg of cattle dung yields 40 litres of biogas — a figure every KTU examiner accepts.

**Step 2: Daily volume.**
$$\begin{aligned}
V_g &= G_y \times M_d \\
V_g &= 0.040 \times M_d
\end{aligned}$$

For $M_d = 25 \, \text{kg/day}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_g &= 0.040 \times 25 \\
V_g &= 1.0 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion:** A KVIC **1 m³/day** plant requires ~**25 kg fresh dung/day** (≈ 2–3 adult cattle). This is the canonical sizing rule.

---

### Derivation 2 — Digester Volume from Hydraulic Retention Time

**Step 1: Definition of HRT.**
Hydraulic Retention Time $\theta$ is the average time a slurry particle spends inside the digester:

$$\theta = \frac{V_d}{Q}$$

where:
- $V_d$ = digester working volume (m³)
- $Q$ = daily slurry feed rate (m³/day)

**Step 2: Solving for $V_d$.**
$$V_d = Q \cdot \theta$$

**Step 3: Determine $Q$.**
With dung-to-water ratio **1:1**, density of slurry ≈ **1070 kg/m³** (≈ 1.07 g/cm³). For $M_d = 25$ kg dung, total slurry mass = $25 + 25 = 50$ kg.

$$Q = \frac{50}{1070} \approx 0.0467 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 4: Apply $\theta = 40$ days.**
$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= 0.0467 \times 40 \\
V_d &\approx 1.87 \; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

*Engineering rule of thumb:* Designers typically take $V_d \approx 2 \times Q$ for KVIC 1 m³/day plant — i.e., **~2 m³ wet digester volume**, then add 20% headspace = total dome ~**2.4 m³**.

---

### Derivation 3 — Energy Available from a 1 m³/day Plant

**Step 1: Daily gas.**
$$V_g = 1.0 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2: Calorific content.**
Average calorific value of biogas $CV = 20 \, \text{MJ/m}^3$.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{th}} &= V_g \times CV \\
E_{\text{th}} &= 1.0 \times 20 = 20 \; \text{MJ/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Convert to kWh.**
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{kWh}} &= \frac{20}{3.6} \\
E_{\text{kWh}} &\approx 5.56 \; \text{kWh/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Useful cooking energy at stove efficiency $\eta = 0.6$.**
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{useful}} &= 0.6 \times 20 \\
E_{\text{useful}} &= 12 \; \text{MJ/day}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* A family cooking 2 hours/day on a 2 kW biogas stove consumes $\approx 14.4$ MJ → a 1 m³ plant supports ≈ **1 family of 4–5 persons**.

---

### Derivation 4 — Slurry Output as Bio-fertilizer

**Step 1:** Slurry output = slurry input (steady state), $Q = 0.0467 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$.

**Step 2:** Annual slurry volume:
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{slurry, yr}} &= Q \times 365 \\
&= 0.0467 \times 365 \\
&\approx 17.05 \; \text{m}^3/\text{year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Mass at 1070 kg/m³:
$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{slurry, yr}} &= 17.05 \times 1070 \\
&\approx 18{,}243 \; \text{kg/year} \\
&\approx 18.2 \; \text{tonnes/year}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Each 1 m³ plant gives ≈ **18–25 tonnes/year** of high-NPK slurry — a KTU favourite one-liner.

---

### Derivation 5 — Gas Storage Capacity of KVIC Drum

**Step 1: Drum geometry.**
A standard KVIC drum of 1 m³ gas capacity is a **mild-steel cylinder, diameter $D_g$ = 1.25 m, height $h_g$ = 0.85 m**.

**Step 2: Geometric volume.**
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{drum}} &= \frac{\pi D_g^2}{4} \times h_g \\
&= \frac{\pi (1.25)^2}{4} \times 0.85 \\
&= 1.042 \; \text{m}^3 \;\; \approx \; 1 \; \text{m}^3 \;\; \text{(as designed)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Net free gas volume (subtract water-seal volume).**
Approximately **75–85% of drum volume is usable gas** (rest displaced by guide-rail intrusion and water seal). For $V_g = 1 \, \text{m}^3$:
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{usable gas}} &= 0.80 \times 1.042 \\
&\approx 0.83 \; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 KVIC Model — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dung Collection Pit] --> B[Mixing Tank 1:1 Dung:Water]
    B --> C[Inlet Pipe]
    C --> D[Underground Masonry Digester V_d approx 2 m3]
    D --> E[Anaerobic Digestion HRT 30 to 40 days]
    E --> F[Biogas Collection]
    F --> G[Floating Mild Steel Drum Inverted Gas Holder]
    G --> H[Constant Pressure Outlet 8 to 10 cm WC]
    H --> I[Gas Scrubber H2S Removal]
    I --> J[Biogas Stove and Lamp and Engine]
    D --> K[Outlet Expansion Chamber]
    K --> L[Bio slurry Storage NPK Fertilizer]

    stepA[Dung Storage] --> stepB[Slurry Preparation]
    stepB --> stepC[Feed Pump / Manual Inlet]
    stepC --> stepD[Digester Wall RCC / Brick]
    stepD --> stepE[Gas Holder Float Guide]
    stepE --> stepF[Drum Anti-corrosive Paint]
```

### 4.2 Janata Model — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Dung + Water Slurry 1:1] --> B1[Inlet Pipe Vertical]
    B1 --> C1[Underground Masonry Fixed Dome Digester]
    C1 --> D1[Anaerobic Digestion HRT 30 to 40 days]
    D1 --> E1[Biogas Accumulation in Rigid Dome Top]
    E1 --> F1[Pressure Builds Up 60 to 100 cm WC]
    F1 --> G1[Slurry Displaced to Compensation Chamber]
    G1 --> H1[Outlet Pipe to Slurry Pit]
    E1 --> I1[Gas Outlet Pipe from Dome Apex]
    I1 --> J1[Pressure Regulator Essential]
    J1 --> K1[Biogas Burner / Lamp / Engine]
    H1 --> L1[Bio Fertilizer Collection]

    stepX[Fixed Brick Dome Cement Plaster] --> stepY[Sand Mould Base Temporary]
    stepY --> stepZ[Dome Cured 28 Days]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph K["KVIC Floating Drum Plant"]
        K1[Slurry Feed] --> K2[Digester]
        K2 --> K3[Gas Rises Under Drum]
        K3 --> K4[Drum Floats Up Gas Stored]
        K4 --> K5[Gas Drawn at Constant Pressure]
    end

    subgraph J["Janata Fixed Dome Plant"]
        J1[Slurry Feed] --> J2[Digester with Rigid Dome]
        J2 --> J3[Gas Accumulates in Dome]
        J3 --> J4[Gas Pressure Pushes Slurry to Compensation Tank]
        J4 --> J5[Gas Drawn at Variable Pressure through Regulator]
    end
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Sectional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["KVIC MODEL Vertical Section"]
        A1[Inlet Pipe] --> B1[Digester Masonry Wall]
        B1 --> C1[Slurry Level]
        D1[Floating Steel Drum Gas Holder Rises and Falls] --> E1[Gas Outlet 8 to 10 cm WC]
        F1[Outlet Expansion Chamber]
    end
    subgraph S2["JANATA MODEL Vertical Section"]
        A2[Inlet Pipe] --> B2[Fixed Masonry Dome]
        B2 --> C2[Gas Space Variable Volume]
        D2[Slurry Pushes Down to Compensation Chamber] --> E2[Outlet Pipe]
        F2[Gas Outlet Variable Pressure 60 to 100 cm WC]
    end
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **"Differentiate between the KVIC and Janata model biogas plants on the basis of (i) gas storage principle, (ii) gas pressure, and (iii) construction material."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | KVIC Model | Janata Model |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Gas storage principle | Floating movable steel drum acts as gas holder | Biogas stored in rigid fixed masonry dome |
| (ii) Gas pressure | Constant (~8–10 cm water column) | Variable (~60–100 cm water column) |
| (iii) Construction material | Brick/masonry digester + mild-steel drum | Entirely brick, cement-plastered masonry |

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **"List any three advantages and two disadvantages of the KVIC floating-drum biogas plant."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Constant gas pressure** → uniform flame at burner without regulator.
2. Visual indication of **gas volume stored** (drum height).
3. **Lower gas leakage** due to welded steel drum.
4. Easier maintenance of digester (drum removable).

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Higher capital cost** because of steel drum.
2. **Corrosion** of steel drum requires periodic repainting (every 2–3 years).
3. Drum weight limits very-large-scale designs.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Two independent choices (**Question A** and **Question B**) are given per KTU 2024 ESE pattern. Answer either.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **construction and working of the KVIC floating-drum biogas plant**. List its **merits and demerits**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A household needs **2 m³ of biogas per day** for cooking. The cattle-dung yield is 0.04 m³/kg. The dung-to-water ratio is **1:1** and slurry density is 1070 kg/m³. Design the **digester volume** for a Hydraulic Retention Time of **40 days**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Inlet pipe**: Cement/HDPE pipe, brings fresh slurry from mixing tank.
- **Digester**: Underground masonry / brick + cement plaster, cylindrical or rectangular; **V_d = 2 × Q** (slurry volume).
- **Outlet/expansion chamber**: Holds displaced slurry as gas builds.
- **Floating drum**: Mild-steel cylinder, inverted, 1.25 m dia × 0.85 m height (for 1 m³ gas); painted with **anti-corrosive bitumen/epoxy**.
- **Guide rails**: Vertical rods prevent drum tilt; allow free vertical movement.
- **Gas outlet**: Pipe at drum top leading to scrubber and appliances.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Dung + water (1:1) → inlet → digester.
2. Anaerobic digestion at **30–40 °C**, HRT **30–40 days** → biogas (CH₄ ~60%, CO₂ ~40%).
3. Gas collects under the floating drum, lifting it.
4. Gas drawn at constant pressure (8–10 cm WC) → scrubber → stove/lamp.
5. Spent slurry overflows into outlet chamber as biofertilizer.

**Merits (0.5 mark each, max 1):** Constant pressure, low leakage, visible gas storage.
**Demerits (0.5 mark each, max 1):** Higher cost, drum corrosion, periodic maintenance.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Dung requirement.**
$$M_d = \frac{V_g}{G_y} = \frac{2}{0.04} = 50 \; \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 2: Total slurry mass (1:1 dilution).**
$$M_s = 50 + 50 = 100 \; \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 3: Daily slurry volume.**
$$Q = \frac{M_s}{\rho_s} = \frac{100}{1070} = 0.0935 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 4: Digester volume at $\theta = 40$ days.**
$$V_d = Q \cdot \theta = 0.0935 \times 40 = 3.74 \; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 5: Adding 20% head-space.**
$$V_{\text{total}} = 1.20 \times 3.74 = 4.49 \; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 6: Selection.**
Adopt **standard KVIC 4 m³/day plant** (closest standard size from KVIC catalogue).

**Final Answer:** A **4 m³/day KVIC biogas plant** with $V_d \approx 3.74 \; \text{m}^3$ wet volume is required, fed by **50 kg dung + 50 L water per day**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **constructional details and working principle of the Janata fixed-dome biogas plant**. How is biogas pressure controlled? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A KVIC plant produces **1.5 m³ biogas/day**. Calculate (i) the daily thermal energy available, (ii) useful cooking energy at 60% stove efficiency, and (iii) the annual bio-slurry output (kg) if HRT = 40 days and 1:1 dilution is used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Brick masonry fixed dome** with cement plaster inside (waterproof).
- Dome built over a **temporary sand mould** that is later removed.
- **Inlet pipe** (vertical), **outlet/compensation chamber** at the side (same level as digester base).
- **Gas outlet pipe** at the apex of the dome.
- No steel components in the digester; only masonry.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Slurry fed through inlet pipe.
2. Digestion produces biogas that **rises into the rigid dome**.
3. As gas accumulates, it **pushes slurry down and out into the compensation chamber** through the outlet — this provides the gas-pressure build-up.
4. Slurry level difference between digester and compensation chamber × density × g = gas pressure.
5. Gas drawn from apex outlet through a **pressure regulator** to maintain constant pressure at burner.

**Pressure control (1 mark):**
- A **weight-loaded or spring-loaded regulator** on the gas pipe maintains constant downstream pressure.
- Alternatively, an **over-flow weir** in compensation chamber holds slurry level constant.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $V_g = 1.5 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$, $CV = 20 \; \text{MJ/m}^3$, $\eta = 0.6$.

**(i) Daily thermal energy.**
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{th}} &= V_g \times CV \\
&= 1.5 \times 20 \\
&= 30 \; \text{MJ/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Useful cooking energy.**
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{useful}} &= \eta \times E_{\text{th}} \\
&= 0.6 \times 30 \\
&= 18 \; \text{MJ/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Annual bio-slurry output.**

Dung mass: $M_d = \dfrac{1.5}{0.04} = 37.5 \; \text{kg/day}$

Slurry mass (1:1): $M_s = 75 \; \text{kg/day}$

Slurry volume: $Q = \dfrac{75}{1070} = 0.0701 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$

Digester volume: $V_d = 0.0701 \times 40 = 2.80 \; \text{m}^3$

**Annual slurry mass (steady-state, output = input):**
$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{slurry, yr}} &= 75 \times 365 \\
&= 27{,}375 \; \text{kg/year} \\
&\approx 27.4 \; \text{tonnes/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** (i) **30 MJ/day**, (ii) **18 MJ/day useful**, (iii) **≈ 27.4 tonnes/year** of bio-slurry.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (lose 1–2 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting to add head-space** (15–25%) to digester volume → marks deducted in design problems.
> 2. Confusing **gas volume** with **digester volume** — a 1 m³/day plant has **V_d ≈ 2 m³**, not 1 m³.
> 3. Using wrong HRT — must state **30–40 days** for mesophilic cattle-dung plants.
> 4. Reporting **CV of biogas** as 55 MJ/m³ (that's *pure methane*); biogas CV is **18–25 MJ/m³**.
> 5. Skipping the **scrubber** mention for Janata plants — H₂S corrodes burners; mandatory in your answer.
> 6. Drawing KVIC plant diagram **without the floating drum** — instant 2-mark cut.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **KVIC** = **floating drum** = constant pressure = steel gas holder = visually shows stored volume.
- **Janata** = **fixed dome** = variable pressure = masonry only = needs regulator & scrubber.
- **HRT (mesophilic)** = **30–40 days**; digester temperature **30–40 °C**.
- **Slurry dilution** = dung : water = **1 : 1**; **TS ≈ 8–10%**.
- **Biogas yield** from cattle dung ≈ **0.04 m³/kg** at HRT 40 days.
- **Biogas composition:** CH₄ ≈ 60%, CO₂ ≈ 40%; CV ≈ **20 MJ/m³**.
- **Optimum pH** = **7.0–7.2**.
- **Digester sizing rule:** $V_d = Q \times \theta$; add 20% head-space.
- **KVIC plant → 1 m³/day** needs ~**25 kg dung + 25 L water + ~2 m³ wet digester**.
- **Energy:** 1 m³/day plant ≈ **5.6 kWh/day** thermal, ≈ **3.3 kWh/day** useful at stove.
- **Slurry output:** ≈ **18–27 tonnes/year** per 1 m³/day plant — high-value organic fertilizer.
- **Janata plant** is **30–40% cheaper** but **higher leakage risk** and **variable pressure**.
- **Steel drum** of KVIC requires **anti-corrosive paint** every 2–3 years.
- **KVIC standard sizes:** 1, 2, 3, 4, 6 m³/day; Janata usually 1–4 m³/day.
- **In India:** National Biogas & Organic Manure Programme (NBOMP) under MNRE is the institutional promoter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomass Program in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT632 - RENEWABLE ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biomass Program in India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the "Biomass Program in India"?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the **Biomass Program in India** refers to the consolidated national framework of policies, schemes, and financial incentives administered primarily by the **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)**, **Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas (MoPNG)**, and the **Ministry of Power (MoP)** to commercially harness **biomass** — solid, liquid, and gaseous — for **power generation, thermal energy, cooking fuel, and transportation fuel** across the country.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The Biomass Program in India is the umbrella set of centrally-sponsored schemes that promote the conversion of agricultural residues (rice straw, bagasse, cotton stalk), agro-industrial waste, forestry residues, municipal solid waste (MSW), industrial effluents, and cattle dung into usable energy (electricity, heat, biogas, bio-CNG, ethanol, biodiesel) through technological interventions such as direct combustion, gasification, anaerobic digestion, and fermentation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Farmer's Waste-to-Wallet Pipeline"

Imagine a small farmer in rural Kerala who cultivates paddy and also keeps two cows. Every season he:

1. **Burns rice straw** in the field (causing pollution).
2. **Lets cow dung** pile up in the open (producing methane that escapes to the atmosphere).

The **Biomass Program in India** is essentially a government-led "**waste-to-wallet**" pipeline that gives this farmer a financed way to:

- Sell the rice straw to a **biomass power plant** (which pays him per tonne).
- Install a **biogas plant** to turn cow dung into cooking gas and organic fertilizer.
- Optionally feed surplus biogas into a **compressed biogas (CBG) plant** that sells it as automotive fuel.

In short: **Waste → Energy → Income → Lower emissions.**

---

## 1.3 Core Components of the Indian Biomass Programme

The biomass program is delivered through multiple parallel schemes. Each scheme has a defined beneficiary (farmer/industry/urban body), technology focus, and central financial assistance (CFA).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Implementing Agencies:**
> - **MNRE** — Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (overall nodal ministry).
> - **BEE** — Bureau of Energy Efficiency.
> - **IIT Delhi / IIT Bombay / TERI** — Knowledge & R&D partners.
> - **PCRA** — Petroleum Conservation Research Association (promotion).
> - **State Nodal Agencies (SNAs)** — execute on-ground installations.

### 1.3.1 Categorisation of Biomass Resources in India

| Resource Category | Examples | Typical Calorific Value (MJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Agricultural residues | Rice straw, wheat straw, sugarcane bagasse, groundnut shell | **12 – 18** |
| Agro-industrial waste | Sugarcane bagasse, rice husk, de-oiled cakes | **14 – 20** |
| Forestry residues | Firewood, sawdust, wood chips | **16 – 20** |
| Animal waste | Cow dung, poultry litter, piggery waste | **12 – 16** (biogas pathway) |
| Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) | Kitchen waste, paper, plastics fraction | **8 – 14** |
| Energy plantations | Napier grass, bamboo, *Pongamia*, *Jatropha* | **14 – 18** |

---

## 1.4 Why is the Biomass Program Critical for India?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India's Biomass Potential (as per MNRE assessments):**
> - **Surplus biomass availability:** ≈ **750 million tonnes/year**.
> - **Estimated power equivalent:** ≈ **25,000 MW** (≈ 25 GW) — equivalent to ~25 large coal plants.
> - **Distributed generation potential (off-grid):** ≈ **15,000 MW**.

India is the **2nd largest agricultural producer globally**, and roughly **30% of agro-residue is burnt in fields** — causing the well-documented **"North-Indian smog crisis"** each October–November. The biomass program therefore solves **three problems simultaneously**:

1. **Energy security** — reduces import dependence on coal/gas.
2. **Pollution control** — eliminates stubble burning.
3. **Rural income** — creates a new revenue stream for farmers ("Annadata to Urjadata").

---

## 1.5 Installed Capacity Snapshot (Approximate, 2024-25)

| Sub-sector | Installed Capacity (approx.) |
|---|---|
| Biomass power (non-bagasse) | ~700 – 1,000 MW |
| Bagasse cogeneration | ~8,500 – 9,000 MW |
| Biogas (off-grid / grid) | ~250 MW |
| Waste-to-Energy (incl. MSW) | ~600 MW |
| Biofuels (ethanol + biodiesel blending) | ~10% blending achieved (E10) |
| Compressed Biogas (CBG) plants under SATAT | 50+ commissioned; 5,000 target by **2030** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Major Schemes Under the Biomass Program

The biomass program is **not one scheme**, but a **family of schemes**. A KTU exam answer MUST list and briefly describe at least the following six flagship schemes.

### 2.1.1 Scheme on "Biomass Cogeneration (Bagasse-based)"

- **Launched:** 1990s, revamped multiple times.
- **Beneficiary:** Sugar mills.
- **Mechanism:** Sugar mills are mandated to install **high-pressure boilers + extraction-cum-condensing steam turbines** so that steam is first used for sugar processing (process heat) and then passed through a turbine for electricity.
- **Target:** All 700+ sugar mills in India to install cogeneration systems.
- **Result:** ~**8,500 MW** of bagasse cogeneration capacity installed — one of the largest distributed renewable capacities in the world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cogeneration Principle:** Sequential extraction of energy from the same fuel — first heat, then power — yields an **overall efficiency of 60–80%** compared to 25–35% for stand-alone power generation.

### 2.1.2 Scheme on "Energy from Urban, Industrial and Agricultural Wastes/Residues"

- **Launched:** 2009 (revised 2018).
- **Beneficiary:** Municipal bodies, industries, and independent power producers.
- **Sub-programmes:**
  - Power from MSW (municipal solid waste).
  - Power from industrial waste (e.g., paper mills, distilleries).
  - Power from agricultural residues (paddy straw, cotton stalk).
- **Technologies promoted:** Combustion, gasification, anaerobic digestion, refuse-derived fuel (RDF).

### 2.1.3 New National Biogas and Organic Manure Programme (NNBOMP)

- **Launched:** 2017–18 (merged earlier schemes).
- **Beneficiary:** Individual households and communities.
- **Mechanism:** Provides **central financial assistance (CFA)** for installing small biogas plants (1 m³ to 25 m³/day) that produce:
  - Cooking gas for the household.
  - Slurry as organic manure for the farm.
- **Target:** 20 million biogas plants by 2030 (as per PMUY-GOBAR-DHAN extension).

### 2.1.4 Biogas Power Generation and Thermal Application Programme (BPGTAP)

- **Target audience:** Medium and large commercial biogas plants.
- **Sizes:** 20 m³/day to 2,500 m³/day of biogas.
- **End uses:**
  - Power generation via biogas gensets (IC engines or micro-turbines).
  - Thermal application in boilers, driers, furnaces (for industries such as dairy, food processing).
- **CFA:** Capital subsidy up to ₹40 lakh per project (varies with category and capacity).

### 2.1.5 SATAT — Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation

- **Launched:** **October 2018** by MoPNG.
- **Target:** Set up **5,000 Compressed Biogas (CBG) plants** by **2030** with an annual production target of **15 million metric tonnes of CBG**.
- **Mechanism:**
  - CBG producers sell gas to oil marketing companies (OMCs) at a **uniform price**.
  - OMCs blend/pure-sell CBG through their **CNG dispensing network**.
  - Offtake guarantee provides **bankability** to the project.
- **Feedstocks:** Agricultural residue, cattle dung, MSW, press mud, distillery spent wash.

### 2.1.6 GOBAR-DHAN (Galvanizing Organic Bio-Agro Resources – Dhan)

- **Launched:** 2018 by Ministry of Drinking Water & Sanitation.
- **Focus:** Convert cattle dung and organic waste into:
  - Biogas (energy).
  - Bio-CNG (fuel).
  - Organic manure (soil health).
- **Linkage:** With Swachh Bharat Mission and SATAT.

---

## 2.2 Hierarchical Structure of the Program

```
                MINISTRY OF NEW & RENEWABLE ENERGY (MNRE)
                              │
        ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐
        │                     │                     │
   BIOGAS DIVISION      WASTE-TO-ENERGY       BIOFUEL DIVISION
        │                     │                     │
   NNBOMP              Industrial Waste         Ethanol Blending
   BPGTAP              Agricultural Waste       Biodiesel Mission
   SATAT (w/ MoPNG)    MSW Programme            National Mission
                       Cogeneration              on Biofuels
```

---

## 2.3 Energy Conversion Pathways Covered Under the Program

The Indian biomass program covers **four thermochemical/biochemical pathways**. A KTU 14-mark answer MUST show this taxonomy.

| Pathway | Process | End Products | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Combustion** | Burning biomass in boiler | Heat / Steam / Power | Sugar mills, rice mills, paper mills |
| **Gasification** | Partial oxidation (O₂-deficient) at 800–1100 °C | Producer gas (CO + H₂ + CH₄) | Decentralised power, thermal drying |
| **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** | Microbial breakdown in absence of O₂ | Biogas (50–70% CH₄) + Digestate (manure) | Cooking, CBG, manure |
| **Fermentation / Trans-esterification** | Sugar → ethanol; Oil → biodiesel | Liquid biofuels | E10/E20 petrol blending, diesel blending |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for any biomass-program numerical.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right) H_2O \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right) CO_2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right) CH_4$ | **Buswell Equation** for complete anaerobic digestion | Stoichiometric |
| 2 | $C_6 H_{12} O_6 \rightarrow 3 CH_4 + 3 CO_2$ | Glucose fermentation (special case) | 1 mol → 3 mol CH₄ |
| 3 | $Y_{biogas} = 0.04 \, V_d$ | Biogas yield from cattle dung ($V_d$ = dung in kg/day) | $0.04$ m³/kg |
| 4 | $E_{biogas} = V \cdot \rho \cdot \eta \cdot \text{CV}$ | Energy from biogas where $V$=volume, $\rho$=density, $\eta$=conversion efficiency, CV=calorific value | MJ |
| 5 | $CV_{CH_4} = 55.5$ MJ/kg | Calorific value of methane | **55.5** MJ/kg |
| 6 | $CV_{biogas} = 0.55 \cdot CV_{CH_4} + 0.45 \cdot CV_{CO_2}$ (weighted) | Calorific value of typical biogas (60% CH₄) | **~22 MJ/m³** |
| 7 | $P = \dot{m} \cdot CV \cdot \eta_{thermal} \cdot \eta_{electrical}$ | Biomass power plant output | kW or MW |
| 8 | $\eta_{cogen} = \dfrac{E_{power} + Q_{heat}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Cogeneration efficiency (typically 60–80%) | — |
| 9 | $HRT = \dfrac{V_{reactor}}{\dot{V}_{feed}}$ | Hydraulic Retention Time (AD design) | 15–40 days |
| 10 | $C:N_{ideal} \approx 20:1$ to $30:1$ | Optimum Carbon-to-Nitrogen ratio for AD | Ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** When a question asks for the **calorific value of biogas**, students often write **55.5 MJ/kg** — that is the CV of **pure methane**. Biogas is only 50–70% methane; the **CV of biogas ≈ 18–24 MJ/m³**. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction if confused.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application in India |
|---|---|
| **Sugar industry** | Cogeneration → 8.5 GW installed capacity. |
| **Dairy industry** | Cattle dung-based biogas plants at Amul cooperatives. |
| **Oil & Gas** | CBG plants in Punjab, Haryana, Maharashtra (under SATAT). |
| **Municipal corporations** | MSW-based WtE plants (Delhi, Indore, Hyderabad). |
| **Transport** | Ethanol-blended petrol (E10 achieved nationwide, E20 in progress by 2025). |
| **Defence / Railways** | Biodiesel pilot for vehicles; ethanol-coal co-firing (NTPC). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Buswell Equation for Glucose (Anaerobic Digestion)

The **Buswell Equation** is the stoichiometric backbone of any KTU biogas numerical. We derive the special case for **glucose** ($C_6 H_{12} O_6$), the model carbohydrate representing biomass.

### Step 1 — General Buswell form

For any organic substrate $C_n H_a O_b$ undergoing complete anaerobic digestion:

$$
C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right) H_2O \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right) CO_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right) CH_4
$$

**Logic:** Each carbon atom ends up either in $CO_2$ or in $CH_4$. Hydrogen balance: every 4 H atoms need 2 H₂O; $CH_4$ consumes all 4 H. Oxygen balance: $CO_2$ and $H_2O$ share the oxygen.

### Step 2 — Substitute values for glucose

For glucose: $n = 6$, $a = 12$, $b = 6$.

**Step 2a — Compute the $H_2O$ coefficient:**

$$
n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2} = 6 - \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0
$$

**Explanation:** Glucose already has the exact hydrogen-to-oxygen ratio of a carbohydrate ($a = 2b$), so no additional water is required for the reaction. The substrate supplies its own oxygen requirement.

**Step 2b — Compute the $CO_2$ coefficient:**

$$
\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} = \frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3
$$

**Explanation:** Of the 6 carbon atoms in glucose, **3 end up as carbon dioxide**.

**Step 2c — Compute the $CH_4$ coefficient:**

$$
\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} = \frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3
$$

**Explanation:** The remaining **3 carbon atoms** combine with hydrogen to form **3 methane molecules**.

### Step 3 — Final balanced equation

$$
C_6 H_{12} O_6 \rightarrow 3 CH_4 + 3 CO_2
$$

### Step 4 — Verification (atom balance)

| Element | LHS | RHS | Balanced? |
|---|---|---|---|
| C | 6 | $3 \times 1 + 3 \times 1 = 6$ | ✓ |
| H | 12 | $3 \times 4 = 12$ | ✓ |
| O | 6 | $3 \times 2 = 6$ | ✓ |

**Result:** Stoichiometric yield of methane from glucose = **3 mol CH₄ per mol glucose**.

### Step 5 — Practical mass yield

**Molecular weights:** Glucose = 180 g/mol; Methane = 16 g/mol.

$$
\text{Mass yield} = \frac{3 \times 16}{180} = 0.267 \, \frac{kg \, CH_4}{kg \, glucose}
$$

**Energy yield (using $CV_{CH_4} = 55.5$ MJ/kg):**

$$
E = 0.267 \times 55.5 = 14.81 \, \frac{MJ}{kg \, glucose}
$$

Since glucose itself has $CV \approx 15.6$ MJ/kg, the **theoretical anaerobic digestion efficiency ≈ 95%** — making it one of the most efficient biomass-to-energy pathways.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Biogas Yield from a Cattle-Dung-Based Plant

### Problem statement
A community biogas plant receives **500 kg of fresh cow dung per day**. Estimate the **daily biogas production** and **electrical energy that can be generated**.

### Step 1 — Biogas yield per kg of dung

Empirical relation for cattle dung:

$$
Y_{biogas} = 0.04 \, m^3 \, / \, kg \, dung
$$

For Indian conditions with 30 days HRT and mesophilic digestion (35 °C), this coefficient is a standard MNRE value.

### Step 2 — Daily biogas volume

$$
V_{biogas} = 500 \times 0.04 = 20 \, m^3 \, / \, day
$$

### Step 3 — Methane content and CV of biogas

Assume **60% methane** in biogas.

$$
CV_{biogas} = 0.60 \times 35.8 \, MJ/m^3 \approx 21.5 \, MJ/m^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Methane CV by volume** = **35.8 MJ/m³** (at STP). This is distinct from the mass-basis value of **55.5 MJ/kg**.

### Step 4 — Daily thermal energy

$$
E_{thermal} = V_{biogas} \times CV_{biogas} = 20 \times 21.5 = 430 \, MJ/day
$$

### Step 5 — Electrical energy (gas engine)

Assume **gas-engine electrical efficiency $\eta_{el} = 35\%$** (typical for 100 kW biogas genset):

$$
E_{electrical} = 430 \times 0.35 = 150.5 \, MJ/day
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to kWh

$$
E_{electrical} = \frac{150.5}{3.6} = 41.8 \, kWh/day
$$

### Step 7 — Continuous power rating

$$
P = \frac{41.8}{24} = 1.74 \, kW
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A 500 kg dung/day plant can continuously run a **~1.7 kW generator** — sufficient to power ~10 rural homes (with ~150 W per home average) or a community water-pump system.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cogeneration Efficiency of a Bagasse Power Plant

### Problem statement
A sugar mill consumes **100 tonnes/hour of bagasse**. The boiler produces **30 tonnes/hour of steam at 40 bar, 440 °C**. Steam first passes through the process line (extracting 12 tonnes/hr at 2.5 bar for sugar processing), and the remaining 18 tonnes/hr is expanded through a condensing turbine to generate electricity. Compute the cogeneration efficiency.

**Data:**
- $CV_{bagasse} = 8.5 \, MJ/kg$ (typical)
- Enthalpy of process steam at 2.5 bar (saturated): $h_{process} = 2,710 \, kJ/kg$
- Enthalpy of inlet steam at 40 bar, 440 °C: $h_{in} = 3,310 \, kJ/kg$
- Enthalpy of exhaust at 0.1 bar (condenser): $h_{out} = 2,150 \, kJ/kg$
- Feedwater enthalpy: $h_{fw} = 420 \, kJ/kg$

### Step 1 — Heat input from bagasse

$$
Q_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{bagasse} \times CV = \frac{100 \times 10^3 \times 8.5 \times 10^6}{3600} \, W
$$

$$
Q_{fuel} = 236.1 \, MW_{th}
$$

### Step 2 — Heat delivered to process

$$
Q_{process} = \dot{m}_{process} \times (h_{process} - h_{fw})
$$

$$
Q_{process} = \frac{12 \times 10^3 \times (2710 - 420) \times 10^3}{3600} = 76.3 \, MW_{th}
$$

### Step 3 — Electrical power generated

$$
P_{electrical} = \dot{m}_{turbine} \times (h_{in} - h_{out}) / 3600
$$

$$
P_{electrical} = \frac{18 \times 10^3 \times (3310 - 2150) \times 10^3}{3600} = 5.8 \, MW_e
$$

(Turbine mechanical + generator efficiency ≈ 90 % already implicit in enthalpy drop selection.)

### Step 4 — Cogeneration efficiency

$$
\eta_{cogen} = \frac{P_{electrical} + Q_{process}}{Q_{fuel}}
$$

$$
\eta_{cogen} = \frac{5.8 + 76.3}{236.1} = 0.348 \approx 34.8\%
$$

**Note:** The remaining ~65 % is lost in flue gas and condenser. Even with such losses, the bagasse-based cogeneration **avoids ~6 MW of grid electricity** and **replaces diesel-fired boilers** for process heat — making it economically & environmentally optimal.

> [!TIP]
> **High-yield exam point:** Modern **high-pressure (80–100 bar) cogeneration** plants achieve $\eta_{cogen} = 60$ – $80\%$ by extracting more work from steam before condensing.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Indian Schemes

| Parameter | NNBOMP | BPGTAP | SATAT (CBG) | Bagasse Cogeneration | MSW Programme |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Beneficiary** | Rural households | Industries / communities | Entrepreneurs / industries | Sugar mills | Urban local bodies |
| **Size range** | 1–25 m³/day | 20–2,500 m³/day | 5–50 tonnes/day CBG | 1–25 MW | 1–15 MW |
| **End product** | Cooking gas + manure | Power / heat | Compressed biogas | Power + process steam | Power |
| **CFA pattern** | ₹5,000–17,000 (household) | Up to ₹40 lakh | Offtake guarantee (₹46/kg) | Accelerated depreciation | Subsidy for capex |
| **Implementing ministry** | MNRE | MNRE | MoPNG | MNRE | MNRE |
| **Status (2024–25)** | ~5 M plants installed | ~250 MW installed | 50+ CBG plants | ~8.5 GW installed | ~600 MW installed |
