# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, similar]`
**Briefly explain the National Biogas and Manure Management Programme (NBMMP) and its objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- NBMMP (now restructured as **NNBOMP** under MNRE) is a centrally-sponsored scheme launched to promote **family-size biogas plants** in rural and semi-urban households.
- **Objectives:** (i) provide **clean cooking fuel** to replace firewood/LPG, (ii) produce **organic manure** (digestate) for farms, (iii) **reduce indoor air pollution**, (iv) **empower women** by reducing drudgery, and (v) reduce greenhouse-gas emissions from cattle dung.
- **Beneficiaries:** Individual households, communities, and institutions. CFA ranges from ₹5,000 to ₹17,000 per plant depending on size and region (NE/hilly states get higher subsidy).

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, similar]`
**What is SATAT? State its key targets.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation)** is a scheme launched by the **Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas (MoPNG) in October 2018**.
- It aims to set up **5,000 Compressed Biogas (CBG) production plants** in India by **2030**.
- Annual CBG production target: **15 million metric tonnes**.
- CBG produced will be sold to **oil marketing companies (OMCs)** at a **uniform price (≈ ₹46/kg)** with an offtake guarantee, ensuring project bankability.
- Feedstocks include agricultural residue, cattle dung, MSW, press mud, and distillery spent wash.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 3]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the major **schemes under the Indian Biomass Programme** with their objectives, target beneficiaries, and current status. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A community biogas plant receives **800 kg of cattle dung per day**. The plant uses a **mesophilic digester (35 °C, 30-day HRT)** with **55% methane content** in biogas. The biogas fuels a **gas-engine generator of 30% electrical efficiency**. Calculate:
  - (i) Daily biogas volume produced.
  - (ii) Daily electrical energy output (kWh).
  - (iii) Continuous electrical power rating (kW).
  - **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A(a)

The Indian biomass program is implemented through multiple complementary schemes:

1. **Biomass Cogeneration Programme (Bagasse-based):**
   - Promotes high-pressure cogeneration in **sugar mills**.
   - Bagasse (a sugar-mill by-product) is burnt in boilers to produce process steam and electricity.
   - **Status:** ~**8,500 MW installed**; nearly all major sugar mills covered.
   - **Benefit:** Mills become **net exporters of electricity** to the grid during the crushing season.

2. **Programme on Energy from Urban, Industrial and Agricultural Wastes:**
   - Targets **MSW**, **industrial effluent**, and **agro-residue**.
   - Technologies: combustion, gasification, RDF.
   - **Status:** ~**600 MW installed**.

3. **New National Biogas and Organic Manure Programme (NNBOMP):**
   - Small (1–25 m³/day) biogas plants for **rural households**.
   - **Subsidy** up to ₹17,000 per plant.
   - **Status:** ~**5 million plants** installed.

4. **Biogas Power Generation and Thermal Application Programme (BPGTAP):**
   - Medium-to-large biogas plants (20–2,500 m³/day).
   - Subsidy up to **₹40 lakh per project**.
   - End uses: power generation or thermal (boilers/driers).

5. **SATAT (MoPNG, 2018):**
   - 5,000 CBG plants target by 2030.
   - Offtake guarantee by OMCs at uniform price.

6. **GOBAR-DHAN:**
   - Converts cattle dung and organic waste into biogas and manure.
   - Linked with Swachh Bharat Mission.

7. **National Mission on Biofuels:**
   - Targets **E20 ethanol blending by 2025–26** and **B5 biodiesel blending**.
   - Includes 2G ethanol from lignocellulosic biomass.

---

### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{dung} = 800 \, kg/day$
- Biogas yield coefficient $Y = 0.04 \, m^3/kg$ dung
- Methane content = $55\%$
- Generator electrical efficiency $\eta_{el} = 30\%$
- $CV_{CH_4} = 35.8 \, MJ/m^3$ (volumetric)

**Step (i) — Daily biogas volume:**

$$
V_{biogas} = 800 \times 0.04 = 32 \, m^3 / day
$$

**Step (ii) — Methane volume and energy:**

$$
V_{CH_4} = 0.55 \times 32 = 17.6 \, m^3 / day
$$

$$
E_{thermal} = 17.6 \times 35.8 = 630.1 \, MJ / day
$$

$$
E_{electrical} = 630.1 \times 0.30 = 189.0 \, MJ / day
$$

Convert to kWh:

$$
E_{electrical} = \frac{189.0}{3.6} = 52.5 \, kWh / day
$$

**Step (iii) — Continuous power rating:**

$$
P = \frac{52.5}{24} = 2.19 \, kW
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Biogas volume: **32 m³/day**
- Electrical energy: **52.5 kWh/day**
- Continuous power: **≈ 2.2 kW**

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 3, Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **SATAT scheme** for Compressed Biogas. Discuss its role in India's energy transition and list the **major feedstocks** that can be used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sugar mill cogeneration plant consumes **120 tonnes/hour of bagasse** with $CV = 8.5 \, MJ/kg$. The boiler generates **35 tonnes/hour of steam at 45 bar, 450 °C**. Process steam extraction = **14 tonnes/hr at 3 bar** (saturated). Remaining steam expands to a condenser at 0.1 bar.
  - Inlet enthalpy $h_{in} = 3{,}330 \, kJ/kg$
  - Process steam enthalpy $h_{process} = 2{,}725 \, kJ/kg$
  - Exhaust enthalpy $h_{out} = 2{,}160 \, kJ/kg$
  - Feedwater enthalpy $h_{fw} = 430 \, kJ/kg$

  Calculate the **cogeneration efficiency** of the plant. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**SATAT — Compressed Biogas Programme:**

The **Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation (SATAT)** scheme was launched on **4 October 2018** by the **Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas (MoPNG)** to establish a sustainable compressed biogas (CBG) ecosystem in India.

**Key Features:**
- **Target:** 5,000 CBG plants by 2030; 15 MMT annual CBG production.
- **Pricing:** Uniform CBG sale price to OMCs ≈ **₹46/kg**.
- **Offtake guarantee** ensures project bankability — a unique feature.
- **Implementing agencies:** OMCs (IOCL, BPCL, HPCL, GAIL) act as **aggregators/offtakers**.

**Role in India's Energy Transition:**
- Reduces **LNG import dependency** (~₹2 lakh crore annual import bill for natural gas).
- Provides a **rural income stream** for farmers (residue collection).
- Lowers **carbon emissions** — CBG is a renewable substitute for fossil CNG.
- Strengthens **waste-to-wealth** narrative and circular economy.

**Major Feedstocks:**
1. Agricultural residues (paddy straw, wheat straw, maize stover).
2. Cattle dung and poultry litter.
3. Press mud (sugar industry by-product).
4. Distillery spent wash (molasses-based ethanol plants).
5. Municipal solid waste (organic fraction).
6. Sewage sludge.

(Diagrams already covered in SECTION 4 — student should reproduce a simplified flowchart.)

---

### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{bagasse} = 120{,}000 \, kg/hr$
- $CV_{bagasse} = 8.5 \, MJ/kg$
- $\dot{m}_{steam,in} = 35{,}000 \, kg/hr$
- $\dot{m}_{steam,process} = 14{,}000 \, kg/hr$
- $\dot{m}_{steam,turbine} = 21{,}000 \, kg/hr$

**Step 1 — Heat input from bagasse:**

$$
Q_{fuel} = 120{,}000 \times 8.5 = 1{,}020{,}000 \, MJ/hr
$$

$$
Q_{fuel} = \frac{1{,}020{,}000}{3.6} = 283{,}333 \, kW = 283.3 \, MW
$$

**Step 2 — Process heat delivered:**

$$
Q_{process} = 14{,}000 \times (2725 - 430) = 14{,}000 \times 2{,}295 = 32{,}130{,}000 \, kJ/hr
$$

$$
Q_{process} = \frac{32{,}130{,}000}{3600} = 8{,}925 \, kW = 8.93 \, MW
$$

**Step 3 — Electrical power from turbine:**

$$
P_{el} = 21{,}000 \times (3330 - 2160) = 21{,}000 \times 1170 = 24{,}570{,}000 \, kJ/hr
$$

$$
P_{el} = \frac{24{,}570{,}000}{3600} = 6{,}825 \, kW = 6.83 \, MW
$$

**Step 4 — Cogeneration efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{cogen} = \frac{P_{el} + Q_{process}}{Q_{fuel}} = \frac{6.83 + 8.93}{283.3}
$$

$$
\eta_{cogen} = \frac{15.76}{283.3} = 0.0556 \approx 5.56\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why this efficiency looks low:** The bagasse has high moisture content (≈ 50%), so a large fraction of $Q_{fuel}$ is lost as **flue-gas latent heat**. With **high-pressure (80–100 bar)** boilers and **drying of bagasse** before combustion, real-world cogeneration efficiency can reach **60–80%**. In this textbook problem the low figure reflects the simplified heat-balance approach.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Confusing **volumetric CV** (35.8 MJ/m³) with **mass CV** (55.5 MJ/kg) of methane — guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **feedwater enthalpy subtraction** when computing process heat (must use $h_{process} - h_{fw}$, not just $h_{process}$).
> - **Mistake 3:** Writing only the **electrical efficiency** instead of **cogeneration (total) efficiency** — full 0 marks on that subpart.
> - **Mistake 4:** Not listing **both ministries (MNRE + MoPNG)** when asked about implementing agency — scheme names get confused.
> - **Mistake 5:** Missing the **stubble-burning link** in any open-ended answer — examiners expect this real-world hook.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Biomass Program in India**

- **Nodal Ministry:** MNRE (overall); MoPNG (SATAT-CBG); MoDWS (GOBAR-DHAN).
- **Total biomass power potential in India:** ≈ **25 GW** (as per MNRE assessments).
- **Installed capacity (2024-25):** Biomass + bagasse cogeneration ≈ **9.5 GW**.
- **Buswell Equation** is the master stoichiometric tool for any AD numerical.
- **Biogas yield from cattle dung:** ≈ **0.04 m³/kg dung/day**.
- **CV of pure methane:** **55.5 MJ/kg (mass)** or **35.8 MJ/m³ (volume at STP)**.
- **CV of typical biogas (60% CH₄):** ≈ **22 MJ/m³**.
- **SATAT** = 5,000 CBG plants by 2030; CBG price ₹46/kg.
- **NNBOMP** targets household biogas; CFA up to ₹17,000.
- **BPGTAP** targets commercial biogas; CFA up to ₹40 lakh.
- **Bagasse cogeneration efficiency** can reach 60–80% with high-pressure systems.
- **Glucose digestion:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 → 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$ — perfectly stoichiometric.
- **Energy yield from AD of glucose** ≈ 14.8 MJ/kg (theoretical).
- **HRT for mesophilic AD:** 15–40 days.
- **Optimum C:N ratio for AD:** 20:1 to 30:1.
- **Ethanol blending:** E10 achieved; E20 target by 2025–26.
- **Two parallel ministries (MNRE & MoPNG)** — frequently confused; remember MoPNG handles **CBG** (because it displaces natural gas).
- **Real-world hook:** Stubble burning in Punjab/Haryana is **directly** targeted by the agricultural-residue-to-power scheme — quote this in any application question.
- **Cogeneration ≠ Power-only:** Always compute **total efficiency** $(P_{el} + Q_{process}) / Q_{fuel}$, not just electrical.
- **"Annadata to Urjadata"** slogan = biomass program philosophy (food-giver → energy-giver).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cell - Principle of Operation, Classification, Conversion Efficiency and Losses, Applications
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT632 - RENEWABLE ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cell - Principle of Operation, Classification, Conversion Efficiency and Losses, Applications

---

# 🔋 FUEL CELL — Principle of Operation, Classification, Conversion Efficiency, Losses & Applications

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **fuel cell** is an *electrochemical energy conversion device* that generates electricity directly from a chemical reaction between a **fuel** (commonly hydrogen) and an **oxidant** (commonly oxygen), producing electricity, water, and heat as by-products — *without combustion* and *without intermediate thermal-to-mechanical conversion*.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a fuel cell is classified under the broader family of **Direct Energy Conversion (DEC)** devices. Unlike a conventional thermal power plant (where chemical energy → heat → mechanical → electrical), a fuel cell skips the Carnot-limited thermal loop and converts chemical free energy into electrical work **isothermally**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference State Constants (must memorize):**
> - Faraday's Constant: **$F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$**
> - Universal Gas Constant: **$R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$**
> - Standard Temperature & Pressure: **$T = 298.15 \text{ K}$, $P = 1 \text{ atm}$**

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Battery That Never Dies"

Imagine a **conventional battery** (like your phone battery) — it stores energy chemically inside sealed compartments. Once reactants are depleted, you must **recharge it** by forcing current back through it. Now imagine a battery where you could simply **keep feeding it hydrogen from a tap** (like a gas stove) and it would **never stop producing electricity** as long as fuel and oxygen are supplied. That is *exactly* a fuel cell.

> 🎯 **Key Analogy Mapping:**
>
> | Battery Concept | Fuel Cell Equivalent |
> |---|---|
> | Anode (stores lithium) | Anode (receives $H_2$ continuously) |
> | Cathode (stores metal oxide) | Cathode (receives $O_2$ continuously) |
> | Sealed body | Electrolyte membrane |
> | Recharge cycle | Refill the fuel tank |

The **fundamental difference**: A battery is a *closed system* (finite reactants), a fuel cell is an *open system* (continuous reactant flow).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### ⚙️ Principle of Operation — The 7-Stage Logic Chain

A fuel cell operates on the **reverse principle of electrolysis of water**. The following is the structured operational logic:

1. **Fuel Delivery:** Hydrogen ($H_2$) is fed to the **anode** side via flow channels.
2. **Oxidant Delivery:** Oxygen ($O_2$ from air) is fed to the **cathode** side.
3. **Anode Half-Reaction (Oxidation):** At the catalyst surface (typically Pt), $H_2$ molecules split into protons and electrons.
4. **Electrolyte Transport:** Protons ($H^+$) migrate through the electrolyte toward the cathode.
5. **External Circuit:** Electrons ($e^-$) are forced through the **external load** (this is the useful DC current).
6. **Cathode Half-Reaction (Reduction):** $O_2$ combines with incoming protons and electrons to form $H_2O$.
7. **By-product Removal:** Water and heat are expelled from the cathode side.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Cell Reaction (for $H_2/O_2$ cell):**
>
> $$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat}$$
>
> The electrons that flow externally constitute the **electric current**.

---

### 📋 Classification of Fuel Cells (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask classification tables in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks). Memorize the electrolyte + operating temperature + efficiency of each type.

| Type | Full Name | Electrolyte | Operating Temp. | Efficiency | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AFC** | Alkaline Fuel Cell | KOH solution | 60–120 °C | 60–70% | Space (Apollo missions) |
| **PEMFC** | Proton Exchange Membrane | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | 60–100 °C | 40–60% | Automobiles (Toyota Mirai) |
| **PAFC** | Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell | Liquid $H_3PO_4$ | 150–200 °C | 36–42% | Stationary CHP |
| **MCFC** | Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell | Molten $(Li/K)_2CO_3$ | 600–700 °C | 50–60% | Large-scale power plants |
| **SOFC** | Solid Oxide Fuel Cell | YSZ ceramic | 800–1000 °C | 50–65% | Industrial power |

**Alternate Classification Axes (often asked):**
- **By Operating Temperature:** Low-T (AFC, PEMFC), Medium-T (PAFC), High-T (MCFC, SOFC).
- **By Fuel Used:** Hydrogen, Methanol (DMFC), Hydrocarbon-reformed.
- **By Direct/Indirect:** Direct fuel cells vs. Fuel cells requiring external reformer.

---

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These equations are MUST-KNOW for KTU ESE Numerical Problems.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Gibbs free energy → EMF | $\Delta G$ in J, $E$ in V |
| 2 | $E^0 = -\dfrac{\Delta G^0}{nF}$ | Standard reversible EMF | V |
| 3 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ | Thermodynamic efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{th,HHV} = 0.83$ (for $H_2/O_2$) | Theoretical max on HHV basis | — |
| 5 | $\eta_{th,LHV} = 0.94$ (for $H_2/O_2$ at 25°C) | Theoretical max on LHV basis | — |
| 6 | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}$ | Nernst Equation (real EMF) | V |
| 7 | $V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ | Actual cell voltage (loss equation) | V |
| 8 | $\eta_{act} = a + b \log i$ | Tafel equation (activation loss) | V |
| 9 | $\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot r$ | Ohmic loss (V = IR) | V |
| 10 | $P_{stack} = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Stack power output | W |

---

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fuel cells are critical in the **Hydrogen Economy roadmap**. Real-world deployments include:
- **NASA Space Shuttle** — used AFCs producing drinking water as a by-product for astronauts.
- **Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo** — PEMFC-based fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs).
- **Bloom Energy Servers** — SOFC-based data center power.
- **Remote Telecom Towers** — AFC backup power in developing regions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔬 Derivation 1 — Reversible Cell EMF from Thermodynamics

For the overall hydrogen–oxygen reaction:

$$H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2} O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)$$

The **maximum useful electrical work** obtainable from a constant-temperature, constant-pressure electrochemical cell equals the **Gibbs free energy change** $\Delta G$.

**Step 1:** Relate electrical work to charge and EMF.

$$\Delta G = -W_{elec, max} = -nFE$$

where $n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction ($n = 2$ for $H_2$).

**Step 2:** Solve for the reversible (open-circuit) EMF.

$$\boxed{E^0 = -\frac{\Delta G^0}{nF}}$$

**Step 3:** Plug in standard values for the $H_2/O_2$ reaction.

At 25 °C, $\Delta G^0 = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
E^0 &= -\frac{(-237{,}200 \text{ J/mol})}{(2)(96{,}485 \text{ C/mol})} \\
E^0 &= \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970} \\
E^0 &= 1.229 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This **1.229 V** is the *theoretical open-circuit voltage* of a single $H_2/O_2$ fuel cell. In practice, operating voltage drops to **0.6–0.8 V** due to losses.

---

### 🔬 Derivation 2 — Thermodynamic (Ideal) Conversion Efficiency

**Step 1:** Define thermodynamic efficiency as the ratio of useful work output to total energy input.

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Electrical work out}}{\text{Heat of combustion in}} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that for combustion of $H_2$:

$$\Delta H_{HHV} = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad (\text{Higher Heating Value, water as liquid})$$

$$\Delta H_{LHV} = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad (\text{Lower Heating Value, water as vapor})$$

**Step 3:** Compute HHV-based efficiency.

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th, HHV} &= \frac{-237.2}{-285.8} \\
&= 0.8299 \\
&\approx 83\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute LHV-based efficiency.

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th, LHV} &= \frac{-237.2}{-241.8} \\
&= 0.9809 \\
&\approx 98\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the difference?** The HHV includes the latent heat of condensation of product water. PEMFCs operate below 100 °C, so the LHV basis is more physically relevant for low-temperature cells.

---

### 🔬 Derivation 3 — Nernst Equation (Operating EMF)

The Nernst equation gives the EMF under non-standard temperature/pressure.

**Step 1:** Start from the fundamental relation between Gibbs free energy and reaction quotient $Q$.

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0$.

$$\begin{aligned}
-nFE &= -nFE^0 + RT \ln Q \\
nFE &= nFE^0 - RT \ln Q \\
E &= E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Write $Q$ for the $H_2/O_2$ reaction. At the cathode, $H_2O$ may be in vapor or liquid form.

$$Q = \frac{[H_2O]}{[H_2][O_2]^{1/2}}$$

Writing in terms of partial pressures:

$$\boxed{E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)}$$

**Step 4:** At 25 °C, with all gases at 1 atm and liquid water product:

$$E = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

---

### 🔬 Derivation 4 — Polarization Losses (Voltage Drop Equation)

Actual cell voltage is always *less* than the reversible EMF. The total loss is decomposed into three categories:

$$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

#### (a) Activation Losses — *Tafel Equation*

Activation loss arises from the **kinetic barrier** of charge transfer at electrodes.

$$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right) = a + b \log(i)$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = charge transfer coefficient (typically 0.5)
- $i_0$ = exchange current density
- $a, b$ = Tafel constants

#### (b) Ohmic Losses — *Linear in Current*

$$\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R_{internal} = i \cdot \frac{l}{\sigma A}$$

where $\sigma$ = electrolyte conductivity, $l$ = electrolyte thickness.

#### (c) Concentration Losses — *Mass Transport Limit*

When current is high, reactant supply becomes diffusion-limited.

$$\eta_{conc} = -\frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(1 - \frac{i}{i_L}\right)$$

where $i_L$ = limiting current density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Quick Reference (Polarization Curve):**
> - Low current → **Activation loss dominates** (steep initial drop)
> - Medium current → **Ohmic loss dominates** (linear drop)
> - High current → **Concentration loss dominates** (sharp cliff)

---

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Voltage Loss Summary

| Loss Type | Origin | Dependence | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Activation | Slow electrode kinetics | $\propto \log(i)$ | Use Pt catalyst, higher T |
| Ohmic | Ionic + electronic resistance | $\propto i$ (linear) | Thinner membrane, higher $\sigma$ |
| Concentration | Mass transport limit | $\propto 1/(1-i/i_L)$ | Higher flow rate, better GDL |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
*Define a fuel cell. What is the basic principle of its operation?*

**Model Answer:**
A fuel cell is an **electrochemical device** that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen) **directly into electrical energy**, with water and heat as by-products, without combustion.

**Basic Principle:**
At the **anode**, hydrogen is oxidized (loses electrons):
$$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$

At the **cathode**, oxygen is reduced (gains electrons):
$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$$

Protons travel through the electrolyte; electrons flow through the external circuit producing current. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*List any three differences between a fuel cell and a battery.*

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Fuel Cell | Battery |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reactants supplied externally (open system) | Reactants sealed inside (closed system) |
| 2 | No recharging needed — just refill fuel | Requires recharging after depletion |
| 3 | Continuous power as long as fuel is supplied | Limited capacity |
| 4 | Higher specific energy (hydrogen) | Lower energy density |
| 5 | Conversion efficiency 40–60% | Coulombic efficiency 80–99% but energy-limited |

**(3 marks — any 3 points with brief explanation)**

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### ✍️ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** with a neat sketch and electrode reactions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for the $H_2/O_2$ fuel cell and compute the reversible EMF at 80 °C when $P_{H_2} = 3 \text{ atm}$, $P_{O_2} = 5 \text{ atm}$, and product water is in vapor form at 1 atm. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**1. Components of PEMFC:**
- **Anode side:** Hydrogen fuel inlet, flow channels, Pt catalyst layer.
- **Cathode side:** Air/oxygen inlet, flow channels, Pt catalyst.
- **Membrane (Nafion):** Solid polymer electrolyte, conducts $H^+$ ions only.

**2. Electrode Reactions:**
- Anode (oxidation):
$$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- \quad (E^0 = 0 \text{ V})$$
- Cathode (reduction):
$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O \quad (E^0 = +1.229 \text{ V})$$
- Net reaction:
$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

**3. Working Principle (stepwise):**
1. $H_2$ gas flows through anode channels and contacts the Pt catalyst, where it splits into $2H^+$ and $2e^-$.
2. Protons migrate through the **proton-conducting membrane** to the cathode.
3. Electrons are blocked by the membrane and forced through the **external circuit** — generating DC current.
4. At the cathode, $O_2$ from air, arriving protons, and returning electrons combine on the Pt catalyst to form $H_2O$.
5. Water is expelled from the cathode; waste heat is removed by a cooling loop.

**4. Key Features (1 mark):**
- Low operating temperature (60–100 °C) → fast start-up.
- Solid electrolyte → no leakage/corrosion.
- High power density (~1 W/cm²).

**(7 marks — component identification, reactions, working, features)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Write the Nernst equation for $H_2/O_2$ cell:**

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

Given: $T = 80 + 273 = 353 \text{ K}$, $n = 2$, $E^0 = 1.229 \text{ V}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute pressure values:**

$$E = 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} \ln\left(\frac{(3)(5)^{1/2}}{1}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature factor:**

$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = \frac{2934.84}{192{,}970} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the logarithm:**

$$\frac{(3)(5)^{1/2}}{1} = 3 \times 2.236 = 6.708$$

$$\ln(6.708) = 1.903$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$\frac{RT}{nF}\ln(\cdots) = (0.01521)(1.903) = 0.02895 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Final EMF:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 1.229 + 0.02895 \\
&= 1.258 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Higher pressure and higher temperature *increase* the reversible EMF slightly compared to standard 25 °C, 1 atm conditions. This is why high-pressure PEMFC systems (e.g., 3 atm) show modestly better performance.

**(7 marks — Nernst derivation 4 marks + numerical substitution 3 marks)**

---

#### ✍️ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Classify fuel cells based on **electrolyte type and operating temperature**. Compare the characteristics of **PEMFC and SOFC** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the various **losses occurring in a fuel cell** with the help of a **polarization curve**. Derive the **Tafel equation** for activation loss. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Classification Table (3 marks):**

| Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temp. | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| AFC | KOH (alkaline) | 60–120 °C | 60–70% |
| PEMFC | Polymer (Nafion) | 60–100 °C | 40–60% |
| PAFC | Phosphoric acid | 150–200 °C | 36–42% |
| MCFC | Molten carbonate | 600–700 °C | 50–60% |
| SOFC | Solid oxide (YSZ) | 800–1000 °C | 50–65% |

**PEMFC vs SOFC Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | PEMFC | SOFC |
|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | Polymer membrane | YSZ ceramic |
| Charge carrier | $H^+$ (proton) | $O^{2-}$ (oxide ion) |
| Operating temp. | 60–100 °C | 800–1000 °C |
| Start-up time | Fast (minutes) | Slow (hours) |
| Catalyst | Platinum required | Nickel-based (no Pt) |
| Fuel flexibility | Pure $H_2$ only | $H_2$, CO, $CH_4$ |
| Efficiency | 40–60% | 50–65% |
| Application | Automobiles | Power plants, CHP |
| Water management | Critical (humidification) | Produced at anode |

**(7 marks — 3 classification + 4 comparison)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Polarization Curve Description (2 marks):**

The polarization curve plots cell voltage $V_{cell}$ vs current density $i$. It has three distinct loss regions:
- **Region I (Low current):** Activation loss dominates.
- **Region II (Medium current):** Ohmic loss dominates (linear).
- **Region III (High current):** Concentration loss dominates (sharp fall).

**Loss Categories (3 marks):**

1. **Activation Loss:** Barrier to charge transfer at electrode. Reduces voltage at low current.
2. **Ohmic Loss:** Resistance to ion flow through electrolyte and electron flow through electrodes. Linear with current.
3. **Concentration Loss:** Depletion of reactants at high current. Causes voltage collapse near $i_L$.

**Tafel Equation Derivation (2 marks):**

From the Butler–Volmer equation in the high-overpotential limit, the activation overpotential $\eta_{act}$ relates to current density $i$ by:

$$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

Defining $a = -\frac{RT}{\alpha nF}\ln(i_0)$ and $b = \frac{2.303 RT}{\alpha nF}$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{act} = a + b \log(i)}$$

**(7 marks — curve 2 + losses 3 + Tafel 2)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Nernst vs Tafel:** Students often mix up the Nernst equation (thermodynamic, relates to concentration/pressure) with Tafel equation (kinetic, relates to activation overpotential).
> 2. **HHV vs LHV Confusion:** Always state which heating value you are using when computing efficiency. PEMFC efficiency is conventionally reported on LHV basis.
> 3. **Sign Convention Errors:** Gibbs free energy for the cell reaction is **negative** ($\Delta G < 0$); the EMF equation is $E^0 = -\Delta G^0 / nF$. Forgetting the minus sign gives a negative voltage.
> 4. **Forgetting $n$:** In the Nernst equation, $n$ is the *electrons transferred per mole of reaction*, not per molecule of fuel. For $H_2/O_2$, $n = 2$.
> 5. **Units of $R$ and $F$:** When $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F$ must be in C/mol (not A·h). Mixing units causes 10× errors.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fuel Cell**

- ✅ Fuel cell = **direct electrochemical converter** (no combustion, no Carnot limit).
- ✅ Net reaction: $H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$ + electricity + heat.
- ✅ Anode = oxidation of fuel; Cathode = reduction of oxidant.
- ✅ Five major types: **AFC, PEMFC, PAFC, MCFC, SOFC** (memorize the electrolyte + temperature for each).
- ✅ Reversible EMF for $H_2/O_2$ = **1.229 V** at 25 °C, 1 atm.
- ✅ Theoretical efficiency: **83% (HHV)** or **98% (LHV)**.
- ✅ Nernst equation: $E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$.
- ✅ Three losses: **Activation** (Tafel), **Ohmic** (linear), **Concentration** (mass transport).
- ✅ Actual operating voltage = **0.6–0.8 V/cell** (much less than 1.229 V).
- ✅ PEMFC = automobile applications; SOFC = stationary power; AFC = space.
- ✅ Faraday's constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol is critical for all derivations.
- ✅ Fuel cells are **open systems**; batteries are **closed systems**.
- ✅ KTU favorite derivations: **Nernst equation, Tafel equation, EMF, and efficiency**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT632 - RENEWABLE ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Energy - Hydrogen Production, Electrolysis, Thermochemical Methods, Hydrogen Storage and Utilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hydrogen as an Energy Carrier

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Hydrogen is the lightest, most abundant chemical element in the universe and is positioned in renewable energy engineering as a **secondary energy carrier** (or energy vector) — not a primary energy source. It stores and transports energy derived from other sources, releasing it on demand via combustion or electrochemical conversion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Energy **Source** vs. Energy **Carrier**
> - Energy **Source** = Naturally occurring (sun, wind, coal, uranium).
> - Energy **Carrier** = Produced from a source to store/move energy (hydrogen, electricity, methane).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Hydrogen Energy Metrics (Memorize these for KTU):**
> - **Higher Heating Value (HHV) = 142 MJ/kg** (water produced as liquid)
> - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) = 120 MJ/kg** (water produced as vapor)
> - **Energy density by mass: ~3× that of gasoline**
> - **Energy density by volume: very low (≈ 8.5 MJ/L liquid H₂ vs 32 MJ/L gasoline)** ← the storage challenge
> - **Ignition energy: 0.02 mJ** (extremely low → safety concern)
> - **Flammability range in air: 4–75 % by volume** (very wide)

### 1.2 The "Hydrogen Economy" Concept

A future energy system in which **H₂ is produced from renewable sources, stored, transported, and used cleanly** across power, heat, transport, and industry sectors. The closed loop is:

> Renewable Source → **Hydrogen Production** → **Storage & Transport** → **End Use** → Water (then loop closes)

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Read this once, remember forever)

> **Analogy — Hydrogen is the "Battery of Gases":**
> Think of a rechargeable lithium-ion battery: you **charge** it with electricity, store energy as chemical potential, then **discharge** later as electricity. Hydrogen works identically: you spend renewable electricity to split water (charge), pump the gas into a tank (store), and later run it through a fuel cell to recover electricity (discharge). The difference — hydrogen is **not bound to a fixed location**; it can be shipped across oceans in tanker trucks and stored for months. Electricity cannot do that.

### 1.4 The Three Color Codes of Hydrogen (Very High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Color Classification (industry standard):**
> - **Grey H₂** — from steam-methane reforming (SMR) of natural gas; **CO₂ emitted**.
> - **Brown/Black H₂** — from coal gasification; **high CO₂ emissions**.
> - **Blue H₂** — Grey/Brown H₂ + **Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS)**.
> - **Green H₂** — from **renewable-powered electrolysis**; **zero CO₂**; the KTU 2024 syllabus "target."
> - **Pink H₂** — from nuclear-powered electrolysis.
> - **Turquoise H₂** — from methane pyrolysis (solid carbon as by-product).

### 1.5 Physical & Chemical Properties Snapshot

| Property | Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Atomic number | 1 | Lightest element |
| Molecular form | H₂ (diatomic) | Low density driving storage challenge |
| Boiling point | −252.87 °C (20.28 K) | Requires cryogenic liquefaction |
| Density (STP gas) | **0.0899 kg/m³** | 1/10th of air → rises/disperses fast |
| Flame temperature in air | 2,045 °C | High process-heat potential |
| Auto-ignition temperature | 500 °C | Lower than gasoline (≈ 280 °C actually higher) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hydrogen Production — Classification

Hydrogen can be produced from **fossil fuels, water, or biomass**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes three primary pathways:

#### (A) Electrolytic Route (Water Electrolysis)
Electrical energy splits water into H₂ and O₂.

$$2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \xrightarrow{\text{Electrical Energy}} 2\text{H}_{2(g)} + \text{O}_{2(g)}$$

**Three commercial electrolyzer technologies** (high-yield):

1. **Alkaline Electrolyzer (AEL)** — mature, cheap, uses KOH electrolyte, 60–70 % efficient, low current density.
2. **Polymer Electrolyte Membrane (PEM)** — pure water, fast response, couples well with intermittent renewables, 70–80 % efficient.
3. **Solid Oxide Electrolyzer Cell (SOEC)** — high temperature (700–850 °C), highest efficiency (85–95 %), uses steam.

#### (B) Thermochemical Route
High-temperature heat (from solar concentrators or nuclear) drives chemical cycles that split water without electrolysis. The most referenced cycle is the **Sulfur–Iodine (S-I) cycle**.

#### (C) Reforming Route (briefly)
- **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR):** $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2$
- **Partial Oxidation of Hydrocarbons**
- **Biomass Gasification**

### 2.2 Electrolysis Thermodynamics — The Core Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two critical voltages — frequently confused in exams:**
> - **Reversible (ideal) voltage: E° = 1.229 V** — derived from **Gibbs free energy**.
> - **Thermoneutral voltage: V_th = 1.481 V** — derived from **enthalpy**.
> - At V < 1.229 V → no reaction.
> - At 1.229 V < V < 1.481 V → reaction runs but needs extra heat input.
> - At V > 1.481 V → reaction self-sustains thermally.

#### Derivation of Reversible Voltage

The reversible cell voltage is obtained from the **change in Gibbs free energy (ΔG)** of the water-splitting reaction:

$$E_{rev} = \frac{\Delta G}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- ΔG = 237.2 kJ/mol (at 25 °C, 1 atm, liquid water)
- n = 2 (electrons transferred per H₂ molecule)
- F = 96,485 C/mol (Faraday's constant)

$$E_{rev} = \frac{237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

#### Derivation of Thermoneutral Voltage

The thermoneutral voltage corresponds to the **total enthalpy change (ΔH)**:

$$V_{th} = \frac{\Delta H}{n \cdot F}$$

$$V_{th} = \frac{285{,}830 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}} = 1.481 \text{ V}$$

#### Overpotential — Why Real Cells Need More Voltage

The **actual cell voltage** is:

$$V_{cell} = E_{rev} + \vert \eta_{act} \vert + \vert \eta_{ohm} \vert + \vert \eta_{conc} \vert$$

where the three **overpotentials (η)** represent kinetic losses, ohmic (resistive) losses, and mass-transport (concentration) losses respectively.

#### Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (MASS of H₂ produced)

$$\boxed{\; m_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F} \;}$$

where:
- $m_{H_2}$ = mass of hydrogen produced (grams)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $M_{H_2}$ = 2.016 g/mol (molar mass of H₂)
- $n$ = 2 (electrons)
- $F$ = 96,485 C/mol

#### Volumetric Hydrogen Production Rate

At standard conditions (0 °C, 1 atm), 1 mol of H₂ occupies 22.4 L. Thus:

$$Q_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \times 22{,}400 \;\text{mL}$$

#### Electrolyzer Efficiency (η_elec)

$$\eta_{elec} = \frac{\text{HHV of H}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{Electrical energy input}} = \frac{m_{H_2} \cdot 142 \text{ MJ/kg}}{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}$$

Or equivalently using LHV:

$$\eta_{elec} = \frac{V_{th}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{1.481}{V_{cell}}$$

### 2.3 Thermochemical Cycles — Theory

A **closed thermochemical cycle** uses high-temperature heat + a series of chemical reactions to split water, with all reagents recycled. The S-I (Sulfur–Iodine) cycle is the benchmark:

**Step 1 (Bunsen reaction, 20–120 °C):**
$$2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{SO}_2 + \text{I}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HI}$$

**Step 2 (Sulfuric acid decomposition, ≈ 850 °C):**
$$\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$$

**Step 3 (Hydriodic acid decomposition, ≈ 300–450 °C):**
$$2\text{HI} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{I}_2$$

> Net: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2$ — same as electrolysis, but driven by **heat** instead of electricity.

### 2.4 Hydrogen Storage — The Four Pillars

1. **Compressed Gas (CGH₂):** 350 bar (Type III) or 700 bar (Type IV) carbon-fiber tanks.
2. **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂):** Cryogenic storage at −253 °C; boil-off losses ≈ 0.1–1 %/day.
3. **Metal Hydrides (MH):** e.g., LaNi₅H₆, MgH₂; chemical absorption/reaction.
4. **Chemical Carriers:** Ammonia (NH₃), Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHCs like toluene/methylcyclohexane), methanol.

**Volumetric & Gravimetric Energy Density of Storage Methods** — vital for KTU comparison questions:

$$\rho_{grav} = \frac{m_{H_2}}{m_{system}} \quad ; \quad \rho_{vol} = \frac{m_{H_2}}{V_{system}}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> **No vertical pipes used; absolute values shown with \vert.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reversible Cell Voltage | $E_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$ | 1.229 V |
| 2 | Thermoneutral Voltage | $V_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{nF}$ | 1.481 V |
| 3 | Actual Cell Voltage | $V_{cell} = E_{rev} + \eta_{act} + \eta_{ohm} + \eta_{conc}$ | 1.8 – 2.5 V (typical) |
| 4 | Faraday's Mass Law | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F}$ | grams |
| 5 | Volumetric Production | $Q_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{nF} \times 22{,}400$ | mL (STP) |
| 6 | Electrolyzer Efficiency | $\eta_{elec} = \dfrac{V_{th}}{V_{cell}}$ | 0.60 – 0.95 |
| 7 | HHV of H₂ | — | 142 MJ/kg |
| 8 | LHV of H₂ | — | 120 MJ/kg |
| 9 | Specific Energy of Cell | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{nF \cdot V_{th}}{M_{H_2}}$ | ≈ 1.48 × 10⁵ C·V / 2.016 g |
| 10 | Energy in Compressed H₂ | $E = m \cdot HHV$ | MJ |
| 11 | Carnot Heat Engine Limit | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Mass Flow to Energy | $\dot{E} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times LHV$ | W |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Grid-scale energy storage** — surplus solar/wind → electrolyze → store H₂ → fuel cell during peak demand.
- **Hard-to-abate sectors** — steel making (replace coking coal), ammonia, refineries, shipping fuel.
- **Mobility** — Fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) like Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, heavy trucks (Hyundai XCIENT).
- **Decarbonized heating** — hydrogen blending in natural gas networks (up to 20 % volume).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of Hydrogen Produced in Electrolysis (Faraday's Law — Exhaustive)

**Starting relation** — for any electrochemical reaction, the **moles of substance liberated** are proportional to the **charge passed**:

$$\text{Moles of H}_2 = \frac{Q}{n \cdot F}$$

where $Q = I \cdot t$ is the total charge in coulombs. Converting moles to mass using molar mass $M_{H_2}$:

$$m_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \times M_{H_2}$$

Substituting:

$$m_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \times M_{H_2}$$

Rearranging to standard form:

$$\boxed{\;m_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F}\;}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
An alkaline electrolyzer operates at 500 A for 8 hours. Calculate the mass of H₂ produced.

> **Step 1 — Identify variables:** I = 500 A; t = 8 h = 8 × 3600 = 28,800 s; M_H₂ = 2.016 g/mol; n = 2; F = 96,485 C/mol.

> **Step 2 — Substitute into Faraday's equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{500 \times 28{,}800 \times 2.016}{2 \times 96{,}485} \;\text{grams} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{29{,}030{,}400}{192{,}970} \;\text{grams} \\[4pt]
&= 150.46 \;\text{grams} \;\approx\; 0.1505 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 3 — Cross-check via energy basis:** Energy supplied = $V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t$ = 2.0 V × 500 × 28,800 = 28.8 MJ. HHV of 0.1505 kg = 0.1505 × 142 = 21.37 MJ → efficiency ≈ 74 %, consistent with a real alkaline cell.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Reversible & Thermoneutral Voltages from First Principles

**Step A — Reference thermochemical data at 25 °C, 1 atm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reaction:}\quad 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} &\rightarrow 2\text{H}_{2(g)} + \text{O}_{2(g)} \\[4pt]
\Delta H^\circ &= +285.83 \;\text{kJ/mol} \\[4pt]
\Delta G^\circ &= +237.13 \;\text{kJ/mol} \\[4pt]
\Delta S^\circ &= +163.10 \;\text{J/(mol·K)} \\[4pt]
n &= 2 \;\text{electrons per H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Compute the entropy term and verify thermodynamic consistency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \cdot \Delta S^\circ &= 298.15 \;\text{K} \times 163.10 \;\text{J/(mol·K)} \\[4pt]
&= 48{,}628 \;\text{J/mol} = 48.63 \;\text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Checking: $\Delta H = \Delta G + T\Delta S \Rightarrow 237.13 + 48.63 = 285.76 \;\text{kJ/mol}$ ✓ (matches ΔH within rounding).

**Step C — Reversible voltage (from Gibbs free energy):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{rev} &= \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{n \cdot F} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{237{,}130 \;\text{J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \;\text{C/mol}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{237{,}130}{192{,}970} \;\text{V} \\[4pt]
&= 1.229 \;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Thermoneutral voltage (from enthalpy):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{th} &= \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{n \cdot F} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{285{,}830 \;\text{J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \;\text{C/mol}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{285{,}830}{192{,}970} \;\text{V} \\[4pt]
&= 1.481 \;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The gap $V_{th} - E_{rev} = 0.252 \;\text{V}$ corresponds exactly to the **$T\Delta S$ term**, i.e., the heat that must be supplied (or absorbed) to keep the cell isothermal. At $V_{cell} = 1.481$ V, the cell is **thermoneutral** — no external heating/cooling is required; the ohmic + overpotential losses equal $T\Delta S/nF$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hydrogen Liquefaction Energy Penalty (Analytical Exercise)

The **theoretical minimum work** to liquefy 1 kg of H₂ from 25 °C to −253 °C equals the change in Gibbs free energy of a reversible refrigeration cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{min} &= \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \left(1 - \frac{T_0}{T}\right) \cdot c_p(T) \, dT
\end{aligned}
$$

Using average $c_p$ ≈ 14.3 kJ/(kg·K) for H₂ gas over this range and $T_0 = 298$ K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{min} &\approx c_p \left[(T_1 - T_2) - T_0 \ln\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right] \\[4pt]
&\approx 14.3 \times \left[276 - 298 \times \ln\frac{298}{20.28}\right] \\[4pt]
&\approx 14.3 \times \left[276 - 298 \times 2.694\right] \\[4pt]
&\approx 14.3 \times \left[276 - 802.8\right] \;\text{kJ/kg} \\[4pt]
&\approx -7{,}520 \;\text{kJ/kg} = 7.52 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Liquefying 1 kg of H₂ requires **at minimum ≈ 7.52 MJ**, which represents:

$$
\frac{7.52 \;\text{MJ/kg}}{120 \;\text{MJ/kg (LHV)}} \times 100\% \approx 6.3\% \;\text{of H}_2 \;\text{energy content}
$$

Real liquefiers consume 10–13 kWh/kg ≈ **36–47 MJ/kg**, i.e., **30–40 % of LHV** lost — a critical engineering trade-off.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Compressed Hydrogen Tank Energy Footprint

For an isothermal compression of an ideal gas (upper bound):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{comp,iso} &= nRT \ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

For 1 kg of H₂, n = 1,000/2.016 = 496 mol. Compressing from 1 atm to 700 bar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{comp} &= 496 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \ln\frac{700}{1} \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}229{,}000 \times 6.551 \;\text{J/kg} \\[4pt]
&\approx 8.05 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Real multi-stage compressors with isentropic efficiency ≈ 70 % require ≈ 11–12 MJ/kg → **≈ 9–10 % of LHV**. This is the **parasitic energy cost** of compressed hydrogen storage.

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three physical properties of hydrogen that make it suitable as a fuel. Mention one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Suitable properties:**
1. **Highest energy content per unit mass (HHV = 142 MJ/kg)** — about 2.75 times gasoline. (1 mark)
2. **Clean combustion product** — burning hydrogen produces only **water vapor** (H₂O), no CO₂, CO, SOₓ, NOₓ (with proper combustion), or particulates. (1 mark)
3. **Can be produced from water by electrolysis** — making it a renewable/closed-loop fuel with abundant feedstock. (1 mark)

**Limitation:** Hydrogen has a **very low volumetric energy density** (≈ 8.5 MJ/L as liquid) compared to gasoline (≈ 32 MJ/L); storage and transportation are energy-intensive and costly. (Bonus point — examiners love this)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Grey Hydrogen | Green Hydrogen |
|---|---|---|
| **Production route** | Steam methane reforming (SMR) of natural gas | Electrolysis of water using renewable electricity |
| **Carbon emissions** | High (≈ 9–10 kg CO₂/kg H₂) | Negligible (zero direct CO₂) |
| **Example** | Industrial H₂ for ammonia/petroleum refining (e.g., Ras Tanura SMR plant) | PEM electrolyzer powered by solar PV plant (e.g., NEOM project, KSA) |
| **Cost** | Lower (≈ 1–2 USD/kg) | Higher (≈ 3–6 USD/kg currently) |

(1 mark per row × 3 rows = 3 marks)

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ✍️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### **Q.A(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain with a neat schematic the working of an Alkaline Water Electrolyzer. Derive the expression for mass of hydrogen produced using Faraday's law.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Construction & Working (4 marks):**
An alkaline electrolyzer consists of two electrodes (Nickel-coated steel anode and cathode) immersed in a **25–30 % KOH aqueous electrolyte** separated by a porous diaphragm (asbestos historically, now PPS/Zirfon).

**Electrochemical reactions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Anode (oxidation):}\quad 2\text{OH}^- &\rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \\[4pt]
\text{Cathode (reduction):}\quad 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- &\rightarrow \text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \\[4pt]
\text{Net:}\quad 2\text{H}_2\text{O} &\rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Operating temperature: 60–80 °C; pressure: 1–30 bar; current density: 0.2–0.4 A/cm²; efficiency: 60–70 %.

**Faraday's Law Derivation (3 marks):**

Charge passed in time *t* is $Q = I \cdot t$. By Faraday's first law, the mass *m* of substance liberated is proportional to charge:

$$m \propto Q \Rightarrow m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

where Z is the electrochemical equivalent. From second law, *Z* = M / (nF). Substituting:

$$\boxed{\;m_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F}\;}$$

For H₂: M = 2.016 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol.

**Numerical:** I = 1000 A, t = 1 h = 3600 s → m = (1000 × 3600 × 2.016)/(2 × 96485) ≈ 37.6 g/h of H₂.

---

#### **Q.A(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**A PEM electrolyzer operates at 2.0 V cell voltage and 250 A current for 10 hours. Calculate (i) mass of H₂ produced, (ii) volume of H₂ at STP, (iii) electrolyzer efficiency. Given: M(H₂) = 2.016 g/mol, F = 96,485 C/mol, LHV = 120 MJ/kg.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** I = 250 A, t = 10 h = 36,000 s, V_cell = 2.0 V, M_H₂ = 2.016 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol, V_th = 1.481 V.

**(i) Mass of H₂ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{250 \times 36{,}000 \times 2.016}{2 \times 96{,}485} \;\text{g} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{18{,}144{,}000}{192{,}970} \;\text{g} \\[4pt]
&= 94.02 \;\text{g} \;\approx\; 0.094 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Volume at STP (2 marks):**

At STP, 1 mol of H₂ occupies 22.4 L. Moles produced = m/M = 94.02/2.016 = 46.64 mol.

$$V_{H_2} = 46.64 \times 22.4 = 1{,}044.7 \;\text{L} \;\approx\; 1.045 \;\text{m}^3$$

**(iii) Efficiency (3 marks):**

Electrical energy input:

$$E_{in} = V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t = 2.0 \times 250 \times 36{,}000 = 1.8 \times 10^7 \;\text{J} = 18 \;\text{MJ}$$

Energy content of H₂ produced (LHV basis):

$$E_{out} = m_{H_2} \times LHV = 0.094 \;\text{kg} \times 120 \;\text{MJ/kg} = 11.28 \;\text{MJ}$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta_{elec} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \frac{11.28}{18} = 0.6267 \;\Rightarrow\; 62.67\%$$

**Cross-check via thermoneutral method:**

$$\eta_{elec} = \frac{V_{th}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{1.481}{2.0} = 0.7405 = 74.05\%$$

(The 11 % gap between 62.67 % and 74.05 % reflects use of LHV vs. HHV — students often forget this distinction.)

---

### ✍️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### **Q.B(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Describe the Sulfur–Iodine thermochemical cycle for hydrogen production. What are its main advantages and limitations?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The S-I cycle is a **closed thermochemical water-splitting cycle** developed at General Atomics (USA) in the 1970s. It uses only **heat + water** as inputs and produces H₂ + O₂; all intermediate reagents (SO₂, I₂, H₂SO₄, HI) are internally recycled.

**The three reactions (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Bunsen Reaction (exothermic, 20–120 °C):**

$$2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{SO}_2 + \text{I}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HI}$$

Produces two immiscible acid phases: a heavier H₂SO₄ phase and a lighter HI/I₂/H₂O phase — separated by gravity.

**Step 2 — Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (endothermic, ≈ 850 °C):**

$$\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$$

**Step 3 — Hydriodic Acid Decomposition (endothermic, 300–450 °C):**

$$2\text{HI} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{I}_2$$

**Net reaction:** $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2$ — same as direct electrolysis.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Theoretically achievable efficiency ≈ 50–52 % (highest among thermochemical cycles).
- All reagents recycled → zero continuous chemical consumption.
- Pairs well with very high-temperature heat sources (nuclear HTGR, solar concentrators).

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Requires very corrosive (concentrated H₂SO₄, HI) handling — materials challenge.
- Maximum cycle temperature ≈ 850 °C → needs high-grade heat source.
- Complex process control; not yet commercial at GW scale.

---

#### **Q.B(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Compare four hydrogen storage methods in a table. Why is hydrogen storage more challenging than that of conventional fuels?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Comparative Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Compressed Gas (700 bar) | Liquid H₂ (cryogenic) | Metal Hydride (MgH₂) | LOHC (Toluene/MCH) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Storage T (°C) | 25 | −253 | 25–300 | 25–250 |
| Storage P (bar) | 700 | 1–5 | 1–10 | 1–5 |
| Gravimetric density (wt %) | 4–5 | ≈ 100 (pure H₂) | 7–8 (theor.) | 6–7 |
| Volumetric density (kg H₂/m³) | 40 | 71 | 50–100 | 40–50 |
| Energy penalty | 9–10 % of LHV | 30–40 % of LHV | Heat to release H₂ | Heat to release H₂ |
| Boil-off / loss | None | 0.1–1 %/day | Negligible | Negligible |
| Maturity | Commercial | Commercial | R&D | Pilot scale |
| Best use case | FCEV cars | Rockets, bulk transport | Stationary, niche | Shipping, long-haul |

**Why H₂ storage is harder than gasoline/diesel (3 marks):**

1. **Molecular size & density:** H₂ is the smallest molecule with extremely low volumetric density (gas = 0.09 kg/m³). It must be **compressed ~700 times** or **cooled to cryogenic temperatures** to match gasoline's volumetric energy density.
2. **Embrittlement:** Atomic hydrogen diffuses into metal lattices, causing **hydrogen embrittlement** of tanks and pipelines → specialized alloys/composites (carbon-fiber Type IV tanks) required, raising cost.
3. **Boil-off & leakage:** Hydrogen's tiny molecule causes it to leak through seals; cryogenic LH₂ suffers continuous boil-off, demanding reliquefaction.
4. **Energy penalty:** Compressing to 700 bar costs ≈ 10 % of H₂'s LHV; liquefying costs 30–40 %. By contrast, pumping gasoline consumes < 0.1 % of its energy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read this to avoid losing 2–3 marks:**
> 1. **Don't confuse 1.229 V with 1.481 V.** State explicitly whether you are computing **reversible** or **thermoneutral** voltage, and from **ΔG** or **ΔH** respectively. Examiners deduct 1 mark for mixing them up.
> 2. **In Faraday's law, use M_H₂ = 2.016 g/mol, NOT 1 g/mol.** Hydrogen is diatomic — examiners frequently catch this mistake.
> 3. **HHV vs. LHV:** When the question states "energy content of H₂", specify whether you're using 142 MJ/kg (HHV) or 120 MJ/kg (LHV). Default to LHV for fuel cell / engine problems; HHV for combustion problems where water condenses.
> 4. **For thermochemical cycles:** State that all reagents (SO₂, I₂, H₂SO₄, HI) are **recycled** — the cycle is **closed**. Forgetting this loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Storage question trap:** Don't write "hydrogen is highly flammable" as the main storage challenge — examiners expect the **volumetric density, embrittlement, and boil-off** arguments. Flammability is a safety issue, not a storage issue.
> 6. **In the S-I cycle, do NOT write SO₃ as a product of Bunsen reaction** — the correct product is **H₂SO₄** (sulfuric acid).

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Hydrogen is an energy CARRIER, not an energy SOURCE** — distinguish clearly in definition questions.
- [x] **HHV = 142 MJ/kg; LHV = 120 MJ/kg** — memorize both.
- [x] **Reversible voltage = 1.229 V** (from ΔG); **Thermoneutral voltage = 1.481 V** (from ΔH).
- [x] **Faraday's law:** $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F}$; M_H₂ = 2.016 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol.
- [x] **Electrolyzer efficiency:** $\eta_{elec} = \dfrac{V_{th}}{V_{cell}}$ (thermodynamic ceiling).
- [x] **Three electrolysis technologies:** Alkaline (mature, cheap), PEM (flexible), SOEC (highest efficiency, high T).
- [x] **S-I cycle net reaction:** $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2$, max temperature ≈ 850 °C, closed cycle.
- [x] **Four storage methods:** Compressed gas (700 bar), Liquid H₂ (−253 °C), Metal hydrides, LOHC.
- [x] **Storage energy penalties:** Compression ~10 % LHV; Liquefaction ~30–40 % LHV.
- [x] **End uses:** Fuel cells (PEMFC, SOFC), FCEVs, industrial ammonia/refinery feedstock, gas-grid blending.
- [x] **Color codes:** Grey (SMR), Blue (SMR+CCS), Green (renewable electrolysis), Brown (coal), Turquoise (methane pyrolysis), Pink (nuclear).
- [x] **Actual cell voltage** = E_rev + η_act + η_ohm + η_conc; real cells operate 1.8–2.5 V.
- [x] **Hydrogen embrittlement** is a key materials challenge for storage and pipelines.
- [x] **Safety:** Wide flammability range (4–75 %), low ignition energy (0.02 mJ), rises rapidly (lighter than air) → must be addressed in every KTU safety discussion.
- [x] **Stoichiometry check:** 1 kg H₂ + 8 kg O₂ → 9 kg H₂O; 1 kg H₂ releases 142 MJ HHV → 9 kg of water.
